**(b)** Describe $L(M)$ in plain English. Draw the state diagram and identify the class of languages this DFA belongs to. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Formal Proofs (7 marks):**

**Proof 1: $w = abab \in L(M)$**

We compute $\delta^*(p, abab)$ step by step using the recursive definition.

*Base step ŌĆö read first symbol $a$ (i.e., process substring "a" with $w = \varepsilon$):*

$$\delta^*(p, a) = \delta(\delta^*(p, \varepsilon), a) = \delta(p, a) = q$$

*Inductive step ŌĆö read next symbol $b$ (process "ab"):*

$$\delta^*(p, ab) = \delta(\delta^*(p, a), b) = \delta(q, b) = p$$

*Inductive step ŌĆö read next symbol $a$ (process "aba"):*

$$\delta^*(p, aba) = \delta(\delta^*(p, ab), a) = \delta(p, a) = q$$

*Inductive step ŌĆö read final symbol $b$ (process "abab"):*

$$\delta^*(p, abab) = \delta(\delta^*(p, aba), b) = \delta(q, b) = p$$

*Conclusion:* We have $\delta^*(p, abab) = p$. Since $p \notin F = \{r\}$, we must conclude that **$abab \notin L(M)$** ŌĆö a useful lesson in always checking the final state.

Let us try a corrected example: $w = aab$.

$$\delta^*(p, a) = \delta(p, a) = q$$

$$\delta^*(p, aa) = \delta(q, a) = r$$

$$\delta^*(p, aab) = \delta(r, b) = r \in F$$

So $aab \in L(M)$. Ō£ō

**Proof 2: $\varepsilon \notin L(M)$**

By the base case of the extended transition function:

$$\delta^*(p, \varepsilon) = p$$

Since $p \notin F$ (only $r$ is final), by the definition of $L(M)$:

$$\varepsilon \notin L(M) \quad \blacksquare$$

*(Marks breakdown: 4 marks for step-by-step $\delta^*$ trace, 1 mark for invoking definition of acceptance, 1 mark for $\varepsilon$ base case, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

**Part (b) ŌĆö Plain English Description and State Diagram (7 marks):**

**State semantics:**
- $p$ = "no $a$'s seen recently, or last symbol was $b$" (initial)
- $q$ = "exactly one $a$ has been read consecutively" (last symbol was $a$)
- $r$ = "two or more consecutive $a$'s have been read" (accepting trap)

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sP
    sP --> sQ : a
    sP --> sP : b
    sQ --> sR : a
    sQ --> sP : b
    sR --> sR : a
    sR --> sR : b
```

**Plain English description of $L(M)$:**

> $L(M)$ is the set of all strings over $\{a, b\}$ that contain the substring $aa$ (i.e., at least two consecutive $a$'s appear somewhere in the string).

**Class of languages:** This DFA accepts a **regular language**. The corresponding regular expression is $(a + b)^* aa (a + b)^*$.

*(Marks breakdown: 2 marks for state semantics, 2 marks for state diagram, 2 marks for plain English description, 1 mark for identifying the language class.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Language of a DFA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$ base case** ŌĆö many students begin traces assuming $w \neq \varepsilon$. Always state $\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q$ explicitly when needed. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing the order of induction** ŌĆö the recursive rule is $\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$ where $a$ is the **last** symbol, not the first. Reading right-to-left is a frequent error. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Not marking the start state with an incoming arrow** in diagrams ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct a mark for missing the start arrow. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting to identify final states** with double circles. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Conflating $L(M)$ with $M$ itself** ŌĆö $L(M)$ is a *set of strings*, $M$ is a *machine*. Examiners look for precise notation. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Mis-stating the acceptance formula** as $\delta(q_0, w)$ instead of $\delta^*(q_0, w)$. The starred version is required for strings of length $\geq 2$. **(-2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Language of a DFA**

- **DFA tuple:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ ŌĆö memorize the 5 components and their types.
- **Acceptance formula (HIGH-PRIORITY):** $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \in F\}$.
- **Extended transition function:**
    - Base: $\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q$
    - Recursive: $\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$ ŌĆö *with $a$ as the rightmost symbol*.
- **$\varepsilon$ membership rule:** $\varepsilon \in L(M) \iff q_0 \in F$.
- **Determinism rule:** $\delta$ must be *total* ŌĆö every state-symbol pair has **exactly one** defined transition.
- **Number of transitions** in a complete DFA over $\Sigma$: $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$.
- **All DFA languages are regular**, and all regular languages are accepted by some DFA (Kleene's Theorem ŌĆö Module 2).
- **$\Sigma^*$ is the universe** of all possible strings; $L(M) \subseteq \Sigma^*$ always.
- **Complement trick:** Swap $F$ with $Q \setminus F$ to get $\overline{L(M)}$.
- **Product construction:** Used to prove closure under intersection (Module 2).
- **Empty language DFA:** $F = \emptyset$ (or $F$ unreachable from $q_0$).
- **Universal language DFA:** $F = Q$, so $L(M) = \Sigma^*$.
- **Always draw the start arrow** and mark final states with double circles in diagrams.
- **Tracing order:** read input left-to-right, update state symbol by symbol.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Language in Formal Language Theory?

Before we define a **Regular Language**, we must establish the foundational building blocks of formal language theory. Every language is constructed from a hierarchy of abstractions.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Foundational Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **alphabet** ($\Sigma$) is a finite, non-empty set of symbols. A **string** (or word) is a finite sequence of symbols drawn from $\Sigma$. A **language** is any set of strings over an alphabet $\Sigma$.

For example, let $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$. Then the set $L = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$ is a language containing all binary strings of length **2**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Regular Language

A language $L$ over alphabet $\Sigma$ is called a **Regular Language** if and only if it can be described by one of the following three equivalent formalisms:

1. **Regular Expression (RE)** ŌĆö A compact, algebraic descriptive notation.
2. **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** ŌĆö A deterministic state machine with finite memory.
3. **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** ŌĆö A generalized state machine allowing parallel computation paths.

Kleene's Theorem (1956) formally proves that these three representations describe exactly the same class of languages, namely the **regular languages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The class of regular languages is the **smallest class** of languages containing the empty set $\emptyset$, the empty string $\{\varepsilon\}$, and the singleton sets $\{a\}$ for each $a \in \Sigma$, **closed** under union, concatenation, and Kleene star.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Vending Machine

Imagine a simple coin-operated vending machine. The machine has a **finite number of states**: it remembers whether you have inserted Ōé╣5, Ōé╣10, Ōé╣15, or Ōé╣20. When you insert a coin (an input symbol), it deterministically transitions to a new state. The machine dispenses a product (accepts the string) only when it reaches the "Ready" state after receiving a valid sequence of coins.

> **Analogy Mapping:**
> - **Alphabets** $\rightarrow$ The set of coin denominations (Ōé╣5, Ōé╣10).
> - **Strings** $\rightarrow$ Sequences of coins inserted.
> - **States** $\rightarrow$ The cumulative money tracker inside the machine.
> - **Accepting State** $\rightarrow$ The "Ready to dispense" indicator.
> - **Regular Language** $\rightarrow$ The set of all valid coin sequences that successfully release a product.

The machine has **finite memory** ŌĆö it cannot remember an arbitrary history of coins. This "finite memory" is precisely what makes a language regular.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Regular languages underpin real-world systems such as **lexical analyzers in compilers**, **pattern matching in text editors (grep, regex)**, **network protocol validation**, **digital circuit design**, and **search engines**. Every time you validate an email format or check a password pattern, you are using the theory of regular languages.

## 1.4 The Set of All Possible Languages

Given an alphabet $\Sigma$, there are uncountably infinite possible languages (since the power set of $\Sigma^*$ is uncountable). However, the set of **regular languages** is only countably infinite. This means most languages are **not** regular ŌĆö a profound fact we will explore using the **Pumping Lemma**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:**
> Students often confuse "regular" with "common" or "simple." In formal language theory, *regular* has a strictly mathematical meaning and refers to languages recognized by finite-state machines ŌĆö **not** languages that are easy to speak!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Foundational Mathematical Notation

| **Symbol** | **Name** | **Meaning** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\Sigma$ | Alphabet | Finite set of symbols |
| $\Sigma^*$ | Kleene Star (Universal Set) | Set of all strings over $\Sigma$ including $\varepsilon$ |
| $\Sigma^+$ | Kleene Plus | Set of all non-empty strings over $\Sigma$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Empty String | String of length zero |
| $\vert w \vert$ | Length of String | Number of symbols in string $w$ |
| $L_1 \cup L_2$ | Union | Strings in $L_1$ or $L_2$ or both |
| $L_1 \cdot L_2$ | Concatenation | Strings formed by joining a string from $L_1$ with one from $L_2$ |
| $L^*$ | Kleene Star | Zero or more concatenations of $L$ |
| $L^+$ | Kleene Plus | One or more concatenations of $L$ |
| $w^R$ | Reverse of $w$ | $w$ read backwards |
| $L^R$ | Reverse Language | $\{w^R \mid w \in L\}$ |
| $\emptyset$ | Empty Set | Language with no strings |
| $\mid$ | Such That | Set-builder notation separator |

## 2.2 Regular Expression ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Regular Expression (RE)** over alphabet $\Sigma$ is defined recursively as follows:

**Base Cases:**
- $\emptyset$ is a regular expression denoting the empty set.
- $\varepsilon$ is a regular expression denoting the set $\{\varepsilon\}$.
- For each $a \in \Sigma$, $a$ is a regular expression denoting the set $\{a\}$.

**Recursive Cases:** If $r_1$ and $r_2$ are regular expressions, then so are:
- $(r_1 \mid r_2)$ ŌĆö Union (some textbooks use $+$ or $\cup$).
- $(r_1 \cdot r_2)$ ŌĆö Concatenation (often written as $r_1 r_2$).
- $(r_1^*)$ ŌĆö Kleene Star (zero or more repetitions).
- $(r_1^+)$ ŌĆö Kleene Plus (one or more repetitions), equivalent to $r_1 \cdot r_1^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precedence Order (KTU Board Standard):**
> Highest to lowest: **Kleene star $(*)$** $\rightarrow$ **Concatenation $(\cdot)$** $\rightarrow$ **Union $(\mid)$**.
> Example: $a \cdot b^* \mid c$ is parsed as $((a \cdot (b^*)) \mid c)$.

## 2.3 Precedence Table for Regular Expression Operators

| **Precedence Level** | **Operator** | **Description** | **Example** |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (Highest) | $*$ | Kleene Star (zero or more) | $a^*$ |
| 2 | $\cdot$ | Concatenation | $ab$ |
| 3 (Lowest) | $\mid$ | Union (Alternation) | $a \mid b$ |

## 2.4 Algebraic Laws of Regular Expressions

These laws are crucial for **simplification** questions in KTU exams:

> [!NOTE]
> **Identities for Regular Expressions** (where $r$, $s$, $t$ are any REs):

**Commutative Law:**
- $r \mid s = s \mid r$

**Associative Laws:**
- $(r \mid s) \mid t = r \mid (s \mid t)$
- $(rs)t = r(st)$

**Distributive Laws:**
- $r(s \mid t) = rs \mid rt$
- $(s \mid t)r = sr \mid tr$

**Identity Elements:**
- $r \mid \emptyset = r$
- $r \cdot \varepsilon = \varepsilon \cdot r = r$

**Annihilator (Zero Element):**
- $r \cdot \emptyset = \emptyset \cdot r = \emptyset$

**Idempotent Law:**
- $r^* = (r^*)^* = (r^*)r^* = r^* r^*$

**Absorption Laws:**
- $r^* = (r \mid \varepsilon)^*$
- $r^* = \varepsilon \mid r \mid rr \mid rrr \mid \dots$

## 2.5 Closure Properties of Regular Languages

Regular languages are **closed** under the following operations. This means if $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, the resulting language is also regular.

| **Operation** | **Definition** | **Closed?** |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Union | $L_1 \cup L_2$ | **Yes** |
| Intersection | $L_1 \cap L_2$ | **Yes** |
| Complement | $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$ | **Yes** |
| Concatenation | $L_1 \cdot L_2$ | **Yes** |
| Kleene Star | $L_1^*$ | **Yes** |
| Reversal | $L^R$ | **Yes** |
| Difference | $L_1 - L_2$ | **Yes** |
| Homomorphism | $h(L)$ | **Yes** |
| Inverse Homomorphism | $h^{-1}(L)$ | **Yes** |
| Quotient | $L_1 / L_2$ | **Yes** |
| Substitution | $f(L)$ | **Yes** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Closure Matters in Engineering:**
> If you build a DFA for pattern $A$ and another DFA for pattern $B$, you can automatically construct a DFA for the combined pattern using the **Cartesian Product Construction**. This is the foundation of compiler lexer generators like **Lex** and **Flex**.

## 2.6 Finite Automaton ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a 5-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

where:
- $Q$ is a finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ is a finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the alphabet).
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the **transition function**.
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the **initial (start) state**.
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **accepting (final) states**.

A **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** has the same components except:
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_{\varepsilon} \rightarrow 2^Q$ (returns a set of states, including the empty set $\varepsilon$-transitions).
- A string is accepted if **at least one** computation path ends in a final state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Kleene's Theorem ŌĆö The Grand Unification

> **Kleene's Theorem (1956):**
> A language $L$ is regular if and only if it is recognized by some DFA, recognized by some NFA, and described by some regular expression.

This is the **most important theorem** in Module 1. It is the bridge connecting three equivalent representations. The proof consists of three sub-proofs:

**Proof Sketch Roadmap:**

1. **RE $\Rightarrow$ NFA:** Convert the regular expression to an NFA using Thompson's Construction.
2. **NFA $\Rightarrow$ DFA:** Convert the NFA to a DFA using the Subset Construction.
3. **DFA $\Rightarrow$ RE:** Convert the DFA to a regular expression using State Elimination.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation ŌĆö Constructing a DFA

**Problem:** Design a DFA that accepts all binary strings over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that contain the substring **"01"**.

**Step 1: Identify the States**

We need to track the progress of scanning for "01":
- $q_0$: Initial state (no relevant suffix seen).
- $q_1$: Last symbol read was a "0" (potential start of "01").
- $q_2$: Accepting state ŌĆö "01" has been seen.

**Step 2: Define the Transition Function $\delta$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= q_0 \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= q_2 \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= q_2 \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= q_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Define the Components**

$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_2\})
$$

**Step 4: Verification of a Sample String**

Consider the string $w = 00101$. We trace the computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State Trace: } & q_0 \xrightarrow{0} q_1 \xrightarrow{0} q_1 \xrightarrow{1} q_2 \xrightarrow{0} q_2 \xrightarrow{1} q_2 \\
\text{Final State: } & q_2 \in F \Rightarrow \text{String is ACCEPTED.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Regular Expression for the Same Language**

The DFA accepts all strings containing "01" anywhere. The corresponding regular expression is:

$$
R = (0 \mid 1)^* \cdot 01 \cdot (0 \mid 1)^*
$$

## 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö RE to NFA via Thompson's Construction

**Problem:** Convert $R = (a \mid b)^* a$ to an NFA.

**Step 1: Decompose the Expression**

We process operators by structural induction on the regular expression tree.

- The base symbols $a$ and $b$ each become a 2-state NFA.
- Apply **union** for $a \mid b$ ŌĆö creates a new start state with $\varepsilon$-transitions branching to the two NFAs.
- Apply **star** for $(a \mid b)^*$ ŌĆö adds a new start state with $\varepsilon$-transitions to both an accepting copy of the inner NFA and a direct accept path.
- Apply **concatenation** with $a$ ŌĆö links the accepting state of the star to a new NFA for $a$.

**Step 2: Resulting NFA Structure**

The constructed NFA has the following property:

$$
N_{\text{RE}} = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_{\text{start}}, \{q_{\text{accept}}\})
$$

where $\vert Q_N \vert \leq 2 \times \text{(number of symbols and operators in R)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In Part B (14-mark) questions, when asked to convert RE to DFA, do it in two stages: (1) RE $\to$ NFA, then (2) NFA $\to$ DFA. Skipping a stage often loses 2-3 marks.

## 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö NFA to DFA via Subset Construction

**Problem:** Convert the NFA from Section 3.3 to a DFA.

**Step 1: Initialize the DFA Start State**

The start state of the DFA is the **$\varepsilon$-closure** of the NFA's start state. The $\varepsilon$-closure of a state $q$ is the set of all states reachable from $q$ using only $\varepsilon$-transitions.

$$
D_{\text{start}} = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_{\text{start}})
$$

**Step 2: Iteratively Compute Transitions**

For each new subset of NFA states $S$ and each input symbol $a$:

$$
\delta_D(S, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\left(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)\right)
$$

**Step 3: Identify Accepting States in the DFA**

A DFA state $S$ is accepting if and only if $S$ contains at least one NFA accepting state:

$$
F_D = \{S \subseteq Q_N \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}
$$

## 3.5 The Pumping Lemma ŌĆö Proving Non-Regularity

> **Pumping Lemma Statement:**
> If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p$ (the pumping length) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ satisfying:
> 1. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ (i.e., $y \neq \varepsilon$)
> 2. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ (loop is near the start)
> 3. $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ (the string can be pumped)

**Step-by-Step Proof Strategy (for non-regularity):**

**Step 1:** Assume for contradiction that $L$ is regular. By the pumping lemma, there exists a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose a specific string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$. This is the **adversarial choice** ŌĆö the string must be cleverly picked to break the pumping property.

**Step 3:** For every possible decomposition $s = xyz$ satisfying conditions (1) and (2), show that there exists some $i \geq 0$ such that $xy^i z \notin L$.

**Step 4:** This contradiction proves that $L$ cannot be regular.

**Worked Example ŌĆö $L = \{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\}$**

**Adversarial Choice:** Let $s = 0^p 1^p$ where $p$ is the pumping length.

**Decomposition:** The substring $y$ must lie entirely within the first $p$ symbols (by condition 2). Since $s$ starts with $p$ zeros, $y$ consists of only zeros: $y = 0^k$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Pump It:** Consider $i = 0$. Then $xy^0 z = 0^{p-k} 1^p$. This string has $p-k$ zeros followed by $p$ ones. Since $k \geq 1$, we have $p-k \neq p$, so the counts of zeros and ones are unequal. Therefore $xy^0 z \notin L$.

**Contradiction:** $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:**
> The Pumping Lemma is a **necessary** condition for regularity, NOT sufficient. A language satisfying the pumping lemma is not necessarily regular! (Counterexamples exist using the technique of "pumping on both sides.")

## 3.6 The Myhill-Nerode Theorem (Advanced)

> **Myhill-Nerode Theorem:**
> A language $L$ is regular if and only if the number of equivalence classes of the relation $\sim_L$ is finite, where:
> $$x \sim_L y \iff (\forall z \in \Sigma^*) \; (xz \in L \iff yz \in L)$$

**Intuition:** Two strings $x$ and $y$ are equivalent if no future input (suffix $z$) can distinguish whether we are at a point where $L$ would accept. The number of equivalence classes equals exactly the **minimum number of states** in any DFA recognizing $L$.

This theorem underpins DFA minimization algorithms such as **Hopcroft's Algorithm**.

## 3.7 Decision Problems for Regular Languages

| **Problem** | **Decidable?** | **Algorithm** |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Is $L$ empty? | Yes | Check if any accepting state is reachable from start. |
| Is $L$ finite? | Yes | Check for cycles on a path to an accepting state. |
| Is $L = \Sigma^*$? | Yes | Check if all states are accepting after minimization. |
| Are $L_1 = L_2$? | Yes | Check DFA equivalence via product construction. |
| Is $L$ regular? | Undecidable (in general) | For specific families, use the pumping lemma. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Chomsky Hierarchy ŌĆö Position of Regular Languages

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Chomsky Hierarchy] --> nodeA
    nodeA[Type 0 Recursively Enumerable] --> nodeB
    nodeB[Type 1 Context Sensitive] --> nodeC
    nodeC[Type 2 Context Free] --> nodeD
    nodeD[Type 3 Regular Languages]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[DFA Recognized]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[NFA Recognized]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Regular Expression Described]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE[Lowest Power Maximum Restriction]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeD3 --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF[Highest Power Minimum Restriction]
```

**Explanation:** Regular languages (Type 3) form the **most restricted** and computationally simplest class in the Chomsky hierarchy. Each higher class strictly contains the class below it.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a DFA Processor

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInput[Input Tape] --> nodeHead[Read Head]
    nodeHead --> nodeCtrl[Control Unit]
    nodeCtrl --> nodeTrans[Transition Function Delta]
    nodeTrans --> nodeState[Current State Register]
    nodeState --> nodeTrans
    nodeState --> nodeAccept{Accepting State Reached}
    nodeAccept --> nodeYes[ACCEPT String]
    nodeAccept --> nodeNo[REJECT String]
```

**Explanation:** A DFA processes input one symbol at a time. The control unit consults the transition function and the current state to determine the next state. When the input is exhausted, the final state determines accept or reject.

## 4.3 Equivalence Triangle ŌĆö Kleene's Theorem

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRE[Regular Expression] -->|Thompson Construction| nodeNFA
    nodeNFA -->|Subset Construction| nodeDFA
    nodeDFA -->|State Elimination| nodeRE
    nodeNFA -->|Direct Conversion| nodeDFA
    nodeDFA -->|Hopcroft Minimization| nodeDFAMin[Minimal DFA]
```

**Explanation:** All three representations are interconvertible. The triangle shows the bidirectional equivalence. The minimal DFA is the unique (up to renaming) most efficient DFA for a given language.

## 4.4 DFA State Diagram for Strings Ending in "01"

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ0((q0 Start)) -->|0| nodeQ1((q1))
    nodeQ0 -->|1| nodeQ0
    nodeQ1((q1 Seen 0)) -->|0| nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|1| nodeQ2(((q2 Accept)))
    nodeQ2 -->|0| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|1| nodeQ2
```

**Explanation:** 
- $q_0$: No relevant suffix yet.
- $q_1$: Just read a "0" (potential prefix of "01").
- $q_2$: Have read "01" ŌĆö this is a trap accepting state. Once we reach $q_2$, we stay there regardless of further input.

## 4.5 NFA State Diagram for RE = a Star ab

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS((Start)) -->|a| nodeA((A))
    nodeS -->|epsilon| nodeF(((Final Accept)))
    nodeA -->|epsilon| nodeS
    nodeA -->|b| nodeF
```

**Explanation:** This NFA has $\varepsilon$-transitions enabling the Kleene star. The $\varepsilon$-loop from $A$ back to $S$ allows multiple $a$'s, and the $\varepsilon$-transition from $S$ to the final state allows zero $a$'s.

## 4.6 Decision Procedure Flowchart ŌĆö Is a Language Regular?

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ1[Language L Given] --> nodeQ2{Finite memory sufficient?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3[Try to construct FA]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ4[Use Pumping Lemma for contradiction]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ5{FA Constructed Successfully?}
    nodeQ5 -->|Yes| nodeQ6[Regular Language]
    nodeQ5 -->|No| nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ7{Contradiction Found?}
    nodeQ7 -->|Yes| nodeQ8[Not Regular]
    nodeQ7 -->|No| nodeQ9[Possibly Regular Re-examine]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A ŌĆö SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define a *regular language*. State Kleene's theorem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A language $L$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is called a **regular language** if it can be recognized by a finite automaton (DFA or NFA), or equivalently, if it can be denoted by a regular expression.

**Kleene's Theorem** (1956): A language $L$ is regular if and only if it can be described by a regular expression, recognized by a DFA, and recognized by an NFA. In other words, the class of languages defined by regular expressions is exactly the class of languages recognized by finite automata.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is the difference between a DFA and an NFA? Give one example where an NFA is more compact than its equivalent DFA.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **DFA** | **NFA** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (single state) | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_{\varepsilon} \rightarrow 2^Q$ (set of states) |
| Determinism | Unique next state for each input | Multiple possible next states |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Acceptance | Reaching a single final state | At least one computation path reaches a final state |

**Example:** The language $L = \{w \in \{0, 1\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with } 11\}$ requires a DFA with 3 states but can be expressed as an NFA with only 3 states that is more intuitive. A more dramatic example is $L = \{w \in \{0, 1\}^* \mid \text{the 10th symbol from the right is 1}\}$, which requires an NFA with 11 states but a DFA with $2^{10} = 1024$ states.

---

## PART B ŌĆö LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Construct a DFA over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ that accepts all strings containing the substring **"aba"**. Show the transition table, formal definition, and verify with two sample strings.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Write a regular expression for the same language and prove its correctness using Kleene's theorem.

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify States (1 mark)**

States needed to track progress toward "aba":
- $q_0$: Initial state (no useful progress).
- $q_1$: Last symbol was "a" (prefix "a" matched).
- $q_2$: Last two symbols were "ab" (prefix "ab" matched).
- $q_3$: Substring "aba" detected ŌĆö **accepting state**.

**Step 2: Transition Table (3 marks)**

| **State** | **a** | **b** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| $q_2$ | $q_3$ | $q_0$ |
| $*(q_3)$ | $q_3$ | $q_3$ |

**Step 3: Formal Definition (1 mark)**

$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}, \{a, b\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_3\})
$$

**Step 4: Verification (2 marks)**

**Sample 1:** $w_1 = \text{"baba"}$
- $q_0 \xrightarrow{b} q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2 \xrightarrow{a} q_3$
- Final state $q_3 \in F$ ŌåÆ **ACCEPTED**.

**Sample 2:** $w_2 = \text{"abbab"}$
- $q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2 \xrightarrow{b} q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$
- Final state $q_2 \notin F$ ŌåÆ **REJECTED** (no "aba" found).

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Construct the Regular Expression (3 marks)**

The language consists of all strings containing "aba" as a substring. Any such string can be written as:

$$
R = (a \mid b)^* \, aba \, (a \mid b)^*
$$

Explanation:
- The prefix $(a \mid b)^*$ ŌĆö any string before "aba".
- The middle $aba$ ŌĆö the required substring.
- The suffix $(a \mid b)^*$ ŌĆö any string after "aba".

**Step 2: Convert RE to NFA using Thompson's Construction (2 marks)**

The NFA has:
- Start state $q_s$.
- An NFA for $(a \mid b)^*$ with $\varepsilon$-transitions.
- An NFA for $aba$ connected in sequence.
- An NFA for $(a \mid b)^*$ accepting into $q_f$.

**Step 3: Apply Kleene's Theorem (2 marks)**

By Kleene's theorem, since $R$ is a regular expression, there exists a DFA recognizing $L(R)$. The DFA constructed in part (a) is exactly that DFA. Thus, $L(R) = L(M)$, and the language is regular.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the **Pumping Lemma** for regular languages. Use it to prove that the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **not** regular.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the regular expression $R = (00 \mid 11)^*$. (i) Generate the minimal DFA. (ii) Show three example strings accepted by this DFA. (iii) Give a regular expression for the complement of $L(R)$.

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Pumping Lemma Statement (2 marks)**

> **Pumping Lemma:** If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (called the *pumping length*) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ where:
> 1. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
> 2. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$
> 3. $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Step 2: Proof Idea (2 marks)**

Since $L$ is regular, there exists a DFA $M$ with $p$ states recognizing $L$. For any string $s$ of length $\geq p$, by the Pigeonhole Principle, the DFA must visit some state twice. The portion of the string between these two visits forms the loop $y$, which can be repeated or removed without leaving the accepting state.

**Step 3: Proof for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ not being regular (3 marks)**

Assume for contradiction that $L$ is regular. Let $p$ be the pumping length.

**Adversarial Choice:** Pick $s = a^p b^p \in L$. Clearly $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$.

**Decomposition:** By the pumping lemma, $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. Since the first $p$ characters of $s$ are all $a$'s, the substring $y$ must consist entirely of $a$'s. Write $y = a^k$ where $1 \leq k \leq p$.

**Pumping Down:** Consider $i = 0$. Then $xy^0 z = a^{p-k} b^p$. Since $k \geq 1$, the number of $a$'s is strictly less than the number of $b$'s. Thus $a^{p-k} b^p \notin L$.

**Contradiction:** This violates condition (3) of the pumping lemma. Therefore, $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**(i) Minimal DFA for $R = (00 \mid 11)^*$ (3 marks)**

The language consists of all strings of even length where the symbols appear in pairs ŌĆö specifically, pairs of "00" or "11" concatenated. The minimal DFA has three states:

| **State** | **0** | **1** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\rightarrow q_0$ (empty) | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| $q_1$ (read "0") | $q_0$ | **dead** |
| $q_2$ (read "1") | **dead** | $q_0$ |
| $*q_0$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

Accepting state: $q_0$ (empty string accepted).

**(ii) Three example accepted strings (1 mark)**

- $\varepsilon$ (empty string)
- "0011" 
- "000011110000"

**(iii) Regular Expression for the Complement (3 marks)**

The complement $\overline{L(R)} = \Sigma^* - L(R)$ consists of all strings that **do not** consist of pairs of matching symbols. The minimal DFA for the complement is obtained by swapping accepting and non-accepting states in the DFA above. The corresponding regular expression is:

$$
\overline{L(R)} = (0 \mid 1)^* \, (01 \mid 10) \, (0 \mid 1)^*
$$

This captures all strings containing an adjacent "01" or "10" pattern, which breaks the rule of identical-symbol pairs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify all three pumping lemma conditions** in proofs ŌĆö students often only check condition (3) and miss (1) and (2). This costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DFA and NFA transition functions** ŌĆö DFAs return a single state, NFAs return a set. Using the wrong notation loses a full mark.
> 3. **Incorrect state minimization** ŌĆö students often produce a correct but non-minimal DFA. For full marks in 14-mark questions, you must show the minimization steps (e.g., table-filling algorithm).
> 4. **Writing RE ambiguity** ŌĆö always parenthesize the RE to avoid precedence errors. The expression $ab^* \mid c$ means $(a(b^*)) \mid c$, not $((ab)^*) \mid c$.
> 5. **Pumping Lemma misconception** ŌĆö many students use the pumping lemma to PROVE regularity. It only proves NON-regularity. To prove regularity, construct a FA or write an RE.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Regular Languages**

**Core Definitions to Memorize:**
- [ ] Alphabet, string, language, Kleene closure $\Sigma^*$, $\Sigma^+$
- [ ] Regular expression ŌĆö base cases and recursive cases
- [ ] DFA ŌĆö the 5-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$
- [ ] NFA ŌĆö differences from DFA (sets of states, $\varepsilon$-transitions)
- [ ] Regular language ŌĆö language described by RE or recognized by FA

**Critical Theorems:**
- [ ] **Kleene's Theorem**: RE $\Leftrightarrow$ NFA $\Leftrightarrow$ DFA
- [ ] **Pumping Lemma** (three conditions, all must be checked)
- [ ] **Myhill-Nerode Theorem** (relates to DFA minimization)
- [ ] **Closure properties** of regular languages (15+ operations)

**Conversion Algorithms to Master:**
- [ ] **RE to NFA** ŌĆö Thompson's Construction (structural induction)
- [ ] **NFA to DFA** ŌĆö Subset Construction ($\varepsilon$-closure based)
- [ ] **DFA Minimization** ŌĆö Table-filling (equivalence) algorithm
- [ ] **DFA to RE** ŌĆö State elimination method

**Algebraic Identities (for simplification problems):**
- [ ] $r \cdot \varepsilon = r$ and $r \cdot \emptyset = \emptyset$
- [ ] $r^* = \varepsilon \mid r \mid rr \mid rrr \mid \dots$
- [ ] Distributive: $r(s \mid t) = rs \mid rt$
- [ ] Idempotent: $r^{**} = r^*$

**Common Examples to Recognize:**
- [ ] $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ ŌĆö **NOT regular**
- [ ] $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^*\}$ ŌĆö palindromes ŌĆö **NOT regular**
- [ ] $L = \{0^n 1^n 0^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ ŌĆö **NOT regular**
- [ ] $L = (a \mid b)^*$ ŌĆö all binary strings ŌĆö **regular**
- [ ] $L = a^* b^* a^*$ ŌĆö regular (RE exists)

**Exam Strategy Reminders:**
- [ ] Always parenthesize regular expressions to avoid precedence errors.
- [ ] In conversion problems, show **all intermediate steps** ŌĆö skipping loses marks.
- [ ] For Pumping Lemma proofs: adversarial choice + decomposition + pump + contradiction.
- [ ] For closure proofs: construct an FA or use existing closure results.
- [ ] Practice drawing state diagrams neatly ŌĆö examiners value clarity.

**Engineering Applications (for viva/long-answer context):**
- [ ] Lexical analyzers in compilers (Lex/Flex).
- [ ] Pattern matching tools (grep, sed, awk).
- [ ] Digital circuit design (sequential logic minimization).
- [ ] Network protocol validation (packet format checkers).
- [ ] Search engine query processing.
- [ ] Text editor "find and replace" operations.

**Key Formulas at a Glance:**
- Number of strings of length $n$ over $\Sigma$: $\vert \Sigma \vert^n$
- Number of languages over $\Sigma$ (uncountable): $2^{\vert \Sigma^* \vert}$
- Number of regular languages over $\Sigma$ (countable): infinite but countable
- DFA size bound from NFA: at most $2^n$ states
- Myhill-Nerode equivalence: number of equivalence classes = minimum DFA states

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Finite Automata/Accepter (NFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Finite Automata/Accepter (NFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NFA

A **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** is formally defined as a **5-tuple**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

where each component has a precise mathematical meaning:

- **$Q$** : A **finite** non-empty set of states.
- **$\Sigma$** : A **finite** non-empty set of input symbols (alphabet).
- **$\delta$** : The transition function $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ (a mapping into the **power set** of $Q$).
- **$q_0$** : The **start/initial state**, with $q_0 \in Q$.
- **$F$** : The set of **final (accepting) states**, with $F \subseteq Q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction from DFA:** Unlike a DFA where $\delta(q, a)$ returns exactly **one** state, in an NFA the transition function $\delta(q, a)$ returns a **set** of states (which may be empty, a singleton, or multiple states). This "set of possible next states" is what gives NFA its nondeterministic nature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Parallel Universe Reader"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a reader who, upon seeing a word in a book, **guesses** all possible next chapters simultaneously. In one universe he reaches chapter 3, in another chapter 7, and in a third he hits a dead end. The book is considered **"accepted"** if **at least one** of these parallel universes eventually lands on the final chapter. The reader does not need to commit to one path ŌĆö the mere *existence* of a successful path is enough.
>
> - **DFA** = A careful reader who takes exactly one path; the book is accepted only if that single path works.
> - **NFA** = A guessing reader; the book is accepted if **any one** of his guesses succeeds.

### 1.3 String Acceptance in an NFA

A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by an NFA $M$ if there exists **at least one** sequence of transitions (one sequence of choices) that reads the entire string and lands in a final state. Formally:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing \,\}$$

where $\delta^*$ is the **extended transition function** that processes an entire string.

### 1.4 Types of NFA

| Type | Definition | Key Feature |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Standard NFA** | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ | Multiple transitions allowed, no $\varepsilon$-moves |
| **NFA with $\varepsilon$-transitions ($\varepsilon$-NFA)** | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ | Allows state change **without consuming input** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The symbol $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) denotes the **empty string** ŌĆö a transition on $\varepsilon$ changes state **without reading any input symbol**.

### 1.5 Why Study NFA? (Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications of NFA in engineering:**
> - **Lexical analyzers** in compilers (e.g., `grep`, `lex`, `flex`) use NFA-based pattern matching.
> - **Regular expression engines** internally convert regex ŌåÆ NFA ŌåÆ DFA for fast matching.
> - **Network packet filters** and **intrusion detection systems** use NFA for pattern recognition.
> - **Text editors** (search-and-replace) and **DNA sequence matching** in bioinformatics rely on NFA logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of an NFA

The behaviour of an NFA can be broken down into the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Configuration Setup:** The NFA begins in the unique start state $q_0 \in Q$. The current configuration is the ordered pair $(q, w)$ where $q$ is the current state and $w$ is the remaining input string.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Nondeterministic Choice:** On reading a symbol $a$ in state $q$, the NFA may transition to **any state** in the set $\delta(q, a)$. If $\delta(q, a) = \varnothing$ (empty), that branch **dies**.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Parallel Exploration:** The NFA conceptually spawns one "copy" of itself for every state in $\delta(q, a)$. All copies proceed in parallel.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Acceptance Test:** The string is accepted if and only if **at least one** of the parallel computation branches ends in a final state $f \in F$ after consuming the entire input.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö $\varepsilon$-Closure (if applicable):** Before reading any input, the NFA must include all states reachable via any sequence of $\varepsilon$-transitions. This is the **$\varepsilon$-closure** operation.

### 2.2 The $\varepsilon$-Closure Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$\varepsilon$-closure($q$):** The set of all states reachable from state $q$ using **zero or more** $\varepsilon$-transitions. By convention, $q \in \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$.

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q) = \{\, p \in Q \mid p \text{ is reachable from } q \text{ using only } \varepsilon\text{-transitions} \,\}$$

For a **set of states** $S \subseteq Q$:

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$$

**Recursive Algorithm to compute $\varepsilon$-closure($q$):**
1. Initialize $E = \{q\}$.
2. Repeat until no change: For every state $p \in E$, add all states in $\delta(p, \varepsilon)$ to $E$.
3. Return $E$.

### 2.3 Extended Transition Function $\delta^*$

The extended transition function processes a **string** (not just a single symbol):

$$\delta^* : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow 2^Q$$

Defined **recursively** as:

$$\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$$

$$\delta^*(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \delta^*(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$$

For $\varepsilon$-NFA, it becomes:

$$\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q\})$$

$$\delta^*(q, wa) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\!\left(\, \bigcup_{p \in \delta^*(q, w)} \delta(p, a) \,\right)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Note |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | NFA tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $\delta$ returns a **set** of states |
| 2 | DFA tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $\delta$ returns a **single** state |
| 3 | $\varepsilon$-closure (single) | $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q) = \{p \mid p \xrightarrow{\varepsilon^*} \text{from } q\}$ | Always includes $q$ itself |
| 4 | $\varepsilon$-closure (set) | $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$ | Distributive over union |
| 5 | String acceptance | $w \in L(M) \iff \delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing$ | Existence of at least one accepting path |
| 6 | Rejection | $w \notin L(M) \iff \delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F = \varnothing$ | No computation path leads to acceptance |
| 7 | Subset construction | $D_{state} = 2^{N_{state}} = \mathcal{P}(Q)$ | Worst-case: $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ states |
| 8 | Equivalence theorem | $L(\text{NFA}) = L(\text{DFA}) = L(\varepsilon\text{-NFA})$ | All three models accept **regular languages** |
| 9 | Power set bound | $\vert D_{states} \vert \leq 2^{\vert N_{states} \vert}$ | Exponential blow-up possible |
| 10 | Number of NFAs vs. DFAs | For $n$ states, NFAs strictly outnumber DFAs | More compact representations exist |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pumping the table is the surest way to break markdown** ŌĆö the column delimiter is the `|` character. Always use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` inside any cell that needs an absolute-value-like symbol.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of NFA Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** NFA models are foundational in the design of **regular expression engines**. Tools like `grep`, `awk`, and programming language `regex` libraries (PCRE, Python's `re` module) typically implement the **Thompson's construction** algorithm, which builds an $\varepsilon$-NFA from a regex, then converts it to a DFA via subset construction for fast matching. This is the bridge between **theoretical computer science** and **practical software engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Subset Construction: NFA ŌåÆ DFA

**Problem:** Convert the following NFA $N$ into an equivalent DFA.

**Given NFA $N$:**
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (final state)
- $\delta$ defined as:

| State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|-------|---------|---------|
| $q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\varnothing$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| $q_2$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the start state of the DFA.**  
The DFA start state is the set containing only the NFA start state:

$$D_{q_0} = \{q_0\}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the transition from $\{q_0\}$ on input 0.**

$$D_{\{q_0\}}(0) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

This is a **new** DFA state. Label it $A = \{q_0, q_1\}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the transition from $\{q_0\}$ on input 1.**

$$D_{\{q_0\}}(1) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$$

This loops back to the start state.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute transitions from $A = \{q_0, q_1\}$ on input 0.**

$$D_A(0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_0, q_1\} = A$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute transitions from $A = \{q_0, q_1\}$ on input 1.**

$$D_A(1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

This is a **new** DFA state. Label it $B = \{q_0, q_2\}$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute transitions from $B = \{q_0, q_2\}$ on input 0.**

$$D_B(0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_0, q_1\} = A$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute transitions from $B = \{q_0, q_2\}$ on input 1.**

$$D_B(1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_0\}$$

Loops back to start state.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Identify final states.**  
The DFA accepts if the NFA could have ended in $q_2$. So any DFA state containing $q_2$ is final:

$$D_F = \{\, S \subseteq Q \mid q_2 \in S \,\} = \{B\}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Construct the final DFA transition table.**

| DFA State | Input 0 | Input 1 | Final? |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|
| $\to \{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | No |
| $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | No |
| $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | **Yes** |

**Step 10 ŌĆö Verify with sample string $w = 01$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^*(\{q_0\}, 01) &= D_{D_{\{q_0\}}(0)}(1) \\
&= D_{\{q_0, q_1\}}(1) \\
&= \{q_0, q_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $q_2 \in \{q_0, q_2\}$, the string **01** is **accepted** ŌĆö correct, since 01 ends in "01" and the NFA was designed to accept strings ending in "01". $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö $\varepsilon$-Closure Computation

**Problem:** Given the $\varepsilon$-NFA below, compute $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0)$ and $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0, q_2\})$.

**Given $\varepsilon$-transitions:**
- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$
- $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$
- $\delta(q_2, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with $E = \{q_0\}$.** (Always include the start state itself.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Process $q_0$:**  
Add $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$ to $E$.  
Now $E = \{q_0, q_1\}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Process $q_1$:**  
Add $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$ to $E$.  
Now $E = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Process $q_2$:**  
Add $\delta(q_2, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$ to $E$.  
No change.

**Step 5 ŌĆö All states processed. Terminate.**

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö For a set: $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0, q_2\}) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) \cup \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_2)$.**

Compute $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_2)$:
- Start with $\{q_2\}$.
- $\delta(q_2, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$, so no additions.
- $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_2) = \{q_2\}$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0, q_2\}) &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) \cup \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_2) \\
&= \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} \\
&= \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö DFA vs NFA vs $\varepsilon$-NFA

| Property | DFA | NFA (no $\varepsilon$) | $\varepsilon$-NFA |
|----------|-----|------------------------|-------------------|
| Transition type | $Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | $Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ | $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ |
| Number of transitions from $q$ on $a$ | Exactly 1 | Zero, one, or many | Zero, one, or many |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Acceptance | Unique path | At least one path | At least one path (with $\varepsilon$-moves) |
| Recognized language class | Regular | Regular | Regular |
| State count for equivalent machine | Baseline (smallest) | May be smaller | May be smaller |
| Constructability from regex | Indirect (subset first) | Indirect | Direct (Thompson's construction) |
| Conversion cost | ŌĆö | Exponential in worst case | Polynomial to NFA, then exponential to DFA |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Nondeterministic | Nondeterministic |
| Used in practice | Hardware, fast matching | Theoretical compactness | Regex engines (`grep`, `lex`, `sed`) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NFA State Diagram (Mermaid)

The NFA below accepts all binary strings that **end with the substring "01"**.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s0 : 0
    s0 --> s0 : 1
    s0 --> s1 : 0
    s1 --> s2 : 1
    s2 --> [*]
    s2 : FINAL
    s0 : START
```

**Reading the diagram:** The state $s_0$ has **two** transitions on input `0` ŌĆö one looping back to $s_0$ and one moving to $s_1$. This is the hallmark of nondeterminism: a single input symbol can lead to multiple next states.

---

### 4.2 $\varepsilon$-NFA Schematic (Mermaid)

The $\varepsilon$-NFA below accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^m \mid n, m \geq 1\}$ (one or more `a`'s followed by one or more `b`'s). Notice the $\varepsilon$-transition from the start state, which "guesses" when the $a$'s end and the $b$'s begin.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> qA
    qA --> qA : a
    qA --> qB : epsilon
    qB --> qB : b
    qB --> qF : epsilon
    qF --> [*]
    qA : q0
    qA : START
    qB : q1
    qF : q2
    qF : FINAL
```

**Key insight:** The NFA "nondeterministically guesses" the boundary between the $a$-block and the $b$-block via the $\varepsilon$-transition.

---

### 4.3 Subset Construction Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

This block-level architecture diagram shows the data flow during the **subset construction** algorithm that converts an NFA into an equivalent DFA.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input NFA with states Q] --> B[Initialize DFA start state as set containing only NFA start state]
    B --> C{Is worklist empty?}
    C -- No --> D[Pick a DFA state S from worklist]
    D --> E[For each input symbol a in Sigma]
    E --> F[Compute union of delta p a for all p in S]
    F --> G{Is the resulting set T already in DFA?}
    G -- No --> H[Add T as new DFA state and push to worklist]
    G -- Yes --> I[Reuse existing DFA state]
    H --> J[Add transition from S to T on symbol a]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Mark S as processed]
    K --> C
    C -- Yes --> L[Identify DFA final states: any state containing an NFA final state]
    L --> M[Output Equivalent DFA]
```

**Step-by-step reading:**
1. Seed the DFA start state as the singleton NFA start state.
2. Use a **worklist** to iteratively expand reachable subsets.
3. For each subset $S$ and each input symbol, compute the **union of NFA transitions**.
4. Add only **new** subsets as new DFA states.
5. Any DFA state that **contains at least one** NFA final state becomes a DFA final state.

---

### 4.4 Conversion Equivalence Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Regular Expression] --> B[Thompson Construction]
    B --> C[Epsilon NFA]
    C --> D[Subset Construction]
    D --> E[DFA]
    E --> F[Minimization Hopcroft]
    F --> G[Minimal DFA]
    C -.->|NFA equivalence| H[NFA without epsilon]
    H --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** This is the **canonical pipeline** used by tools like `lex`/`flex` and `grep`. The regular expression is first converted to an $\varepsilon$-NFA (linear in the regex size), then to a DFA (exponential worst case), then minimized for efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**
**Define a Nondeterministic Finite Accepter (NFA). How does it differ from a DFA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Nondeterministic Finite Accepter (NFA) is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ is a transition function that maps a state and an input symbol (or $\varepsilon$) to a **set of states** (a subset of $Q$).

**Key differences from a DFA:**

| Aspect | DFA | NFA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Transition function codomain | A single state $Q$ | A set of states $2^Q$ |
| Transitions per (state, symbol) | Exactly 1 | Zero, one, or many |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed (in $\varepsilon$-NFA) |
| Acceptance mechanism | Unique computation path | Existence of at least one accepting path |
| Compactness | Often larger | Often more compact |

A string $w$ is accepted by an NFA if there exists **at least one** sequence of choices that reads $w$ and ends in a final state.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]**
**What is an $\varepsilon$-closure of a state? Compute $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_0)$ for the following $\varepsilon$-NFA:**

- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$
- $\delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$
- $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$
- $\delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_2\}$
- $\delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_1, q_2\}$

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The $\varepsilon$-closure of a state $q$, denoted $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$, is the set of all states reachable from $q$ using **zero or more** $\varepsilon$-transitions. It always includes $q$ itself.

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) &= \{q_0\} \cup \delta(q_0, \varepsilon) \cup \delta(q_1, \varepsilon) \cup \ldots \\
&= \{q_0\} \cup \{q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} \\
&= \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step trace:**
1. Start: $E = \{q_0\}$.
2. Process $q_0$: add $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$ ŌåÆ $E = \{q_0, q_1\}$.
3. Process $q_1$: add $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$ ŌåÆ $E = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.
4. Process $q_2$: $\delta(q_2, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$ ŌåÆ no change.

**Final answer:** $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting that a DFA state in the subset construction may contain **multiple** NFA states, and the DFA must transition to the **union** of all corresponding NFA transitions.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Failing to identify **all reachable** DFA states ŌĆö many students stop after the first few transitions, missing nested subsets.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Marking the wrong final states ŌĆö a DFA state is final **if and only if** it contains **at least one** NFA final state, not all of them.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Computing $\varepsilon$-closure for a state but forgetting to **include the state itself**.
> - **Mark loss:** Missing the start state of the DFA (must be a **singleton** containing the NFA start state) costs at least 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct an NFA that accepts the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w$ contains the substring "**ab**"$\}$. Draw the transition diagram and formally define the NFA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the NFA obtained in part (a) into an equivalent DFA using the subset construction algorithm. Show all reachable states and identify the final states.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö NFA Construction [7 marks]:**

**Intuition:** The NFA needs to "guess" when the substring "ab" begins. It can stay in the start state reading arbitrary symbols, but the moment it sees an `a`, it **nondeterministically guesses** that this is the start of the "ab" substring and moves to a new state that looks for the `b`.

**Formal Definition:**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (final state)

**Transition function $\delta$:**

| State | Input $a$ | Input $b$ |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| $q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\varnothing$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| $q_2$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ |

**Transition Diagram (described):** State $q_0$ loops on both $a$ and $b$ to itself; from $q_0$ on $a$, there is an additional arrow to $q_1$; $q_1$ on $b$ goes to $q_2$; $q_2$ is a **dead/final state** with no outgoing transitions.

**Verification with $w = bab$:** Path: $q_0 \xrightarrow{b} q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$. Ō£ō (accepted because ends in $q_2 \in F$)

**Verification with $w = ba$:** Path: $q_0 \xrightarrow{b} q_0 \xrightarrow{a} \{q_0, q_1\}$. Two branches ŌĆö one dies at $q_0$ (since next is end-of-input, $q_0 \notin F$), the other dies at $q_1$ (since end-of-input, $q_1 \notin F$). Ō£Ś (rejected ŌĆö correct, since "ba" doesn't contain "ab").

**[Awarded 7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for transition table, 2 for verification/example, 1 for correct final state identification]**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Subset Construction [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** DFA start state $A = \{q_0\}$.

**Step 2:** From $A = \{q_0\}$:

$$
\delta_A(a) = \delta(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} = B \quad \text{(new state)}
$$

$$
\delta_A(b) = \delta(q_0, b) = \{q_0\} = A
$$

**Step 3:** From $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$:

$$
\delta_B(a) = \delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_1, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_0, q_1\} = B
$$

$$
\delta_B(b) = \delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_1, b) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} = C \quad \text{(new state)}
$$

**Step 4:** From $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$:

$$
\delta_C(a) = \delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_2, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing = B
$$

$$
\delta_C(b) = \delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_2, b) = \{q_0\} \cup \varnothing = A
$$

**Step 5:** All transitions are now defined for the three reachable states $\{A, B, C\}$. No new states are generated.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Identify DFA final states:** Any state containing the NFA final state $q_2$ is final.

$$D_F = \{C\} = \{\{q_0, q_2\}\}$$

**Final DFA Transition Table:**

| DFA State | Input $a$ | Input $b$ | Final? |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| $\to A = \{q_0\}$ | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $A = \{q_0\}$ | No |
| $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | No |
| $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $A = \{q_0\}$ | **Yes** |

**Verification with $w = ab$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^*(A, ab) &= \delta_{\delta_A(a)}(b) \\
&= \delta_B(b) \\
&= C \in D_F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Accepted** Ō£ō (correct ŌĆö "ab" contains "ab").

**Verification with $w = ba$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^*(A, ba) &= \delta_{\delta_A(b)}(a) \\
&= \delta_A(a) \\
&= B \notin D_F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rejected** Ō£ō (correct ŌĆö "ba" does not contain "ab").

**[Awarded 7 marks: 2 for algorithm steps, 2 for transition table, 1 for final state identification, 2 for verification]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **$\varepsilon$-closure** of a set of states. Construct an $\varepsilon$-NFA for the regular expression $R = (a \mid b)^* aba$ and explain the role of $\varepsilon$-transitions in the construction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the $\varepsilon$-NFA from part (a) into an equivalent NFA without $\varepsilon$-transitions, and then into an equivalent DFA using the subset construction algorithm. Tabulate your results.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö $\varepsilon$-Closure Definition & Construction [7 marks]:**

**Definition [2 marks]:** The $\varepsilon$-closure of a set of states $S \subseteq Q$, denoted $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S)$, is the set of all states reachable from any state in $S$ using **zero or more** $\varepsilon$-transitions:

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$$

where $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$ is the set of all states reachable from $q$ via a sequence of $\varepsilon$-moves (including $q$ itself).

**$\varepsilon$-NFA for $R = (a \mid b)^* aba$ via Thompson's Construction [5 marks]:**

Following the standard Thompson's construction rules:

| Regex Component | States Created | Transitions |
|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| $a$ (atom) | Two states with an $a$-edge | $a$-edge between them |
| $b$ (atom) | Two states with a $b$-edge | $b$-edge between them |
| $a \mid b$ | Union construction | $\varepsilon$-edges from new start to each, $\varepsilon$-edges back from each to new end |
| $(a \mid b)^*$ | Kleene star construction | $\varepsilon$-loop from inner-end back to inner-start, and a bypass $\varepsilon$-edge |
| $aba$ | Concatenation | Sequential $a$, $b$, $a$ edges with intermediate $\varepsilon$-connections |

**State set (labeling):** Let the final $\varepsilon$-NFA have states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6, s_7\}$ where $s_0$ is the start and $s_7$ is the final state.

**Key transitions:**
- $\delta(s_0, \varepsilon) = \{s_1, s_5\}$ (start of Kleene star, both bypass and entry)
- $\delta(s_1, \varepsilon) = \{s_2, s_4\}$ (union split: $a$ and $b$ paths)
- $\delta(s_2, a) = \{s_3\}$
- $\delta(s_3, \varepsilon) = \{s_6\}$ (union join)
- $\delta(s_4, b) = \{s_5\}$ *(internal state numbering may vary)*
- $\delta(s_5, \varepsilon) = \{s_1, s_6\}$ (Kleene loop-back and exit)
- $\delta(s_6, a) = \{s_7\}$
- $\delta(s_7, b) = \{s_8\}$
- $\delta(s_8, a) = \{s_9\}$ (final state $s_9$)

**Role of $\varepsilon$-transitions:** $\varepsilon$-transitions allow the construction to **compose** sub-expressions **without consuming input**. They act as "control flow" edges that wire together the building blocks (atoms, union, concatenation, Kleene star) of the regex into a single machine.

**[Awarded 7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for Thompson's construction, 2 for explanation of $\varepsilon$-role]**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Conversion to NFA and then DFA [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Eliminate $\varepsilon$-transitions [3 marks]:**

For every state $q$ and every input symbol $a$, define a new transition:

$$\delta'(q, a) = \delta^*(q, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\!\left(\, \bigcup_{p \in \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)} \delta(p, a) \,\right)$$

**Key formula used in eliminating $\varepsilon$:** A state $p$ is in $\delta'(q, a)$ if and only if there exists a state $r$ such that:
- $r$ is reachable from $q$ using only $\varepsilon$-transitions (i.e., $r \in \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$),
- $p$ is reachable from $r$ on input $a$ (i.e., $p \in \delta(r, a)$),
- $p$ is reachable from itself using $\varepsilon$-transitions (i.e., $p \in \varepsilon\text{-closure}(p)$).

The new start state's $\varepsilon$-closure becomes the new start set. A state $q$ is a final state in the $\varepsilon$-free NFA iff $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q) \cap F_{\text{old}} \neq \varnothing$.

**Resulting NFA (no $\varepsilon$):** The new transition table has states $\{s_0', s_1', \ldots\}$ with transitions only on $a$ and $b$, no $\varepsilon$-edges. For example:
- $\delta'(s_0', a) = \{s_3, s_7, s_9\}$ (combining $a$-paths from the union and concatenations)
- $\delta'(s_0', b) = \{s_5, s_7\}$
- (Detailed enumeration depends on the exact Thompson numbering.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Subset construction to DFA [4 marks]:**

Apply the subset construction algorithm to the $\varepsilon$-free NFA:

- DFA start: $D_0 = \{s_0'\}$.

For each new subset reached, compute:

$$D_{\{S\}}(a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta'(q, a)$$

- $D_0 = \{s_0'\}$
- $D_0(a) = \{s_3', s_7', s_9'\}$ ŌåÆ label as $D_1$
- $D_0(b) = \{s_5', s_7'\}$ ŌåÆ label as $D_2$
- Compute transitions from $D_1$ and $D_2$ on $a$ and $b$...
- Continue until closure (no new subsets appear).

**Final DFA states:** Any subset containing the original NFA's final state is final in the DFA. After minimization (if asked), the DFA has 5 states (typical result for $(a\mid b)^*aba$).

**[Awarded 7 marks: 3 for $\varepsilon$-elimination, 4 for subset construction]**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for NFA (Module 1):**

- **NFA Tuple:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$.

- **NFA accepts a string $w$** if and only if $\delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing$ (at least one path leads to a final state).

- **DFA accepts a string** if and only if $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$ (the **unique** computation path ends in a final state).

- **$\varepsilon$-closure($q$):** All states reachable from $q$ via zero or more $\varepsilon$-transitions; **always includes $q$**.

- **$\varepsilon$-closure($S$):** $\bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$.

- **Subset construction:** DFA states are **subsets** of NFA states; the DFA start state is the **singleton** containing the NFA start state.

- **DFA final states** = all DFA subsets that contain **at least one** NFA final state.

- **Worst-case blow-up:** $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ DFA states from an NFA with $\vert Q \vert$ states.

- **Equivalence theorem:** $L(\text{DFA}) = L(\text{NFA}) = L(\varepsilon\text{-NFA})$ ŌĆö all three recognize exactly the **regular languages**.

- **Thompson's construction:** A systematic, linear-size procedure to convert a regex into an $\varepsilon$-NFA.

- **Practical use:** Regex engines (`grep`, `lex`, `awk`, Python `re`) use NFA ŌåÆ DFA internally for fast text matching.

- **Quick memory trick:** NFA = "**N**ondeterministic = **N**umerous choices"; DFA = "**D**eterministic = **D**ecisive single path".

- **Common exam traps to avoid:**
  1. Confusing the codomain of $\delta$ in DFA ($Q$) vs NFA ($2^Q$).
  2. Forgetting that a DFA state in subset construction may contain **multiple** NFA states.
  3. Stopping subset construction before all reachable states are explored.
  4. Marking DFA final states incorrectly (must contain at least one NFA final state, not all).
  5. Forgetting to include the state itself in $\varepsilon$-closure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NFA with Epsilon Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NFA with Epsilon Transitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of ╬Ą-NFA

An **NFA with Epsilon Transitions (╬Ą-NFA or NFA-╬Ą)** is formally defined as a **5-tuple** mathematical structure that extends the standard Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton by allowing transitions on the empty string **╬Ą** (epsilon), in addition to the input alphabet symbols.

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

Where the components are defined as:

| Component | Notation | Description |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **Finite States** | $Q$ | A finite, non-empty set of internal configurations of the machine. |
| **Input Alphabet** | $\Sigma$ | A finite set of input symbols the machine can consume. |
| **Transition Function** | $\delta$ | A function mapping $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$. |
| **Start State** | $q_0 \in Q$ | The unique initial configuration. |
| **Final States** | $F \subseteq Q$ | The set of accepting configurations. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Distinction from a Standard NFA:** In a regular NFA, the transition function only operates on input symbols. In an **╬Ą-NFA**, the domain is extended to include the empty string **╬Ą**, meaning the machine can change states *without consuming any input symbol*. This is a powerful modeling feature that simplifies the design of automata.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Free Teleportation" Model

Imagine you are navigating a building with multiple rooms (states) connected by labeled doors (transitions). In a standard NFA, you can only move between rooms by **opening a labeled door and walking through it** (consuming a symbol). 

In an **╬Ą-NFA**, there exists a special kind of **"free teleportation portal"** (the ╬Ą-transition) that lets you instantly jump from one room to another **without spending any energy or opening any door**. You just appear in the new room, ready to consume the next input symbol from there. 

This "free teleportation" (╬Ą) makes designing complex automata much easier, because the engineer can draw **spontaneous, symbol-free jumps** to simplify state diagrams. However, the **language accepted is identical** to that of a standard NFA or DFA ŌĆö ╬Ą-NFA does not accept any new languages, it only provides a *more convenient representation*.

## 1.3 The Language Accepted by an ╬Ą-NFA

A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is accepted by an ╬Ą-NFA $M$ if there exists **at least one sequence** of transitions (using both regular symbols and ╬Ą-moves) that leads from the start state $q_0$ to some final state in $F$, consuming the string $w$ in the process.

$$L(M) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset \}$$

Where $\hat{\delta}$ is the **extended transition function** that accounts for epsilon closures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** Every language accepted by an ╬Ą-NFA is a **regular language**, and conversely, every regular language can be accepted by some ╬Ą-NFA. This proves that: **DFA ŌēĪ NFA ŌēĪ ╬Ą-NFA** in terms of expressive power. The three models are computationally equivalent.

---

## 1.4 Epsilon Closure ŌĆö The Foundational Concept

The **╬Ą-closure** of a state (or set of states) is the most critical concept required to reason about ╬Ą-NFAs. It is defined formally as:

**Epsilon Closure of a Single State $q$:** 
$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q) = \{ p \in Q \mid p \text{ is reachable from } q \text{ using zero or more } \varepsilon \text{-transitions} \}$$

**Epsilon Closure of a Set of States $S$:** 
$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property:** Every state is in its own ╬Ą-closure because reaching yourself with **zero** ╬Ą-transitions is always valid.
> 
> $$q \in \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q) \quad \text{(ALWAYS TRUE)}$$

This property is essential for all mathematical operations involving ╬Ą-NFAs, including conversion algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Epsilon Transitions Exist ŌĆö The Engineering Motivation

In real-world finite automaton design, engineers often need to compose multiple sub-machines (union, concatenation, Kleene star) into a single unified automaton. Drawing such composite machines **without** ╬Ą-transitions is extremely tedious, often requiring artificial state duplication. The introduction of ╬Ą-transitions:

- **Simplifies the Thompson's Construction** for regular expression to NFA conversion.
- **Enables clean modular design** where sub-automata are joined with zero-symbol "wires".
- **Reduces the number of states** needed in many practical designs.
- Provides a **canonical intermediate form** used in compiler design (lexical analyzers like Lex/Flex internally use ╬Ą-NFAs).

## 2.2 The Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$ ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The **extended transition function** $\hat{\delta}(q, w)$ returns the set of all states reachable from state $q$ by consuming the entire string $w$, including all possible ╬Ą-jumps taken before, during, and after consuming the symbols.

It is defined **recursively** as follows:

**Base Case (empty string):**
$$\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$$

**Recursive Case (string $w = xa$ where $a \in \Sigma$):**
$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}\left( \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a) \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the closure at the end?** After consuming the last input symbol, the machine may still take **spontaneous ╬Ą-jumps** to reach additional states. These must be included in the final reachable set, hence the outer ╬Ą-CLOSURE operation.

## 2.3 Conversion Algorithm: ╬Ą-NFA ŌåÆ NFA (The Key Theorem)

This is the **most frequently asked algorithm** in KTU board exams for this topic. Given an ╬Ą-NFA $M_\varepsilon = (Q, \Sigma, \delta_\varepsilon, q_0, F_\varepsilon)$, we construct an equivalent NFA $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta', q_0, F')$ **without** ╬Ą-transitions.

### Step-by-Step Conversion Logic

1. **Preserve** the states, input alphabet, and start state unchanged.
2. **Compute** the ╬Ą-closure of the start state. This becomes the new effective start configuration.
3. **For every state $q$ and every input symbol $a \in \Sigma$**, compute the new transition:
$$\delta'(q, a) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}\left( \delta_\varepsilon(\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q), a) \right)$$
4. **Redefine the final states** as:
$$F' = \{ q \in Q \mid \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q) \cap F_\varepsilon \neq \emptyset \}$$
   That is, $q$ is a final state in the new NFA if its ╬Ą-closure contains **any** final state of the original ╬Ą-NFA.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **╬Ą-NFA Formal Definition** | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | Foundation |
| 2 | **Transition Domain** | $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ | Defines ╬Ą-capability |
| 3 | **╬Ą-Closure (Single State)** | $\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$ | Reachable states via ╬Ą only |
| 4 | **╬Ą-Closure (Set)** | $\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$ | Set operations |
| 5 | **Reflexive Property** | $q \in \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$ | Base case of closure |
| 6 | **Extended $\hat{\delta}$ Base** | $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q)$ | Empty string acceptance |
| 7 | **Extended $\hat{\delta}$ Recursive** | $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(\bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q,w)} \delta(p, a))$ | General string consumption |
| 8 | **Language Accepted** | $L(M) = \{ w \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset \}$ | Acceptance condition |
| 9 | **╬Ą-NFA ŌåÆ NFA Transition** | $\delta'(q, a) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(\delta_\varepsilon(\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q), a))$ | Conversion algorithm |
| 10 | **New Final States** | $F' = \{ q \mid \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(q) \cap F \neq \emptyset \}$ | Conversion algorithm |
| 11 | **Equivalence Theorem** | $\mathcal{L}(DFA) = \mathcal{L}(NFA) = \mathcal{L}(\varepsilon\text{-NFA})$ | Power hierarchy |
| 12 | **Maximum States Bound** | $\text{States}_{\varepsilon\text{-NFA}} \leq \text{States}_{DFA}$ (in worst case) | Complexity |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lexical Analyzers in Compilers**: Tools like **Lex** and **Flex** internally convert user-given regular expressions into ╬Ą-NFAs using **Thompson's Construction** before subsequently converting to optimized DFAs.
- **Network Protocol Design**: ╬Ą-transitions model "timeout" or "idle" states where the system remains in a configuration without new input.
- **Pattern Matching Engines**: Modern grep and regex engines (PCRE, RE2) often use ╬Ą-NFA-based backtracking or NFA simulation for efficient pattern matching.
- **Model Checking**: ╬Ą-transitions are used in **Labeled Transition Systems (LTS)** to model asynchronous, silent system actions in formal verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks to "eliminate ╬Ą-transitions", examiners expect students to (1) compute the transition table using the formula, AND (2) clearly identify the new set of final states. Skipping step (2) is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing ╬Ą-Closures

**Problem:** Consider the following ╬Ą-NFA with states $\{A, B, C, D\}$, start state $A$, final state $\{D\}$, and transitions:

- $\delta(A, \varepsilon) = \{B\}$
- $\delta(B, a) = \{C\}$
- $\delta(B, \varepsilon) = \{D\}$
- $\delta(C, b) = \{C\}$
- $\delta(D, a) = \{D\}$

**Step 1: Compute $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($A$)**

Start with $\{A\}$ (always include the state itself).
Apply all possible ╬Ą-transitions iteratively until no new states are added.

- Initial set: $\{A\}$
- From $A$, take $\varepsilon$ to $B$: add $B$. New set: $\{A, B\}$
- From $B$, take $\varepsilon$ to $D$: add $D$. New set: $\{A, B, D\}$
- From $D$, no outgoing ╬Ą-transitions. Stop.

$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(A) = \{A, B, D\}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($B$)**

- Initial: $\{B\}$, then from $B$ via $\varepsilon$ to $D$. 

$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(B) = \{B, D\}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($C$)**

$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(C) = \{C\}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($D$)**

$$\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(D) = \{D\}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Converting ╬Ą-NFA to NFA

**Problem:** Given the same ╬Ą-NFA above, construct an equivalent NFA $M'$ with no ╬Ą-transitions.

We need to compute $\delta'(q, a)$ for all $q \in \{A, B, C, D\}$ and all $a \in \{a, b\}$.

**Step A: Compute $\delta'(A, a)$**

Using the formula:
$$\delta'(A, a) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}\left( \delta_\varepsilon(\varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(A), a) \right)$$

We know $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($A$) $= \{A, B, D\}$.

Now compute $\delta_\varepsilon$ on each state in $\{A, B, D\}$ for input $a$:

- $\delta_\varepsilon(A, a) = \emptyset$ (no transition from $A$ on $a$)
- $\delta_\varepsilon(B, a) = \{C\}$
- $\delta_\varepsilon(D, a) = \{D\}$

Union: $\emptyset \cup \{C\} \cup \{D\} = \{C, D\}$

Apply ╬Ą-closure to $\{C, D\}$:
- $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($C$) $= \{C\}$
- $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($D$) $= \{D\}$

$$\delta'(A, a) = \{C, D\}$$

**Step B: Compute $\delta'(A, b)$**

Apply the same procedure for symbol $b$:

- $\delta_\varepsilon(A, b) = \emptyset$
- $\delta_\varepsilon(B, b) = \emptyset$
- $\delta_\varepsilon(D, b) = \emptyset$

Union: $\emptyset$

$$\delta'(A, b) = \varepsilon\text{-CLOSURE}(\emptyset) = \emptyset$$

**Step C: Compute $\delta'(B, a)$**

$\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($B$) $= \{B, D\}$.

- $\delta_\varepsilon(B, a) = \{C\}$
- $\delta_\varepsilon(D, a) = \{D\}$

Union: $\{C, D\}$. Apply closure: $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($\{C, D\}$) $= \{C, D\}$.

$$\delta'(B, a) = \{C, D\}$$

**Step D: Compute $\delta'(B, b)$**

- $\delta_\varepsilon(B, b) = \emptyset$
- $\delta_\varepsilon(D, b) = \emptyset$

$$\delta'(B, b) = \emptyset$$

**Step E: Compute $\delta'(C, a)$**

$\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($C$) $= \{C\}$.

- $\delta_\varepsilon(C, a) = \emptyset$ (only $b$-transition from $C$)

$$\delta'(C, a) = \emptyset$$

**Step F: Compute $\delta'(C, b)$**

- $\delta_\varepsilon(C, b) = \{C\}$

Apply closure: $\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($\{C\}$) $= \{C\}$.

$$\delta'(C, b) = \{C\}$$

**Step G: Compute $\delta'(D, a)$ and $\delta'(D, b)$**

$\varepsilon$-CLOSURE($D$) $= \{D\}$.

- $\delta_\varepsilon(D, a) = \{D\}$, closure gives $\{D\}$
