## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State Kleene's Theorem. Why is it important in the context of converting regular expressions to finite automata?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Kleene's Theorem** (1956) states that a language $L$ is **regular** if and only if it is recognized by a finite automaton. Formally:
> $$\mathcal{L}_{RE} = \mathcal{L}_{DFA} = \mathcal{L}_{NFA} = \mathcal{L}_{NFA\text{-}\varepsilon}$$
> where $\mathcal{L}_{RE}$ is the family of languages denoted by regular expressions, and the others are languages accepted by the corresponding automata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Importance:** It guarantees that for **every** regular expression, there **exists** at least one finite automaton that accepts exactly the same language. This justifies the existence of a **mechanical conversion algorithm** (Thompson's Construction + Subset Construction) ŌĆö without this theorem, conversion would be undecidable in general.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is an ╬Ą-closure of a state? How is it used in the subset construction algorithm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **╬Ą-closure** of a state $q$, denoted $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$, is the set of **all states reachable from $q$ using zero or more ╬Ą-transitions**:
$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q) = \{p \in Q \mid q \xrightarrow{\varepsilon^*} p\}$$

**In subset construction**, after computing the move of a set of NFA states on input $a$, the ╬Ą-closure of the resulting set must be computed to account for all states reachable "for free" without consuming any input.

$$\delta_D(S, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\left(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)\right)$$

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either choice A or choice B must be answered in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Actual Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Construct an NFA-╬Ą for the regular expression $R = (a \mid b)^{*}ab$ using Thompson's construction. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the NFA-╬Ą obtained in (a) into an equivalent DFA using subset construction. Draw the final DFA transition diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Base NFAs for $a$ and $b$**

$$N_a: q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \quad\quad N_b: q_2 \xrightarrow{b} q_3$$

**Step 2: Union $(a \mid b)$** (1.5 marks)

- New start $q_s$, new final $q_f$.
- $q_s \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_0$ and $q_s \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_2$.
- $q_1 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_f$ and $q_3 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_f$.

**Step 3: Kleene Star $(a \mid b)^{*}$** (2 marks)

- New start $q_{start}$, new final $q_{end}$.
- $q_{start} \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_{end}$ (accept empty).
- $q_{start} \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_s$ (enter loop).
- $q_f \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_{end}$ (exit loop).
- $q_f \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_s$ (loop back).

**Step 4: Concatenation with $ab$** (1.5 marks)

- Add states: $p_0 \xrightarrow{a} p_1$, $p_2 \xrightarrow{b} p_3$.
- $q_{end} \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} p_0$.
- $p_1 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} p_2$.
- $p_3$ becomes the new final state.

**NFA-╬Ą transition table:** (1 mark)

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_{start}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_{end}, q_s\}$ |
| $q_s$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ |
| $q_0$ | $\{q_1\}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| $q_2$ | ŌĆö | $\{q_3\}$ | ŌĆö |
| $q_f$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_{end}, q_s\}$ |
| $q_{end}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{p_0\}$ |
| $p_0$ | $\{p_1\}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| $p_1$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{p_2\}$ |
| $p_2$ | ŌĆö | $\{p_3\}$ | ŌĆö |

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1: ╬Ą-closure of start state** (1 mark)

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_{start}) = \{q_{start}, q_{end}, q_s, q_0, q_2, p_0\} = A$$

**Step 2: Move and ╬Ą-closure computations** (4 marks)

$$\text{Move}(A, a) = \delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(p_0, a) = \{q_1, p_1\}$$
$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_1, p_1\}) = \{q_1, q_f, q_{end}, q_s, q_0, q_2, p_0, p_2\} = B \text{ (non-final)}$$

$$\text{Move}(A, b) = \delta(q_2, b) = \{q_3\}$$
$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_3\}) = \{q_3, q_f, q_{end}, q_s, q_0, q_2, p_0\} = C \text{ (non-final)}$$

Continuing this process yields the **final DFA** (2 marks):

| DFA State | $a$ | $b$ | Final? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow A$ | $B$ | $C$ | No |
| $B$ | $B$ | $D$ | No |
| $C$ | $B$ | $C$ | No |
| $D$ | $B$ | $C$ | **Yes** |

The DFA accepts exactly $(a \mid b)^{*}ab$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the subset construction algorithm for converting an NFA-╬Ą to a DFA. Define ╬Ą-closure and move operations formally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the subset construction algorithm to convert the NFA-╬Ą accepting the language $(0 \mid 1)^{*}00$ into a DFA. Show the transition table. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Subset Construction Algorithm** (Rabin-Scott, 1959) is a procedure that converts any **NFA-╬Ą** $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$ into an equivalent **DFA** $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, S_0, F_D)$ where each DFA state corresponds to a **subset of NFA states**. (2 marks)

**Formal Definitions:**

**Move Operation:** For a set of states $T \subseteq Q_N$ and input symbol $a \in \Sigma$:
$$\text{Move}(T, a) = \bigcup_{q \in T} \delta_N(q, a)$$

**╬Ą-closure Operation:** For a state $q$:
$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q) = \{p \in Q_N \mid q \xrightarrow{\varepsilon^*} p\}$$

For a set $T$:
$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(T) = \bigcup_{q \in T} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q)$$

**Algorithm Steps:** (4 marks)

1. Compute $S_0 = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0\})$ ŌĆö initial DFA state.
2. For each unprocessed DFA state $S$ and each $a \in \Sigma$:
   - Compute $T = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\text{Move}(S, a))$.
   - If $T \notin Q_D$, add $T$ as a new DFA state.
   - Define $\delta_D(S, a) = T$.
3. Mark $S \in F_D$ iff $S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$.
4. Terminate when no new DFA states are produced.
5. **Complexity:** The DFA has at most $2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$ states.

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**NFA-╬Ą for $(0 \mid 1)^{*}00$:** (Built via Thompson's construction ŌĆö 2 marks)

| State | $0$ | $1$ | $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ (start) | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_f, q_s\}$ |
| $q_f$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_2, q_s\}$ |
| $q_s$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $\{q_1, q_3\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{q_f\}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| $q_3$ | ŌĆö | $\{q_f\}$ | ŌĆö |
| $q_2$ | $\{q_4\}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| $q_4$ (final) | $\{q_5\}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| $q_5$ (final) | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

**Subset Construction:** (4 marks)

- $A = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_f, q_s, q_2, q_1, q_3\}$
- $\delta_D(A, 0) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_f, q_4\}) = \{q_f, q_s, q_2, q_1, q_3, q_4\} = B$
- $\delta_D(A, 1) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_f\}) = A$
- Continue iteratingŌĆ”

**Final DFA Transition Table:** (1 mark)

| DFA State | $0$ | $1$ | Final? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow A$ | $B$ | $A$ | No |
| $B$ | $C$ | $A$ | No |
| $C$ | $D$ | $A$ | No |
| $D$ | $D$ | $A$ | **Yes** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting ╬Ą-closure** after the `Move` step. Partial credit is given for `Move`, but full closure computation is mandatory.
> 2. **Missing final-state marking rule**: A DFA subset state is final **iff** it contains **at least one** original NFA final state. Students often mark all subsets that "look" accepting.
> 3. **Drawing the NFA-╬Ą with too many/few ╬Ą-transitions**: Each base RE operator has a **specific** ╬Ą-wiring. Drawing extra ╬Ą-arrows (e.g., a direct ╬Ą from $q_{start}$ to $q_f$ for a concatenation) breaks the construction.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in subset construction**: Examiners want to see the *unprocessed queue*, the *Move*, the *╬Ą-closure*, and the *new state addition* ŌĆö not just the final table.
> 5. **Not verifying language equivalence**: After conversion, test the DFA on **at least two example strings** (one accepted, one rejected) to prove $L(M) = L(R)$.
> 6. **Confusing NFA ŌåÆ DFA with RE ŌåÆ FA**: These are two distinct phases. Some students skip Phase 1 and try to build a DFA directly from the RE, losing 3ŌĆō5 marks.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Kleene's Theorem** guarantees the existence of conversion: $RE \iff NFA\text{-}\varepsilon \iff DFA$.
- Ō£ģ The **two-phase pipeline** is universal: (Phase 1) Thompson's Construction builds NFA-╬Ą, (Phase 2) Subset Construction yields DFA.
- Ō£ģ **Thompson's Construction has 5 base cases** (atomic NFA-╬Ą fragments): $\varepsilon$, $\emptyset$, $a$, Union, Concatenation, Kleene Star ŌĆö *memorize the ╬Ą-wiring of each*.
- Ō£ģ **Subset Construction** produces a DFA where each state is a **subset of NFA states**.
- Ō£ģ **╬Ą-closure(q)** = set of states reachable from $q$ via $\varepsilon$-transitions only (reflexive closure).
- Ō£ģ **DFA final state rule**: $S \in F_D$ iff $S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$ (i.e., the subset contains at least one NFA final).
- Ō£ģ **Maximum DFA size**: $2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$ states ŌĆö exponential in NFA size.
- Ō£ģ The conversion preserves **language semantics**: $L(M_{DFA}) = L(R_{RE})$ exactly.
- Ō£ģ **Real-world applications** include Lex/Flex compilers, grep-style search, and network IDS engines.
- Ō£ģ **KTU 14-mark questions** typically pair Thompson's Construction (7 marks) with Subset Construction (7 marks) ŌĆö practice both phases for a single RE.
- Ō£ģ Always **label every state** in your NFA-╬Ą and DFA diagrams; unlabelled states cost marks.
- Ō£ģ Always **show ╬Ą-closure calculations** for **at least 3 new DFA states** in your solution; don't skip steps.
- Ō£ģ **Mermaid diagram tips** for your own notes: prefix every node ID with letters (e.g., `S0`, not `0`) to avoid parser errors.
- Ō£ģ **Cross-check** by feeding at least one accepted and one rejected string through your final DFA.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regular Grammar (Examples), Closure Properties of Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regular Grammar ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Regular Grammar** is a type of formal grammar defined by the 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where:
- $V$ is a finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (variables)
- $T$ is a finite set of **terminal symbols**, with $V \cap T = \emptyset$
- $P$ is a finite set of **production rules**
- $S \in V$ is the designated **start symbol**

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard)**
> A grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is called a **regular grammar** if every production in $P$ belongs to one of the two specific restricted forms listed below.

A production rule in a regular grammar is restricted to **one of the following two forms**:

| Type | Form of Production | Why "Right/Left" Linear? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Right Linear Grammar (RLG)** | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Non-terminal appears on the **right**-most position |
| **Left Linear Grammar (LLG)** | $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Non-terminal appears on the **left**-most position |

where $A, B \in V$, $a \in T$, and $\varepsilon$ is the empty string.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> A single grammar must be **either** right-linear **or** left-linear. A grammar that mixes both forms is **not** a regular grammar. Regular grammars generate **exactly the class of Regular Languages**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **The "Factory Assembly Line" Analogy**
> Imagine a grammar as a **factory assembly line** producing strings. 
> - **Right Linear Grammar** is like a conveyor belt moving **left to right**: A non-terminal $A$ hands a terminal part $a$ to the next station $B$. Each station can only pass parts *forward*. This perfectly mirrors how a **DFA reads input left-to-right**.
> - **Left Linear Grammar** is the same conveyor belt, but parts are passed **right to left**: $A$ tells $B$ to append a terminal $a$ to whatever $B$ eventually produces. This mirrors an NFA that processes its transitions in reverse.
> 
> Both assembly lines are powerful enough to build *every* regular pattern (e.g., "any number of `a`'s followed by exactly one `b`"), but **not strong enough** to build nested patterns like $a^n b^n$ ŌĆö that requires a more powerful "factory" called a Context-Free Grammar.

## 1.3 Why Regular Grammars Matter

Regular grammars sit at the **bottom of the Chomsky Hierarchy** but are arguably the most practically useful, since:
- They correspond **one-to-one** with Finite Automata (DFA/NFA).
- They form the theoretical backbone of **lexical analysis** in compilers (the `lex`/`flex` tool generates RLG rules to tokenize source code).
- They have a clean, closed algebraic structure under set operations ŌĆö the **closure properties** we study next.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Regular Grammar

### 2.1.1 Right Linear Grammar (RLG) ŌĆö The Default Standard

Every production in an RLG has the form:
$$A \rightarrow aB \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow \varepsilon$$

- **Logic Step 1:** A non-terminal $A$ is replaced by exactly **one** terminal $a$, optionally followed by **at most one** non-terminal $B$.
- **Logic Step 2:** Because only one terminal is produced per derivation step, a string of length $n$ requires exactly $n$ derivation steps ŌĆö making the derivation length predictable.
- **Logic Step 3:** The derivation always **terminates** when all non-terminals are replaced by terminal-only rules ($A \rightarrow a$) or by $\varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why RLG is preferred in textbooks and exams**
> RLG directly maps to a DFA: each non-terminal $A$ corresponds to a DFA state, and the rule $A \rightarrow aB$ corresponds to the transition $\delta(A, a) = B$.

### 2.1.2 Left Linear Grammar (LLG)

Every production in an LLG has the form:
$$A \rightarrow Ba \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow \varepsilon$$

- **Logic Step 1:** A non-terminal $A$ is replaced by **at most one** non-terminal $B$ followed by exactly **one** terminal $a$.
- **Logic Step 2:** LLG naturally maps to an NFA whose transitions are traversed in **reverse**, beginning from the accepting states.
- **Logic Step 3:** LLG and RLG generate the **same class of languages** ŌĆö every LLG can be algorithmically converted to an equivalent RLG (and vice versa).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Statement | Proof Mechanism | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Union** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 \cup L_2$ regular | NFA with new $\varepsilon$-branching start state | Very High |
| **Concatenation** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 L_2$ regular | NFA with $\varepsilon$-transitions from $F_1$ to $q_{02}$ | Very High |
| **Kleene Star** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow L^*$ regular | NFA with new start/accept state and back-edges | Very High |
| **Complement** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow \overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$ regular | DFA, swap accepting and non-accepting states | Very High |
| **Intersection** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 \cap L_2$ regular | Product DFA construction | Very High |
| **Reversal** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow L^R$ regular | Reverse NFA transitions, swap start/accept | High |
| **Difference** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 - L_2$ regular | $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$ (De Morgan's Law) | High |
| **Homomorphism** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow h(L)$ regular | Substitute symbols in regex with $h(a)$ | Medium |
| **Inverse Homomorphism** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow h^{-1}(L)$ regular | Modify DFA to also process $h(a)$ | Medium |
| **Substitution** | $L, L_a$ regular $\Rightarrow f(L)$ regular | Replace each $a$ in $L$ with regex for $L_a$ | Low |

> [!WARNING] **Pipes in Math ŌĆö Markdown Survival Tip**
> When writing modular set operations like "$\Sigma^*$" in tables, **avoid** the bare ASCII pipe $\vert$ inside a LaTeX expression within a markdown row. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., `$\Sigma^{\ast} \mid L$` written as `$\Sigma^{\ast} \mid L$`). The author must use `$\mid$` to prevent table-row corruption.

## 2.3 The "Big Idea" Behind Closure Properties

The set of regular languages is a **closed** family under the operations listed above. Proofs use one of three engineering blueprints:

1. **NFA Construction Blueprint:** Start with NFAs $N_1$ for $L_1$ and $N_2$ for $L_2$. Add a small number of new states and $\varepsilon$-transitions to combine them.
2. **DFA Manipulation Blueprint:** Convert the DFA (must be total) into a new DFA by swapping states (complement) or taking the Cartesian product (intersection).
3. **Regular Expression Manipulation Blueprint:** Apply the operation symbolically to the regex of $L$ and produce a new valid regex.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Regular Grammar & Closure |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Compiler Design** | Lexical analyzers (scanners) for tokens, identifiers, numbers |
| **Network Protocol Verification** | Checking valid packet formats; closure under union/intersection allows building complex rule sets |
| **Pattern Matching Engines** | `grep`, `awk`, regular expressions in text editors |
| **Digital Circuit Design** | Sequential circuits with finite memory correspond to regular languages |
| **Bioinformatics** | Identifying DNA/protein motifs ŌĆö finite patterns are regular |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Constructing an RLG

**Language:** $L = \{a^n b \mid n \geq 1\}$ ŌĆö one or more $a$'s followed by exactly one $b$.

> [!NOTE] **Step-by-Step Construction Strategy**
> 1. Identify the *loop* portion (the $a^n$ part) ŌĆö needs recursive production.
> 2. Identify the *terminating* portion (the final $b$) ŌĆö needs a terminal-only rule.
> 3. Verify that the start symbol is correctly placed.

**Step 1:** Define the start symbol $S$. It must generate at least one $a$ before moving to the "loop" state.

$$S \rightarrow aA$$

**Step 2:** Define the looping state $A$, which generates more $a$'s (zero or more additional) before terminating.

$$A \rightarrow aA \quad \text{(recurse for more } a\text{)}$$
$$A \rightarrow b \quad \text{(terminate with the final } b\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Combine. The complete RLG is:

$$G: \quad S \rightarrow aA, \quad A \rightarrow aA \mid b$$

**Verification by Derivation** of the string $aab$:

$$S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow aab$$

Ō£ö The string is generated. This grammar is **right-linear** (all non-terminals appear on the right). Ō£ö

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Constructing an RLG for Union of Two Patterns

**Language:** $L = \{ab, ba\}$ ŌĆö exactly the strings $ab$ and $ba$.

**Step 1:** Use the start symbol $S$ to branch ŌĆö it can either start producing $ab$ or $ba$.

$$S \rightarrow aA \quad \text{(begin the } ab \text{ path)}$$
$$S \rightarrow bB \quad \text{(begin the } ba \text{ path)}$$

**Step 2:** Each branch must terminate with the second symbol.

$$A \rightarrow b, \quad B \rightarrow a$$

**Complete RLG:**

$$G: \quad S \rightarrow aA \mid bB, \quad A \rightarrow b, \quad B \rightarrow a$$

**Derivation of $ab$:** $S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow ab$ Ō£ö
**Derivation of $ba$:** $S \Rightarrow bB \Rightarrow ba$ Ō£ö

## 3.3 Closure Property Proof 1 ŌĆö Union ($L_1 \cup L_2$)

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular languages, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is also regular.

**Proof by NFA Construction:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume existence of NFAs.** Let $N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{01}, F_1)$ and $N_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{02}, F_2)$ be NFAs such that $L(N_1) = L_1$ and $L(N_2) = L_2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the combined NFA $N$.** Define $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:

$$Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\} \quad \text{(add one new start state)}$$
$$q_0 \text{ is a new state, not in } Q_1 \cup Q_2$$
$$F = F_1 \cup F_2 \quad \text{(keep the original accepting states)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the transition function $\delta$.** For every $q \in Q$ and $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$:

$$\delta(q, a) = \begin{cases} \delta_1(q, a) & \text{if } q \in Q_1 \\ \delta_2(q, a) & \text{if } q \in Q_2 \\ \{q_{01}, q_{02}\} & \text{if } q = q_0 \text{ and } a = \varepsilon \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Language Equivalence Argument.** A string $w$ is accepted by $N$ iff there exists a path in $N$ from $q_0$ to some $f \in F$ consuming $w$. The first $\varepsilon$-transition from $q_0$ non-deterministically chooses $q_{01}$ (path through $N_1$) or $q_{02}$ (path through $N_2$). Therefore:

$$L(N) = L(N_1) \cup L(N_2) = L_1 \cup L_2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion.** Since $N$ is an NFA, $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Closure Property Proof 2 ŌĆö Concatenation ($L_1 L_2$)

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 L_2$ is regular.

**Proof by NFA Construction:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume NFAs $N_1$ and $N_2$ as in Section 3.3.**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct $N$ for concatenation.** Define:

$$Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2, \quad q_0 = q_{01}, \quad F = F_2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Transition function:**

- $\delta(q, a) = \delta_1(q, a)$ for $q \in Q_1 - F_1$ and $a \in \Sigma$
- $\delta(q, a) = \delta_1(q, a) \cup \delta_2(q, a)$ for $q \in F_1$ and $a \in \Sigma$ (adds $\varepsilon$-like ability: $F_1$ can also act as start of $N_2$)
- More precisely, modify $N_1$: for each $f \in F_1$ and each $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, add transitions from $f$ to mimic $q_{02}$'s outgoing behavior, AND make all original $F_1$ states **non-accepting** so that strings must continue into $N_2$ to be accepted.
- $\delta(q, a) = \delta_2(q, a)$ for $q \in Q_2$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Argument.** Any accepted string $w = xy$ must be split such that $N_1$ accepts $x$ (reaching some $f_1 \in F_1$), then the $\varepsilon$-transition pushes the simulation into $q_{02}$, and $N_2$ accepts $y$ (reaching $f_2 \in F_2$). Hence $L(N) = L_1 L_2$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Closure Property Proof 3 ŌĆö Kleene Star ($L^*$)

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $L^*$ is regular.

**Proof by NFA Construction:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume $N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{01}, F_1)$ with $L(N_1) = L$.**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$:**

$$Q = Q_1 \cup \{q_0\}$$
$$q_0 \text{ is a new state, } q_0 \notin Q_1$$
$$F = F_1 \cup \{q_0\} \quad \text{(new start state is also accepting ŌĆö accepts } \varepsilon\text{)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Transition function:**

- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_{01}\}$ ŌĆö enter the original NFA
- For each $f \in F_1$: add $\delta(f, \varepsilon) \supseteq \{q_{01}\}$ ŌĆö loop back to start (so multiple concatenations are possible)
- All other transitions remain as in $\delta_1$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Argument.** $N$ accepts $\varepsilon$ (via the new accepting start state). It accepts any concatenation $w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k$ where each $w_i \in L$ by looping: each $w_i$ drives the machine from $q_{01}$ to some $F_1$, the $\varepsilon$-transition returns to $q_{01}$, and the cycle repeats. Thus $L(N) = L^*$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Closure Property Proof 4 ŌĆö Complement ($\overline{L}$)

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$ is regular.

**Proof by DFA Construction:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume a DFA $D = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $L(D) = L$.**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Prerequisite**
> The DFA $D$ must be **complete** (total), meaning $\delta(q, a)$ is defined for **every** $q \in Q$ and **every** $a \in \Sigma$. If any transition is missing, add a "dead state" $d$ with self-loops on every input.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct $\overline{D} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q - F)$.** That is, the **only change** is swapping the accepting set $F$ with its complement $Q - F$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Argument.** A string $w$ is accepted by $D$ iff $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$. It is accepted by $\overline{D}$ iff $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in Q - F$. These two conditions are mutually exclusive and exhaustive (every string reaches exactly one state on completion). Therefore:

$$L(\overline{D}) = \Sigma^* - L(D) = \Sigma^* - L = \overline{L}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion.** $\overline{L}$ is recognized by the DFA $\overline{D}$, so it is regular. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Closure Property Proof 5 ŌĆö Intersection ($L_1 \cap L_2$)

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular.

**Proof by Product DFA Construction:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume DFAs** $D_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{01}, F_1)$ and $D_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{02}, F_2)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the product DFA** $D = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$:

$$Q = Q_1 \times Q_2 \quad \text{(Cartesian product of states)}$$
$$q_0 = (q_{01}, q_{02})$$
$$F = F_1 \times F_2 \quad \text{(accepting iff both components accept)}$$
$$\delta((p, q), a) = (\delta_1(p, a), \delta_2(q, a))$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Argument.** A string $w$ drives $D_1$ from $q_{01}$ to some $p$ and $D_2$ from $q_{02}$ to some $q$. The product DFA tracks both simultaneously. Acceptance requires $p \in F_1$ AND $q \in F_2$. Hence $L(D) = L_1 \cap L_2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Connection ŌĆö De Morgan**
> Intersection can also be proved via Union + Complement:
> $$L_1 \cap L_2 = \overline{\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}}$$
> This requires the **algebraic closure of the family** under union and complement, and is a classic exam technique.

## 3.8 Closure Property Proof 6 ŌĆö Reversal ($L^R$)

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $L^R$ is regular.

**Proof by NFA Reversal:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with** $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L(N) = L$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct $N^R$:**

- **Reverse every transition** of $N$ (if $\delta(p, a) \ni q$, then $N^R$ has a transition from $q$ to $p$ on $a$).
- **Add a new start state** $q_0'$ and an $\varepsilon$-transition from $q_0'$ to **every** original accepting state $f \in F$ (this handles the case where $F$ has multiple states).
- **Make $q_0$ the sole accepting state** of $N^R$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Argument.** A path $q_0 \to \cdots \to f$ in $N$ consuming $w = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$ becomes, in $N^R$, a path $f \to \cdots \to q_0$ consuming $a_n \cdots a_2 a_1 = w^R$. Thus $L(N^R) = L^R$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Reference for Proofs

| Operation | Symbolic Form | Construction Type | Key Trick |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Union | $L_1 \cup L_2$ | NFA with new start | $\varepsilon$-branching |
| Concatenation | $L_1 L_2$ | NFA modification | $\varepsilon$-transitions from $F_1$ to $q_{02}$ |
| Star | $L^*$ | NFA with new start/accept | Loop back from $F_1$ to $q_{01}$ |
| Complement | $\overline{L}$ | DFA modification | Swap $F$ and $Q - F$ |
| Intersection | $L_1 \cap L_2$ | Product DFA | $(p, q)$ pair-wise simulation |
| Reversal | $L^R$ | NFA reversal | Reverse arrows, swap roles |
| Difference | $L_1 - L_2$ | $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$ | Combine two closures |
| Homomorphism | $h(L)$ | Regex substitution | $h(a)$ replaces each $a$ in regex |
| Inv. Homomorphism | $h^{-1}(L)$ | DFA modification | DFA reads $h(a)$ symbol-by-symbol |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NFA Construction for Union of $L_1$ and $L_2$

The following Mermaid block illustrates the standard NFA construction for $L_1 \cup L_2$. A new start state `s0` non-deterministically dispatches to either component NFA via $\varepsilon$-transitions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0((S NEW))
    s1((Q01))
    s2((Q02))
    f1((F1 ACC))
    f2((F2 ACC))

    s0 -->|EPS| s1
    s0 -->|EPS| s2
    s1 -->|READ A| f1
    s2 -->|READ B| f2
```

**Reading Guide:** From state `S NEW`, the NFA can either take the upper path (recognizing $L_1$) or the lower path (recognizing $L_2$), without consuming any input symbol.

## 4.2 NFA Construction for Concatenation $L_1 L_2$

The block diagram below shows the $\varepsilon$-bridges from the accepting states of $L_1$ to the start state of $L_2$. The original accepting states of $L_1$ are demoted to non-accepting (note the double-circle change).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q01((Q01 START L1))
    f1a((F1A DEMOTED))
    f1b((F1B DEMOTED))
    q02((Q02 START L2))
    f2((F2 ACC L2))

    q01 -->|A| f1a
    q01 -->|A| f1b
    f1a -->|EPS| q02
    f1b -->|EPS| q02
    q02 -->|B| f2
```

**Reading Guide:** $L_1$'s accepting states now serve as **gateways** to $L_2$ ŌĆö they are not themselves accepting anymore. The string must traverse $L_1$ first, then $L_2$.

## 4.3 NFA Construction for Kleene Star $L^*$

The Mermaid block below shows the three critical $\varepsilon$-transitions: (1) new start state to original start, (2) original accepting states back to original start, and (3) new start state is itself accepting (handles $\varepsilon$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sNew((S NEW ACC))
    q01((Q01 ORIGINAL))
    f1a((F1A))
    f1b((F1B))

    sNew -->|EPS| q01
    sNew -.->|SELF ACCEPT EPS| sNew
    f1a -->|EPS LOOP| q01
    f1b -->|EPS LOOP| q01
    q01 -->|A| f1a
    q01 -->|A| f1b
```

**Reading Guide:** The dashed self-loop on `S NEW` represents the fact that the new start state is **also** an accepting state (it accepts $\varepsilon \in L^*$). The loops from $F_1$ back to $Q_{01}$ allow multiple concatenations of $L$.

## 4.4 Derivation Tree of a Sample String (Right Linear)

For the RLG $G: S \rightarrow aA,\ A \rightarrow aA \mid b$, the string $aab$ is derived as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["S"]
    n2["aA"]
    n3["aaA"]
    n4["aab"]

    n1 -->|APPLY S->aA| n2
    n2 -->|APPLY A->aA| n3
    n3 -->|APPLY A->b| n4
```

**Reading Guide:** Each node is a **sentential form**; each edge is a single derivation step. The path from $S$ to the terminal-only string represents one valid leftmost derivation.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Closure Property Proof Pipeline

For the more abstract proofs (intersection, difference, inverse homomorphism), the proof pipeline can be visualized as a functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1["INPUT LANGUAGE 1"]
        L1["L1 REGULAR"]
    end
    subgraph IN2["INPUT LANGUAGE 2"]
        L2["L2 REGULAR"]
    end

    L1 --> A1["NFA1 / DFA1"]
    L2 --> A2["NFA2 / DFA2"]

    A1 --> OP["OPERATION MODULE: UNION / CONCAT / STAR / COMPLEMENT / INTERSECT"]
    A2 --> OP

    OP --> OUT["OUTPUT NFA / DFA"]
    OUT --> RES["L OUT IS REGULAR"]
```

**Reading Guide:** This block-level view abstracts the specific construction details. Every closure proof follows the same pattern: take input automata, apply a transformation module, produce an output automaton. The conclusion is always that the resulting language is regular.

## 4.6 Product DFA Construction for Intersection

The product DFA pairs up states. For two DFAs with states $\{p_1, p_2\}$ and $\{q_1, q_2\}$, the product DFA has 4 composite states. Acceptance requires **both** components to be accepting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(("(P1, Q1) START"))
    a1(("(P1, Q2)"))
    a2(("(P2, Q1)"))
    a3(("(P2, Q2) ACC"))
    dead(("(P2, Q2) DEAD"))

    start -->|A| a1
    start -->|B| a2
    a1 -->|A| a3
    a2 -->|B| dead
    a3 -->|A| a3
    a3 -->|B| a3
```

**Reading Guide:** Only composite states where the first coordinate is in $F_1$ **AND** the second coordinate is in $F_2$ become accepting. This guarantees both $D_1$ and $D_2$ accept the string.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define a regular grammar. Distinguish between right-linear and left-linear grammars with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **regular grammar** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is a formal grammar in which every production is of the restricted form $A \rightarrow aB$, $A \rightarrow a$, or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

| Aspect | Right Linear Grammar | Left Linear Grammar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form | $A \rightarrow aB$ | $A \rightarrow Ba$ |
| Position of non-terminal | Right side | Left side |
| Example rule | $S \rightarrow aS \mid b$ | $S \rightarrow Sa \mid b$ |
| Language generated | $\{a^n b \mid n \geq 0\}$ | $\{b a^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ |

> **Key Distinction:** Both generate the same family of languages, but a single grammar must be exclusively one type. Mixing both forms in one grammar is not allowed.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Paper]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**State the closure properties of regular languages. Prove closure under union using NFA construction.**

**Model Answer (Condensed for 3 marks):**

Regular languages are **closed** under union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection, reversal, difference, homomorphism, and inverse homomorphism.

**Closure under Union ŌĆö Proof Sketch:**

Let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be regular, with NFAs $N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{01}, F_1)$ and $N_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{02}, F_2)$.

Construct $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with:
- $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}$ where $q_0$ is a new start state
- $F = F_1 \cup F_2$ (original accepting states retained)
- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_{01}, q_{02}\}$ ŌĆö non-deterministic branching
- All other transitions inherited from $N_1$ and $N_2$

Then $L(N) = L(N_1) \cup L(N_2) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Hence $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. ŌłÄ

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö OPTION 1

**(a) Construct a regular grammar (right-linear) for the language $L = \{a^n b^m \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 1\}$ over the alphabet $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$. Also, show the derivation of the string $aaabbb$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the structure.** The string must start with at least one $a$, then continue with zero or more $a$'s (extending the $a$-block), and then have at least one $b$, possibly with more $b$'s (extending the $b$-block).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define non-terminals and productions.**

- $S \rightarrow aS$ ŌĆö generates the $a$-block (must be at least one, so start with this)
- $S \rightarrow aA$ ŌĆö transitions to the $b$-block (consumes the first required $a$)

- $A \rightarrow bA$ ŌĆö generates additional $b$'s (after the first $b$)
- $A \rightarrow b$ ŌĆö terminates the string

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final Grammar.**

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$
$$V = \{S, A\}, \quad T = \{a, b\}$$
$$P: \quad S \rightarrow aS \mid aA, \quad A \rightarrow bA \mid b$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derivation of $aaabbb$:**

$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aaaA \Rightarrow aaabA \Rightarrow aaabbA \Rightarrow aaabbb$$

All seven derivation steps are valid. The string $aaabbb$ has three $a$'s and three $b$'s, satisfying $n = 3 \geq 1$ and $m = 3 \geq 1$. Ō£ö

**Verification of Regularity:** The grammar is right-linear. It accepts the language $L = a^+ b^+ = \{a^n b^m \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 1\}$. Ō£ö

---

**(b) Prove that regular languages are closed under Kleene star. Use an NFA construction with a clear diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $L^*$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumption.** Let $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be an NFA with $L(N) = L$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construction.** Build $N^* = (Q^*, \Sigma, \delta^*, q_0', F^*)$:

- $Q^* = Q \cup \{q_0'\}$ where $q_0' \notin Q$
- $q_0'$ is the new start state
- $F^* = F \cup \{q_0'\}$ ŌĆö new start state is also accepting (so $\varepsilon \in L^*$)
- Transition rules:
  - $\delta^*(q_0', \varepsilon) \ni q_0$ ŌĆö enter the original NFA
  - For every $f \in F$: $\delta^*(f, \varepsilon) \ni q_0$ ŌĆö loop back to original start
  - All other transitions: $\delta^*(q, a) = \delta(q, a)$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Diagram.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sNew((S0 PRIME ACC))
    q0((Q0))
    f1((F1))
    f2((F2))

    sNew -->|EPS| q0
    sNew -.->|SELF EPS ACC| sNew
    f1 -->|EPS| q0
    f2 -->|EPS| q0
    q0 -->|A| f1
    q0 -->|A| f2
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Correctness Argument.**

- The empty string $\varepsilon$ is accepted because $q_0' \in F^*$.
- Any non-empty string $w = w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k$ with each $w_i \in L$ is accepted as follows: $N^*$ starts at $q_0'$, takes $\varepsilon$ to $q_0$, processes $w_1$ to some $f_1 \in F$, takes $\varepsilon$ back to $q_0$, processes $w_2$ to some $f_2 \in F$, and so on, until the last $w_k$ terminates at some $f_k \in F \subseteq F^*$.
- Conversely, any accepted string follows this structure.

Therefore $L(N^*) = L^*$, which is regular. ŌłÄ

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Module 2]**

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö OPTION 2 (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a) Construct a regular grammar for the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w \text{ contains the substring } ba\}$. Show the derivation of $aba$ and $bba$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize the structure.** The language contains all strings over $\{a, b\}$ that have at least one occurrence of the substring $ba$. We can break the string into three parts: *prefix* (any string), the substring $ba$, and *suffix* (any string).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define non-terminals.**

- $S$ ŌĆö start; represents the "before substring" phase
- $A$ ŌĆö represents "we have just seen the $b$ that begins the substring"
- $B$ ŌĆö represents "we have already seen the substring $ba$"; once reached, $B$ stays in $B$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Productions.**

- $S \rightarrow aS \mid bA \mid \varepsilon$ ŌĆö loop on $a$; on $b$, transition to $A$ (potential start of $ba$)
- $A \rightarrow aB$ ŌĆö on $a$ after $b$, we have $ba$! Move to $B$
- $A \rightarrow bA$ ŌĆö on another $b$, stay in $A$
- $A \rightarrow a$ ŌĆö could be end of string (string was just $ba$)
- $B \rightarrow aB \mid bB \mid \varepsilon$ ŌĆö once $ba$ has been seen, any continuation is fine

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Grammar.**

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$
$$V = \{S, A, B\}, \quad T = \{a, b\}$$
$$P: \quad S \rightarrow aS \mid bA \mid \varepsilon$$
$$\quad A \rightarrow aB \mid bA \mid a$$
$$\quad B \rightarrow aB \mid bB \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derivation of $aba$:**

$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow abA \Rightarrow aba$$

Verifying: prefix "$a$", substring "$ba$", suffix is empty. Ō£ö

**Step 6 ŌĆö Derivation of $bba$:**

$$S \Rightarrow bA \Rightarrow bbA \Rightarrow bba$$

Verifying: prefix empty, substring "$ba$", suffix is empty. Ō£ö

**Verification of Regularity:** All productions are right-linear. Ō£ö

---

**(b) Prove that regular languages are closed under complement. State the necessary condition on the DFA before construction.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumption.** Let $D = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be a DFA with $L(D) = L$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Necessary Condition (Prerequisite for 3 Marks)**
> The DFA $D$ must be **complete** (or **total**). This means for every state $q \in Q$ and every input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, the transition $\delta(q, a)$ must be **defined** (i.e., not undefined). If the given DFA has missing transitions, we add a "dead" or "trap" state $d$ with $\delta(d, a) = d$ for all $a \in \Sigma$, and route all undefined transitions to $d$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construction.** Build $\overline{D} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q - F)$:
- States, alphabet, start state, and transition function are **identical** to $D$.
- The **only change** is the accepting set becomes $Q - F$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Proof of Correctness.**

For any string $w \in \Sigma^*$, let $q = \delta^*(q_0, w)$ be the unique state reached by $D$ (and $\overline{D}$, since they have the same transitions) on consuming $w$.

- $w \in L(D)$ iff $q \in F$ ŌĆö accepted by $D$
- $w \in L(\overline{D})$ iff $q \in Q - F$ ŌĆö accepted by $\overline{D}$

These two conditions are **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive** (every $w$ leads to exactly one state, and that state is in exactly one of $F$ or $Q - F$).

Therefore $L(\overline{D}) = \Sigma^* - L(D) = \overline{L}$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Worked Illustration.** Consider $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ and $L = \{a\}$.

- DFA for $L$: $q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1$ (accept), $q_0 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$ (dead, non-accept), $q_1 \xrightarrow{a} q_2$, $q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$, $q_2 \xrightarrow{a} q_2$, $q_2 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$.
- Swap $F$: Now $q_1$ is non-accepting, $q_0$ and $q_2$ are accepting. The accepted language becomes $\Sigma^* - \{a\} = \{\varepsilon, b, aa, ab, ba, bb, \ldots\}$, which is exactly $\overline{L}$. Ō£ö

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the "Total DFA" prerequisite in Complement (5ŌĆō7 marks lost):** Many students directly swap accepting and non-accepting states without ensuring the DFA is complete. If the original DFA has undefined transitions, the swapped DFA will *crash* (not accept or reject) on those inputs, making the proof **invalid**. Always add a dead state if necessary.
> 
> **2. Mixing right-linear and left-linear rules in one grammar (2ŌĆō3 marks lost):** A grammar like $S \rightarrow aA \mid Ba$ is **not regular** by KTU's strict definition. Pick **one** form and stay consistent.
> 
> **3. Missing the $\varepsilon$-transition in NFA constructions (3ŌĆō5 marks lost):** In union, concatenation, and Kleene star proofs, the **new transitions** are *all* $\varepsilon$-transitions on the new start state. Forgetting to mark them as $\varepsilon$ (or confusing them with symbol transitions) breaks the construction.
> 
> **4. Confusing $L_1 \cap L_2$ with $L_1 \cdot L_2$ (2ŌĆō3 marks lost):** Intersection is **set intersection** (product DFA), while concatenation is **string concatenation** (NFA modification). These are entirely different operations.
> 
> **5. Forgetting to verify the start symbol produces valid derivations (1ŌĆō2 marks lost):** Always show 1ŌĆō2 sample derivations to demonstrate the constructed grammar/NFA works.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Pin This for Last-Minute Revision**

### Core Definitions
- **Regular Grammar $G$:** 4-tuple $(V, T, P, S)$ with all productions of restricted form.
- **Right Linear Grammar (RLG):** $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- **Left Linear Grammar (LLG):** $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- Regular grammars generate **exactly the regular languages** (same class as finite automata and regular expressions).

### Construction Strategies
- **Loop** in a language $\rightarrow$ recursive production (e.g., $A \rightarrow aA$).
- **Concatenation of patterns** $\rightarrow$ a non-terminal **chain** $S \rightarrow aA \rightarrow bB \rightarrow \cdots$.
- **Union of patterns** $\rightarrow$ a start symbol with **multiple alternative productions**.
- **Optional occurrence** $\rightarrow$ the $\varepsilon$-production at the end of a chain.
- **Required occurrence followed by anything** $\rightarrow$ a "trap" accepting state with self-loops.

### The "Big 6" Closure Properties (MUST MEMORIZE)
1. **Union** $L_1 \cup L_2$ ŌĆö NFA with $\varepsilon$-branching new start
2. **Concatenation** $L_1 L_2$ ŌĆö $\varepsilon$-bridges from $F_1$ to $q_{02}$
3. **Kleene Star** $L^*$ ŌĆö new accepting start + $\varepsilon$-loops from $F$ back to start
4. **Complement** $\overline{L}$ ŌĆö complete DFA, swap $F$ and $Q - F$
5. **Intersection** $L_1 \cap L_2$ ŌĆö product DFA with $F = F_1 \times F_2$
6. **Reversal** $L^R$ ŌĆö reverse NFA arrows, swap start and accept

### The "Smaller 4" Closure Properties
7. **Difference** $L_1 - L_2$ ŌĆö use $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$
8. **Homomorphism** $h(L)$ ŌĆö substitute each symbol $a$ in the regex of $L$ with $h(a)$
9. **Inverse Homomorphism** $h^{-1}(L)$ ŌĆö modify DFA to process $h(a)$ symbol by symbol
10. **Substitution** $f(L)$ ŌĆö replace each symbol $a$ in regex of $L$ with regex for $f(a)$

### Critical Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- ŌØī Mixing right-linear and left-linear rules in one grammar
- ŌØī Forgetting the "total DFA" requirement before complement
- ŌØī Confusing intersection ($\cap$) with concatenation ($\cdot$)
- ŌØī Missing $\varepsilon$-transitions in NFA constructions
- ŌØī Forgetting to verify derivations with sample strings

### KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Topics
- ŌŁÉ Constructing RLGs for given language descriptions (very frequent)
- ŌŁÉ Proving closure under union, concatenation, star (very frequent)
- ŌŁÉ Proving closure under complement with the DFA-completeness caveat (very frequent)
- ŌŁÉ Product DFA construction for intersection (frequent)
- ŌŁÉ Conversions between regex, DFA, NFA, and RLG (frequent)

### Mnemonics to Lock It In
- **"RLG = Right Non-terminal"** ŌĆö non-terminals on the right of the production.
- **"Complement = Complement the F"** ŌĆö only $F$ changes; everything else stays.
- **"Star = Start is Accept, and F loops to Start"** ŌĆö three new $\varepsilon$-transitions.
- **"Union = New start, two epsilons"** ŌĆö new $q_0$ with $\varepsilon$ to both old starts.
- **"Intersection = Independent parallel reading"** ŌĆö $(p, q)$ tracks both DFAs at once.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages (Formal Statement)**
>
> Let $L$ be a regular language. Then there exists a constant $n \geq 1$ (called the *pumping length*) such that every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$ can be written as $w = xyz$ satisfying the three simultaneous conditions:
>
> 1. $\vert xy \vert \leq n$
> 2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
> 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for every integer $i \geq 0$

The lemma is a **necessary condition for regularity**, not a sufficient one. It is primarily used as a *proof-by-contradiction* tool to demonstrate that certain languages are **NOT regular** (i.e., they cannot be recognized by any DFA/NFA/regular expression).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **DFA with a finite number of states** (say, $n$ states). Consider reading a long string of length $\geq n$ through this machine. As the DFA processes the input, it transitions from one state to the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pigeonhole Principle Analogy:** If you have $n$ pigeonholes (states) and $\geq n+1$ pigeons (input symbols), at least two pigeons must share the same hole. In DFA terms, if a string has $\geq n$ symbols, by the time we finish reading it, the DFA must have revisited some state at least twice.

When the DFA revisits a state (say $q_k$) at two different positions in the input, the **sub-string read between these two visits** (call it $y$) corresponds to a loop in the state-transition graph. The brilliant insight is:

- You can **travel around this loop ZERO times** ŌåÆ still end up accepting (if the original was accepted).
- You can **travel around the loop ONCE, TWICE, ... infinitely many times** ŌåÆ still end up accepting.

This "looping" property is precisely what the pumping lemma captures. **The substring $y$ can be "pumped" (repeated) any number of times without leaving the language.**

### 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU Examinations

- The pumping lemma is the **standard tool** for proving non-regularity.
- KTU examiners frequently test it with a 14-mark Part-B question involving application to a specific language.
- It is a **high-weightage topic** appearing in nearly every KTU ESE under Module 2.

### 1.4 High-Yield Constants and Parameters

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|:------:|:--------|:--------------|
| $n$ | Pumping length (machine-dependent) | $n \geq 1$ |
| $w$ | String in $L$ to be decomposed | $\vert w \vert \geq n$ |
| $x, y, z$ | Three substrings of $w$ | $w = xyz$ |
| $i$ | Number of pump iterations | $i \geq 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The pumping lemma is **proved using the Pigeonhole Principle** applied to the states of a DFA. The proof is essential for KTU answers and is often asked as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Existence of a DFA:**
If $L$ is regular, by definition there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $p = \vert Q \vert$ states that recognizes $L$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set the pumping length:**
Choose $n = p$ (the number of states in the DFA). This is the pumping length.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take any long string:**
Let $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ (where $w_i \in \Sigma$) be any string in $L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Define the sequence of traversed states:**
Define the sequence of states $r_0, r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n$ where:
- $r_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $r_{i+1} = \delta(r_i, w_{i+1})$ for $0 \leq i < n$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Pigeonhole Principle:**
The sequence has $n+1$ states but the DFA has only $n = p$ distinct states. Therefore, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least two states in the sequence must be identical. That is, there exist indices $0 \leq j < k \leq n$ such that $r_j = r_k$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Define the decomposition:**
- $x = w_1 w_2 \dots w_j$  (substring that brings the DFA from $r_0$ to $r_j$)
- $y = w_{j+1} w_{j+2} \dots w_k$  (substring that loops the DFA from $r_j$ back to $r_k = r_j$)
- $z = w_{k+1} w_{k+2} \dots w_n$  (substring that brings the DFA from $r_k$ to the final state $r_n$)

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verify the three conditions:**

- **Condition 1:** $\vert xy \vert = k \leq n$ Ō£ō (since $k \leq n$)
- **Condition 2:** $\vert y \vert = k - j \geq 1$ Ō£ō (since $j < k$)
- **Condition 3:** For any $i \geq 0$, the string $xy^{i}z$ is accepted:
  - Reading $x$ takes DFA from $r_0$ to $r_j$.
  - Reading $y$ takes DFA from $r_j$ to $r_k = r_j$ (loop closure).
  - So reading $y$ any number of times keeps the DFA at $r_j$.
  - Reading $z$ then takes it from $r_j$ to $r_n$, which must be an accepting state.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters |
|:-----|:---------------|
| Existence of DFA | Regularity is assumed, so the DFA is our certificate. |
| $n = p$ | Pumping length is tied to the number of states ŌĆö the machine's "memory capacity". |
| Pigeonhole | Forces a state revisit when the string is too long. |
| Loop closure | The revisited state forms a cycle that can be traversed any number of times. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table ŌĆö All Conditions & Standard Template**

| # | Symbol / Statement | Formal Form | Meaning |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | Regular language property | $L$ is regular $\Rightarrow$ $\exists n \geq 1$ | Existence of pumping length |
| 2 | String length condition | $\forall w \in L, \vert w \vert \geq n$ | Long enough string |
| 3 | Decomposition | $w = xyz$ | Three-part split |
| 4 | Length of prefix | $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ | Loop lies in first $n$ symbols |
| 5 | Non-empty pump | $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ | Pumping segment has at least one symbol |
| 6 | Closure under pump | $xy^{i}z \in L, \forall i \geq 0$ | Pumping preserves language membership |
| 7 | Contrapositive (for proof) | $L$ is not regular if $\forall n, \exists w$ that fails | Negation template |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Detects whether lexical-token patterns require a DFA (regular) or a context-free grammar (pushdown).
- **Network Protocol Verification:** Identifies whether traffic patterns are expressible by finite-state machines.
- **Pattern Matching Engines:** Justifies why complex patterns (e.g., palindromes, matching brackets) need richer computational models.
- **Model Checking:** Distinguishes properties verifiable by finite-state exploration versus those requiring unbounded memory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Proof of the Pumping Lemma

> [!NOTE]
> The complete proof is given below. Every algebraic and logical step is explicitly shown ŌĆö no shortcuts are taken.

**Theorem:** If $L$ is a regular language, then $L$ satisfies the pumping lemma.

**Proof:**

Let $L$ be a regular language. By definition, there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L = L(M)$.

Let $p = \vert Q \vert$ denote the number of states of $M$. Set $n = p$.

Let $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_m$ be any string in $L$ with $m = \vert w \vert \geq n$.

We trace the execution of $M$ on $w$ by defining:

$$
r_0 = q_0
$$
$$
r_{i+1} = \delta(r_i, w_{i+1}) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq i < m
$$

This gives us a sequence of $m+1$ states: $r_0, r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m$.

Since $m \geq n = p$, we have:

$$
m + 1 \geq p + 1 > p = \vert Q \vert
$$

By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, the $m+1$ states $r_0, r_1, \dots, r_m$ (pigeons) cannot all be distinct among the $p$ states of $Q$ (pigeonholes). Therefore, there exist indices $j$ and $k$ with $0 \leq j < k \leq n$ such that:

$$
r_j = r_k
$$

Now define the decomposition $w = xyz$ as follows:

$$
x = w_1 w_2 \dots w_j
$$
$$
y = w_{j+1} w_{j+2} \dots w_k
$$
$$
z = w_{k+1} w_{k+2} \dots w_m
$$

**Verification of Condition 1:** $\vert xy \vert = k \leq n$. This holds because $k \leq n$ by construction.

**Verification of Condition 2:** $\vert y \vert = k - j \geq 1$. This holds because $j < k$.

**Verification of Condition 3:** We show that for any $i \geq 0$, the string $xy^{i}z$ is accepted by $M$.

- After reading $x$, the DFA is in state $r_j$.
- After reading $y$ once, the DFA moves from $r_j$ to $r_k = r_j$ (a loop).
- Therefore, after reading $y$ any number of times, the DFA remains in state $r_j$.
- After reading $z$, the DFA moves from $r_j$ to $r_m$, which is the same final state reached on the original string $w$.

Since $w \in L$, we have $r_m \in F$, and thus $r_m$ is an accepting state. Hence $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.

$\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Standard Negation Template (For Proving Non-Regularity)

To prove that a language $L$ is **not regular**, we use the **contrapositive** of the pumping lemma. The negation reads as:

> "There is **no** pumping length $n$ such that for all strings $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$, the decomposition $w = xyz$ satisfying all three conditions exists."

Equivalently, the game-theoretic form is:

> For every $n \geq 1$ (chosen by the adversary), there exists a string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$ (chosen by us) such that for every decomposition $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, the string $xy^{i}z \notin L$ for some $i \geq 0$.

**Roles:**
- **Adversary (opponent):** Chooses $n$.
- **You (prover):** Choose the string $w$ and find a value of $i$ that breaks the pump.

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Proving $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is Not Regular

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **classic KTU question**. Master the structure of this proof.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume the contrary:**
Suppose $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is regular. Then by the pumping lemma, there exists a pumping length $n$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose the string:**
Let the string be $w = a^n b^n$. Clearly $w \in L$ and $\vert w \vert = 2n \geq n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Analyze every valid decomposition:**
By the pumping lemma, $w = xyz$ where $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $w$ starts with $n$ a's, both $x$ and $y$ consist **entirely of $a$'s**. Therefore, $y = a^k$ for some $k \geq 1$, and $z = a^{n-k} b^n$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pump down with $i = 0$:**
Consider the pumped-down string $xy^0 z = xz = a^{n-k} b^n$.

The number of $a$'s is $n - k$ and the number of $b$'s is $n$. Since $k \geq 1$, we have $n - k < n$, so $n - k \neq n$.

Therefore, $xz \notin L$, contradicting the pumping lemma.

**Conclusion:** $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Proving $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{0,1\}^*\}$ is Not Regular

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume the contrary:**
Suppose $L$ is regular. Then there is a pumping length $n$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose the string:**
Let $w = 0^n 1^n 0^n 1^n$. Clearly $w \in L$ and $\vert w \vert = 4n \geq n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Analyze every valid decomposition:**
By the pumping lemma, $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \leq n$, both $x$ and $y$ lie entirely within the first $n$ symbols of $w$, which are all $0$'s. So $y = 0^k$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pump up with $i = 2$:**
Consider $xy^2 z$. This string has $n + k$ zeros at the start (instead of $n$), followed by $1^n 0^n 1^n$. So:

$$
xy^2 z = 0^{n+k} 1^n 0^n 1^n
$$

For this to be in $L$, the two halves must match. The first half is $0^{n+k} 1^n$ and the second half is $0^n 1^n$. Since $k \geq 1$, the first half is strictly longer than the second half. Therefore $xy^2 z \notin L$, contradicting the pumping lemma.

**Conclusion:** $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common KTU Languages and Their Pumping-Lemma Behavior

| Language $L$ | Regular? | Pumping Length Strategy | Counter $i$ |
|:-------------|:--------:|:------------------------|:-----------:|
| $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ | ŌØī | $w = a^n b^n$ | $i = 0$ |
| $\{w w \mid w \in \{0,1\}^*\}$ | ŌØī | $w = 0^n 1^n 0^n 1^n$ | $i = 2$ |
| $\{a^n \mid n \text{ is prime}\}$ | ŌØī | $w = a^p$ where $p$ is a chosen prime | $i = p+1$ |
| $\{a^n b^m c^n \mid n,m \geq 0\}$ | ŌØī | $w = a^n b^n c^n$ | $i = 0$ |
| $\{a^* b^*\}$ | Ō£ģ | Pump within $a^*$ or $b^*$ | ŌĆö |
| $\{w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid \vert w \vert \text{ even}\}$ | Ō£ģ | Pump any single symbol pair | ŌĆö |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Pumping Lemma Logic Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete logical flow of the Pumping Lemma proof and its application as a non-regularity test.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Given Language L"] --> nodeB["Is L Regular?"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Assume Yes - Find DFA with p states"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Set Pumping Length n equals p"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Pick w in L with length at least n"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Trace DFA on w - Generate State Sequence"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Apply Pigeonhole Principle"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Find indices j less than k with r_j equals r_k"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Define x, y, z decomposition"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Verify Three Conditions"]
    nodeJ --> condK["xy length at most n"]
    nodeJ --> condL["y length at least 1"]
    nodeJ --> condM["xy to the i z stays in L for all i"]
    condK --> doneN["Pumping Lemma Holds"]
    condL --> doneN
    condM --> doneN
    doneN --> outP["Conclusion: L may be regular"]

    nodeB --> brQ["No - Want to Prove Non Regular"]
    brQ --> brR["Adversary Chooses n"]
    brR --> brS["You Choose w in L with length at least n"]
    brS --> brT["For Every Valid Decomposition xyz"]
    brT --> brU["Find i such that xy to the i z not in L"]
    brU --> brV["Contradiction Established"]
    brV --> outW["Conclusion: L is Not Regular"]
```

### 4.2 DFA State-Traversal Diagram ŌĆö Visualizing the Pumping Loop

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagram shows how the Pigeonhole Principle forces a state revisit when $\vert w \vert$ exceeds the number of DFA states, creating a loop that the substring $y$ can traverse.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0["State q0 Start"] -->|x| s1["State rj Loop Entry"]
    s1 -->|y loop| s1
    s1 -->|z| s2["State rm Accept"]
    s1 -.pump i equals 0.-> s1
    s1 -.pump i equals 1.-> s1
    s1 -.pump i equals 2.-> s1
    s1 -.pump i equals k.-> s1
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The DFA reads $x$ and reaches state $r_j$.
- From $r_j$, reading $y$ takes it back to $r_j$ (a self-loop).
- The substring $z$ then takes it from $r_j$ to $r_m$, an accepting state.
- Pumping $y$ zero, one, two, or any number of times never leaves $r_j$, so the final accepting state is unchanged.

### 4.3 Decision Tree ŌĆö Application of Pumping Lemma

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA["Input: Language L"] --> qB{"Is L finite?"}
    qB -->|Yes| qC["L is Regular - Pumping Lemma Trivially Holds"]
    qB -->|No| qD{"Try to Construct a DFA or Regex"}
    qD -->|Success| qE["L is Regular"]
    qD -->|Failure| qF["Apply Pumping Lemma Contrapositive"]
    qF --> qG["Choose Adversary n"]
    qG --> qH["Pick String w"]
    qH --> qI{"For every decomposition, does pumping break L?"}
    qI -->|Yes| qJ["Contradiction - L is Not Regular"]
    qI -->|No| qK["Try different w or pumping value i"]
    qK --> qH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*State the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. Mention all three conditions clearly.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pumping Lemma Statement:**
>
> If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists an integer $n \geq 1$ (called the pumping length) such that for every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$, $w$ can be decomposed as $w = xyz$ satisfying:
>
> 1. $\vert xy \vert \leq n$
> 2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
> 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for every $i \geq 0$

**(1 mark)** for the existence statement of $n$. **(1 mark)** for stating conditions 1 and 2. **(1 mark)** for stating condition 3.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
*What is the role of the Pigeonhole Principle in proving the Pumping Lemma?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The proof of the Pumping Lemma considers a DFA with $p$ states recognizing the regular language $L$. Set $n = p$. For any string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$, the DFA traverses a sequence of $\vert w \vert + 1 \geq n + 1$ states. Since there are only $n$ distinct states, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least two states in the sequence must be identical. This state-revisit creates a loop, which becomes the pumpable substring $y$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose marks on Pumping Lemma questions due to:
> 1. **Forgetting to state all three conditions explicitly** ŌĆö even one missing condition costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Choosing $w$ such that the pump does not break the language** ŌĆö always verify $xy^{i}z \notin L$ for some $i$.
> 3. **Vague handling of "for every decomposition"** ŌĆö be specific about which characters $y$ contains.
> 4. **Skipping the initial "Assume L is regular"** ŌĆö the proof is by contradiction; this step is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]`

**Prove that the language $L = \{a^{n^2} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is not regular using the Pumping Lemma.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö State the Pumping Lemma and Outline the Proof Strategy [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of the Pumping Lemma** (3 marks):
If $L$ is regular, $\exists n \geq 1$ such that $\forall w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$, $w = xyz$ where $\vert xy \vert \leq n$, $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.

**Outline of the Proof Strategy** (4 marks):
We use proof by contradiction. We assume $L$ is regular. We let the pumping length be $n$. We choose a string $w \in L$ of length $\geq n$. We then show that pumping the substring $y$ either increases or decreases the length of the string to a value that is not a perfect square, thereby obtaining a contradiction. The key idea is that consecutive perfect squares differ by at least $2n+1$ for $n \geq 1$, so any length change of at most $n$ cannot keep the length as a perfect square.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Complete the Proof that $L = \{a^{n^2} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is Not Regular [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume for contradiction** (1 mark):
Suppose $L = \{a^{n^2} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $n \geq 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose a string in $L$** (1 mark):
Let $k = n$ and consider the string $w = a^{k^2}$. Then $w \in L$ and $\vert w \vert = k^2 \geq n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Pumping Lemma** (1 mark):
By the Pumping Lemma, $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. Let $\vert y \vert = m$ where $1 \leq m \leq n$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pump up with $i = 2$** (1 mark):
Consider $xy^2 z$. The length of this string is:

$$
\vert xy^2 z \vert = \vert xz \vert + \vert y \vert = (k^2 - m) + 2m = k^2 + m
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Show contradiction** (2 marks):
We need to show that $k^2 + m$ is not a perfect square for $1 \leq m \leq n = k$.

The next perfect square after $k^2$ is $(k+1)^2 = k^2 + 2k + 1$. The difference is:

$$
(k+1)^2 - k^2 = 2k + 1
$$

Since $1 \leq m \leq k$ and $2k + 1 > k$ for $k \geq 1$, we have $k^2 < k^2 + m < k^2 + 2k + 1 = (k+1)^2$. So $k^2 + m$ lies strictly between two consecutive perfect squares and therefore is **not a perfect square**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclusion** (1 mark):
Hence $xy^2 z \notin L$, contradicting the Pumping Lemma. Therefore $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.4 Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]`

**Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that the language $L = \{a^n b^m c^n \mid n, m \geq 1\}$ is not regular.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Formal Statement of the Pumping Lemma and Proof by Contradiction Setup [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Formal Statement of the Pumping Lemma** (3 marks):
Let $L$ be a regular language. Then $\exists n \geq 1$ such that $\forall w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$, we can write $w = xyz$ where:
- $\vert xy \vert \leq n$
- $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
- $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Proof Setup** (4 marks):
We argue by contradiction. Suppose $L = \{a^n b^m c^n \mid n, m \geq 1\}$ is regular. Then there exists a pumping length $n \geq 1$. Now pick a string in $L$ that has the property that pumping any substring of length $\leq n$ in its first part destroys the count balance between $a$'s and $c$'s. The string we choose is $w = a^n b^n c^n$. We have $w \in L$ and $\vert w \vert = 3n \geq n$. The contradiction will arise by showing that no matter how $w$ is decomposed, the pumped string is not in $L$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Complete the Proof [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose $w$** (2 marks):
By the Pumping Lemma, $w = a^n b^n c^n$ can be written as $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \leq n$, both $x$ and $y$ must be substrings of the first $n$ symbols of $w$, which are all $a$'s. Hence $x = a^p$ and $y = a^q$ for some non-negative integers $p, q$ with $p + q \leq n$ and $q \geq 1$. Then $z = a^{n - p - q} b^n c^n$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pump down with $i = 0$** (1 mark):
Consider $xy^0 z = xz = a^{n - q} b^n c^n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Show $xz \notin L$** (3 marks):
For $xz$ to be in $L$, it must have the form $a^s b^t c^s$ for some $s, t \geq 1$. We see $s = n - q$, $t = n$, and indeed $s + s = 2n - 2q$ while the number of $a$'s and $c$'s in $xz$ are $n - q$ and $n$ respectively.

For $xz$ to be in $L$, the count of $a$'s must equal the count of $c$'s, i.e., $n - q = n$. But $q \geq 1$, so $n - q \neq n$. Therefore $xz \notin L$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion** (1 mark):
This contradicts the Pumping Lemma's requirement that $xy^0 z \in L$. Hence $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **Definition:** Pumping Lemma is a **necessary (not sufficient)** condition for regularity. Used as a **proof-by-contradiction** tool.

- **Three Conditions to Memorize:**
  - $\vert xy \vert \leq n$ ŌåÆ "loop lies within the first $n$ symbols"
  - $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ ŌåÆ "pump is non-empty"
  - $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ ŌåÆ "pumping preserves language membership"

- **Proof Strategy (6 Steps):**
  1. Assume $L$ is regular
  2. Apply Pumping Lemma ŌåÆ get $n$
  3. Choose $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq n$
  4. Decompose $w = xyz$ (use the conditions to constrain $y$)
  5. Find $i$ such that $xy^{i}z \notin L$
  6. Conclude $L$ is not regular

- **Pigeonhole Principle:** The cornerstone of the proof. With $p$ states and $\geq p+1$ transitions, at least one state is revisited.

- **Choice of $i$:** Either $i = 0$ (pump down) or $i = 2$ (pump up) ŌĆö these are the two most common and should be tried first.

- **Constraint $\vert xy \vert \leq n$:** This means $y$ is "close to the start" of the string. Use this to determine what characters $y$ contains.

- **Common Pitfalls:**
  - ŌØī Forgetting the "for all $i \geq 0$" quantifier
  - ŌØī Choosing $w$ such that $y$ might be empty
  - ŌØī Not showing the contradiction for **every** valid decomposition
  - ŌØī Confusing the pumping lemma with closure properties

- **Languages That ARE Regular** (where the lemma holds trivially):
  - $L = a^* b^*$
  - $L = (ab)^*$
  - $L = \{w \mid \vert w \vert \text{ is even}\}$

- **Languages That Are NOT Regular** (where the lemma fails):
  - $L = \{a^n b^n\}$
  - $L = \{w w\}$
  - $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$
  - $L = \{a^{n^2}\}$
  - $L = \{a^p \mid p \text{ is prime}\}$

- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** The four standard examples ($a^n b^n$, $ww$, $a^n b^n c^n$, $a^{n^2}$) cover ~80% of KTU's past pumping-lemma questions. Master these four.

- **Memory Aid for Decomposition:** Think of $w = xyz$ as:
  - $x$ = the part **before** the loop
  - $y$ = the **loop** itself
  - $z$ = the part **after** the loop

- **Final Tip:** Always **state the pumping lemma explicitly** at the start of your answer. KTU examiners award marks for the formal statement, even before the proof begins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumping Lemma as a Tool to Prove Non-Regularity of Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pumping Lemma as a Tool to Prove Non-Regularity of Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumping Lemma (For Regular Languages):**
> Let $L$ be a **regular language**. Then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (called the *pumping length*) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed into three substrings $s = xyz$ satisfying the following three conditions:
>
> 1. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for every integer $i \geq 0$ (pumping property)
> 2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ (the middle part is non-empty)
> 3. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ (the pumpable region lies within the first $p$ symbols)

The lemma is a **necessary condition for regularity**. Therefore, its **contrapositive** is used as a tool to prove that a language is **non-regular**: if a language fails the pumping test for *every* possible decomposition, it cannot be recognized by any DFA/NFA, and hence is not regular.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rubber-Band Analogy:**
> Imagine a long rubber band that represents an accepted string $s$ inside a regular language $L$. The DFA that recognizes $L$ has only a **finite** number of states, say $p$. As the DFA reads the input string, it transitions from state to state.
>
> - If the string is longer than $p$ symbols, the DFA **must revisit some state** at least twice (by the **Pigeonhole Principle**).
> - The portion of the input that loops between these two repeated states is the segment $y$.
> - Because $y$ corresponds to a loop in the state diagram, we can "pump" it: stretch it (pump up, $i > 1$) or remove it (pump down, $i = 0$), and the DFA will still accept the resulting string, since it ends in the same accepting state.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pumping Length ($p$)** | A non-negative integer (Ōēź 1) that depends only on the language $L$. |
| **Decomposition ($xyz$)** | A partition of the string $s$ into three contiguous substrings. |
| **Pumped String ($xy^{i}z$)** | The string obtained by repeating $y$ exactly $i$ times. |
| **Adversary (Foe / Opponent)** | The hypothetical opponent who picks the decomposition to try to *keep* the string inside $L$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Misconception Alert:** The Pumping Lemma is often misunderstood. It is **NOT** an *if-and-only-if* condition. Some non-regular languages *can* be pumped (the lemma gives a necessary, but not sufficient, condition). Hence, **pumping succeeds $\not\Rightarrow$ regular**, but **pumping fails $\Rightarrow$ not regular**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Pumping Lemma

The statement can be broken down into a chain of logical quantifiers, which is essential for KTU board answers:

$$
\forall L \text{ regular} \;\Rightarrow\; \exists\, p \geq 1 \; \text{such that}\; \forall\, s \in L, \; \vert s \vert \geq p \;\Rightarrow\; \exists\, x,y,z : (s = xyz) \;\wedge\; (\text{Conditions 1, 2, 3 hold})
$$

The proof technique (Proof by Contradiction / Game Strategy) flips this logic:

$$
\exists L \; \text{non-regular} \;\Leftarrow\; \forall\, p \geq 1, \; \exists\, s \in L, \; \vert s \vert \geq p \; \text{s.t.}\; \forall\, x,y,z, \; \text{at least one condition fails}
$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Condition

- **Condition 1 ($xy^{i}z \in L$):** Because $y$ corresponds to a cycle in the DFA, repeating it $i$ times still traces a valid path to an accept state.
- **Condition 2 ($\vert y \vert \geq 1$):** Ensures we are genuinely "pumping" something. If $y = \varepsilon$, the decomposition is trivial and useless.
- **Condition 3 ($\vert xy \vert \leq p$):** Anchors the pumpable region to the **first $p$ characters**, ensuring that the loop is detected within the bound given by the Pigeonhole Principle.

## 2.3 The Adversary Game (How to Apply the Lemma)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU's Favourite Question Format:** "Prove that the language $L$ is not regular." The expected answer follows a 4-step recipe:

| Step | Role | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Assume Regular** | Hypothesis | Suppose $L$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, it has a pumping length $p$. |
| **2. Player Picks $s$** | You (the prover) | Choose a string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ that *you suspect* cannot be pumped. |
| **3. Adversary Picks $xyz$** | Opponent | The opponent (fictitious) chooses any valid decomposition respecting conditions 2 & 3. |
| **4. You Find a Pumping $i$** | You | Find some $i \geq 0$ such that $xy^{i}z \notin L$, contradicting the lemma. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Pumping length of language $L$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| $s$ | Chosen long string in $L$ | $\vert s \vert \geq p$ |
| $xyz$ | Decomposition of $s$ | $s = xyz$ |
| $\vert y \vert$ | Length of pumpable middle | $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ |
| $\vert xy \vert$ | Length of prefix containing $y$ | $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ |
| $xy^{i}z$ | Pumped string (must remain in $L$) | $\forall i \geq 0$ |
| $\lnot \text{regular}(L)$ | Conclusion when pumping fails | Counter-example decomposition exists |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pumping Lemma is not merely a theoretical artefact. Its underlying principle (state-space exhaustion via the Pigeonhole Principle) appears in:

- **Compiler Design:** Detecting whether lexical token patterns can be implemented with finite automata (vs. requiring pushdown automata).
- **Network Protocol Verification:** Establishing that a protocol's state space is bounded.
- **Model Checking:** Proving certain software/hardware properties are undecidable or non-regular.
- **Pattern Matching Engines:** Used implicitly when regex engines (DFA-based) fail on certain inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> The Pumping Lemma is the standard tool in KTU examinations whenever the question asks you to *prove non-regularity*. Alternatives (MyhillŌĆōNerode Theorem, Closure Properties) are Module 2 extensions and may also be tested.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of the Pumping Lemma (For Regular Languages)

**Statement:** If $L$ is regular, then $L$ satisfies the Pumping Lemma.

**Proof:**

Let $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be a DFA that recognizes $L$, with $\vert Q \vert = n$ states.

- **Choose $p = n$ (the pumping length).**

- Let $s = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_m \in L$ with $m \geq p = n$.

- Trace the path of $M$ on input $s$. Since $M$ starts at $q_0$ and processes $m \geq n+1$ symbols, the sequence of states visited has at least $n+1$ states:

$$
q_0, q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_m
$$

- By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, since there are only $n$ distinct states in $Q$, at least two states in the above sequence must be identical. Let $q_i = q_j$ for some $0 \leq i < j \leq n$.

- Decompose $s$ as:

$$
x = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_i, \quad y = s_{i+1} s_{i+2} \cdots s_j, \quad z = s_{j+1} s_{j+2} \cdots s_m
$$

- **Verifying the three conditions:**

  - **Condition 2:** $\vert y \vert = j - i \geq 1$ because $i < j$. Ō£ö
  - **Condition 3:** $\vert xy \vert = j \leq n = p$ because $j \leq n$. Ō£ö
  - **Condition 1:** For any $i \geq 0$, the string $xy^{i}z$ is accepted by $M$ because looping around the cycle $q_i \to q_j$ any number of times still ends at the same state $q_j$, from which the suffix $z$ leads to an accepting state. Ō£ö

Hence the lemma is proved. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$

**Goal:** Prove $L$ is not regular.

**Step 1: Assume regular.** Then there exists a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2: Choose $s$.** Let $s = a^{p} b^{p}$. Note $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$, and $s \in L$. Ō£ö

**Step 3: Adversary picks $xyz$.** By the lemma, $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, the substring $y$ must consist entirely of $a$'s (because the first $p$ characters of $s$ are all $a$'s). So $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4: Pick $i = 2$.** Then:

$$
xy^{2}z = a^{p} a^{k} b^{p} = a^{p+k} b^{p}
$$

Since $k \geq 1$, we have $p + k \neq p$. Therefore $xy^{2}z \notin L$ because the number of $a$'s no longer equals the number of $b$'s.

**Conclusion:** The pumped string is not in $L$, contradicting the Pumping Lemma. Hence $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ (Even-Length Palindromes of Doubled Form)

**Goal:** Prove $L$ is not regular.

**Step 1:** Assume $L$ is regular with pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose $s = a^{p} b \, a^{p} b$. Then $\vert s \vert = 2p+2 \geq p$ and $s \in L$ (here $w = a^{p}b$).

**Step 3:** Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, the substring $y$ lies entirely within the first block of $a$'s. So $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Choose $i = 2$. Then:

$$
xy^{2}z = a^{p+k} b \, a^{p} b
$$
