This string has the form $a^{p+k} b a^{p} b$, which is **not** of the form $ww$ for any $w$ (the first half ends in $b$ but the second half ends in $b$ only at the very end; symmetry is broken).

**Conclusion:** $xy^{2}z \notin L$, contradiction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$

**Goal:** Prove $L$ is not regular.

**Step 1:** Assume regular with pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose $s = a^{p} b^{p} c^{p}$. Then $s \in L$ and $\vert s \vert = 3p \geq p$. Ō£ö

**Step 3:** Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, both $x$ and $y$ lie in the prefix $a^{p}$, so $y = a^{k}$ with $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Choose $i = 2$:

$$
xy^{2}z = a^{p+k} b^{p} c^{p}
$$

For $s'$ to be in $L$, we need $p+k = p = p$, which is impossible since $k \geq 1$.

**Conclusion:** $s' \notin L$, contradiction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Summary Table of Proof Strategies

| Language | Chosen String $s$ | Where does $y$ lie? | Pumping $i$ | Resulting Pumping Failure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a^{n}b^{n}$ | $a^{p}b^{p}$ | In $a$-block | 2 | Unequal counts of $a$ and $b$ |
| $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$ | $a^{p}b^{p}c^{p}$ | In $a$-block | 2 | First count exceeds the rest |
| $ww$ | $a^{p}ba^{p}b$ | In first $a$-block | 2 | Loses palindrome structure |
| $(01)^{*}$ | N/A ŌĆö already regular | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | Pumping always works |
| Equal $0$'s and $1$'s | $0^{p}1^{p}$ | In $0$-block | 2 | Counts unequal |
| Primes $1^{p}$ where $p$ is prime | $1^{q}$ for $q$ prime, $q \geq p$ | Anywhere | $i$ = makes composite | $1^{qi}$ has length $qi$, not prime for some $i$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Diagram: Pumping Decomposition

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the structure of a string $s$ and its decomposition $xyz$, along with the pumped variants.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["String s"] --> B["Prefix x"]
    A --> C["Middle y (loop region)"]
    A --> D["Suffix z"]
    C --> E["Pumped y^0 (removed)"]
    C --> F["Pumped y^1 (original)"]
    C --> G["Pumped y^2 (doubled)"]
    C --> H["Pumped y^k (k times)"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pumping Lemma Adversary Game

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHASE1["Phase 1: Assumption"]
        A1["Assume L is regular"] --> A2["Pumping length p exists"]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2["Phase 2: Choice of String"]
        B1["Prover picks s in L"] --> B2["Ensure length >= p"]
        B2 --> B3["Design s to be 'fragile'"]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3["Phase 3: Decomposition"]
        C1["Adversary picks x, y, z"] --> C2["Subject to |xy| <= p and |y| >= 1"]
    end
    subgraph PHASE4["Phase 4: Contradiction"]
        D1["Prover selects i"] --> D2["Show xy^i z not in L"]
        D2 --> D3["Contradiction reached"]
        D3 --> D4["L is NOT regular"]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
    PHASE3 --> PHASE4
```

## 4.3 State-Cycle Diagram: Why Pumping Works in DFAs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q0["q0 (start)"] --> q1["q1"]
    q1 --> q2["q2"]
    q2 --> q3["q3"]
    q3 --> q2
    q2 --> q4["q4"]
    q4 --> qf["qf (accept)"]
    subgraph LOOP["Loop region forms y"]
        q2
        q3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle between $q_2$ and $q_3$ represents the substring $y$. Pumping $y$ corresponds to traversing this cycle $i$ times. The path still ends at $q_f$, an accept state, regardless of $i$.

## 4.4 Visual Aid: Pumping Operation on String $a^n b^n$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG["Original: a^p b^p"]
        O1["aaaa...a"] --> O2["bbbb...b"]
    end
    subgraph PUMP0["i=0: a^(p-k) b^p"]
        P01["aaa...a"] --> P02["bbbb...b"]
    end
    subgraph PUMP1["i=1: a^p b^p (original)"]
        P11["aaaa...a"] --> P12["bbbb...b"]
    end
    subgraph PUMP2["i=2: a^(p+k) b^p"]
        P21["aaaaa...a"] --> P22["bbbb...b"]
    end
    ORIG --> PUMP0
    ORIG --> PUMP1
    ORIG --> PUMP2
```

> [!WARNING]
> Notice that in PUMP0 and PUMP2, the counts of $a$'s and $b$'s are unequal. Hence neither pumped string is in $L$, which contradicts the lemma and forces $L$ to be non-regular.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State the Pumping Lemma for regular languages. List its three conditions clearly.**

**Model Answer:**

The Pumping Lemma states that if $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (pumping length) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be written as $s = xyz$ where:

1. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for every $i \geq 0$,
2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and
3. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for conditions 1 & 2, 1 for condition 3 and explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**"Pumping Lemma is a necessary but not sufficient condition for regularity." Justify the statement.**

**Model Answer:**

- A language satisfying the Pumping Lemma **may still be non-regular** (sufficient condition fails). For example, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$ is non-regular, but the Pumping Lemma in its contrapositive form is used to *prove* non-regularity.
- However, if a language **fails** the Pumping Lemma, then it is *definitely* not regular. Hence it is a **necessary condition**.

*(3 marks: 1 for stating necessity, 1 for stating insufficiency, 1 for example or justification.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 | Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Prove that the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **not regular** using the Pumping Lemma. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable example, explain why the Pumping Lemma cannot be used to prove a language is regular. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Assume** $L$ is regular. By the Pumping Lemma, $L$ has a pumping length $p$.
2. **Choose** $s = a^{p}b^{p} \in L$ with $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$.
3. The adversary decomposes $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. Since the first $p$ symbols are all $a$'s, $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.
4. **Pump** with $i = 2$:

$$
xy^{2}z = a^{p}b^{p} \cdot a^{k} \cdot \text{(suffix)} = a^{p+k}b^{p}
$$

Wait ŌĆö more carefully: $x = a^{p-k}$, $y = a^{k}$, $z = b^{p}$. So:

$$
xy^{2}z = a^{p-k} \cdot a^{k} \cdot a^{k} \cdot b^{p} = a^{p+k}b^{p}
$$

5. Since $k \geq 1$, the number of $a$'s exceeds the number of $b$'s, so $xy^{2}z \notin L$.
6. **Contradiction.** Hence $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The Pumping Lemma provides only a **necessary condition** for regularity. Consider:

- $L_1 = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is non-regular, but $L_2 = \{a^{n}b^{m} \mid n,m \geq 1\}$ is regular and trivially satisfies pumping.
- Conversely, there exist non-regular languages that *can* be pumped. For instance, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} : n \geq 0\} \cup \{ab\}^*$ ŌĆö although non-regular in part, small strings can be pumped without violation.
- The Pumping Lemma only gives a **necessary** condition, not sufficient. Hence it cannot be used to *prove* regularity. To prove regularity, one must construct a DFA, NFA, or regular expression.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> 1. Do **NOT** choose $s$ with $\vert s \vert < p$ ŌĆö the lemma does not apply.
> 2. Do **NOT** claim $y = b^{k}$ without justification. Always state explicitly that $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ forces $y$ into the $a$-block.
> 3. Always mention the **quantifier order** ($\forall p, \exists s, \forall x,y,z, \exists i$) in your opening lines. Board examiners reward this for 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

**(a)** State and prove the Pumping Lemma for regular languages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Pumping Lemma, show that $L = \{0^{n}1^{n}0^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is not regular. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete proof.)*

**Statement:** If $L$ is regular, then there exists $p \geq 1$ such that for all $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$, we can write $s = xyz$ with:

1. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$,
2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and
3. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$.

**Proof:** Let $M$ be a DFA with $n$ states recognizing $L$. Take $p = n$. Any $s = s_1 \cdots s_m \in L$ with $m \geq n$ yields a state trace of length $m+1 \geq n+1$, which by the Pigeonhole Principle has a repeated state. The decomposition $xyz$ corresponding to the cycle satisfies all three conditions, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Assume** $L$ is regular. Let $p$ be its pumping length.
2. **Choose** $s = 0^{p}1^{p}0^{p} \in L$ with $\vert s \vert = 3p \geq p$. Ō£ö
3. By the Pumping Lemma, $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$. Since the first $p$ characters of $s$ are all $0$'s, $y = 0^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.
4. **Pump** with $i = 2$:

$$
xy^{2}z = 0^{p-k} \cdot 0^{k} \cdot 0^{k} \cdot 1^{p}0^{p} = 0^{p+k}1^{p}0^{p}
$$

5. For $xy^{2}z \in L$, we would need $p+k = p = p$, which contradicts $k \geq 1$.
6. **Contradiction.** Hence $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. Forgetting to state the **assumption that $L$ is regular** at the start ŌĆö lose 1 mark.
> 2. Choosing a string $s$ that is **too short** or that does not belong to $L$ ŌĆö lose 2 marks.
> 3. Failing to **explicitly justify** why $y$ must lie in the first block of $0$'s ŌĆö lose 1 mark.
> 4. Not specifying the value of $i$ used for pumping ŌĆö lose 1 mark.
> 5. Forgetting to state the **conclusion** ("Hence $L$ is not regular") ŌĆö lose 0.5 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ŌśÉ The Pumping Lemma is a **necessary** condition (not sufficient) for regularity.
- ŌśÉ It is used to prove **non-regularity** via *proof by contradiction*.
- ŌśÉ The pumping length $p$ depends on the language; you do not pick it ŌĆö the adversary does.
- ŌśÉ The three conditions are: (1) $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$, (2) $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, (3) $\vert xy \vert \leq p$.
- ŌśÉ Quantifier order: $\forall L \text{ regular} \Rightarrow \exists p, \forall s, \exists x,y,z, \forall i$.
- ŌśÉ Always choose a string of the form $a^{p}b^{p}$, $a^{p}b^{p}c^{p}$, or similar **symmetric** structures.
- ŌśÉ The substring $y$ is forced into a specific region by the constraint $\vert xy \vert \leq p$.
- ŌśÉ Common languages proved non-regular: $\{a^{n}b^{n}\}$, $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$, $\{ww\}$, $\{0^{n}1^{n}0^{n}\}$, $\{w \mid \#_{a}(w) = \#_{b}(w)\}$.
- ŌśÉ Regular languages that satisfy the lemma: $a^{*}$, $(ab)^{*}$, $\Sigma^{*}$, all finite languages.
- ŌśÉ The Pumping Lemma *cannot* prove regularity ŌĆö use DFA/NFA/Regex for that.
- ŌśÉ Alternative methods: **MyhillŌĆōNerode Theorem** and **Closure Properties** (covered in Module 2 next).
- ŌśÉ Be precise with notation: $\vert s \vert$ for length, $\#$ for count, $\in$ for membership, $\notin$ for non-membership.
- ŌśÉ In a 7-mark proof, allocate: 1 mark (assumption) + 1 mark (choice of $s$) + 2 marks (decomposition analysis) + 2 marks (pumping and contradiction) + 1 mark (conclusion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context-Free Language (CFL) and Context-Free Grammar (CFG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Context-Free Language (CFL) and Context-Free Grammar (CFG) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Context-Free Grammar (CFG) A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical tool used to describe the syntax of programming languages, natural languages, and structured data. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a CFG is formally defined as a **4-tuple**: $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ Where each component has a strict mathematical meaning: | Symbol | Component | Meaning | |--------|-----------|---------| | $V$ | Set of Variables (Non-Terminals) | Uppercase letters representing syntactic categories (e.g., $S, A, B$) | | $T$ | Set of Terminals | Lowercase letters/symbols forming the actual strings of the language | | $P$ | Set of Production Rules | Finite set of rewrite rules of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | | $S$ | Start Symbol | A distinguished variable in $V$ from which derivation begins | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Context-Free Property**: In every production $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, the Left-Hand Side (LHS) **must be a single non-terminal** ($\alpha \in V$). The rule can be applied regardless of the surrounding "context" ŌĆö hence the name **Context-Free**. ### 1.2 Context-Free Language (CFL) A **Context-Free Language (CFL)** is any language that can be generated by some Context-Free Grammar. If $G$ is a CFG, then the language generated by $G$ is denoted $L(G)$ and consists of all strings of terminals derivable from the start symbol $S$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition Highlight**: A language $L$ is a CFL if there exists a CFG $G$ such that $L = L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Recipe Grammar" ­¤Ź░ Imagine teaching a friend to bake a cake using only **context-free instructions**: - A **Cake** is made of Layers and Frosting. - A **Layer** is either Vanilla or Chocolate. - **Frosting** is Sugar-cream. No matter where the word "Layer" appears ŌĆö beginning, middle, or end of the recipe ŌĆö the rule "Layer ŌåÆ Vanilla" can ALWAYS be applied. The rule doesn't depend on what comes before or after. **This is "Context-Freedom."** Similarly: - The non-terminal **``** can be replaced by ` + ` regardless of where it appears in the input. - A `` in English grammar can be rewritten as ` ` without caring about surrounding paragraphs. This contrasts with **Context-Sensitive Grammars**, where rules like "$aB \rightarrow aC$" only apply when the right context exists. ### 1.4 Example CFG ŌĆö The Language $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ A classic KTU-favorite CFG generating the balanced language: $$G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$ with productions: - $S \rightarrow aSb$ - $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ Here: $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$, $S$ is the start symbol, and $L(G) = \{\varepsilon, ab, aabb, aaabbb, \ldots\}$. > [!TIP] > **Quick Exam Tip**: Always identify the **4 components** explicitly when a question asks "Define a CFG for the language $L$." Examiners award marks for stating $V$, $T$, $P$, $S$ separately. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Production Rule A production rule has the form: $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad \text{where } \alpha \in V \text{ and } \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$$ - **LHS ($\alpha$)**: ALWAYS exactly one non-terminal symbol. This is what makes the grammar *context-free*. - **RHS ($\beta$)**: A string of terminals, non-terminals, or the empty string $\varepsilon$. ### 2.2 Types of Productions (Classification for KTU Board Exams) | Production Type | Form | Significance | |-----------------|------|--------------| | **Epsilon Production** | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Generates empty string | | **Unit Production** | $A \rightarrow B$ | Where $A, B \in V$ (single non-terminal to non-terminal) | | **Mixed Production** | $A \rightarrow xBy$ | Standard recursive production | | **Terminal-Mixed** | $A \rightarrow aBb$ | Combines terminals & non-terminals | ### 2.3 Derivation ŌĆö The "Heart" of CFL Generation **Derivation** is the stepwise process of applying production rules starting from $S$ to obtain a string of terminals. | Symbol | Meaning | Read As | |--------|---------|---------| | $\Rightarrow$ | Single-step derivation | "yields in one step" | | $\Rightarrow^*$ | Zero or more steps | "derives" (reflexive transitive closure) | | $\Rightarrow^+_{lm}$ | Leftmost derivation | "derives via leftmost replacement" | | $\Rightarrow^+_{rm}$ | Rightmost derivation | "derives via rightmost replacement" | ### 2.4 Leftmost vs. Rightmost Derivation - **Leftmost Derivation (LMD)**: At each step, the **leftmost non-terminal** is replaced first. - **Rightmost Derivation (RMD)**: At each step, the **rightmost non-terminal** is replaced first. - A string $w \in L(G)$ is an **ambiguous string** if it has **more than one LMD** OR **more than one parse tree**. - A **grammar is ambiguous** if it generates at least one ambiguous string. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact**: For every parse tree, there is EXACTLY **one LMD** and EXACTLY **one RMD**. So multiple parse trees $\Leftrightarrow$ multiple LMDs $\Leftrightarrow$ ambiguity. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Notation | Boundary Condition | |---------|---------------------|---------------------| | CFG Definition | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V \cap T = \emptyset$, $S \in V$ | | Language Generated | $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ | $w$ must contain only terminals | | Derivation Step | $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | If $A \rightarrow \gamma \in P$ | | LMD | Replace **leftmost** variable first | Used for predictive parsing | | RMD | Replace **rightmost** variable first | Used for bottom-up parsing | | Chomsky Normal Form | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | No $\varepsilon$-productions except $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | Pumping Lemma (CFL) | $w = uvxyz$ with $\vert uvxy \vert \leq p$, $\vert vy \vert \geq 1$ | $uv^ixy^iz \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of CFG/CFL | |-------|------------------------| | **Compiler Design** | Parsing expressions, type-checking, syntax analysis (YACC, ANTLR use CFGs) | | **Programming Languages** | Defining grammar of C, Java, Python (BNF notation is essentially CFG) | | **XML/HTML Parsing** | Validating document structure (balanced tags form a CFL) | | **Natural Language Processing** | Syntax trees, sentence structure analysis | | **Bioinformatics** | RNA secondary structure prediction (balanced base pairs) | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Generating $a^2b^2$ Using $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ **Grammar**: $G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, \{S \rightarrow aSb, S \rightarrow \varepsilon\}, S)$ **Target String**: $w = aabb$ **Leftmost Derivation (LMD)** ŌĆö always replace the **leftmost** non-terminal: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aSb && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ ŌĆö only choice)} \\ &\Rightarrow a(aSb)b && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to inner } S) \\ &\Rightarrow aa(Sb)b && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow aa(\varepsilon)b && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow aa\varepsilon b && \text{(Concatenate)} \\ &\Rightarrow aab b && \text{(Simplify } \varepsilon \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow aabb && \text{Final string achieved} \end{aligned} $$ > **Read as**: $S \Rightarrow^*_{lm} aabb$. The grammar is unambiguous here since only one LMD exists. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Arithmetic Expression Grammar (Classic Ambiguity) **Grammar**: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid id$$ **Target String**: $w = id + id * id$ **Derivation A (LMD Path 1)** ŌĆö gives $+$ lower precedence than $*$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\Rightarrow E + E && \text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E+E \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + E && \text{(Replace leftmost } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + E * E && \text{(Replace middle } E \rightarrow E*E \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + id * E && \text{(Leftmost } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + id * id && \text{(Rightmost } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation B (LMD Path 2)** ŌĆö gives $*$ lower precedence than $+$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\Rightarrow E * E && \text{(Apply } E \rightarrow E*E \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow E + E * E && \text{(Replace leftmost } E \rightarrow E+E \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + E * E && \text{(Replace leftmost } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + id * E && \text{(Replace middle } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow id + id * id && \text{(Replace rightmost } E \rightarrow id \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Two distinct LMDs exist for the SAME string** $id + id * id$. Therefore, this grammar is **AMBIGUOUS**. In KTU board exams, this is the standard counterexample used to demonstrate ambiguity. --- ### 3.3 Parse Tree Construction for $id + id * id$ (Path 1) **Tree representation** (root $E$ at top, leaves at bottom): $$ \begin{array}{c} \text{Step 1: Root} \rightarrow E \\ \text{Step 2: } E \rightarrow E + E \text{ (split into 3 children)} \\ \text{Step 3: Left } E \rightarrow id \text{ (leaf)} \\ \text{Step 4: Right } E \rightarrow E * E \text{ (split into 3)} \\ \text{Step 5: Both inner } E \rightarrow id \text{ (leaves)} \end{array} $$ The leaves read left-to-right yield: $id + id * id$ Ō£ō This tree gives $*$ **higher precedence** (computed first). A second parse tree (Path 2) would give $+$ higher precedence, confirming ambiguity. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Derivation Types | Criterion | Leftmost Derivation | Rightmost Derivation | |-----------|---------------------|----------------------| | Replacement Order | Leftmost non-terminal first | Rightmost non-terminal first | | Parsing Strategy | **Top-down parsing** (recursive descent, LL) | **Bottom-up parsing** (LR, LALR) | | Used By | Predictive parsers, ANTLR | YACC, Bison | | Uniqueness Test | Multiple LMDs ŌåÆ Ambiguous grammar | Multiple RMDs ŌåÆ Ambiguous grammar | | Equivalence | One parse tree Ōåö One LMD Ōåö One RMD | Universally true | --- ### 3.5 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Removing Useless Symbols **Original Grammar**: $$S \rightarrow aA \mid BB, \quad A \rightarrow a, \quad B \rightarrow aA, \quad C \rightarrow cC$$ **Step 1**: Find **generating** symbols (can derive a terminal string): - $A \rightarrow a$ Ō£ō (generates terminal) - $B \rightarrow aA \rightarrow aa$ Ō£ō - $S \rightarrow aA \rightarrow aa$ Ō£ō - $C \rightarrow cC$ Ō£Ś (only generates non-terminals ŌĆö never terminates) **Step 2**: Find **reachable** symbols (from $S$): - $S$ reaches $A$ (via $S \rightarrow aA$) and $B$ (via $S \rightarrow BB$) - $C$ is NOT reachable from $S$ Ō£Ś **Result**: Remove $C$. Clean grammar becomes: $$S \rightarrow aA \mid BB, \quad A \rightarrow a, \quad B \rightarrow aA$$ --- ### 3.6 Conversion to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) **CNF Rules**: 1. Every production is either $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. 2. No $\varepsilon$-productions (except $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if $\varepsilon \in L$). 3. No unit productions $A \rightarrow B$. 4. No terminals mixed with non-terminals on RHS (length > 1). **Example Conversion**: Convert $S \rightarrow aAb \mid \varepsilon$, $A \rightarrow aA \mid a$ to CNF. **Step 1**: Introduce new non-terminals for terminals in mixed positions: - $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$, where $C_a \rightarrow a$, $C_b \rightarrow b$ - $A \rightarrow C_a A \mid a$ **Step 2**: Break RHS with more than 2 non-terminals ŌĆö already satisfied. **Final CNF**: $$S \rightarrow C_a A C_b, \quad C_a \rightarrow a, \quad C_b \rightarrow b, \quad A \rightarrow C_a A \mid a$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Strategy**: When asked to convert to CNF, follow the **4-step algorithm** ŌĆö (1) eliminate $\varepsilon$, (2) eliminate unit, (3) eliminate useless, (4) normalize length to Ōēż 2. Marks are awarded stepwise. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö CFG Derivation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Start Symbol S]) --> step1{Apply Production} step1 -->|"S ŌåÆ aSb"| step2[S becomes aSb] step1 -->|"S ŌåÆ ╬Ą"| step3[S becomes empty] step2 --> step1 step3 --> acceptNode([Accept: All terminals generated]) style startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style step1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style acceptNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Parse Tree for `id + id * id` ```mermaid graph TD rootNode[Root: E] --> leftChild[Left E] rootNode --> plusNode[Terminal: +] rootNode --> rightChild[Right E] leftChild --> idNode1[Leaf: id] rightChild --> mulLeft[Inner E] rightChild --> starNode[Terminal: *] rightChild --> mulRight[Inner E] mulLeft --> idNode2[Leaf: id] mulRight --> idNode3[Leaf: id] style rootNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style idNode1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style idNode2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style idNode3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö CFL Hierarchy & Relationships ```mermaid graph TD regLang[Regular Languages] --> cflLang[Context-Free Languages] cflLang --> cslLang[Context-Sensitive Languages] cslLang --> recursiveLang[Recursive Languages] cflLang --> pdaModel[Accepted by Pushdown Automata] regLang --> dfaModel[Accepted by DFA/NFA] style regLang fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style cflLang fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style pdaModel fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö CFG Simplification Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR inputG[Original CFG] --> stepA[Step 1: Remove ╬Ą-productions] stepA --> stepB[Step 2: Remove unit productions] stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Remove useless symbols] stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Convert to CNF] stepD --> finalG[Simplified CFG in CNF] style inputG fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style finalG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style stepA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style stepB fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style stepC fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style stepD fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Ambiguity Resolution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startG[Given Grammar G] --> checkQ{Does any string
have 2 parse trees?} checkQ -->|"No"| unambigG[Unambiguous Grammar] checkQ -->|"Yes"| ambigG[Ambiguous Grammar] ambigG --> fixM[Apply Disambiguation Rules] fixM --> newG[Equivalent Unambiguous Grammar] fixM --> rewriteG[Rewrite using precedence levels] style ambigG fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style unambigG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style newG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### ­¤ōÜ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define a Context-Free Grammar. What is a Context-Free Language? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where: - $V$ = finite set of variables (non-terminals) - $T$ = finite set of terminals, with $V \cap T = \emptyset$ - $P$ = finite set of production rules of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$ - $S \in V$ = start symbol A **Context-Free Language (CFL)** is a language that can be generated by some CFG. Formally, if $G$ is a CFG, then $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ is a CFL. Examples: $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, set of palindromes. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` What is the difference between Leftmost Derivation and Rightmost Derivation? Why is this distinction important? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Leftmost Derivation (LMD) | Rightmost Derivation (RMD) | |--------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | Rule Applied | Leftmost non-terminal first | Rightmost non-terminal first | | Used In | Top-down parsers (LL) | Bottom-up parsers (LR) | **Importance**: Both must be unique for an unambiguous grammar. If a string has more than one LMD (or RMD), the grammar is **ambiguous**. This distinction determines parser implementation choice in compiler design. --- ### ­¤ōÜ PART B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Internal Choice Rule**: Answer ANY ONE from Q(A) and Q(B). Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Construct a CFG for the language $L = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ and show the derivation of the string $aabbbb$. **(b)** Explain the concept of ambiguity in CFGs with a suitable example. Show two distinct parse trees for the string $id + id + id$ using the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid id$. --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(a) (7 marks)** **CFG Construction:** Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where: - $V = \{S\}$ - $T = \{a, b\}$ - $P$: $S \rightarrow aaSbb \mid aabb$ **Logic**: Each iteration adds one 'a' and two 'b's. Base case is $aabb$ (when $n = 1$). **Derivation of $aabbbb$ (n = 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aaSbb && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aaSbb \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow aa(aabb)bb && \text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aabb \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaaabbbb && \text{(Concatenate, $n=2$)} \\ &\Rightarrow aabbbb && \text{Final string (note: } aabbbb \text{ has 2 a's and 4 b's)} \end{aligned} $$ Wait ŌĆö checking: $aabbbb$ has **2 a's** and **4 b's** = $a^2 b^4$ Ō£ō (which is $a^n b^{2n}$ with $n = 2$). $$ \boxed{S \Rightarrow^* aabbbb \in L(G)} $$ **Verification**: $L(G) = \{aabb, aabbbb, aaabbbbbb, \ldots\} = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ Ō£ō --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(b) (7 marks)** **Definition**: A CFG $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two or more LMDs). **Grammar**: $E \rightarrow E + E \mid id$ **String**: $w = id + id + id$ **Parse Tree 1** (Left-associative: $(id + id) + id$): ``` E /|\ E + E /|\ | E + E id | | id id ``` **Parse Tree 2** (Right-associative: $id + (id + id)$): ``` E /|\ id + E /|\ E + E | | id id ``` Since **two distinct parse trees** exist for the same string $id + id + id$, the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid id$ is **AMBIGUOUS**. **Resolution**: Use precedence ŌĆö rewrite as: $$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T, \quad T \rightarrow id$$ This new grammar is unambiguous. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define CFG and derive the language generated by the grammar $S \rightarrow aS \mid bS \mid \varepsilon$. Identify the language class. **(b)** State and explain the Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages. Prove that $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **NOT** a Context-Free Language. --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(a) (7 marks)** **Definition**: A CFG is $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where the LHS of every production is a single non-terminal. **Given Grammar**: - $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$, $P = \{S \rightarrow aS, S \rightarrow bS, S \rightarrow \varepsilon\}$, $S$ = start symbol **Language Generated**: Any string generated will be a sequence of $a$'s and $b$'s followed by the empty string. Since $\varepsilon$ acts as a base, the grammar generates: $$L(G) = \{a, b\}^*$$ That is, **all strings over the alphabet $\{a, b\}$**, including $\varepsilon$. **Verification by Induction**: - **Base case**: $S \Rightarrow \varepsilon$ Ō£ō - **Inductive step**: If $S \Rightarrow^* w$, then $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aw$ and $S \Rightarrow bS \Rightarrow bw$ **Language Class**: $L(G) = \{a, b\}^*$ is actually a **regular language** (every regular language is also a CFL, since CFGs ŌŖā Regular Grammars). --- ### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(b) (7 marks)** **Pumping Lemma for CFL**: For any CFL $L$, there exists a constant $p$ (pumping length) such that every string $w \in L$ with $|w| \geq p$ can be decomposed as: $$w = uvxyz$$ satisfying three conditions: 1. $|vxy| \leq p$ 2. $|vy| \geq 1$ 3. $uv^ixy^iz \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ **Proof that $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT a CFL** (by contradiction): **Assume** $L$ is a CFL. Then the pumping lemma applies. Let $p$ be the pumping length. **Step 1**: Choose $w = a^p b^p c^p \in L$ with $|w| = 3p \geq p$. **Step 2**: Decompose $w = uvxyz$ with $|vxy| \leq p$ and $|vy| \geq 1$. Since $|vxy| \leq p$, the substring $vxy$ can span **at most two of the three symbol groups** (since each group has exactly $p$ symbols). This means $vxy$ cannot contain all three symbols $a$, $b$, $c$. **Case 1**: $vxy$ contains only $a$'s and $b$'s (no $c$). Pump down with $i = 0$: $w' = uxyz = a^{p-|v|_a} b^{p-|v|_b} c^p$ (with at least one $a$ or $b$ removed). Now $w'$ has unequal counts ŌĆö either fewer $a$'s than $c$'s, or fewer $b$'s than $c$'s. So $w' \notin L$. **Case 2**: $vxy$ contains only $b$'s and $c$'s (no $a$). By symmetric argument, pumping yields $w' \notin L$. **Case 3**: $vxy$ contains only $a$'s (or only $b$'s or only $c$'s). Pumping removes some but not others, breaking the equality. **Conclusion**: In all cases, we find $i$ such that $uv^ixy^iz \notin L$, contradicting the pumping lemma. Therefore, **$L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT a Context-Free Language**. ŌłÄ --- ### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (KTU Board Examiners Report These Yearly):** > > 1. **Forgetting to state the 4 components** $(V, T, P, S)$ when defining a CFG ŌĆö costs 1 full mark. > 2. **Confusing LMD with RMD**: Writing "leftmost derivation" when actually replacing the rightmost variable. Read your derivation twice. > 3. **Not specifying both parse trees fully**: For ambiguity, examiners require **complete trees with all nodes labeled**, not just text descriptions. > 4. **Pumping Lemma Proof Errors**: Most students forget the **constraint $|vxy| \leq p$**, which is critical for the bound argument. > 5. **Ambiguity vs. Inherent Ambiguity**: A grammar may be ambiguous but the language may still be unambiguous (inherent ambiguity is a language property, not a grammar property). > 6. **CNF Conversion Steps**: Skipping the intermediate steps costs marks. Show ALL 4 phases of conversion. --- ### ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Ō£ģ A **CFG** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ with LHS restricted to single non-terminals. - Ō£ģ **CFL** = language generated by some CFG. CFLs ŌŖā Regular Languages strictly. - Ō£ģ **LMD** replaces the leftmost non-terminal; **RMD** replaces the rightmost. - Ō£ģ **Ambiguity** = existence of Ōēź 2 parse trees (equivalently Ōēź 2 LMDs) for the same string. - Ō£ģ **CNF** restricts productions to $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ only. - Ō£ģ **Pumping Lemma for CFL** uses string decomposition $w = uvxyz$ with $|vxy| \leq p$, $|vy| \geq 1$. - Ō£ģ The classic example $a^n b^n c^n$ is **NOT** a CFL ŌĆö use it in proofs. - Ō£ģ Standard ambiguous grammar: $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid id$. - Ō£ģ Disambiguation strategy: Introduce precedence levels (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$). - Ō£ģ CFLs are accepted by **Pushdown Automata (PDA)** ŌĆö a stack-augmented finite automaton. - Ō£ģ **Closure properties**: CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal. NOT closed under intersection or complement. - Ō£ģ Remember the **CFL Hierarchy**: Regular ŌŖé DCFL ŌŖé CFL ŌŖé Context-Sensitive ŌŖé Recursive ŌŖé RE. - Ō£ģ For KTU exams, ALWAYS show step-by-step derivations and clearly state which production is applied at each step.

topics.title: Designing CFGs for CFLs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Designing CFGs for CFLs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition: Context-Free Grammar (CFG)

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical model used to generate **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)**. It provides a finite set of recursive replacement rules that define how strings belonging to a language can be constructed.

Formally, a CFG is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

where the four components have the following precise meanings:

| Symbol | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Set of Variables (Non-Terminals) | Placeholder symbols that generate sub-patterns |
| $T$ | Set of Terminals | The actual alphabet (tokens) of the language |
| $P$ | Set of Productions | Rules of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$ |
| $S$ | Start Symbol | The designated non-terminal from $V$ where derivation begins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining property of a CFG is that **the Left-Hand Side (LHS) of every production must consist of exactly ONE non-terminal**, with **NO surrounding context**. This is the literal meaning of "context-free."

## The Derived Language

The **language generated by a grammar G**, denoted $L(G)$, is the set of all strings consisting purely of terminals that can be derived from the start symbol $S$ through a finite sequence of production applications.

$$L(G) = \{ w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w \}$$

The notation $\Rightarrow^*$ means "derives in zero or more steps."

## Conceptual Analogy: The Recipe Book

Imagine you are teaching a **robot chef** how to cook. You cannot give it a single rigid instruction like "make a 3-layer chocolate cake." Instead, you give it a **recipe book**:

1. **Variables (Non-Terminals)** are the *task names* written in caps: `<CAKE>`, `<BATTER>`, `<FROSTING>`. They represent "things-to-be-defined."
2. **Terminals** are the actual *ingredients* and actions: `flour`, `egg`, `mix`, `bake`. These never get replaced.
3. **Productions** are the *recipe steps*: `<CAKE>` ŌåÆ `<BATTER>` `<BAKE>`.
4. **Start Symbol** is the *master task*: `<CAKE>`.

The robot starts with `<CAKE>` and keeps replacing task names with ingredients/actions until no task names remain ŌĆö leaving only a final list of real ingredients. That final list is the **string in the language**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A CFG is like a **recursive recipe book** for strings. To design one, we identify the smallest "building blocks" (base cases) and figure out how smaller pieces combine to form larger pieces (recursive cases).

## Example: Grammar for Arithmetic Expressions

$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid id$$

- $V = \{E\}$
- $T = \{+, *, (, ), id\}$
- $P$ = the four rules above
- $S = E$

The string $id + id * id$ belongs to $L(G)$ because we can derive it step-by-step from $E$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Design Strategies

## General Methodology for Designing a CFG

Designing a CFG for a given language $L$ is fundamentally an act of **recursive pattern recognition**. The following structured approach is endorsed by KTU examiners.

### Step 1: Decompose the Language

Break the language description into a **base case** (smallest valid string) and a **recursive case** (how a valid string can be built from smaller valid strings).

### Step 2: Identify Matching/Counting Constraints

Look for:
- **Equal counts**: $a^n b^n$ ŌåÆ push then pop pattern
- **Palindromic structure**: $w w^R$ ŌåÆ reverse requires recursion
- **Order constraints**: must appear in specific order
- **Nested structures**: balanced parentheses

### Step 3: Map Patterns to Productions

For each recursive pattern, write a production. For each base case, write a terminal production.

### Step 4: Verify

Prove (informally) that:
- Every string generated by the grammar belongs to $L$ (**Soundness**).
- Every string in $L$ can be generated (**Completeness**).

## The Core CFG Design Patterns (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for CFG Design**

| Language Pattern | Production Rules | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ | Match each 'a' with one 'b' symmetrically |
| $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ | Empty base case included |
| $L = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid \varepsilon$ | Generate palindrome by recursive mirroring |
| $L = \{a^n b^m \mid n,m \geq 1\}$ | $S \rightarrow AB; \; A \rightarrow aA \mid a; \; B \rightarrow bB \mid b$ | Independent counting (product structure) |
| $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSBC \mid aBC; \; CB \rightarrow BC; \; bB \rightarrow bb; \; cC \rightarrow cc$ | Uses intermediate variable to enforce ordering |
| $L = \{a^i b^j c^k \mid i+j=k\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSc \mid T; \; T \rightarrow bTc \mid \varepsilon$ | Pair a's and c's, b's and c's independently |
| $L = \{a^m b^n \mid m \leq n\}$ | $S \rightarrow aSb \mid Tb; \; T \rightarrow Tb \mid \varepsilon$ | Number of b's Ōēź number of a's |
| Balanced Parentheses | $S \rightarrow SS \mid (S) \mid ()$ | Concatenation + nesting |

## Derivation Concepts

### Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$)

At each step, replace the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form.

### Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$)

At each step, replace the **rightmost non-terminal**. Rightmost derivations are particularly important because they directly correspond to **bottom-up parsing** strategies used by compilers (LR parsers).

### Parse Tree

A **parse tree** is a graphical representation of a derivation where:
- **Root** = Start symbol $S$
- **Internal nodes** = Non-terminals (variables)
- **Leaves** = Terminals (or $\varepsilon$)
- **Children of a node** = RHS of the production applied

### Ambiguity

A CFG is **ambiguous** if there exists **at least one string** in $L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct parse tree** (equivalently, more than one distinct leftmost derivation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Note:** When asked to "prove the grammar is unambiguous," you must either show uniqueness of parse trees for every string, or provide a justification (e.g., "operator precedence in $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ removes the ambiguity of the original grammar").

## Real-World Engineering Utility

CFG design is the **theoretical bedrock of compiler construction**:

1. **Syntax Analysis (Parsing)**: Compilers for C, Java, Python use CFG-based parsers (LL, LR, LALR) to validate program syntax.
2. **XML/JSON Validation**: Document type definitions (DTDs) and JSON Schema are essentially CFG-based validators.
3. **Natural Language Processing**: Early parsers (Chomsky's work) used CFGs to model English sentence structure.
4. **DNA Sequence Analysis**: Computational biology uses CFGs to model RNA secondary structure folding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Examples

## Example 1: Derivation of $id + id * id$ from the Arithmetic Expression Grammar

Consider $G$: $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid id$.

**Leftmost Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow_{lm} E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow_{lm} E + E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow_{lm} id + E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow_{lm} id + id * E \\
  &\Rightarrow_{lm} id + id * id
\end{aligned}
$$

Each arrow represents **one application** of a production, always replacing the **leftmost non-terminal** first.

## Example 2: Proving $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ Generates Exactly $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

### Soundness ($L(G) \subseteq \{a^n b^n\}$)

By **structural induction on the number of derivation steps**:

**Base case:** $S \Rightarrow ab$. Here $n = 1$, and $a^1 b^1 = ab$. Ō£ō

**Inductive step:** Assume $S \Rightarrow^* a^k b^k$. Apply production $S \rightarrow aSb$:

$$
S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow^* a \cdot a^k b^k \cdot b = a^{k+1} b^{k+1}
$$

By induction, every derived string has the form $a^n b^n$. Ō£ō

### Completeness ($\{a^n b^n\} \subseteq L(G)$)

By **mathematical induction on $n$**:

**Base case ($n=1$):** $ab \in L(G)$ via direct application of $S \rightarrow ab$.

**Inductive step:** Assume $a^k b^k \in L(G)$. We must show $a^{k+1} b^{k+1} \in L(G)$:

$$
S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow^* a \cdot (a^k b^k) \cdot b = a^{k+1} b^{k+1}
$$

By induction, every $a^n b^n$ is in $L(G)$. Ō£ō

## Example 3: Designing CFG for $L = \{a^i b^j c^k \mid i + j = k\}$

**Decomposition:**
- Each 'c' must be "paired" with either an 'a' (from the front) or a 'b' (from the middle).
- First generate matching pairs of $a$'s and $c$'s.
- Then generate matching pairs of $b$'s and $c$'s.

**Production Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSc \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow bTc \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample Derivation for $a^2 b c^3$** (where $i=2, j=1, k=3$ so $i+j=3$):

$$
S \Rightarrow aSc \Rightarrow aaScc \Rightarrow aaTcc \Rightarrow aabTccc \Rightarrow aabccc
$$

**Verification:** All strings in $L(G)$ have the form $a^i b^j c^{i+j}$. The first recursive rule $S \rightarrow aSc$ increments both $i$ and $k$ by 1. The second rule $S \rightarrow T$ freezes $i$, then $T \rightarrow bTc$ increments both $j$ and $k$ by 1. Finally $T \rightarrow \varepsilon$ freezes $j$. The invariant $k = i + j$ is preserved throughout. Ō£ō

## Example 4: Converting Informal Language Description to CFG

**Language:** $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ has equal number of 0's and 1's}\}$.

**Key insight:** Equal counts means we can *cancel* matched pairs. So we can think of strings as built from "01" and "10" pairs arranged in any order.

**Approach 1 (Direct):** The cleanest grammar uses a recursive strategy where a valid string is either:
- the empty string (0 zeros, 0 ones)
- or a valid string with a "01" or "10" pair inserted anywhere

This leads to a non-trivial grammar. A cleaner approach is to introduce a "non-terminal that has one extra 0" and "non-terminal that has one extra 1":

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow 0S1S \mid 1S0S \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation for $0011$:**

$$
S \Rightarrow 0S1S \Rightarrow 00S1S1S \Rightarrow 001S1S \Rightarrow 0011S \Rightarrow 0011
$$

(Each step applies $S \rightarrow 0S1S$ or $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to the leftmost non-terminal, building the matching pairs recursively.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## Diagram 1: Anatomy of a Context-Free Grammar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CFG["CFG = ( V , T , P , S )"]
        direction TB
        A["V : Variables / Non-Terminals"]
        B["T : Terminal Symbols"]
        C["P : Production Rules"]
        D["S : Start Symbol (in V)"]
    end

    subgraph ProdForm["Production Form"]
        direction LR
        E["LHS: Single Non-Terminal A"]
        F["Arrow"]
        G["RHS: String in (V union T)*"]
        E --> F --> G
    end

    subgraph GenProcess["Generation Process"]
        direction TB
        H["S"] -->|"Apply P1"| I["String with V and T"]
        I -->|"Apply P2"| J["More terminals appear"]
        J -->|"Apply Pn"| K["All terminals : w in L G"]
    end

    A -.example.-> A1["S, A, B"]
    B -.example.-> B1["a, b, 0, 1"]
    C -.example.-> C1["S to aSb, S to ab"]
    D -.example.-> D1["S"]
```

## Diagram 2: Parse Tree for $id + id * id$

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["E"] --> LeftE["E"]
    Root --> MulSym[" * "]
    Root --> RightE["E"]
    LeftE --> LL["E"]
    LeftE --> PlusSym[" + "]
    LeftE --> LR["E"]
    LL --> id1["id"]
    LR --> id2["id"]
    RightE --> id3["id"]
```

**Interpretation:** This tree shows one of the valid parse trees for $id + id * id$. The fact that there exists *another* valid parse tree (with different grouping) demonstrates that this grammar is **ambiguous**.

## Diagram 3: CFG Design Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given Language L"] --> Step1["Decompose L into Base Case + Recursive Case"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Identify structural patterns: counts, nesting, palindrome, order"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Assign Non-Terminals to each sub-pattern"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Write Productions: base cases use terminals, recursive cases use V and T"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Verify Soundness: every derivation stays in L"]
    Step5 --> Step6{"Does the grammar capture ALL of L?"}
    Step6 -->|"No"| Step1
    Step6 -->|"Yes"| Step7["Verify Completeness: every w in L is derivable"]
    Step7 --> Done["Final CFG Ready"]
```

## Diagram 4: The $a^n b^n$ Grammar as a Recursive Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["S"] --> a1["a"]
    S1 --> S2["S"]
    S1 --> b1["b"]
    S2 --> a2["a"]
    S2 --> S3["S"]
    S2 --> b2["b"]
    S3 --> a3["a"]
    S3 --> b3["b"]
    S3 --> ab["ab"]
```

**Interpretation:** This parse tree corresponds to the derivation $S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aaabbb$ for $n=3$. The recursive structure visually mirrors the symmetric pairing of $a$'s and $b$'s.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Context-Free Grammar. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:

- $V$ is a **finite set of variables (non-terminals)**
- $T$ is a **finite set of terminals**, disjoint from $V$
- $P$ is a **finite set of production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$
- $S \in V$ is the **designated start symbol**

The language generated is $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$. The term *context-free* means the LHS of every production is a single non-terminal, with no surrounding context required.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between leftmost and rightmost derivation with an example. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **derivation** is a sequence of applications of production rules starting from $S$.

- **Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$):** At each step, the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is replaced.
- **Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$):** At each step, the **rightmost non-terminal** is replaced.

**Example using** $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ **for string** $aabb$:

**Leftmost:**

$$S \Rightarrow_{lm} aSb \Rightarrow_{lm} aabb$$

**Rightmost:**

$$S \Rightarrow_{rm} aSb \Rightarrow_{rm} aabb$$

Both yield the same string but exercise different replacement orders. Rightmost derivations form the basis of bottom-up (LR) parsing used by compilers like YACC/Bison.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start symbol declaration** ŌĆö Always state which variable is $S$.
> 2. **Omitting the base case** ŌĆö A grammar with only recursive rules (no $\varepsilon$ or terminal-only production) generates the empty language.
> 3. **Mixing terminals and non-terminals carelessly** ŌĆö Remember $V \cap T = \emptyset$.
> 4. **Not justifying with derivation** ŌĆö When asked to "show the grammar generates X," you MUST provide a concrete derivation.
> 5. **Confusing ambiguity with multiple derivations** ŌĆö Ambiguity requires multiple *parse trees*, not just multiple derivation orderings.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Design a CFG for the language $L_1 = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. Verify with derivation for $n=3$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Decomposition:** Each 'a' must be paired with exactly **two** 'b's. So the recursive step produces one 'a' followed by two 'b's around a smaller instance.

**Grammar $G_1$:**

$$S \rightarrow aSbb \mid abb$$

**Verification of components:**
- $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$, $S$ is start symbol.
- Base case: $S \rightarrow abb$ generates $n=1$ string.
- Recursive case: $S \rightarrow aSbb$ adds one 'a' at front and two 'b's at end.

**Derivation for $n = 3$ (i.e., string $aabbbbbb$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSbb \\
  &\Rightarrow a(aSbb)bb \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSbbbb \\
  &\Rightarrow aa(abb)bbbb \\
  &\Rightarrow aaabbbbbb
\end{aligned}
$$

The final string $aaabbbbbb$ has 3 a's and 6 b's. Ō£ō

---

#### (b) Design a CFG that generates all **palindromes** over the alphabet $\{0, 1\}$. Provide a sample derivation for $0110$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Pattern Recognition:** A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. So we can build one by placing matching characters symmetrically around a smaller palindrome.

**Grammar $G_2$:**

$$S \rightarrow 0S0 \mid 1S1 \mid 0 \mid 1 \mid \varepsilon$$

**Verification of components:**
- $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{0, 1\}$
- $\varepsilon$ generates the empty palindrome
- $0 \mid 1$ generate single-character palindromes
- $0S0$ and $1S1$ recursively wrap symmetric pairs

**Derivation for $0110$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow 0S0 \\
  &\Rightarrow 01S10 \\
  &\Rightarrow 0110
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification of completeness:** Every palindrome can be constructed by this method because we can strip the matching outer characters one pair at a time until we reach the empty string or a single character. The recursive rules handle the "one pair stripped" step, and the base productions handle the termination. Ō£ō

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

#### (a) Construct a CFG for $L = \{w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w)\}$, where $n_a(w)$ and $n_b(w)$ denote the number of a's and b's respectively. Show derivation for $aabb$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Key Insight:** Strings with equal numbers of a's and b's can be built by inserting **adjacent pairs** "ab" or "ba" anywhere into a valid string.

**Grammar $G_3$:**

$$S \rightarrow aSbS \mid bSaS \mid \varepsilon$$

**Verification of components:**
- $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$
- $\varepsilon$ base case: zero a's, zero b's Ō£ō
- $S \rightarrow aSbS$: adds one 'a' and one 'b' (net count remains equal) Ō£ō
- $S \rightarrow bSaS$: adds one 'b' and one 'a' (net count remains equal) Ō£ō

**Derivation for $aabb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSbS \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSbS bS \\
  &\Rightarrow aa\varepsilon b b S \\
  &\Rightarrow aabb
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof sketch of correctness:** The recursive rules $aSbS$ and $bSaS$ each add exactly one 'a' and one 'b' to the string in nested fashion. Since the base case is $\varepsilon$ (which trivially has equal counts), by induction every derived string has $n_a = n_b$.

---

#### (b) Design a CFG for $L = \{a^m b^n c^p d^q \mid m + p = n + q\}$. Show a sample derivation for $m=2, n=2, p=1, q=1$ (the string $aabcdd$). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Key Insight:** The constraint $m + p = n + q$ can be rewritten as $m - q = n - p$. We can think of it as: "generate matching pairs that contribute equally to both sides."

**Strategy:** First, pair a's with b's ($a^n b^n$), then independently generate extra b's and c's, then pair c's with d's. However, a cleaner construction uses a "balance" approach:

**Grammar $G_4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSd \mid A \\
A &\rightarrow bAc \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait ŌĆö this generates $a^m b^n c^n d^m$, a special case. For the general $m+p = n+q$, we need additional flexibility.

**Refined Grammar** (using two interleaved counter mechanisms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSd \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow bTc \mid U \\
U &\rightarrow aU \mid bU \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar generates strings where the 'a' count equals the 'd' count at the top level (via $aSd$), and within the middle we can freely distribute b's and c's paired with additional unpaired a's and b's.

**Derivation for $aabcdd$** ($m=2, n=1, p=1, q=1$ ŌåÆ $m+p=3, n+q=2$... incorrect example).

**Corrected sample:** Let us verify for $aabbcd$ ($m=2, n=2, p=1, q=1$ ŌåÆ $m+p=3, n+q=3$ Ō£ō):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSd \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSdd \\
  &\Rightarrow aaTdd \\
  &\Rightarrow aabTcdd \\
  &\Rightarrow aabbUcdd \\
  &\Rightarrow aabbc\varepsilon dd \\
  &\Rightarrow aabbcdd
\end{aligned}
$$

The string $aabbcdd$ has $m=2, n=2, p=1, q=1$ with $m+p = 3 = n+q$. Ō£ō

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CFG Design**

- ŌśÉ **Always specify all four components** $(V, T, P, S)$ of the grammar explicitly.
- ŌśÉ **LHS rule:** Every production must have exactly one non-terminal on the LHS ŌĆö no exceptions.
- ŌśÉ **Base case is mandatory:** Without a terminal-producing production, only infinite derivations or the empty string are possible.
- ŌśÉ **Pattern recognition is the key skill:** Look for *counts*, *symmetry*, *nesting*, and *order constraints*.
- ŌśÉ **The $a^n b^n$ trick:** $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ (or $\mid ab$ for $n \geq 1$) is the prototype for "match one from each side."
- ŌśÉ **For $a^n b^n c^n$:** You need an intermediate non-terminal because CFGs cannot count three equalities natively ŌĆö use $CB \rightarrow BC$ style intermediate rewriting.
- ŌśÉ **Two methods to generate $L$:** *Top-down* (start from $S$, decompose into cases) and *bottom-up* (combine smaller strings). Top-down is standard in textbooks.
- ŌśÉ **Ambiguity check:** If a problem says "is this grammar ambiguous?" ŌĆö try to find **two distinct parse trees** for one string.
- ŌśÉ **Empty string handling:** If $\varepsilon \in L$, you must include a production of the form $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (unless $S$ is the LHS of some derivation leading to terminals only).
- ŌśÉ **Don't confuse CFG with Regular Grammar:** Regular grammars have restricted productions ($A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$); CFGs allow arbitrary strings on the RHS.
- ŌśÉ **Verification step:** Always show at least one derivation to demonstrate that a sample string is generated.
- ŌśÉ **Common KTU pitfall:** Forgetting that $V$ and $T$ are **disjoint sets**; do not use the same symbol for both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Derivation

In the formal language theory of context-free grammars (CFGs), a **derivation** is a finite sequence of rewriting steps that transforms the **start symbol** of a grammar into a string of terminal symbols by repeatedly applying production rules.

Given a context-free grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where:
- $V$ = set of non-terminals (variables)
- $T$ = set of terminals
- $P$ = set of production rules
- $S$ = start symbol

A **derivation** of a string $w \in T^*$ is a sequence:
$$\alpha_0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 \Rightarrow \dots \Rightarrow \alpha_n = w$$
where each $\alpha_i$ is a string over $V \cup T$, $\alpha_0 = S$, and each step $\alpha_i \Rightarrow \alpha_{i+1}$ replaces **one** non-terminal in $\alpha_i$ with the right-hand side of some production in $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sentential Form**: Any string $\alpha_i$ (where $0 \le i \le n$) in the derivation sequence is called a *sentential form*. A *sentence* is a sentential form consisting entirely of terminals.

## 1.2 Leftmost Derivation (LMD) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leftmost Derivation (LMD):** A derivation in which, at every step, the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is the one selected for replacement.

Formally, a derivation $\alpha_0 \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_1 \Rightarrow_{lm} \dots \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_n$ is a leftmost derivation if, at each step $i$, the non-terminal replaced is the **leftmost** non-terminal in $\alpha_i$.

## 1.3 Rightmost Derivation (RMD) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rightmost Derivation (RMD):** A derivation in which, at every step, the **rightmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is the one selected for replacement.

Formally, a derivation $\alpha_0 \Rightarrow_{rm} \alpha_1 \Rightarrow_{rm} \dots \Rightarrow_{rm} \alpha_n$ is a rightmost derivation if, at each step $i$, the non-terminal replaced is the **rightmost** non-terminal in $\alpha_i$.

This is sometimes called a **canonical derivation** in compiler theory.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Cooking a Recipe Step-by-Step**

Imagine you are following a recipe written as a sequence of **abbreviated instructions** (like "MIX flour, eggs, sugar, then BAKE"). Each instruction is a "non-terminal" that needs to be expanded into the actual physical steps (terminals).

- **Leftmost Derivation (LMD)** is like a chef who always fully expands the **first** unfinished instruction before moving to the next. They expand left-to-right.
- **Rightmost Derivation (RMD)** is like a chef who jumps to the **last** unfinished instruction and works backward, expanding right-to-left.

Both end up with the same finished dish (the same terminal string $w$), but the **order of mental steps** is different. The "menu of actions" (parse tree) is the same, but the *trace of decisions* (derivation) differs.

## 1.5 Why This Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic is foundational for understanding:
- **Parse Trees** (Module 2 continuation)
- **Ambiguity in Grammars** (Module 2)
- **Top-Down and Bottom-Up Parsing** (Module 3 onwards)
- **Compiler Construction** (PCCSS401D in KTU 2024 scheme)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Choosing Which Non-Terminal to Expand

The fundamental distinction between LMD and RMD lies in a single rule: **the order in which non-terminals are rewritten**.

### 2.1.1 Algorithm for Leftmost Derivation

1. **Initialize**: Let the current string be $S$ (the start symbol).
2. **Scan** the current string from left to right.
3. **Identify** the **leftmost non-terminal** symbol (call it $A$).
4. **Choose** a production $A \rightarrow \beta$ from the grammar's production set.
5. **Replace** exactly one occurrence of $A$ (the leftmost) with $\beta$.
6. **Repeat** steps 2ŌĆō5 until no non-terminals remain.
7. **Output**: The final string is a sentence derived by LMD.

### 2.1.2 Algorithm for Rightmost Derivation

1. **Initialize**: Let the current string be $S$.
2. **Scan** the current string from right to left.
3. **Identify** the **rightmost non-terminal** symbol (call it $A$).
4. **Choose** a production $A \rightarrow \beta$.
5. **Replace** exactly one occurrence of $A$ (the rightmost) with $\beta$.
6. **Repeat** steps 2ŌĆō5 until no non-terminals remain.
7. **Output**: The final string is a sentence derived by RMD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (Unique Equivalence):** For any context-free grammar $G$ and any string $w \in L(G)$, the number of distinct leftmost derivations of $w$ equals the number of distinct rightmost derivations of $w$ equals the number of distinct parse trees for $w$. This is critical for the *ambiguity* test.

## 2.2 Sentential Forms: Leftmost vs Rightmost

- A **left-sentential form** is a sentential form that can appear in some leftmost derivation.
- A **right-sentential form** is a sentential form that can appear in some rightmost derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> Every left-sentential form is also a sentential form, and every right-sentential form is a sentential form, but the *set* of left-sentential forms and the *set* of right-sentential forms are not necessarily identical for an arbitrary grammar.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Derivation** | $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ if $\exists$ a production $A \rightarrow \gamma$ such that $\alpha = \alpha_1 A \alpha_2$ and $\beta = \alpha_1 \gamma \alpha_2$ | One-step relation |
| **LMD ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$)** | Replace **leftmost** non-terminal at each step | Used by **top-down parsers** (e.g., Recursive Descent, LL(1)) |
| **RMD ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$)** | Replace **rightmost** non-terminal at each step | Used by **bottom-up parsers** (e.g., LR parsers shift-reduce) |
| **$\Rightarrow^*$** | Reflexive-transitive closure of $\Rightarrow$ (zero or more steps) | $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \beta$ means $\beta$ is derivable from $\alpha$ |
| **Language of G** | $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ | Set of all terminal strings |
| **Ambiguity Count** | $\#\text{LMD}(w) = \#\text{RMD}(w) = \#\text{ParseTrees}(w)$ | KTU High-Yield Identity |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Compiler Design**: LMD corresponds to predictive parsing (LL parsers), RMD corresponds to shift-reduce parsing (LR parsers). The reverse of an RMD is precisely the sequence of reductions a bottom-up parser performs.
- **Natural Language Processing**: Derivations model how sentences are generated from grammar rules.
- **XML/JSON Parsers**: Derivation trees (ASTs) are constructed via derivations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Example: Arithmetic Expression Grammar

Consider the classic ambiguous grammar for arithmetic expressions used in KTU textbooks:

$$G: \quad E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$

- $V = \{E\}$
- $T = \{+, *, (, ), \text{id}\}$
- Start symbol: $E$

We will derive the string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ using both LMD and RMD.

## 3.2 Leftmost Derivation of "id + id * id"

**Step 1:** Start with the start symbol.
$$E$$

**Step 2:** Apply $E \rightarrow E + E$ (leftmost $E$ is replaced first; this choice represents "addition being the outermost operation").
$$E \Rightarrow E + E$$

**Step 3:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **leftmost** $E$ (the one before $+$).
$$E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E$$

**Step 4:** Apply $E \rightarrow E * E$ to the **leftmost** $E$ in the remaining non-terminals.
$$\text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E$$

**Step 5:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **leftmost** $E$.
$$\text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E$$

**Step 6:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **leftmost** (and only) $E$ remaining.
$$\text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

### Complete LMD Sequence (concise)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E & \Rightarrow_{lm} E + E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + E * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation**: At each step, we located the leftmost non-terminal and applied a chosen production. Notice that the string is built **left-to-right** in spirit; the leftmost portion is finalized first.

## 3.3 Rightmost Derivation of "id + id * id"

**Step 1:** Start with the start symbol.
$$E$$

**Step 2:** Apply $E \rightarrow E + E$ (we will rewrite the rightmost $E$ later; the outermost structure is decided first).
$$E \Rightarrow E + E$$

**Step 3:** Apply $E \rightarrow E * E$ to the **rightmost** $E$.
$$E + E \Rightarrow E + E * E$$

**Step 4:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **rightmost** $E$.
$$E + E * E \Rightarrow E + E * \text{id}$$

**Step 5:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **rightmost** remaining $E$ (the middle one).
$$E + E * \text{id} \Rightarrow E + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

**Step 6:** Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the **rightmost** remaining $E$.
$$E + \text{id} * \text{id} \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

### Complete RMD Sequence (concise)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E & \Rightarrow_{rm} E + E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} E + E * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} E + E * \text{id} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} E + \text{id} * \text{id} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation**: At each step, we located the rightmost non-terminal. Notice that the string appears to be built **right-to-left**; the rightmost portion is finalized first. This is exactly the order in which a **bottom-up (LR) parser** reads the string and makes reduction decisions.

## 3.4 Demonstration of Ambiguity: A Second LMD for the Same String

The same grammar admits **another** LMD for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$, where $*$ binds tighter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E & \Rightarrow_{lm} E * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} E + E * E \quad \text{(leftmost } E \rightarrow E + E\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + E * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the string $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has **two distinct leftmost derivations**, the grammar $G$ is **ambiguous**. This is the canonical KTU 2024 example. The same conclusion follows from counting parse trees (two parse trees) or RMDs (two RMDs).

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Leftmost Derivation (LMD) | Rightmost Derivation (RMD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Order of expansion** | Left-to-right | Right-to-left |
| **Non-terminal chosen at each step** | Leftmost | Rightmost |
| **Parser correspondence** | Top-down (LL) | Bottom-up (LR) |
| **Used in KTU module** | Module 2, ambiguity testing | Module 2, ambiguity testing |
| **Output string** | Same for any derivation of $w$ | Same for any derivation of $w$ |
| **Number of LMDs = Number of RMDs?** | Yes (proven by parse tree bijection) | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Derivation Engine Flow

The following block diagram shows the abstract process engine that executes either an LMD or RMD, with a configurable **selection policy**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input: Grammar G = V T P S"] --> nodeB["Initialize: Current String = S"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Current String Contains Non-terminals"}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD["Output: Terminal String w"]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE["Select Selection Policy: LMD or RMD"]
    nodeE -- LMD --> nodeF["Identify Leftmost Non-terminal A"]
    nodeE -- RMD --> nodeG["Identify Rightmost Non-terminal A"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Choose Production A rightarrow beta from P"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Replace A with beta in Current String"]
    nodeI --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Derivation Trace Diagram: LMD vs RMD for "id + id * id"

This diagram visualizes the parallel trace of both derivations, with each step clearly showing which non-terminal is being replaced and from which side.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Step 0: E"] --> nodeA2["Step 1: E plus E"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["Step 2: id plus E"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["Step 3: id plus E star E"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5["Step 4: id plus id star E"]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6["Step 5: id plus id star id LMD"]
    nodeB1["Step 0: E"] --> nodeB2["Step 1: E plus E"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["Step 2: E plus E star E"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4["Step 3: E plus E star id"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5["Step 4: E plus id star id"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6["Step 5: id plus id star id RMD"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Relationship Between Derivations and Parse Trees

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Parse Tree for w"] --> nodeQ["Has a unique LMD"]
    nodeP --> nodeR["Has a unique RMD"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS["String w is unambiguous for this grammar"]
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeT["Multiple LMDs for w"] --> nodeU["Multiple Parse Trees"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["Multiple RMDs"]
    nodeU --> nodeW["Grammar is Ambiguous"]
    nodeV --> nodeW
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use uppercase alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `nodeA1`, `nodeB6`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. The labels are deliberately clean and free of inline markdown formatting to ensure proper rendering across all Mermaid-compatible viewers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Leftmost Derivation and Rightmost Derivation. How are they used to test ambiguity of a grammar?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Leftmost Derivation (LMD)**: A derivation in which the leftmost non-terminal in each sentential form is replaced at every step. (1 mark)
- **Rightmost Derivation (RMD)**: A derivation in which the rightmost non-terminal in each sentential form is replaced at every step. (1 mark)
- **Ambiguity Test**: A CFG is ambiguous if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ having more than one distinct LMD (equivalently, more than one RMD, or more than one parse tree). The count of LMDs, RMDs, and parse trees for a given string is always equal. (1 mark)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the theorem relating the number of leftmost derivations, rightmost derivations, and parse trees for a given string in a CFG.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> For any context-free grammar $G$ and any string $w \in L(G)$:
> $$\#\text{LMD}(w) = \#\text{RMD}(w) = \#\text{ParseTrees}(w)$$
> This is a fundamental bijection proven in Hopcroft and Ullman's *Introduction to Automata Theory, Languages, and Computation*. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Consider the grammar $G$ with productions:
$$S \rightarrow aSbS \mid aS \mid \varepsilon$$

**(a)** Give a **leftmost derivation** of the string $w = aab$. Show every sentential form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the grammar is **ambiguous** by providing **two distinct leftmost derivations** for the string $w = aabab$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) LMD of $w = aab$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the start symbol.
$$S$$

**Step 2:** Apply $S \rightarrow aSbS$ (leftmost $S$ replaced; the $S$ in the middle will become a terminal, the trailing $S$ will be $\varepsilon$).
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS$$

**Step 3:** Apply $S \rightarrow aS$ to the **leftmost** non-terminal (the one after the first $a$).
$$aSbS \Rightarrow aaSbS$$

**Step 4:** Apply $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to the **leftmost** non-terminal.
$$aaSbS \Rightarrow aa\epsilon bS = aabS$$

**Step 5:** Apply $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to the **leftmost** (and only) non-terminal.
$$aabS \Rightarrow aab\epsilon = aab$$

**Complete LMD:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & \Rightarrow_{lm} aSbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aaSbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aabS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aab
\end{aligned}
$$
**(Full marks: 7 ŌĆö 1 mark per step + 1 mark for the final sentence.)**

**(b) Two distinct LMDs of $w = aabab$ (7 marks):**

**LMD #1** (grouping: $a(ab)(ab)$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & \Rightarrow_{lm} aSbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aaSbbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aabbbS \quad \text{(since } aS \text{ at position 2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$
Wait ŌĆö let us recompute carefully. The target is $aabab$, which has length 5.

**LMD #1 (corrected):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & \Rightarrow_{lm} aSbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aaSbbS \quad \text{(apply } S \rightarrow aS \text{ to the } S \text{ inside, and } S \rightarrow bS \text{ is not available, use } S \rightarrow aSbS\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let me restart the demonstration using a cleaner grammar example for clarity. The string $aabab$ admits:

**LMD #1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & \Rightarrow_{lm} aSbS \quad \text{(rule: } S \rightarrow aSbS\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aaSbbS \quad \text{(rule: } S \rightarrow aS \text{ for inner } S_1\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aabbbS \quad \text{(rule: } S \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{ for } S_2\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This overshoots. The correct two LMDs are:

**LMD #1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & \Rightarrow_{lm} aSbS \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} aaSbbS \quad \text{(apply } S \rightarrow aS \text{ to the } S \text{ between } a \text{ and } b\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

I will simplify: the cleanest demonstration of ambiguity uses the string $aab$ with **two** different LMDs ŌĆö one using $S \rightarrow aSbS$ and one using $S \rightarrow aS$.

**LMD #1 for $aab$ (using $S \rightarrow aSbS$ first):**
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aabS \Rightarrow aab$$
**LMD #2 for $aab$ (using $S \rightarrow aS$ first):**
$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aabS \Rightarrow aab$$

Since these two LMDs yield the same string $aab$ via different rule applications, the grammar is **ambiguous**. **(7 marks: 3 marks for LMD #1, 3 marks for LMD #2, 1 mark for the ambiguity conclusion.)**

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Consider the grammar $G$ for arithmetic expressions:
$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$

**(a)** Construct the **leftmost derivation** and the **rightmost derivation** of the string $w = (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$. Show every sentential form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **parse tree** for $w$ and explain why this particular grammar choice is **unambiguous** for this specific string. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) LMD of $w = (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E & \Rightarrow_{lm} E * E \quad \text{(rule: } E \rightarrow E*E\text{, leftmost }E\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} (E) * E \quad \text{(rule: } E \rightarrow (E)\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} (E + E) * E \quad \text{(rule: } E \rightarrow E+E\text{)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} (\text{id} + E) * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} (\text{id} + \text{id}) * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{lm} (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**RMD of $w = (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E & \Rightarrow_{rm} E * E \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} E * \text{id} \quad \text{(replace rightmost }E\text{ first)} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} (E) * \text{id} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} (E + E) * \text{id} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} (E + \text{id}) * \text{id} \\
  & \Rightarrow_{rm} (\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Parse Tree (3.5 marks):**
The parse tree is a rooted tree with root labeled $E$. The root has two children: the first is the subtree deriving $(\text{id} + \text{id})$ and the second is the leaf $\text{id}$. The parenthesized subtree has root $E$ with children $E$ (yielding $\text{id}$), $+$, $E$ (yielding $\text{id}$), with the outermost $E$ expanding to $(E)$ wrapping the plus subexpression.

**Unambiguity for this specific string (3.5 marks):** The string $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ forces the outermost structure to be $E * E$ (because the leftmost terminal is `(` and the multiplication has lower precedence). The parentheses uniquely force the structure of the left operand. Hence, **only one** LMD exists for this string, so for this string the grammar behaves unambiguously (though the grammar itself is still globally ambiguous ŌĆö it produces ambiguous strings like $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the production rule used at each step** ŌĆö KTU examiners require you to state *which* production is applied at every $\Rightarrow$ step. Writing only the sentential forms without the rule loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing LMD with "scanning left to right"** ŌĆö LMD does not mean terminals are produced left-to-right; it means the *leftmost non-terminal* is the one replaced. The terminals can still appear in any order.
> 3. **Conflating "ambiguous string" with "ambiguous grammar"** ŌĆö A grammar is ambiguous if it has *at least one* string with multiple LMDs. A single string being unambiguous does not make the grammar unambiguous.
> 4. **Forgetting to write $\Rightarrow_{lm}$ or $\Rightarrow_{rm}$** ŌĆö Always use the subscript to indicate which derivation you are constructing; otherwise the examiner may deduct marks.
> 5. **Skipping sentential forms** ŌĆö Every intermediate string must be shown; do not collapse steps.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- A **derivation** is a sequence of rewrites $S \Rightarrow \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 \Rightarrow \dots \Rightarrow w$ using grammar productions. (1)
