- **LMD** replaces the **leftmost** non-terminal at every step; **RMD** replaces the **rightmost** non-terminal at every step. (2)
- Both LMD and RMD produce the **same string** $w$, but the **sequence of sentential forms** differs. (3)
- A **sentential form** is any intermediate string; a **sentence** is a sentential form with no non-terminals. (4)
- The key identity: $\#\text{LMD}(w) = \#\text{RMD}(w) = \#\text{ParseTrees}(w)$ for any $w$ in any CFG. (5)
- A grammar is **ambiguous** if $\exists\, w \in L(G)$ with $\#\text{LMD}(w) \ge 2$. (6)
- LMD corresponds to **top-down (LL) parsing**; RMD corresponds to **bottom-up (LR) parsing**. (7)
- Always annotate each step with the **production rule used**: e.g., $E \Rightarrow_{lm} E + E$ *via* $E \rightarrow E + E$. (8)
- The canonical KTU example uses $E \rightarrow E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$; the string $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has two LMDs. (9)
- Ambiguity is **undecidable** in general for CFGs ŌĆö no algorithm exists that always correctly determines ambiguity. (10)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parse Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parse Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Parse Tree** (also called a **Derivation Tree** or **Concrete Syntax Tree**) is an ordered tree representation of the syntactic structure of a string generated by a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. It graphically depicts how the start symbol of a grammar can be rewritten, through successive application of production rules, to derive a particular terminal string.

Formally, given a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$, a parse tree for a string $w \in T^*$ is a rooted, ordered tree satisfying four strict properties:

1. **Root Property:** The root node is labeled with the grammar's **start symbol** $S$.
2. **Internal Node Property:** Every internal node is labeled with a non-terminal $A \in V$ (variables).
3. **Leaf Node Property:** Every leaf node is labeled with a terminal $a \in T$, or with the empty string $\varepsilon$. The leaves are read from **left to right** to form the **yield** of the tree.
4. **Production Application Property:** If an internal node is labeled $A$ and its children are labeled $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (from left to right), then $A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ **must be a valid production** $A \to \alpha$ in $P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Parse trees form the conceptual bridge between a grammar's abstract rules and the actual strings it generates. In KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) questions, you are almost always required to **construct a parse tree for a given string** using a given CFG and then use it to prove **ambiguity** or compute the **yield**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **family tree** of a royal dynasty. The **root** is the founding monarch (the start symbol $S$). Each child of a royal node represents an offspring produced by a royal decree (a production rule $A \to BC$). As the tree grows downward through generations, you eventually reach the youngest generation ŌĆö the **leaves** ŌĆö who are the common people (terminal symbols). The leaves read left-to-right form the visible "story" or sentence of the kingdom, while the internal structure (ancestors) is the hidden grammar generating it.

Equivalently, think of a parse tree as a **structured sentence diagram** in English grammar: the sentence splits into Subject + Predicate; the Subject splits into Article + Noun; and so on, until you reach the actual words.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction ŌĆö Parse Tree vs. Syntax Tree (Abstract):** A parse tree (concrete syntax tree) preserves every grammar artifact, including punctuation and non-terminals that are eliminated later. An "abstract syntax tree" (AST) is a condensed version used in compilers. KTU's TOC syllabus focuses strictly on the **concrete parse tree**.

### 1.3 Fundamental Components of a Parse Tree

| Component | Symbol Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Root | $S$ (Start Symbol) | Topmost node; always a non-terminal |
| Internal Nodes | $A \in V$ (Non-terminals) | Created by applying production rules |
| Leaves | $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ | Terminals or $\varepsilon$; read L-to-R for yield |
| Yield | $w \in T^*$ | Concatenation of leaf labels from left to right |
| Children | $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ | Ordered (left-to-right matters) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Yield of a Tree:** The string obtained by concatenating the labels of the leaves from **left to right**. The parse tree represents a derivation $S \xRightarrow{*} w$ if and only if its yield is $w$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Operational Rules

A parse tree is built using the following controlled, recursive logic:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Initialization:** Create the root node and label it with the start symbol $S$.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Selection:** Pick a non-internalized non-terminal leaf node (a leaf labeled by some $A \in V$). This is the "currently expanding node."
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Rule Application:** Choose a production $A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n \in P$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Branching:** Create $n$ children for node $A$, labeled $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ in that exact left-to-right order. Each $X_i$ becomes a leaf initially.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Termination Check:** Repeat Steps 2ŌĆō4 until **every leaf is either a terminal $a \in T$ or $\varepsilon$**.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Yield Extraction:** Read all leaf labels left-to-right to obtain the yield $w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the order matters:** A parse tree is an **ordered tree**. Swapping the left-to-right order of children for the same production can yield a different string, even though it represents a structurally similar derivation.

### 2.2 Core "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why root = $S$?** Because every derivation in a CFG begins at the start symbol. A parse tree must mirror the syntactic generation of the string, which originates at $S$.
- **Why internal nodes are non-terminals only?** Because only non-terminals can be rewritten by productions. Once a terminal appears, it cannot be further expanded.
- **Why leaves are terminals or $\varepsilon$?** Terminals are atomic, irreducible units of the language. $\varepsilon$ productions allow a non-terminal to vanish and produce the empty string.
- **Why left-to-right yield?** The grammar's concatenation semantics for sentential forms is strictly ordered.

### 2.3 Relationship: Parse Tree Ōåö Derivations (Core Theorem)

There is a fundamental equivalence, often tested in KTU:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Parse Tree Ōćö Derivation):** Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a CFG. Then for any string $w \in T^*$:
> $$S \xRightarrow{*} w \quad \iff \quad \text{There exists a parse tree with root } S \text{ and yield } w$$
> Furthermore, the number of parse trees for $w$ equals the number of distinct **leftmost derivations** of $w$, which also equals the number of distinct **rightmost derivations** of $w$.

**Consequence:** If a string has **two or more parse trees**, then it has **two or more leftmost derivations**, and the grammar is called **ambiguous**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Parse Tree | $T$ | Ordered rooted tree satisfying 4 properties |
| Yield | $\text{yield}(T) = w$ | Concatenation of leaf labels, left to right |
| CFG | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V$: non-terminals, $T$: terminals, $P$: rules, $S$: start |
| Leftmost Derivation | $S \xRightarrow[l]{*} w$ | Always rewrites the leftmost non-terminal first |
| Rightmost Derivation | $S \xRightarrow[r]{*} w$ | Always rewrites the rightmost non-terminal first |
| Ambiguity | $\vert \text{parse trees for } w \vert \geq 2$ | More than one parse tree $\Rightarrow$ ambiguous |
| Inherent Ambiguity | $\forall G'$ with $L(G) = L(G'), G' \text{ is ambiguous}$ | Language itself is ambiguous |
| Tree Height | $h(T)$ | Length of longest root-to-leaf path |
| Branching Factor | $n$ | Number of children per internal node (variable) |
| Yield Length | $\vert w \vert$ | Equals the number of leaves with non-$\varepsilon$ labels |
| Parse Tree Count | $\#\text{PT}(w)$ | Equals $\#\text{LMD}(w) = \#\text{RMD}(w)$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parse trees are the literal backbone of:

- **Compilers & Interpreters:** The frontend (parser/Lex+Yacc/Bison) builds parse trees (or ASTs) from source code. Every valid `if-else` in C/Java is verified by traversing a parse tree.
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Syntactic parsers in linguistics build parse trees for sentences to extract meaning.
- **XML / JSON Validators:** Document Object Model (DOM) trees are essentially parse trees enforcing a context-free schema.
- **Database Query Parsers:** SQL queries are parsed into trees for execution plan optimization.
- **Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure prediction uses generalized parse tree (Stochastic Context-Free Grammars).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Building a Parse Tree for a String

**Grammar $G$:**

$$
S \to a\,S\,b \,\vert\, \varepsilon
$$

**Target String:** $w = aabbb$ (Note: $|w|_a = 3$, $|w|_b = 3$)

**Step-by-Step Parse Tree Construction:**

**Step 1:** Root is $S$. Apply $S \to aSb$. Tree:

```
        S
      / | \
     a  S  b
```

**Step 2:** The middle leaf is $S$ (non-terminal). Apply $S \to aSb$ again:

```
        S
      / | \
     a  S  b
      /|\ 
     a S  b
```

**Step 3:** The new middle leaf is $S$. Apply $S \to aSb$ again:

```
            S
          / | \
         a  S  b
          /|\ 
         a S  b
          /|\ 
         a S  b
```

**Step 4:** The deepest middle leaf is $S$. Apply $S \to \varepsilon$:

```
            S
          / | \
         a  S  b
          /|\ 
         a S  b
          /|\ 
         a (╬Ą) b
```

**Step 5 ŌĆö Yield Extraction (Left-to-Right leaf read):**

$$w = a \cdot a \cdot a \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot b \cdot b = aaabbb$$

But wait, the target was $aabbb$, not $aaabbb$. Let us re-trace: We need $|a| = |b| = 2$, so we need to use $S \to aSb$ exactly twice.

**Corrected Tree for $w = aabb$:**

**Step 1:** Apply $S \to aSb$, then apply $S \to aSb$ to the middle, then apply $S \to \varepsilon$ to the deepest.

**Tree Structure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & S \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & a \quad S \quad b \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & a \quad S \quad b \\
\text{Level 3:} \quad & \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Leftmost Derivation (LMD):**

$$
S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aabb
$$

**Rightmost Derivation (RMD):**

$$
S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aabb
$$

In this symmetric case, LMD = RMD because the structure is balanced.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Proving Ambiguity

**Grammar $G$:**

$$
E \to E + E \mid E \ast E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}
$$

**String:** $w = \text{id} + \text{id} \ast \text{id}$

**Parse Tree #1 (groups as $(\text{id}+\text{id}) \ast \text{id}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root} & : E \\
\text{Apply } E &\to E \ast E \\
\text{Left child} & : E \to E + E \to \text{id} + \text{id} \\
\text{Right child} & : E \to \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree #2 (groups as $\text{id} + (\text{id} \ast \text{id})$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root} & : E \\
\text{Apply } E &\to E + E \\
\text{Left child} & : E \to \text{id} \\
\text{Right child} & : E \to E \ast E \to \text{id} \ast \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **two distinct parse trees exist** for the same string, the grammar is **ambiguous**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** For Part B (14-mark) questions, students are expected to (a) construct **two** parse trees, (b) write **two** leftmost derivations, and (c) explicitly state the conclusion "$\therefore$ the grammar is ambiguous."

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Parse Tree vs. Leftmost vs. Rightmost Derivation

| Property | Parse Tree | Leftmost Derivation (LMD) | Rightmost Derivation (RMD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | 2D tree | 1D sequence of sentential forms | 1D sequence of sentential forms |
| Expansion order | All non-terminals expandable in any order | Leftmost non-terminal first | Rightmost non-terminal first |
| Uniqueness | May not be unique (ambiguity) | May not be unique | May not be unique |
| Equivalence | 1 parse tree $\equiv$ 1 LMD $\equiv$ 1 RMD | 1 LMD $\equiv$ 1 RMD if unambiguous | Same as LMD count |
| Visual benefit | Shows hierarchy & grouping | Shows rewrite order | Shows canonical form |
| KTU question type | "Draw the parse tree" | "Show the LMD" | "Show the RMD" |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Theorem: Equivalence of Parse Trees and Derivations

**Claim:** If a parse tree for $w$ exists, then there exists a leftmost derivation of $w$ from $S$.

**Proof by Structural Induction on Tree Height $h(T)$:**

**Base Case ($h(T) = 1$):** The tree has a single node labeled $S$. The only way the yield is in $T^*$ is if $S \to \varepsilon$ is a production, or the tree is degenerate. The LMD is $S \Rightarrow \varepsilon$, matching the yield.

**Inductive Step:** Assume the claim holds for all parse trees of height $\leq h$. Consider a tree $T$ of height $h+1$ with root $S$ and children $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ via the production $S \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$.

Let $T_i$ be the subtree rooted at $X_i$ with yield $w_i$. By the **induction hypothesis**, each $T_i$ has a leftmost derivation. The LMD of $T$ is constructed as:

$$
S \Rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n \xRightarrow[l]{*} w_1 X_2 \cdots X_n \xRightarrow[l]{*} w_1 w_2 \cdots X_n \xRightarrow[l]{*} w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n = w
$$

The "$\xRightarrow[l]{*}$" steps are valid because the leftmost non-terminal being rewritten is always in some $T_i$ where the induction hypothesis applies. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The reverse direction** (derivation $\Rightarrow$ parse tree) is also provable by induction on the number of derivation steps, but the tree may be obtained in many different orderings ŌĆö all collapsing to the **same parse tree**. This non-uniqueness of construction is precisely what allows **ambiguity**.

### 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical / Structural Verifications

**Verification Table for a Small Grammar:**

Let $G$ be: $S \to 0S1 \mid \varepsilon$. Compute the parse tree structure and yield for $|w| = 4$.

| Derivations Used | Tree Levels | Yield | LMD Step Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \to \varepsilon$ | 1 | $\varepsilon$ | 1 |
| $S \to 0S1$ | 2 | $01$ | 2 |
| $S \to 0S1 \to 00S11$ | 3 | $0011$ | 3 |
| $S \to 0S1 \to 00S11 \to 000S111$ | 4 | $000111$ | 4 |

**Pattern Recognition:** Yield of a height-$n$ tree is $0^n 1^n$, so the language is $L = \{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, a classic non-regular CFL.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Generic Parse Tree Architecture

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the hierarchical structure of a parse tree, showing how the root, internal nodes, leaves, and yield extraction interact.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ROOT NODE: Start Symbol S"] --> B["Internal Node A1 - Nonterminal"]
    A --> C["Internal Node A2 - Nonterminal"]
    A --> D["Leaf Node - Terminal a1"]
    B --> E["Leaf Node - Terminal a2"]
    B --> F["Internal Node B1 - Nonterminal"]
    B --> G["Leaf Node - Terminal a3"]
    C --> H["Leaf Node - Terminal a4"]
    C --> I["Leaf Node - Terminal a5"]
    F --> J["Leaf Node - Terminal a6"]
    F --> K["Leaf Node - epsilon"]

    L["YIELD EXTRACTION"] --> A
    L --> M["Read leaves left to right"]
    M --> N["Concatenate to form string w"]
    N --> O["Final Yield: a2 a6 a3 a1 a4 a5"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The topmost box is the root. Boxes that branch further down are **internal non-terminals**. Boxes with no outgoing arrows are **leaves** (terminals or $\varepsilon$). The right-hand column simulates the yield extraction process.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Construction of a Parse Tree

This flowchart illustrates the iterative construction algorithm.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Initialize root with Start Symbol S"] --> S2["Step 2: Pick a non-terminal leaf node A"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Select production A to X1 X2 ... Xn"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Create n ordered children under A"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Check termination - all leaves terminals or epsilon?"]
    S5 --> S6{"All leaves terminal?"}
    S6 -- NO --> S2
    S6 -- YES --> S7["Step 6: Extract yield by left-to-right leaf read"]
    S7 --> S8["Final Output: Yield w from root S"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Ambiguity Detection Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Given Grammar G and String w"] --> P2["Attempt Parse Tree Construction 1"]
    P2 --> P3{"Parse Tree 1 Found?"}
    P3 -- NO --> P4["String w NOT in L of G"]
    P3 -- YES --> P5["Record Tree 1 Structure and Yield"]
    P5 --> P6["Attempt Parse Tree Construction 2 Different grouping"]
    P6 --> P7{"Second Distinct Tree?"}
    P7 -- NO --> P8["Grammar is UNAMBIGUOUS for w"]
    P7 -- YES --> P9["Grammar is AMBIGUOUS"]
    P8 --> P10["Confirm by showing unique LMD and RMD"]
    P9 --> P11["Confirm by showing two different LMDs"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö Concrete Parse Tree for a Specific Example

For grammar $S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$ and string $w = aabb$:

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["S ROOT"] --> X1["a terminal"]
    R --> M["S nonterminal internal"]
    R --> X2["b terminal"]
    M --> Y1["a terminal"]
    M --> N["S nonterminal internal"]
    M --> Y2["b terminal"]
    N --> Z1["epsilon leaf"]
```

**Yield Extraction Path (left-to-right):** $a$ (from R's first child) $\to a$ (from M's first child) $\to \varepsilon$ (from N) $\to b$ (from M's second child) $\to b$ (from R's third child) = $aabb$ Ō£ō

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 ŌĆö Parse Tree vs. Derivation Equivalence Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Parse Tree T with yield w"] -- "expand children top-down" --> B["Any Order Derivation"]
    B -- "restrict to leftmost" --> C["Leftmost Derivation LMD"]
    B -- "restrict to rightmost" --> D["Rightmost Derivation RMD"]
    A -- "one-to-one in unambiguous case" --> C
    A -- "one-to-one in unambiguous case" --> D
    C -- "reverse construction" --> A
    D -- "reverse construction" --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "show that the parse tree corresponds to the LMD," always list the sentential forms and circle the leftmost non-terminal that is being expanded at each step.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a *parse tree*. State any two conditions that a tree must satisfy to be a parse tree of a Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$.

**Model Answer:**

A parse tree for a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is a labeled ordered tree in which:

- **Condition 1:** The root is labeled with the start symbol $S$.
- **Condition 2:** Every internal node is labeled with a non-terminal $A \in V$, and every leaf is labeled with a terminal $a \in T$ or with $\varepsilon$.
- **Condition 3:** If an internal node labeled $A$ has children $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (in order), then $A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ must be a production in $P$.
- **Condition 4:** The yield (left-to-right leaf concatenation) is the derived string $w \in T^*$.

*(Any two conditions accepted for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the *yield* of a parse tree? How is it related to the derivation of a string?

**Model Answer:**

The **yield** of a parse tree is the string obtained by reading the labels of its leaves from **left to right** and concatenating them.

**Relation to derivation:** The yield of a parse tree is the string $w$ derived from the start symbol $S$ via the grammar's production rules. Formally, $S \xRightarrow{*} w$ if and only if there exists a parse tree whose root is $S$ and whose yield is $w$. The parse tree thus provides a 2-dimensional graphical certificate of the 1-dimensional derivation sequence.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module 2 questions typically carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students choose **one of two** alternatives.

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

Consider the grammar:

$$
S \to aS \mid bS \mid \varepsilon
$$

**(a)** Construct the parse tree for the string $w = aab$ and show its **leftmost derivation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that this grammar generates the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with the last generated symbol, no constraint}\}$ and determine whether the grammar is ambiguous. Justify with a parse tree if ambiguous. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parse Tree for $w = aab$:**

```
                S
              / | \
             a  S
              /|\
             a  S
                |
                b
```

Wait ŌĆö the tree must terminate all leaves as terminals. Let us redo:

**Step-by-step:**

- Level 0: Root = $S$
- Apply $S \to aS$: Children = $a$, $S$
- The middle $S$ is expanded via $S \to aS$: Children = $a$, $S$
- The new middle $S$ is expanded via $S \to bS$: Children = $b$, $S$
- The new $S$ is expanded via $S \to \varepsilon$: yields $\varepsilon$

**Yield (left-to-right):** $a \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \varepsilon = aab$ Ō£ō

**Leftmost Derivation:**

$$
S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aabS \Rightarrow aab
$$

Each step rewrites the **leftmost** non-terminal. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Language and Ambiguity Analysis:**

The grammar generates $L = \{a, b\}^*$ ŌĆö all strings over $\{a, b\}$ including $\varepsilon$. This is because at each step we can choose $a$, $b$, or terminate with $\varepsilon$.

**Ambiguity Check:** Consider $w = ab$. We can derive it two ways:

**Tree 1:** $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow abS \Rightarrow ab$ (apply $S \to aS$, then $S \to bS$, then $S \to \varepsilon$)

**Tree 2:** $S \Rightarrow bS \Rightarrow abS \Rightarrow ab$ (apply $S \to bS$, then $S \to aS$, then $S \to \varepsilon$)

Wait, the order of application is the same; let me check the trees:

**Parse Tree 1 (apply $S \to aS$ first):**

```
       S
     / | \
    a  S
      /|\
     b  ╬Ą
```

Yield: $a \cdot b \cdot \varepsilon = ab$ Ō£ō

**Parse Tree 2 (apply $S \to bS$ first):**

```
       S
     / | \
    b  S
      /|\
     a  ╬Ą
```

Yield: $b \cdot a \cdot \varepsilon = ba$ ŌĆö This is **$ba$, not $ab$**! So this is for a different string.

Let me re-examine: For $w = ab$, the only parse tree structure that yields $ab$ is the first one above. The second tree yields $ba$. So the grammar might **not** be ambiguous.

**Conclusion:** Try $w = a$. We can apply $S \to aS$ then $S \to \varepsilon$, giving one tree. Try $w = aa$: $S \to aS \to aaS \to aa$, one tree. **The grammar is unambiguous** because the leftmost symbol of the yield always uniquely determines which production was applied at the root.

Hence $L = \{a, b\}^*$ and **the grammar is unambiguous**. (7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

Consider the grammar:

$$
G: S \to S + S \mid S \ast S \mid a
$$

**(a)** Construct two distinct parse trees for the string $w = a + a \ast a$ and write the corresponding leftmost derivations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Is $G$ ambiguous? Can you construct an equivalent unambiguous grammar? Show the parse tree for $w$ in the new grammar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two Parse Trees for $w = a + a \ast a$:**

**Parse Tree 1** (groups as $(a + a) \ast a$):

```
              S
           /  |  \
          S   *   S
        /|\       |
       S + S      a
       |   |
       a   a
```

**LMD 1:**

$$
S \Rightarrow S \ast S \Rightarrow (S + S) \ast S \Rightarrow (a + S) \ast S \Rightarrow (a + a) \ast S \Rightarrow (a + a) \ast a
$$

**Parse Tree 2** (groups as $a + (a \ast a)$):

```
              S
           /  |  \
          S   +   S
       /|\       /|\
      S * S     S   (none)
      |   |
      a   a     a
```

**LMD 2:**

$$
S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow a + S \Rightarrow a + S \ast S \Rightarrow a + a \ast S \Rightarrow a + a \ast a
$$

Both yield $a + a \ast a$ but with different groupings. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Ambiguity & Unambiguous Reformulation:**

Since **two distinct parse trees** exist for the same string, the grammar $G$ is **ambiguous**.

**Equivalent Unambiguous Grammar $G'$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\to E + T \mid T \\
T &\to T \ast F \mid F \\
F &\to a
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar enforces the standard precedence: $\ast$ binds tighter than $+$, and associates left-to-right.

**Parse Tree for $w = a + a \ast a$ in $G'$:**

```
                   E
                /  |  \
               E    +   T
               |       /|\
               T      T * F
               |      |   |
               F      F   a
               |      |
               a      a
```

**LMD in $G'$:**

$$
E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow T + T \Rightarrow F + T \Rightarrow a + T \Rightarrow a + T \ast F \Rightarrow a + F \ast F \Rightarrow a + a \ast F \Rightarrow a + a \ast a
$$

**Uniqueness:** Every string in $L(G)$ has **exactly one** parse tree in $G'$, confirming $G'$ is unambiguous. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to terminate all leaves.** Students often leave a non-terminal at a leaf. Every leaf must be a terminal or $\varepsilon$. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Drawing unordered trees.** The left-to-right order of children matters. A reversed order changes the yield. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing LMD and RMD.** LMD rewrites the **leftmost** non-terminal; RMD rewrites the **rightmost** non-terminal. Mixing them up loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to conclude ambiguity.** Just drawing two trees is not enough. Explicitly state "$\therefore$ the grammar is ambiguous" or "the language is inherently ambiguous" as required. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Confusing parse tree with syntax tree.** KTU expects a **parse tree** (concrete), not an AST. Do not strip away non-terminals from internal nodes.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A parse tree is a labeled, ordered, rooted tree representing the derivation of a string from a CFG's start symbol.
- **Four Conditions:** (1) Root = $S$, (2) internal nodes = non-terminals, (3) leaves = terminals/$\varepsilon$, (4) children correspond to a production applied to parent.
- **Yield:** Left-to-right concatenation of leaf labels = derived string.
- **Equivalence:** Parse tree $\iff$ derivation (leftmost, rightmost, or any order).
- **LMD Ōćö RMD Ōćö Parse Tree count:** In the unambiguous case, all three are unique per string. In the ambiguous case, multiple of each exist.
- **Ambiguity:** A grammar is ambiguous if **some string** in its language has **two or more distinct parse trees**.
- **Inherent Ambiguity:** A language is inherently ambiguous if **every** grammar generating it is ambiguous (e.g., $\{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m\}$).
- **Tree Construction Algorithm:** Initialize with $S$, repeatedly expand non-terminal leaves using productions, terminate when all leaves are terminals, read yield L-to-R.
- **KTU Standard Examples:** $E \to E + E \mid E \ast E \mid a$ is the textbook ambiguous arithmetic grammar. Always test with strings like $a + a \ast a$ or $a + a + a$ to expose ambiguity.
- **Verification Trick:** If the number of distinct LMDs of $w$ in $G$ exceeds 1, then $G$ is ambiguous. If it equals 1 for all $w$, $G$ is unambiguous.
- **Real-world impact:** Parse trees underpin every compiler frontend (Lex/Yacc/Bison/ANTLR), NLP parsers, and XML schema validators.
- **Mnemonic ŌĆö "RINT":** **R**oot = $S$, **I**nternal = non-terminal, **N**ode ordering = left-to-right, **T**erminals at leaves.
- **Examiner's Favorite Question:** "Show that the grammar is ambiguous by constructing two parse trees for the string $w = \ldots$" ŌĆö memorize this format.
- **Order of Operations in $G'$:** Use precedence cascade $E \to T \to F$ with $+$ at the top and $a$ at the bottom to disambiguate arithmetic grammars.
- **Yield Length Formula:** For a parse tree of height $h$ with $k$ internal nodes using productions of average arity $r$, the yield length is at most $r^k$ (worst case).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity in CFGs and CFLs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ambiguity in CFGs and CFLs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Ambiguous Grammar):**
> A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ such that $w$ has **two or more distinct derivation trees** (also called **parse trees**) in $G$. Equivalently, $w$ has **two or more distinct leftmost derivations** (or **two or more distinct rightmost derivations**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Inherently Ambiguous CFL):**
> A Context-Free Language (CFL) $L$ is called **inherently ambiguous** if **every** CFG that generates $L$ is ambiguous. In other words, there is no unambiguous grammar for $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation vs. Parse Tree Equivalence:**
> For a CFG, the following three conditions are **equivalent** for ambiguity testing:
> 1. Existence of two distinct parse trees for some string $w$.
> 2. Existence of two distinct leftmost derivations of $w$.
> 3. Existence of two distinct rightmost derivations of $w$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ambiguity in grammar like giving **two different sets of directions** to reach the same destination on a map.

- Imagine you tell a friend: *"Go straight, then turn left"* (Direction Set A) **and also** *"Turn left, then go straight"* (Direction Set B). Both directions ultimately take your friend to the **same restaurant**, but the **paths are structurally different**.
- In a programming language compiler, this is a **disaster**. If a statement like `a + b * c` can be parsed in two different ways, the compiler might generate two different machine codes ŌĆö one where `+` is evaluated first, and another where `*` is evaluated first. This leads to **non-deterministic behavior** in software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Impact:** Ambiguity in programming language grammars (like the classic `if-then-else` ambiguity in C/Pascal) is precisely why language designers spend enormous effort creating **unambiguous grammars** ŌĆö so that every valid program has **exactly one** parse tree, ensuring deterministic compilation.

---

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Theory of Computation

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Decidability** | The question *"Is a given CFG ambiguous?"* is **undecidable** (no algorithm exists for all CFGs). |
| **Compiler Design** | Unambiguous grammars ŌåÆ unique parse trees ŌåÆ deterministic compilers. |
| **Language Class** | DCFL (Deterministic CFL) ŌŖŖ CFL, and inherently ambiguous languages **cannot** be DCFLs. |
| **Parsing Algorithms** | LL(k), LR(k), LALR parsers all require unambiguous grammars to function correctly. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Ambiguity

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** A grammar is **unambiguous** if and only if **every** string in its language has **exactly one** parse tree. The grammar is ambiguous if there exists **at least one** string with multiple parse trees.

The logical steps for analyzing ambiguity in a CFG $G$:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the language $L(G)$:** Determine what strings the grammar generates.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Pick a candidate string $w \in L(G)$:** Often a short, structured string (e.g., for arithmetic, pick something like `a + b * c`).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Attempt to construct two distinct parse trees for $w$:** If successful, the grammar is **ambiguous**.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Confirm the trees are structurally different:** Two trees differing only in the order of independent sibling productions are NOT distinct. They must differ in the **hierarchical structure** of internal node labeling.

### 2.2 Why Ambiguity Arises ŌĆö Common Causes

- **Undistinguished operator precedence:** In arithmetic expressions, if `+` and `*` are at the same recursion level.
- **Left recursion combined with common prefixes:** Leads to multiple ways to associate operands.
- **Dangling else problem:** The classic `if E then S` vs `if E then S else S` conflict.
- **Unrestricted recursion:** Grammar allows the same string to be derived through two different recursive paths.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Formal Statement | Notes / KTU Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| **Ambiguous Grammar** | $\exists\, w \in L(G)$ such that $w$ has $\geq 2$ distinct parse trees | Most common KTU definition |
| **Equivalent via LMD** | $w$ has $\geq 2$ distinct leftmost derivations | Used as alternative test |
| **Equivalent via RMD** | $w$ has $\geq 2$ distinct rightmost derivations | Symmetric to LMD |
| **Inherently Ambiguous CFL** | $\forall G'$ with $L(G') = L$, $G'$ is ambiguous | No unambiguous grammar exists |
| **Unambiguous Grammar** | $\forall\, w \in L(G)$, $w$ has exactly 1 parse tree | Ideal target for compiler design |
| **Decidability** | "$G$ is ambiguous?" is **undecidable** for general CFGs | Famous result by Cantor (1962) |
| **Ambiguity Test (LMD)** | $w \Rightarrow^*_{lm} \alpha_1$ and $w \Rightarrow^*_{lm} \alpha_2$ with $\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$ | Practical test method |
| **Parse Tree Count** | $\vert T_G(w) \vert = 1$ for unambiguous $G$, $\forall w \in L(G)$ | $T_G(w)$ = set of parse trees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction:** Ambiguity is a property of the **grammar** (or equivalently, the **language-grammar pair**), NOT the language alone. A language may have both an ambiguous and an unambiguous grammar. The term *"inherently ambiguous"* applies only when **no** unambiguous grammar exists.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Construction:** YACC, Bison, ANTLR ŌĆö all grammar generators produce warnings or errors for ambiguous productions.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Sentence structures in English are inherently ambiguous (e.g., *"I saw the man with the telescope"*).
- **XML/HTML Parsing:** Browser engines require unambiguous grammars to render markup consistently.
- **Programming Language Design:** Languages like Java, Python, C++ have meticulously designed unambiguous grammars documented in their specifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Classic Example: Arithmetic Expression Grammar (Ambiguous)

Consider the CFG $G_1$ for arithmetic expressions with `+` and `*`:

$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid a$$

**Claim:** $G_1$ is ambiguous.

**Proof by construction of two parse trees for $w = a + a * a$:**

**Derivation 1 (Parse Tree showing `*` has higher precedence ŌĆö natural reading):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow E + (E * E) \\
  &\Rightarrow E + (a * E) \\
  &\Rightarrow E + (a * a) \\
  &\Rightarrow a + (a * a)
\end{aligned}$$

**Parse Tree 1 (precedence: `*` over `+`):**

```
        E
       /|\
      / | \
     E  +  E
     |     /|\
     a    / | \
         E * E
         |   |
         a   a
```

**Derivation 2 (Parse Tree showing `+` evaluated first ŌĆö unnatural):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow (E + E) * E \\
  &\Rightarrow (a + E) * E \\
  &\Rightarrow (a + a) * E \\
  &\Rightarrow (a + a) * a
\end{aligned}$$

**Parse Tree 2 (precedence: `+` over `*`):**

```
          E
         /|\
        / | \
       E  *  E
      /|\     |
     / | \    a
    E  +  E
    |     |
    a     a
```

**Conclusion:** Two distinct parse trees exist for $w = a + a * a$, therefore $G_1$ is **ambiguous**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Identifying Ambiguity

| Step | Check | Action if True | Action if False |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Does $G$ have two productions with the same non-terminal on LHS and overlapping structure? | Strong indicator of ambiguity | Continue testing |
| **2** | Pick a short string $w$ that combines the conflicting features | Proceed to Step 3 | Try another string |
| **3** | Construct parse tree #1 for $w$ | Record it | Grammar too constrained; revisit |
| **4** | Can you build a structurally different parse tree #2 for $w$? | **Grammar is AMBIGUOUS** | Test more strings |
| **5** | Have you tested all "boundary" strings in $L(G)$? | If no ambiguity found, likely unambiguous | If some show ambiguity, ambiguous |

### 3.3 Constructing an Unambiguous Equivalent Grammar

For the ambiguous grammar $G_1$ above, an **unambiguous** equivalent $G_2$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\
F &\rightarrow a
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** The string $a + a * a$ now has **only one** parse tree (with `*` correctly binding tighter than `+`):

```
         E
        / \
       /   \
      E     T
      |     |
      T     T * F
      |     |   |
      F     F   a
      |     |
      a     a
```

Wait ŌĆö that gives 3 levels. The correct tree is:

```
            E
          /   \
         E     T
         |    / \
         T   T   F
         |   |   |
         F   F   a
         |   |
         a   a
```

This single tree enforces `*` precedence over `+`. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** The languages are equal: $L(G_1) = L(G_2)$, but $G_2$ is **unambiguous** while $G_1$ is **ambiguous**. This demonstrates that ambiguity is a **grammar property**, not a **language property**.

### 3.4 Inherent Ambiguity ŌĆö The Classic Example

The language $L = \{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^m b^n c^n\}$ (where $m, n \geq 1$) is **inherently ambiguous**.

**Reasoning (Sketch):**
- Any CFG generating $L$ must handle the "overlap" region where $m = n$ in both parts.
- In the overlap region $a^n b^n c^n$, the grammar **cannot decide** whether the string was generated by the first component (with $m = n$) or the second component (with $m = n$).
- This forces two distinct parse trees for these overlapping strings, making **every** grammar for $L$ ambiguous.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ambiguous grammar** with **inherently ambiguous language**. Always verify: can you find *any* unambiguous grammar? If yes, the language is NOT inherently ambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ambiguity Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given CFG G and String w in L of G"] --> B{"Can you construct two or more distinct parse trees for w?"}
    B -- "Yes, structurally different trees exist" --> C["G is AMBIGUOUS"]
    B -- "No, only one tree possible" --> D{"Test all w in L of G"}
    D -- "All w have exactly one tree" --> E["G is UNAMBIGUOUS"]
    D -- "Some w yields multiple trees" --> C
    C --> F{"Does there exist ANY unambiguous grammar G prime with L of G prime = L of G?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["L is AMBIGUOUS grammar only, NOT inherently ambiguous"]
    F -- "No, ALL grammars for L are ambiguous" --> H["L is INHERENTLY AMBIGUOUS CFL"]
    E --> I["L is a UNAMBIGUOUS CFL"]
    G --> I
    H --> J["L is NOT a DCFL Deterministic CFL"]
```

### 4.2 Parse Tree Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tree1["Parse Tree 1: MUL binds TIGHTER"]
        R1["E"] --> L1["E"]
        R1 --> OP1["PLUS"]
        R1 --> R1b["E"]
        L1 --> A1["a"]
        R1b --> L1b["E"]
        R1b --> OP1b["MUL"]
        R1b --> R1c["E"]
        L1b --> A2["a"]
        R1c --> A3["a"]
    end

    subgraph Tree2["Parse Tree 2: PLUS binds TIGHTER"]
        R2["E"] --> L2["E"]
        R2 --> OP2["MUL"]
        R2 --> R2b["E"]
        L2 --> L2b["E"]
        L2 --> OP2b["PLUS"]
        L2 --> R2c["E"]
        L2b --> B1["a"]
        R2c --> B2["a"]
        R2b --> B3["a"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Language Classes (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["REGULAR LANGUAGES"] --> CFL["CONTEXT-FREE LANGUAGES"]
    R --> DCFL["DETERMINISTIC CFLs"]
    DCFL --> CFL
    CFL --> UCFL["UNAMBIGUOUS CFLs subset of CFL"]
    UCFL --> CFL
    CFL -. "INHERENTLY AMBIGUOUS" .-> IA["INHERENTLY AMBIGUOUS CFLs"]
    IA --> CFL
    DCFL --> X["PROPER SUBSET"]
    UCFL --> Y["OVERLAPS with DCFL but NEITHER contains the other fully"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** The relationships are:
> - **Regular ŌŖŖ DCFL ŌŖŖ Unambiguous CFL ŌŖŖ CFL**
> - **Inherently Ambiguous CFLs ŌŖé CFL** but disjoint from **Unambiguous CFLs**
> - **DCFL and Unambiguous CFLs are incomparable** (neither is a subset of the other)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define ambiguous grammar. Show that the grammar $S \rightarrow SS \mid a$ is ambiguous.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A CFG $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists a string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two or more distinct leftmost derivations).

**Construction for $w = aaa$:**

**Parse Tree 1 (Left-associative grouping):**

```
      S
     /|\
    / | \
   S  S  S
   |  |  |
   a  a  a
```

**Leftmost Derivation 1:**

$$S \Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aSS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aaa$$

**Parse Tree 2 (Right-associative grouping):**

```
        S
       /|\
      / | \
     S  S  a
     |  |
     a  a
```

Wait, this also gives 3 leaves. Let me correct ŌĆö the alternative structure:

```
      S
     /|\
    / | \
   S  S  a
   |
   S
   |
   a
   |
   a
```

**Leftmost Derivation 2:**

$$S \Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow SSS \Rightarrow aSS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aaa$$

The two parse trees are structurally different. Hence $G$ is **ambiguous**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**What is an inherently ambiguous CFL? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A CFL $L$ is **inherently ambiguous** if **every** CFG $G$ that generates $L$ is ambiguous. There exists no unambiguous grammar for $L$.

**Example:** $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is the classic example (though it requires careful proof using Ogden's Lemma or the Parikh property).

A more accessible example: $L = \{a^n b^n c^m \mid m, n \geq 1\} \cup \{a^m b^n c^n \mid m, n \geq 1\}$.

For the second example, in the overlapping region $a^n b^n c^n$, any grammar cannot uniquely decide whether the string arose from the first component (where $m = n$) or the second component (where $m = n$), forcing ambiguity. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define ambiguous grammar and inherently ambiguous CFL. Discuss the decidability of ambiguity in CFGs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the grammar $G$ with productions:
$$S \rightarrow aS \mid aSb \mid \epsilon$$
Prove that $G$ is unambiguous. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Definition of Ambiguous Grammar:**
A CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ having **two or more distinct parse trees**. (1 mark)

**Definition of Inherently Ambiguous CFL:**
A CFL $L$ is **inherently ambiguous** if for **every** CFG $G$ with $L(G) = L$, $G$ is ambiguous. (1 mark)

**Decidability of Ambigu:**

The problem *"Given a CFG $G$, is $G$ ambiguous?"* is **undecidable**. (2 marks)

**Justification:**
- The result was proven by Cantor (1962) and later refined by Floyd and others.
- The proof uses a **reduction from the Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)**, which is known to be undecidable.
- Sketch: Given any instance of PCP, one can construct a CFG $G$ such that $G$ is ambiguous if and only if the PCP instance has a solution. Since PCP is undecidable, ambiguity testing is also undecidable.
- **Consequence:** There is no algorithm that can correctly determine ambiguity for **all** CFGs. (3 marks)

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Claim:** $G: S \rightarrow aS \mid aSb \mid \epsilon$ is unambiguous.

**Observation about the language:**
The grammar generates $L(G) = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ where $n$ is the number of `a`'s, and the recursion structure enforces that `a`'s come before `b`'s in matched pairs. Wait ŌĆö let me re-examine. The grammar $S \rightarrow aS \mid aSb \mid \epsilon$ actually generates $L = \{a^m b^n \mid m \geq n\}$ (more `a`'s than `b`'s, with each `b` matched by a preceding `a`).

**Proof of Unambiguity:**

We show that every string in $L(G)$ has a **unique** parse tree. (1 mark)

**Approach: Strong Induction on the length of the string $w$.**

**Base Case:** $w = \epsilon$. The only derivation is $S \Rightarrow \epsilon$, giving a single tree. (1 mark)

**Inductive Step:** Assume every string of length $< n$ has a unique parse tree. Consider a string $w$ of length $n \geq 1$.

Since $w \in L(G)$, $w$ must start with $a$ (the only way to produce non-empty terminal strings). So $w = a \cdot w'$ for some $w'$.

**Case Analysis on the first production applied:**

- **Case 1: $S \Rightarrow aS$:** Then $w' \in L(G)$, and by inductive hypothesis, $w'$ has a unique parse tree rooted at $S$. So the full tree is unique. (2 marks)
- **Case 2: $S \Rightarrow aSb$:** Then $w' = w_1 b w_2$ where $w_1 \in L(G)$ and $w_2 = \epsilon$ (since $S$ must derive the remaining string). The structure forces $w_1 = w''$ such that $S \Rightarrow^* w_1$ and then the terminal $b$ matches. By inductive hypothesis, the parse tree for $w_1$ is unique. (2 marks)

**Uniqueness of the first symbol's production:**
The key insight: the first symbol of any non-empty $w \in L(G)$ is $a$, and the number of `a`'s in $w$ uniquely determines the structure. Specifically, if $w$ has $k$ `a`'s and $\ell$ `b`'s (with $k \geq \ell$), then the parse tree must use exactly $\ell$ applications of $S \rightarrow aSb$ and $(k - \ell)$ applications of $S \rightarrow aS$, in a uniquely determined interleaving. (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** Since every string has a unique parse tree, $G$ is **unambiguous**. $\blacksquare$ (Bonus point for clear writing)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Consider the grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid a$$
**(i)** Show that the grammar is ambiguous by giving two parse trees for $a + a * a$. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Construct an unambiguous grammar for the same language. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a proof sketch why the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is NOT inherently ambiguous, but $\{a^n b^n\} \cup \{b^n c^n\}$ might be. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a)(i) [4 marks]**

The grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid a$ is ambiguous.

**Two distinct parse trees for $w = a + a * a$:**

**Tree 1 (`*` binds tighter):**

```
        E
       /|\
      / | \
     E  +  E
     |    /|\
     a   E * E
         |   |
         a   a
```

**Tree 2 (`+` binds tighter):**

```
        E
       /|\
      / | \
     E  *  E
    /|\     |
   E + E    a
   |   |
   a   a
```

Two distinct parse trees exist $\Rightarrow$ grammar is **ambiguous**. (4 marks)

---

**(a)(ii) [3 marks]**

**Unambiguous equivalent grammar:**

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$

$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$

$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid a$$

This enforces `*` to bind tighter than `+`, giving a unique parse tree for every valid expression. (3 marks)

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Part 1: $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is NOT inherently ambiguous.**

A CFG for $L_1$ can be constructed as:
$$S \rightarrow aSbc \mid abc$$

For this grammar, every string $a^n b^n c^n$ has a **unique** parse tree (the recursion must apply exactly $n-1$ times the first production and once the second, in a fixed order). Therefore $L_1$ has an unambiguous grammar, so it is **not inherently ambiguous**. (3 marks)

**Part 2: $L_2 = \{a^n b^n\} \cup \{b^n c^n\}$ is inherently ambiguous.**

The strings in the **overlap region** $b^n$ (with no $a$'s and no $c$'s) ŌĆö wait, let me reconsider. The overlap of $L_2$ is actually empty in standard form. The correct inherently ambiguous example is $L = \{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^m b^n c^n\}$.

**Proof Sketch (for the corrected $L$):**

- In the **overlap region** where $m = n$, the strings $a^n b^n c^n$ lie in both components.
- Any CFG generating $L$ must somehow "decide" whether to parse an $a^n b^n c^n$ string via the first production set (for $a^n b^n c^m$ with $m = n$) or the second set (for $a^m b^n c^n$ with $m = n$).
- By the **Parikh Property** or **Ogden's Lemma** combined with a pumping argument, one can show that for **every** grammar $G$ generating $L$, there exists some string in the overlap that admits two distinct parse trees.
- Therefore, no unambiguous grammar exists for $L$, making it **inherently ambiguous**. (4 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often claim any grammar with multiple productions for the same non-terminal is ambiguous. This is **WRONG**. Multiple productions are normal; ambiguity requires **multiple parse trees for the same string**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **left-recursive** with **ambiguous**. Left recursion can exist in unambiguous grammars (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to verify that the candidate string actually belongs to $L(G)$ before constructing parse trees. Always check $w \in L(G)$ first.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing two trees that differ only in the visual placement of subtrees (e.g., left vs. right child of the same parent) but are structurally identical. The trees must have **different hierarchical decomposition** of non-terminals.
> 5. **Mark Deduction:** In 14-mark questions, failing to provide a **rigorous proof** (not just an example) typically costs 3-4 marks. Always state the claim, construct counterexamples/proofs explicitly, and conclude clearly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Ambiguous Grammar:** $\exists\, w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ distinct parse trees (equivalently, LMDs or RMDs).
- **Unambiguous Grammar:** $\forall\, w \in L(G)$, exactly 1 parse tree exists.
- **Inherently Ambiguous CFL:** No unambiguous grammar exists for the language.
- **Decidability:** The ambiguity problem for CFGs is **undecidable** (Cantor, 1962).
- **Classic Ambiguous Grammar:** $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid a$ (no precedence).
- **Classic Unambiguous Fix:** Introduce precedence levels $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow a$.
- **Parse Tree Equivalence:** Two distinct LMDs $\Leftrightarrow$ two distinct RMDs $\Leftrightarrow$ two distinct parse trees.
- **String Membership First:** Always verify $w \in L(G)$ before claiming ambiguity via $w$.
- **Grammar vs. Language:** Ambiguity is a property of the **grammar**; inherent ambiguity is a property of the **language**.
- **Hierarchy:** Regular ŌŖŖ DCFL ŌŖŖ Unambiguous CFL ŌŖŖ CFL, and Inherently Ambiguous CFLs ŌŖé CFL (disjoint from Unambiguous CFLs).
- **Overlap Trap:** Languages like $\{a^n b^n c^m\} \cup \{a^m b^n c^n\}$ are inherently ambiguous due to the overlap $a^n b^n c^n$.
- **KTU Favorite:** The "dangling else" problem and arithmetic expression grammars are the most frequently tested examples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplification of Context-Free Grammars - Eliminating Useless Symbols and Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simplification of Context-Free Grammars - Eliminating Useless Symbols and Productions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:
- $V$ is a finite set of **non-terminals** (variables)
- $T$ is a finite set of **terminals** (alphabet)
- $P$ is a finite set of **productions** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$
- $S \in V$ is the **start symbol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition ŌĆö Useful Symbol:**
> A symbol $X \in (V \cup T)$ is called **useful** if and only if there exists at least one derivation of the form:
> $$S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta \Rightarrow^* w \text{ where } w \in T^*$$
> That is, $X$ must be both **reachable** from $S$ AND must be able to **derive a terminal string**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition ŌĆö Useless Symbol:**
> A symbol $X$ is **useless** if it is **NOT useful**. In other words, $X$ fails to satisfy *at least one* of the following two conditions:
> 1. **Reachability Condition:** $X$ cannot be reached from the start symbol $S$ (i.e., no derivation $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta$).
> 2. **Generative Condition:** $X$ cannot derive any terminal string (i.e., no derivation $X \Rightarrow^* w$ for $w \in T^*$).

A **useless production** is any production that contains at least one useless symbol (either in the left-hand side or right-hand side).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "Editing a School Magazine"**
>
> Imagine you are the editor of a school magazine. After all articles are submitted, you discover that:
> - Some articles (productions) reference authors (non-terminals) who never actually wrote anything (non-generating).
> - Some authors (non-terminals) are listed in the masthead but their articles are never cited anywhere in the magazine (unreachable).
>
> These authors and their articles are **"useless"** to the final magazine. A good editor would remove them to keep the magazine clean, efficient, and meaningful.
>
> Similarly, in CFG simplification, we **identify and remove** symbols and productions that contribute **nothing** to the language generated by the grammar. The simplified grammar generates the **exact same language** but is more elegant and easier to analyze.

## 1.3 Why Simplification Matters (Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU 2024 Scheme emphasize this topic?**
>
> 1. **Compiler Design:** Real-world grammars in compilers (e.g., YACC/Bison specifications) often contain redundant rules. Simplification reduces the parser's memory footprint.
> 2. **Decidability Proofs:** Many theorems in TOC (e.g., the pumping lemma for CFLs) assume grammars in simplified form.
> 3. **Equivalence Testing:** Two grammars are equivalent only if they generate the same language. Removing useless symbols makes equivalence easier to verify.
> 4. **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) and Greibach Normal Form (GNF):** Both require the grammar to be free of useless symbols as a prerequisite step.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Categories of Useless Symbols

A symbol becomes useless due to **exactly one of two reasons**, and we must eliminate them using a **two-step algorithm**:

| Category | Question to Ask | Algorithm Name |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Non-Generating Symbols** | Can this symbol eventually derive a string of only terminals? | *Eliminate Non-Generating Symbols* |
| **Unreachable Symbols** | Can this symbol be reached from $S$ by following productions? | *Eliminate Unreachable Symbols* |

The order matters! We **must first eliminate non-generating symbols**, and *then* eliminate unreachable symbols. Reversing the order may leave some useless symbols behind (a classic KTU exam trap!).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Two-Step Approach

A useful symbol must satisfy **both** conditions simultaneously:

$$\text{Useful}(X) \iff \text{Generating}(X) \land \text{Reachable}(X)$$

This is a **logical AND** (conjunction). For $X$ to be useless, it must fail at least one condition. Therefore, we need two separate algorithms ŌĆö one for each condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Rule ŌĆö Order of Elimination:**
> 1. **STEP 1:** Eliminate **non-generating** symbols (and productions containing them).
> 2. **STEP 2:** Eliminate **unreachable** symbols (and productions containing them).
> 3. **NEVER** reverse this order. If you eliminate unreachable symbols first, a symbol that was previously reachable may become non-generating after that step, and you'll miss it.

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 ŌĆö Finding Non-Generating Symbols

A non-terminal $A$ is **generating** if $A \Rightarrow^* w$ for some $w \in T^*$.

**Procedure (Iterative):**
1. **Initialize:** Mark all terminals in $T$ as generating.
2. **Iterate:** For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$:
   - If **every symbol** in $\alpha$ is already marked as generating, then mark $A$ as generating.
3. **Repeat Step 2** until no new symbols can be marked.
4. Any non-terminal that is **not marked** is a **non-generating symbol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of this as a "virus spread" in reverse ŌĆö terminals are "infected" with the ability to produce output, and this ability spreads backwards through productions. If a non-terminal's body is fully "infected," the non-terminal itself becomes generating.

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 ŌĆö Finding Reachable Symbols

A symbol $X$ is **reachable** if $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta$ for some $\alpha, \beta$.

**Procedure (Iterative):**
1. **Initialize:** Mark $S$ (start symbol) as reachable.
2. **Iterate:** For each marked non-terminal $A$ and each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$:
   - Mark **every symbol** in $\alpha$ as reachable.
3. **Repeat Step 2** until no new symbols can be marked.
4. Any symbol that is **not marked** is an **unreachable symbol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** This is a "forward propagation" ŌĆö we start at the root of the derivation tree ($S$) and follow every production, marking all symbols we encounter. Any symbol not reached is disconnected from the rest of the grammar.

## 2.4 The Final Simplification Procedure

| Step | Action | Outcome |
|------|--------|---------|
| **1** | Identify all generating symbols using Algorithm 1 | Set $\text{Gen} \subseteq V$ |
| **2** | Remove all non-generating symbols from $V$ and all productions that contain or derive from them | New $G_1 = (V_1, T, P_1, S)$ |
| **3** | Identify all reachable symbols in $G_1$ using Algorithm 2 | Set $\text{Reach} \subseteq V_1 \cup T$ |
| **4** | Remove all unreachable symbols and all productions that contain them | New $G_2 = (V_2, T_2, P_2, S)$ |
| **5** | Verify $L(G_2) = L(G)$ | Equivalent simplified grammar |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Boundary / Conditions |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Useful Symbol | $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta \Rightarrow^* w, \ w \in T^*$ | $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$ |
| 2 | Generating Symbol | $X \Rightarrow^* w$ for some $w \in T^*$ | Applies to non-terminals only |
| 3 | Reachable Symbol | $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta$ | $S$ is the start symbol |
| 4 | Useless Symbol | $\neg(\text{Generating} \land \text{Reachable})$ | Must fail at least one condition |
| 5 | Language Preservation | $L(G_{\text{simplified}}) = L(G_{\text{original}})$ | The grammar changes; language does NOT |
| 6 | Generating set fixpoint | $\text{Gen}_0 = T, \ \text{Gen}_{i+1} = \text{Gen}_i \cup \{A \mid A \rightarrow \alpha, \ \alpha \in \text{Gen}_i^*\}$ | Iterate until $\text{Gen}_{i+1} = \text{Gen}_i$ |
| 7 | Reachable set fixpoint | $\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}, \ \text{Reach}_{i+1} = \text{Reach}_i \cup \{X \mid A \rightarrow \alpha X \beta, \ A \in \text{Reach}_i\}$ | Iterate until $\text{Reach}_{i+1} = \text{Reach}_i$ |
| 8 | CFG Definition | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V \cap T = \emptyset$ |
| 9 | Order of Elimination | Non-generating $\rightarrow$ Unreachable | **NEVER reverse this order** |
| 10 | Time Complexity | $O(\vert P \vert \cdot \vert V \cup T \vert)$ per iteration | At most $\vert V \cup T \vert$ iterations |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> - **Compiler Construction:** Tools like YACC generate millions of productions. Useless symbols increase parse-table size quadratically.
> - **Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure grammars use CFGs; simplification reduces computational load.
> - **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Probabilistic CFGs (PCFGs) used in speech recognition benefit from size reduction.
> - **Formal Verification:** Model checkers use CFG-derived automata; smaller grammars = faster verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example (14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Consider the CFG $G$ with the following productions:
>
> $S \rightarrow aA \mid B \mid CA$
> $A \rightarrow aB \mid b \mid aAa$
> $B \rightarrow bB \mid b$
> $C \rightarrow bCb \mid a$
> $D \rightarrow EA$
> $E \rightarrow aC$
>
> Where $V = \{S, A, B, C, D, E\}$ and $T = \{a, b\}$. Simplify the grammar by removing all useless symbols and productions.

### STEP 1: Find All Generating Symbols

We use Algorithm 1 (bottom-up iterative marking).

| Iteration | Newly Marked as Generating | Reason |
|-----------|----------------------------|--------|
| **Initial (i = 0)** | $a, b$ | All terminals are trivially generating |
| **i = 1** | $B$ | Production $B \rightarrow b$ has body $b \in T$, all generating. |
| **i = 2** | $C$ | Production $C \rightarrow a$ has body $a \in T$, all generating. |
| **i = 3** | $A$ | Production $A \rightarrow b$ has body $b$, all generating. (Also $A \rightarrow aB$ works since $B$ and $a$ are both generating.) |
| **i = 4** | $S$ | Production $S \rightarrow B$ has body $B$, all generating. |
| **i = 5** | (none) | $D$ and $E$ cannot be marked. |
| **i = 6** | (none) | **Fixed point reached.** |

**Generating set:** $\text{Gen} = \{a, b, A, B, C, S\}$

> [!WARNING]
> **$D$ and $E$ are non-generating.** Look closely:
> - $D \rightarrow EA$: For $D$ to be generating, $E$ must be generating. But $E$ requires $C$ which is generating ŌĆö actually, $E \rightarrow aC$ where $a, C$ are generating, so $E$ **should** be generating!
> - Wait, let me recompute: $E \rightarrow aC$, and $a \in T$, $C \in \text{Gen}$ ŌåÆ so $E$ is generating.
> - $D \rightarrow EA$: $E, A$ are both generating ŌåÆ so $D$ is generating.
> - Let me redo this carefully below.

### STEP 1 (Corrected): Find All Generating Symbols

| Iteration | Newly Marked | Justification (Production used) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------------------|
| **Init** | $\{a, b\}$ | Terminals are generating by definition |
| **Pass 1** | $\{C\}$ | $C \rightarrow a$, body $a \in T$ Ō£ō |
| **Pass 2** | $\{B, E\}$ | $B \rightarrow b$ Ō£ō; $E \rightarrow aC$, body $\{a, C\}$ all generating Ō£ō |
| **Pass 3** | $\{A, D\}$ | $A \rightarrow b$ Ō£ō (or $A \rightarrow aB$); $D \rightarrow EA$, body $\{E, A\}$ all generating Ō£ō |
| **Pass 4** | $\{S\}$ | $S \rightarrow B$ Ō£ō (body $B$ is generating) |
| **Pass 5** | (none) | **Fixed point reached** |

$$\text{Gen} = \{S, A, B, C, D, E, a, b\}$$

In this particular example, **all non-terminals are generating**. So Step 2 is a no-op!

### STEP 2: Remove Non-Generating Symbols and Their Productions

Since $\text{Gen} = V \cup T$, **no symbols are removed**. Grammar $G_1 = G$.

### STEP 3: Find All Reachable Symbols (in $G_1$)

We use Algorithm 2 (forward propagation from $S$).

| Iteration | Symbols Marked as Reachable | Productions Examined |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Init** | $\{S\}$ | Start symbol |
| **Pass 1** | $\{S, a, A, B, C\}$ | $S \rightarrow aA$ ŌåÆ adds $a, A$; $S \rightarrow B$ ŌåÆ adds $B$; $S \rightarrow CA$ ŌåÆ adds $C, A$ |
| **Pass 2** | $\{S, a, A, B, C, b, bB, aAa\}$ | From $A$: $A \rightarrow aB$ ŌåÆ $a, B$; $A \rightarrow b$ ŌåÆ $b$; $A \rightarrow aAa$ ŌåÆ $a, A, a$; From $B$: $B \rightarrow bB$ ŌåÆ $b, B$; $B \rightarrow b$ ŌåÆ $b$; From $C$: $C \rightarrow bCb$ ŌåÆ $b, C, b$; $C \rightarrow a$ ŌåÆ $a$ |
| **Pass 3** | (no new symbols) | All productions from reachable non-terminals have been examined |
| **Pass 4** | (no new symbols) | **Fixed point reached** |

$$\text{Reach} = \{S, A, B, C, a, b\}$$

### STEP 4: Remove Unreachable Symbols and Their Productions

- $D$ and $E$ are **not** in $\text{Reach}$.
- **Remove** productions: $D \rightarrow EA$ and $E \rightarrow aC$.
- **Remove** non-terminals $D$ and $E$ from $V$.

### STEP 5: Final Simplified Grammar $G_2$

$$V_2 = \{S, A, B, C\}, \quad T_2 = \{a, b\}$$

**Productions of $G_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \mid B \mid CA \\
A &\rightarrow aB \mid b \mid aAa \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
C &\rightarrow bCb \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

### STEP 6: Verification of Language Preservation

We must verify $L(G_2) = L(G)$.

- In the original grammar, $D$ and $E$ can never appear in any derivation starting from $S$ (they are unreachable). Therefore, removing them does not affect any string derivable from $S$.
- All other productions remain unchanged.
- Hence, $L(G_2) = L(G)$. Ō£ō

## 3.2 Second Worked Example (To Demonstrate Non-Generating Removal)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> $S \rightarrow AB \mid a, \quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a, \quad B \rightarrow bB \mid bC, \quad C \rightarrow c$
> $V = \{S, A, B, C\}$, $T = \{a, b, c\}$. Eliminate all useless symbols.

### STEP 1: Find Generating Symbols

| Iteration | Marked | Reason |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| **Init** | $\{a, b, c\}$ | Terminals |
| **Pass 1** | $\{C\}$ | $C \rightarrow c$ |
| **Pass 2** | $\{A\}$ | $A \rightarrow a$ |
| **Pass 3** | $\{S\}$ | $S \rightarrow a$ |
| **Pass 4** | (none) | $B \rightarrow bB$ requires $B$ (not generating); $B \rightarrow bC$ requires $B$ (not generating). So $B$ is **not generating**. |
| **Pass 5** | (none) | Now $S \rightarrow AB$ requires $B$ (not generating), so $S$ loses this production. But $S$ is still generating via $S \rightarrow a$. |

$$\text{Gen} = \{S, A, C, a, b, c\}$$

### STEP 2: Remove Non-Generating Symbols

Remove $B$ and all productions containing $B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
C &\rightarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

### STEP 3: Find Reachable Symbols (in the Reduced Grammar)

| Iteration | Reachable | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Init** | $\{S\}$ | Start |
| **Pass 1** | $\{S, a\}$ | $S \rightarrow a$ |
| **Pass 2** | (no new) | Only $S$ has productions left; $A$ and $C$ are not reachable. |

$$\text{Reach} = \{S, a\}$$

### STEP 4: Remove Unreachable Symbols

Remove $A$ and $C$ and their productions.

### STEP 5: Final Simplified Grammar

$$
S \rightarrow a
$$

**Verification:** The only string generatable by the original grammar is "$a$" (since $B$ was a dead-end dependency). The simplified grammar confirms this. Ō£ō

> [!WARNING]
> **Lesson Learned:** This example shows that even though $A$ and $C$ were generating, they became unreachable after removing the non-generating symbol $B$. This is exactly **why the order matters** ŌĆö we must remove non-generating symbols FIRST.

## 3.3 Mathematical Proof of Correctness (Outline)

**Theorem:** If $G_2$ is obtained from $G$ by the two-step procedure, then $L(G_2) = L(G)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

*Step 1 (Generating ŌåÆ Language Unchanged):* 
Removing non-generating symbols cannot affect $L(G)$ because any production involving a non-generating symbol can never be used in a complete derivation of a terminal string.

*Step 2 (Reachable ŌåÆ Language Unchanged):*
After Step 1, $G_1$ has the same language as $G$. Removing unreachable symbols from $G_1$ does not change the language because unreachable symbols can never participate in a derivation starting from $S$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram ŌĆö The Complete Simplification Pipeline

```mermaid

    StepA1 --> StepA2[Step 2: Remove Non-Generating Symbols and Productions]

    StepA3 --> StepA4[Step 4: Remove Unreachable Symbols and Productions]
    StepA4 --> StepA5[Step 5: Resulting Simplified Grammar G2]
    StepA5 --> VerifyA{Does LG2 equal LG?}
    VerifyA -->|Yes| SuccessA([Simplified Grammar G2])
    VerifyA -->|No| ErrorA([Error: Re-examine Pipeline])
```
