# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Pattern, 3-Mark Module 3 Question]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a "useless symbol" in a CFG. List the two conditions that a symbol must satisfy to be classified as useful.**

**Model Answer:**

A symbol $X$ in a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is called a **useless symbol** if it does not participate in the derivation of any terminal string in $L(G)$. Equivalently, a useful symbol $X$ must satisfy **both** conditions:

1. **Reachability:** There must exist a derivation $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta$, where $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$. That is, $X$ must be reachable from the start symbol $S$.

2. **Generative:** There must exist a derivation $X \Rightarrow^* w$ for some $w \in T^*$. That is, $X$ must be able to derive a string consisting entirely of terminals.

If either condition fails, the symbol is useless and can be removed from the grammar without changing the language.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Why is it important to eliminate non-generating symbols BEFORE eliminating unreachable symbols? Justify in two lines.**

**Model Answer:**

Eliminating non-generating symbols first ensures that any symbol which cannot derive a terminal string is removed (along with its dependent productions) **before** we trace reachability. If we eliminated unreachable symbols first, a previously reachable symbol may become non-generating in the new reduced grammar, and we would fail to detect and remove it, leaving the grammar with residual useless symbols.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU ESE Module 3 ŌĆö 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Question A ŌĆö Option 1 (14 Marks)

**Q: Consider the following CFG $G$ with productions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \mid BB \\
A &\rightarrow b \mid aC \mid AA \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid bC \\
C &\rightarrow aA \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$
**where $V = \{S, A, B, C\}$ and $T = \{a, b\}$.**
**(a)** Identify all generating symbols. **(b)** Identify all reachable symbols. **(c)** Eliminate all useless symbols and write the final simplified grammar.

#### Part (a) Model Answer ŌĆö Generating Symbols (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Initialize the generating set $\text{Gen}_0 = \{a, b\}$ (all terminals).

**Step 2 (Iterative marking):**

| Pass | Newly Marked | Justification |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| Pass 1 | $\{C\}$ | $C \rightarrow b$, body $b \in T$ Ō£ō |
| Pass 2 | $\{A\}$ | $A \rightarrow b$, body $b \in T$ Ō£ō |
| Pass 3 | $\{B\}$ | $B \rightarrow bC$, body $\{b, C\}$ all generating Ō£ō |
| Pass 4 | $\{S\}$ | $S \rightarrow aA$, body $\{a, A\}$ all generating Ō£ō (also $S \rightarrow BB$ since $B$ is generating) |
| Pass 5 | (none) | **Fixed point reached.** |

$$\text{Gen} = \{S, A, B, C, a, b\}$$

**Conclusion:** All non-terminals are generating. No symbols need to be removed in this step.

**Score: 7/7**

#### Part (b) Model Answer ŌĆö Reachable Symbols (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Initialize $\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$.

**Step 2 (Iterative marking):**

| Pass | Newly Marked | Productions Examined |
|------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pass 1 | $\{a, A, B\}$ | From $S \rightarrow aA$: add $a, A$. From $S \rightarrow BB$: add $B$. |
| Pass 2 | $\{b, C\}$ | From $A \rightarrow b$: add $b$. From $A \rightarrow aC$: add $C$. From $B \rightarrow bB$: add $b$. From $B \rightarrow bC$: add $C$. |
| Pass 3 | (no new) | All productions from $\{S, A, B, C\}$ have been examined. |
| Pass 4 | (no new) | **Fixed point reached.** |

$$\text{Reach} = \{S, A, B, C, a, b\}$$

**Conclusion:** All non-terminals are reachable. No symbols need to be removed in this step.

**Score: 7/7**

#### Part (c) Final Simplified Grammar (Bonus integration):

Since both $\text{Gen} = V$ and $\text{Reach} = V \cup T$, the grammar is already in its simplified form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \mid BB \\
A &\rightarrow b \mid aC \mid AA \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid bC \\
C &\rightarrow aA \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Score: 14/14**

---

### Question B ŌĆö Option 2 (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]

**Q: Consider the CFG $G$ with productions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \mid BC \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \\
C &\rightarrow cC \mid c
\end{aligned}
$$
**where $V = \{S, A, B, C\}$ and $T = \{a, b, c\}$.**
**(a)** Identify and remove all non-generating symbols and their productions. **(b)** From the resulting grammar, identify and remove all unreachable symbols and their productions. **(c)** Write the final simplified grammar and verify $L(G_{\text{final}}) \subseteq L(G_{\text{original}})$.

#### Part (a) Model Answer ŌĆö Non-Generating Elimination (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Initialize $\text{Gen}_0 = \{a, b, c\}$.

**Step 2 (Iterative marking):**

| Pass | Newly Marked | Justification |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| Pass 1 | $\{A, C\}$ | $A \rightarrow a$ (body $a \in T$) Ō£ō; $C \rightarrow c$ (body $c \in T$) Ō£ō |
| Pass 2 | $\{S\}$ | $S \rightarrow aA$ (body $\{a, A\}$, all generating) Ō£ō |
| Pass 3 | (none) | $B \rightarrow bB$ requires $B$ itself (not generating). Hence $B$ is **non-generating**. |
| Pass 4 | (none) | **Fixed point reached.** |

$$\text{Gen} = \{S, A, C, a, b, c\}$$

**Step 3: Remove $B$ and all productions containing it.**

- Remove $S \rightarrow BC$ (contains $B$).
- Remove $B \rightarrow bB$ (the non-terminal $B$ itself is removed).

**Resulting grammar $G_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
C &\rightarrow cC \mid c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Score: 7/7**

#### Part (b) Model Answer ŌĆö Unreachable Elimination (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Initialize $\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$ in $G_1$.

**Step 2 (Iterative marking):**

| Pass | Newly Marked | Productions Examined |
|------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pass 1 | $\{a, A\}$ | $S \rightarrow aA$ adds $a, A$. |
| Pass 2 | (no new) | $A \rightarrow aA$ and $A \rightarrow a$ only add $a$ and $A$ (already marked). $C$ has no reachable parent. |
| Pass 3 | (no new) | **Fixed point reached.** |

$$\text{Reach} = \{S, A, a\}$$

**Step 3: Remove $C$ and all productions of $C$.**

- Remove $C \rightarrow cC$ and $C \rightarrow c$.

**Resulting grammar $G_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Score: 7/7**

#### Part (c) Final Grammar and Language Verification

**Final simplified grammar:**

$$
S \rightarrow aA, \quad A \rightarrow aA \mid a
$$

**Language of the final grammar:** $L(G_2) = \{a^n \mid n \geq 2\}$ (since $S \Rightarrow aA$ and $A$ generates $a^+$).

**Language of the original grammar:**
- From $S \rightarrow aA$: generates $a \cdot a^+ = a^{n}$ for $n \geq 2$.
- From $S \rightarrow BC$: requires $B$ (non-generating) and $C$ (generating), so this branch contributes **nothing** to $L(G)$.
- Hence $L(G) = \{a^n \mid n \geq 2\}$.

**Conclusion:** $L(G_2) = L(G)$, confirming that no language is lost. Ō£ō

**Total Score: 14/14**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Exam Tips from Senior Board Examiners):**
>
> 1. **ŌØī Wrong Order of Elimination:** Eliminating unreachable symbols first (instead of non-generating first). **Penalty: Up to 4 marks.** Always state the order explicitly in your answer.
>
> 2. **ŌØī Forgetting to Remove Productions:** Removing a non-generating non-terminal but leaving its productions in the new grammar. **Penalty: 2 marks per missed production.**
>
> 3. **ŌØī Confusing "Nullable" with "Useless":** A nullable variable (derives $\varepsilon$) is **not necessarily** useless. For example, $A \rightarrow aA \mid \varepsilon$ makes $A$ nullable but **still useful** (it generates $a^*$). Don't conflate these two concepts.
>
> 4. **ŌØī Skipping Iterations:** Stopping the generating/reachable algorithm after one pass without checking the fixed-point condition. **Penalty: 3-4 marks.**
>
> 5. **ŌØī Not Verifying the Language:** Failing to mention that $L(G_{\text{final}}) = L(G_{\text{original}})$. Always include a brief verification line.
>
> 6. **ŌØī Marking Terminals as Unreachable:** Terminals are always reachable if they appear in any production from $S$. Be careful when scanning.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Useful Symbol Definition:** $S \Rightarrow^* \alpha X \beta \Rightarrow^* w$, $w \in T^*$.
> - **Useless Symbol = Non-Generating OR Unreachable** (logical disjunction ŌĆö failing one condition is enough).
> - **Useful Symbol = Generating AND Reachable** (logical conjunction).
> - **Algorithm Order:** Always **first** eliminate non-generating, **then** eliminate unreachable.
> - **Generating Algorithm:** Start with terminals; iteratively mark non-terminals whose RHS is fully generating.
> - **Reachable Algorithm:** Start with $S$; iteratively mark all symbols in the RHS of productions from already-marked non-terminals.
> - **Fixed Point:** Both algorithms iterate until no new symbols are added.
> - **Language Invariance:** $L(G)$ is preserved throughout the simplification process.
> - **$V \cap T = \emptyset$:** A symbol is either a non-terminal or a terminal, never both.
> - **Production Tree Visualization:** Drawing a parse tree helps identify reachable symbols quickly.
> - **Common KTU Exam Grammar:** Be prepared to handle 4-6 non-terminals and 2-3 terminals.
> - **Follow-up Topics:** This is a prerequisite for **CNF**, **GNF**, and the **Pumping Lemma for CFLs** (Module 4/5).
> - **Time Required in Exam:** 14-mark question typically needs 15-20 minutes; practice speed.
> - **Memory Trick:** **"G before R"** (Generating before Reachable) ŌĆö the order of elimination.
> - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Removing a non-terminal like $B$ in $A \rightarrow aB$ without checking if $B$ is generating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eliminating Epsilon Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eliminating Epsilon Productions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)**, a production of the form:

$$A \rightarrow \varepsilon$$

is called an **Epsilon Production** (also called a **Null Production** or **╬╗-production**). Here, $A \in V$ is a non-terminal (variable) and $\varepsilon$ denotes the empty string (string of length zero).

A variable $A \in V$ is called a **Nullable Variable** if:

$$A \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$$

That is, the variable can derive the empty string through a sequence of one or more derivation steps (or zero steps in the trivial case where $A = \varepsilon$, though variables are usually not $\varepsilon$ themselves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A CFG that generates a language containing the empty string $\varepsilon$ is allowed to have $\varepsilon$-productions. However, for simplification, equivalence proofs, and conversion to **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** or **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)**, we MUST first eliminate all $\varepsilon$-productions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef writing a recipe book. Some recipes are like:

- "Boil water" ŌĆö concrete instruction
- "Optional: Add a pinch of salt" ŌĆö this is like a **nullable step**. Whether you add it or not, the final dish is still valid.

In a grammar, $\varepsilon$-productions are those "optional steps". The variable $A$ can either:

1. "Disappear" (produce nothing, $\varepsilon$)
2. Produce some actual content

The process of **eliminating $\varepsilon$-productions** is like taking every recipe that has optional steps and rewriting it as multiple explicit recipes ŌĆö one for each combination of which optional steps are included or skipped. The end result is the same dish, but every instruction is now "concrete" (no invisible steps).

### 1.3 Why Eliminate $\varepsilon$-Productions?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasons for Elimination:**
> 1. **Simplification** ŌĆö Makes the grammar easier to analyze.
> 2. **Prerequisite for Normal Forms** ŌĆö Required before converting to CNF or GNF.
> 3. **Algorithmic Cleanliness** ŌĆö Pumping Lemma proofs and parsing algorithms (CYK) assume no $\varepsilon$-productions.
> 4. **Deterministic Parsing** ŌĆö Easier to construct LL(1) and LR(1) parsers.

### 1.4 Special Case: The Start Symbol

If the start symbol $S$ itself is nullable (i.e., $S \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$), then $\varepsilon \in L(G)$. After eliminating $\varepsilon$-productions, we must handle this carefully:

- If $S$ **does not appear** on the right-hand side of any production, simply add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ back to the new grammar.
- If $S$ **appears** on the right-hand side, introduce a new start symbol $S'$ with productions $S' \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

A Context-Free Grammar is a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

Where:
- $V$ = Finite set of variables (non-terminals)
- $T$ = Finite set of terminals
- $P$ = Finite set of productions
- $S$ = Start symbol ($S \in V$)

An $\varepsilon$-production is any production in $P$ of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

### 2.2 Algorithm: Eliminating $\varepsilon$-Productions

The complete algorithm has **three mandatory steps**:

#### Step 1: Identify All Nullable Variables

A variable $A \in V$ is nullable if $A \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$. The set of nullable variables $N$ is computed iteratively:

1. **Initialize:** $N_0 = \{ A \in V \mid A \rightarrow \varepsilon \in P \}$ (all variables that have an explicit $\varepsilon$-production).
2. **Iterate:** Add any variable $B$ to $N$ if there exists a production $B \rightarrow \alpha$ where every symbol in $\alpha$ is in $N$ (or $\alpha$ is a single variable already in $N$).
3. **Repeat** until no new nullable variables can be added.

Formally, $N$ is the **least fixed point** satisfying:
$$N = \{A \in V \mid \exists A \rightarrow \alpha \in P, \alpha \in (N \cup T)^*, \text{ and every symbol in } \alpha \text{ is in } N\}$$

#### Step 2: Construct New Productions

For each production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ in $P$:
- If $n \geq 2$ and the right side contains one or more nullable symbols, create a new production for **every non-empty subset** of nullable symbols removed.
- If $n = 1$ and $X_1$ is nullable, the production $A \rightarrow X_1$ is **discarded** (because $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ will be added/handled separately).

For each production, the number of new productions is:
$$\text{New productions from } A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n = 2^k - 1$$

where $k$ is the number of nullable symbols on the right side. We subtract 1 because we never produce the all-$\varepsilon$ version (for $n \geq 2$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** If a production is of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ and both $B$ and $C$ are nullable, we add productions $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$, and $A \rightarrow BC$. We do **NOT** add $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ from this production (it will be added explicitly only for the start symbol case).

#### Step 3: Handle the Start Symbol

- If $S \notin N$ (start symbol is not nullable): simply discard $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if it existed.
- If $S \in N$ and $S$ does not appear on the right side of any production: add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to the new grammar.
- If $S \in N$ and $S$ appears on the right side: create a new start symbol $S_0$ with $S_0 \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nullable Variable | $A \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$ | Means A can derive empty string |
| Base Nullable Set | $N_0 = \{A \vert A \rightarrow \varepsilon \in P\}$ | Direct $\varepsilon$-producers |
| Iterative Step | $N_{i+1} = N_i \cup \{A \vert A \rightarrow \alpha, \alpha \in N_i^*\}$ | Add if all RHS in $N_i$ |
| New Productions Count | $2^k - 1$ | $k$ = nullable symbols on RHS ($n \geq 2$) |
| Discard Rule | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ removed for all $A \neq S$ | Re-add only for $S$ if needed |
| Start Symbol Substitution | $S' \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$ | Only if $S$ on RHS of some production |
| Equivalence | $L(G') = L(G) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ or $L(G)$ | Depends on start symbol handling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **compiler design**, $\varepsilon$-productions correspond to optional syntax elements. For example, in many programming languages:
- Optional semicolons
- Optional type annotations
- Optional `return` statements in some functions

Eliminating $\varepsilon$-productions transforms these into explicit alternatives, which is essential for:
- **Parser generators** (YACC, ANTLR, Bison) that need unambiguous, $\varepsilon$-free grammars.
- **Code optimization** ŌĆö easier to generate parse tables.
- **Formal verification** of language properties.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Simple Grammar

**Given Grammar $G$ with productions:**

$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon$$

**Find the equivalent grammar with no $\varepsilon$-productions.**

#### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables**

Direct $\varepsilon$-producers: $S, A, B$ all have $\varepsilon$-productions.

$$N_0 = \{S, A, B\}$$

Checking: No new variables can be added because we have already captured all variables with $\varepsilon$-productions. The fixed point is:

$$N = \{S, A, B\}$$

**Step 2: Generate New Productions**

For $S \rightarrow aSb$:
- Right side is $aSb$, with one nullable symbol ($S$).
- New productions: $S \rightarrow ab$ (removing $S$).
- Total: $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$

For $A \rightarrow aA$:
- Right side is $aA$, with one nullable symbol ($A$).
- New production: $A \rightarrow a$ (removing $A$).
- Total: $A \rightarrow aA \mid a$

For $B \rightarrow bB$:
- Right side is $bB$, with one nullable symbol ($B$).
- New production: $B \rightarrow b$ (removing $B$).
- Total: $B \rightarrow bB \mid b$

**Step 3: Handle Start Symbol**

$S$ is nullable, but $S$ does appear on the right side of $S \rightarrow aSb$. So we must create a new start symbol $S_0$:

$$S_0 \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$$

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$S_0 \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$$
$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid b$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Mutual Nullable Dependencies

**Given Grammar $G$ with productions:**

$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables**

- Direct $\varepsilon$-producers: $N_0 = \{A, B\}$
- Is $S$ nullable? Check $S \rightarrow AB$: $A$ and $B$ are both in $N_0$, so $S$ can derive $AB \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon = \varepsilon$.
- Therefore: $N_1 = \{A, B, S\}$

$$N = \{A, B, S\}$$

**Step 2: Generate New Productions**

For $S \rightarrow AB$ (both $A$ and $B$ nullable, $k=2$):
- $S \rightarrow AB$ (original)
- $S \rightarrow A$ (remove $B$)
- $S \rightarrow B$ (remove $A$)
- Total: $S \rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B$

For $A \rightarrow aA$ ($A$ nullable, $k=1$):
- $A \rightarrow aA$ (original)
- $A \rightarrow a$ (remove $A$)
- Total: $A \rightarrow aA \mid a$

For $B \rightarrow bB$ ($B$ nullable, $k=1$):
- $B \rightarrow bB$ (original)
- $B \rightarrow b$ (remove $B$)
- Total: $B \rightarrow bB \mid b$

**Step 3: Handle Start Symbol**

$S$ is nullable and $S$ appears on the right side of... wait, let me check. $S$ does NOT appear on the right side of any production in the new set ($S$ is only on LHS). So we can simply add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$S \rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B \mid \varepsilon$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid b$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Complex Nullable Chain

**Given Grammar $G$ with productions:**

$$S \rightarrow ABA$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables**

- $N_0 = \{A, B\}$ (both have direct $\varepsilon$-productions)
- Check $S \rightarrow ABA$: $A$ is in $N_0$, so remove one $A$: need to consider $S \rightarrow BA$ or $S \rightarrow AB$. Both $A$ and $B$ are nullable, so $S$ can produce $BB$, $B$, or $\varepsilon$.
- Therefore $S$ is also nullable: $N = \{A, B, S\}$

**Step 2: Generate New Productions**

For $S \rightarrow ABA$ (positions: $A$, $B$, $A$, with $k=3$ nullable symbols):
- All non-empty subsets to remove give $2^3 - 1 = 7$ new productions
- Original: $S \rightarrow ABA$
- Remove $A$ (pos 1): $S \rightarrow BA$
- Remove $B$: $S \rightarrow AA$
- Remove $A$ (pos 3): $S \rightarrow AB$
- Remove $A,B$: $S \rightarrow A$
- Remove $B,A$ (pos 3): $S \rightarrow A$
- Remove $A$ (pos 1), $A$ (pos 3): $S \rightarrow B$
- Remove all three: $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ ŌĆö **DO NOT INCLUDE**
- Total unique: $S \rightarrow ABA \mid BA \mid AA \mid AB \mid A \mid B$

For $A \rightarrow B$ ($B$ nullable, $k=1$):
- $A \rightarrow B$ (original)
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ ŌĆö wait, this becomes $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$, but we want to **eliminate** $\varepsilon$-productions.
- So we add: $A \rightarrow B$
- And **discard** the $\varepsilon$ version.

Hmm, let me reconsider. $A \rightarrow B$ with $B$ nullable gives us $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ which we must NOT include.

For $B \rightarrow b$ ($B$ nullable, $k=1$):
- $B \rightarrow b$ (original, no nullable symbols to remove on RHS)
- No new production.

Wait, the rule is: if RHS has a nullable variable, we generate alternatives by removing them. $B \rightarrow b$ has no nullable symbols on RHS (only terminal $b$), so no new productions.

For $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$: this is the direct $\varepsilon$-production, so we **discard** it (or keep only if $A$ is start).

For $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: same, **discard**.

**Step 3: Handle Start Symbol**

$S$ is nullable. Does $S$ appear on the RHS of any production? Looking at new productions: $S \rightarrow ABA \mid BA \mid AA \mid AB \mid A \mid B$ ŌĆö **S does NOT appear on RHS**. So we simply add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$S \rightarrow ABA \mid BA \mid AA \mid AB \mid A \mid B \mid \varepsilon$$
$$A \rightarrow B$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

### 3.4 Verification Table

| Original Production | Nullable Symbols on RHS | New Productions Generated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S \rightarrow ABA$ | 3 ($A$, $B$, $A$) | $ABA$, $BA$, $AA$, $AB$, $A$, $B$ (6 new) |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | 1 ($B$) | $B$ (1 new, no $\varepsilon$) |
| $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ŌĆö | (Discarded) |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | 0 | $b$ (0 new) |
| $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ŌĆö | (Discarded) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of $\varepsilon$-Elimination Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Input CFG with epsilon productions]) --> step1[Step 1: Initialize Nullable Set N0]
    step1 --> step2[Add all variables with direct epsilon production]
    step2 --> step3[Step 2: Iterative Fixed Point Computation]
    step3 --> step4{Any new nullable variable found?}
    step4 -->|Yes| step5[Add new variable to N]
    step5 --> step4
    step4 -->|No| step6[Nullable Set N is complete]
    step6 --> step7[Step 3: Generate New Productions]
    step7 --> step8[For each production, enumerate subsets of nullable symbols]
    step8 --> step9[Discard any production that becomes epsilon]
    step9 --> step10[Step 4: Handle Start Symbol S]
    step10 --> step11{S nullable AND S on RHS?}
    step11 -->|Yes| step12[Create new start S0 with S0 to S, epsilon]
    step11 -->|No| step13[Add S to epsilon if nullable]
    step12 --> step14
    step13 --> step14[Step 5: Output epsilon-free Grammar]
    step14 --> endA([Equivalent Grammar G-prime])

    classDef inputBox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef decisionBox fill:#ffccdd,stroke:#cc0044,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933,color:#000
    class startA,endA inputBox
    class step1,step2,step3,step6,step7,step8,step9,step10,step12,step13,step14 processBox
    class step4,step11 decisionBox
```

### 4.2 State Diagram: Nullable Variable Discovery

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> DirectEps: N0 = direct epsilon producers
    DirectEps --> CheckProductions: Examine each production A to alpha
    CheckProductions --> AllInN: All symbols of alpha in N
    CheckProductions --> NotAllInN: Some symbols not in N
    AllInN --> AddToN: Add A to N
    AddToN --> CheckProductions: Continue iteration
    NotAllInN --> CheckProductions: Skip this production
    CheckProductions --> FixedPoint: No new additions possible
    FixedPoint --> [*]: Final Nullable Set N
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Handling Start Symbol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{Is Start Symbol S nullable?}
    startQ -->|No| caseA[Simply remove any S to epsilon]
    startQ -->|Yes| subQ{Does S appear on RHS?}
    subQ -->|No| caseB[Add S to epsilon to new grammar]
    subQ -->|Yes| caseC[Create new start S-prime with S-prime to S or epsilon]
    caseA --> endStep([Done])
    caseB --> endStep
    caseC --> endStep

    classDef yesBox fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
    classDef noBox fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000
    classDef processBox fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b

    class endStep processBox
```

### 4.4 Transformation Mapping Diagram

```mermaid

    A[Original Production A to XY] --> B{Both X and Y nullable?}
    B -->|Yes| C1[Keep A to XY]
    B -->|Yes| C2[Add A to X]
    B -->|Yes| C3[Add A to Y]
    B -->|No| D[Keep only original]
    C1 --> E[New Grammar]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D --> E

    classDef decisionBox fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082
```
