3. $\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$
4. $\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$
5. $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, \varepsilon)\}$

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace for Input $w = aabb$

We trace every single ID. The stack grows as $a$'s are read and shrinks as $b$'s are read.

| Step | ID | Transition Used | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(q_0, aabb, Z_0)$ | ŌĆö | **Start ID.** State $q_0$, full input unread, only initial marker on stack. |
| 1 | $(q_0, abb, AZ_0)$ | Rule 1 | Read $a$, pop $Z_0$, push $AZ_0$ (one $A$ above $Z_0$). |
| 2 | $(q_0, bb, AAZ_0)$ | Rule 2 | Read $a$, pop $A$, push $AA$ (now two $A$'s above $Z_0$). |
| 3 | $(q_1, b, AZ_0)$ | Rule 3 | Switch to state $q_1$, read $b$, pop $A$, push $\varepsilon$. |
| 4 | $(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ | Rule 4 | Read $b$, pop $A$, push $\varepsilon$. |
| 5 | $(q_2, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ | Rule 5 | $\varepsilon$-move: pop $Z_0$, push $\varepsilon$. **Stack is now empty.** |

The final ID is $(q_2, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ ŌĆö the stack is empty, so $aabb \in N(M_1)$.

### 3.1.3 Trace for Rejected Input $w = aab$

| Step | ID | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(q_0, aab, Z_0)$ | Start. |
| 1 | $(q_0, ab, AZ_0)$ | Read $a$, push $A$. |
| 2 | $(q_0, b, AAZ_0)$ | Read $a$, push another $A$. |
| 3 | $(q_1, \varepsilon, AZ_0)$ | Read $b$, pop one $A$. |
| 4 | **STUCK** | Next input is empty, stack top is $A$. No rule with input $\varepsilon$ and stack top $A$ exists. |

The PDA halts in configuration $(q_1, \varepsilon, AZ_0)$ with **non-empty stack and non-empty input** ŌĆö the string is rejected.

## 3.2 The Equivalence Construction ŌĆö Empty Stack ŌåÆ Final State

> **Theorem:** If $L = N(M_1)$ for some PDA $M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0)$, then there exists a PDA $M_2$ with $L = L(M_2)$.

### 3.2.1 Construction

Define a new PDA:

$$M_2 = (Q \cup \{q_e, q_f\}, \Sigma, \Gamma \cup \{X_0\}, \delta', q_e, X_0, \{q_f\})$$

The new initial stack symbol $X_0$ is a *fresh* marker ŌĆö it distinguishes $M_2$'s actions from $M_1$'s. The new states $q_e$ (entry) and $q_f$ (final) are added.

The transition function $\delta'$ is defined as:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initialize the original machine's stack:**

$$\delta'(q_e, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_0, Z_0 X_0)\}$$

This moves from entry state $q_e$ to $M_1$'s start state $q_0$, and places $M_1$'s initial symbol $Z_0$ on top of $X_0$ (so $X_0$ is at the bottom ŌĆö a safety marker).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simulate $M_1$ exactly:**

$$\delta'(q, a, X) = \delta(q, a, X) \quad \text{for all } q \in Q,\; a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\},\; X \in \Gamma$$

So $M_2$ mimics $M_1$ on the upper portion of its stack.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Detect empty stack of $M_1$ (signaled by seeing $X_0$ on top):**

For every state $q \in Q$, define:

$$\delta'(q, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}$$

Whenever the top of stack becomes $X_0$, it means $M_1$ has emptied its working stack. We nondeterministically jump to the final state $q_f$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Safety rule (the safety net):**

Some non-deterministic path in $M_1$ may not empty its stack; we add:

$$\delta'(q_f, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}$$

This allows the simulation to *safely terminate* in $q_f$ with an empty stack ŌĆö a no-op in $q_f$.

### 3.2.2 Proof of Correctness ($L(M_2) = N(M_1)$)

We must show two inclusions.

**Part A ŌĆö $N(M_1) \subseteq L(M_2)$:**

Suppose $w \in N(M_1)$. Then $(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash_{M_1}^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ for some $q \in Q$.

In $M_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(q_e, w, X_0) &\vdash_{M_2} (q_0, w, Z_0 X_0) && \text{(by Step 1)} \\
&\vdash_{M_2}^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon X_0) && \text{(by Step 2, identical simulation)} \\
&= (q, \varepsilon, X_0) \\
&\vdash_{M_2} (q_f, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) && \text{(by Step 3, since } X_0 \text{ is on top)} \\
&\vdash_{M_2} (q_f, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) && \text{(by Step 4, no-op)}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $q_f \in F$ (the new final state), $w \in L(M_2)$.

**Part B ŌĆö $L(M_2) \subseteq N(M_1)$:**

Suppose $w \in L(M_2)$. Then $(q_e, w, X_0) \vdash_{M_2}^* (q_f, \varepsilon, \alpha)$ for some $\alpha \in \Gamma^*$.

- The only way to enter $q_f$ is via Step 3 or Step 4.
- Step 4 only fires in $q_f$ itself, so the *first* entry into $q_f$ must be via Step 3.
- Step 3 requires $X_0$ to be on top of the stack.
- Just before entering $q_f$, the ID is $(q, \varepsilon, X_0)$ for some $q \in Q$.
- Stripping the $X_0$ marker, the simulation in Steps 1ŌĆō2 shows that the *only* way to reach $(q, \varepsilon, X_0)$ is to have simulated a path of $M_1$ that emptied its working stack ŌĆö that is, $(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash_{M_1}^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$.
- Therefore $w \in N(M_1)$.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 The Reverse Construction ŌĆö Final State ŌåÆ Empty Stack

> **Theorem:** If $L = L(M_1)$ for some PDA $M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_1, Z_1, F)$, then there exists a PDA $M_2$ with $L = N(M_2)$.

### 3.3.1 Construction

Define:

$$M_2 = (Q \cup \{q_0, q_e\}, \Sigma, \Gamma \cup \{X_0\}, \delta', q_0, X_0, \emptyset)$$

Note: $F = \emptyset$ ŌĆö accepts only by empty stack.

The transitions are:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initialize:**

$$\delta'(q_0, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_1, Z_1 X_0)\}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simulate $M_1$:**

$$\delta'(q, a, X) = \delta(q, a, X)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö On reaching a final state of $M_1$, empty the stack:**

For each $q \in F$, and for each $X \in \Gamma \cup \{X_0\}$:

$$\delta'(q, \varepsilon, X) = \{(q_e, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö $q_e$ is a "stack-emptying" state** that pops anything remaining:

$$\delta'(q_e, \varepsilon, X) = \{(q_e, \varepsilon)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma \cup \{X_0\}$$

### 3.3.2 Why It Works

The safety marker $X_0$ ensures that if $M_1$ enters a final state with non-empty stack, $M_2$ can still finish emptying the stack in $q_e$. Conversely, if the stack is already empty (only $X_0$ remains), the same rule applies. The marker is **never** used in $M_1$'s original alphabet, so it cannot interfere with simulation.

## 3.4 Determinism ŌĆö A Subtle But Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** A PDA is **deterministic (DPDA)** if and only if:
> 1. $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1$ for every $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$, $X \in \Gamma$.
> 2. If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \neq \emptyset$, then $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ for **all** $a \in \Sigma$.

**Critical Fact:** **Deterministic CFLs ŌŖŖ Context-Free Languages.**

- Every regular language has a DPDA that accepts it.
- There exist CFLs (e.g., $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^*\}$, the even-length palindromes) that **no DPDA** can accept ŌĆö only a non-deterministic PDA can.
- Acceptance by **final state** is more natural for DPDA; empty-stack acceptance does not work for DPDA in general (because detecting emptiness requires inspecting the entire stack).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Pushdown Automaton

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete functional architecture of a PDA, with the input tape, finite control, and stack memory clearly delineated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT TAPE\nContains string w over Sigma\nRead head moves LEFT to RIGHT"] --> nodeB
    nodeB["FINITE CONTROL\nCurrent state q in Q\nDriven by transition function delta"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["STACK MEMORY\nUnbounded LIFO storage\nOver alphabet Gamma\nTop of stack on the LEFT"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeA

    nodeD["Q : finite set of states"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeE["Sigma : input alphabet"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeF["Gamma : stack alphabet"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeG["q0 : start state"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeH["Z0 : initial stack symbol"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeI["F : set of final states"] -.-> nodeB

    nodeJ["DELTA TRANSITION\nReads current q, a, X\nPops X, pushes alpha, moves to p"] -.-> nodeB
```

## 4.2 State Diagram for the PDA Accepting $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ by Empty Stack

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : a, Z0 / A Z0
    q0 --> q0 : a, A / A A
    q0 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon
    q1 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon
    q1 --> q2 : epsilon, Z0 / epsilon
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the transitions:** Each label `input, stack-top / stack-replacement` follows the convention:

- First symbol = input symbol being read (or $\varepsilon$).
- Second symbol = current stack top being popped.
- String after the slash = symbols being pushed (leftmost becomes new top).

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow of "Language Acceptance" ŌĆö Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start: input string w on tape"] --> nodeRead["Read first/next symbol a"]
    nodeRead --> nodeCheck1{"Any applicable\ntransition exists?\ndelta contains a move\nfor current q, a, X?"}
    nodeCheck1 -- "NO" --> nodeReject["REJECT w\nString not in language"]
    nodeCheck1 -- "YES" --> nodeApply["Apply transition:\nnew state p\nnew stack top alpha"]
    nodeApply --> nodeCheck2{"More input\nsymbols\nremaining?"}
    nodeCheck2 -- "YES" --> nodeRead
    nodeCheck2 -- "NO" --> nodeCheckMode{"Which acceptance\nmode?"}
    nodeCheckMode -- "FINAL STATE" --> nodeFS{"Current state q\nin F?"}
    nodeCheckMode -- "EMPTY STACK" --> nodeES{"Stack empty?\ngamma is empty?"}
    nodeFS -- "YES" --> nodeAcceptFS["ACCEPT w\nw in L of M"]
    nodeFS -- "NO" --> nodeReject
    nodeES -- "YES" --> nodeAcceptES["ACCEPT w\nw in N of M"]
    nodeES -- "NO" --> nodeReject
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of the Equivalence Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["ORIGINAL PDA M1 (Empty-Stack Acceptance)"]
        M1A["States: Q = q0, q1, ..."] --> M1B["Stack alphabet: Gamma"]
        M1B --> M1C["Transitions: delta of M1"]
        M1C --> M1D["Acceptance: stack becomes epsilon"]
    end

    subgraph M2["CONSTRUCTED PDA M2 (Final-State Acceptance)"]
        M2A["New entry state q_e"] --> M2B["New start stack symbol X0"]
        M2B --> M2C["Simulate M1 with stack top Z0 X0"]
        M2C --> M2D["If top becomes X0: stack of M1 is empty"]
        M2D --> M2E["Jump to new final state q_f"]
        M2E --> M2F["ACCEPT w in L of M2"]
    end

    M1 -. "CONVERSION RULE\ndelta-prime construction" .-> M2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Pushdown Automaton (PDA). When is a string said to be accepted by a PDA by empty stack?**

**Model Answer:**

A Pushdown Automaton (PDA) is a 7-tuple

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

where $Q$ is the finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\Gamma$ is the stack alphabet, $\delta$ is the transition function, $q_0$ is the start state, $Z_0$ is the initial stack symbol, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of final states.

A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is said to be accepted by the PDA $M$ **by empty stack** if starting from the initial configuration, the PDA can reach a configuration where the **entire input has been consumed AND the stack is completely empty**. Formally:

$$w \in N(M) \iff (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) \text{ for some } q \in Q$$

The current state is irrelevant; only the empty stack matters. (3 marks)

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between acceptance of a string by a PDA using final state and using empty stack.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Final-State Acceptance | Empty-Stack Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceptance condition** | PDA halts in a state $q \in F$ | PDA empties its stack completely |
| **Stack status at acceptance** | Stack may be non-empty | Stack must be empty |
| **State status at acceptance** | State must be in $F$ | State can be any $q \in Q$ |
| **Notation** | $L(M)$ | $N(M)$ |
| **Use of $F$ in definition** | Required ($F \neq \emptyset$ typical) | Often $F = \emptyset$ |
| **Use of $Z_0$** | Always present | Often a separate marker $X_0$ used in conversions |

Both modes are equivalent in expressive power for non-deterministic PDAs. (3 marks)

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Construct a PDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ by empty stack. Show the sequence of instantaneous descriptions (IDs) for the input $w = aaabbb$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a PDA that accepts the language $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^+\}$ (even-length palindromes) by final state. Show the transition function and explain each rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**PDA Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{Z_0, A\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \emptyset)$$

**Transitions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad & \delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\} \\
\text{(iii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(iv)} \quad & \delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(v)} \quad & \delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace for $w = aaabbb$:**

| Step | ID | Rule Applied |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(q_0, aaabbb, Z_0)$ | ŌĆö (Start) |
| 1 | $(q_0, aabbb, AZ_0)$ | (i) ŌĆö Push first $A$ |
| 2 | $(q_0, abbb, AAZ_0)$ | (ii) ŌĆö Push second $A$ |
| 3 | $(q_0, bbb, AAAZ_0)$ | (ii) ŌĆö Push third $A$ |
| 4 | $(q_1, bb, AAZ_0)$ | (iii) ŌĆö Switch to $q_1$, pop $A$ |
| 5 | $(q_1, b, AZ_0)$ | (iv) ŌĆö Pop another $A$ |
| 6 | $(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ | (iv) ŌĆö Pop last $A$ |
| 7 | $(q_2, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ | (v) ŌĆö $\varepsilon$-move, empty stack |

Final ID: $(q_2, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ ŌĆö stack empty, so $aaabbb \in N(M)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**PDA Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1\}, \{a, b\}, \{Z_0, A, B\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_1\})$$

The PDA has two phases:
- **Phase 1 (state $q_0$):** Push symbols as they are read.
- **Phase 2 (state $q_1$):** Guess the middle; pop and match.

**Transitions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad & \delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, b, Z_0) = \{(q_0, BZ_0)\} \\
\text{(iii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\} \\
\text{(iv)} \quad & \delta(q_0, a, B) = \{(q_0, AB)\} \\
\text{(v)} \quad & \delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_0, BA)\} \\
\text{(vi)} \quad & \delta(q_0, b, B) = \{(q_0, BB)\} \\
\text{(vii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, \varepsilon, A) = \{(q_1, A)\} \\
\text{(viii)} \quad & \delta(q_0, \varepsilon, B) = \{(q_1, B)\} \\
\text{(ix)} \quad & \delta(q_1, a, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(x)} \quad & \delta(q_1, b, B) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(xi)} \quad & \delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of each rule:**

- Rules (i)ŌĆō(vi): In state $q_0$, push the symbol read onto the stack (using $A$ for $a$ and $B$ for $b$).
- Rules (vii)ŌĆō(viii): Non-deterministically guess that we have reached the middle of the string. Switch to $q_1$ without consuming input or changing the stack.
- Rules (ix)ŌĆō(x): In state $q_1$, read the next input symbol and verify that it matches the stack top (popping on match).
- Rule (xi): When only $Z_0$ remains on the stack, remove it and accept.

**Final-state acceptance:** The PDA accepts when it reaches state $q_1$ and the input is fully consumed. (7 marks)

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** State and prove the equivalence theorem: a language $L$ is accepted by some PDA by final state if and only if $L$ is accepted by some PDA by empty stack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the PDA $M_1$ that accepts $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ by empty stack, construct explicitly a PDA $M_2$ that accepts the same language by final state. List all transitions of $M_2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Theorem Statement:**

> If $L = N(M_1)$ for some PDA $M_1$, then there exists a PDA $M_2$ with $L = L(M_2)$, and vice versa.

**Proof (Empty-Stack ŌåÆ Final-State direction):**

Let $M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0)$ accept $L$ by empty stack. Construct:

$$M_2 = (Q \cup \{q_e, q_f\}, \Sigma, \Gamma \cup \{X_0\}, \delta', q_e, X_0, \{q_f\})$$

**Transitions of $M_2$:**

1. $\delta'(q_e, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_0, Z_0 X_0)\}$ ŌĆö Initialize.
2. $\delta'(q, a, X) = \delta(q, a, X)$ for all $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, $X \in \Gamma$ ŌĆö Simulate $M_1$.
3. $\delta'(q, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}$ for all $q \in Q$ ŌĆö Detect empty stack of $M_1$.
4. $\delta'(q_f, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}$ ŌĆö Safety rule.

**Proof that $L(M_2) = N(M_1)$:**

**($\supseteq$):** Suppose $w \in N(M_1)$. Then $(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash_{M_1}^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$. In $M_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(q_e, w, X_0) &\vdash_{M_2} (q_0, w, Z_0 X_0) \\
&\vdash_{M_2}^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon X_0) \\
&\vdash_{M_2} (q_f, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $q_f \in F$, $w \in L(M_2)$.

**($\subseteq$):** Suppose $w \in L(M_2)$. The only way to reach $q_f \in F$ is via rule (3), which requires $X_0$ on top of the stack. Just before that, the simulation in rule (2) must have mirrored an accepting run of $M_1$. Hence $w \in N(M_1)$.

**Reverse direction** (Final-State ŌåÆ Empty-Stack) is similar with a different construction that uses $F$ to trigger stack emptying. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given $M_1$ from Question A of this set, we apply the construction from part (a):

$$M_2 = (\{q_e, q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\}, \{a, b\}, \{Z_0, A, X_0\}, \delta', q_e, X_0, \{q_f\})$$

**Transitions of $M_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(1)} \quad & \delta'(q_e, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_0, Z_0 X_0)\} \\
\text{(2)} \quad & \delta'(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\} \\
\text{(3)} \quad & \delta'(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\} \\
\text{(4)} \quad & \delta'(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(5)} \quad & \delta'(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(6)} \quad & \delta'(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(7)} \quad & \delta'(q_0, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(8)} \quad & \delta'(q_1, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(9)} \quad & \delta'(q_2, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\} \\
\text{(10)} \quad & \delta'(q_f, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification for $w = aabb$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(q_e, aabb, X_0) &\vdash (q_0, aabb, Z_0 X_0) && \text{(Rule 1)} \\
&\vdash (q_0, abb, AZ_0 X_0) && \text{(Rule 2)} \\
&\vdash (q_0, bb, AAZ_0 X_0) && \text{(Rule 3)} \\
&\vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0 X_0) && \text{(Rule 4)} \\
&\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0 X_0) && \text{(Rule 5)} \\
&\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, X_0) && \text{(Rule 6)} \\
&\vdash (q_f, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) && \text{(Rule 9)}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $q_f \in F$, $aabb \in L(M_2)$. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Stack-top notation reversal:** Students often write the stack as if the *rightmost* symbol is on top. KTU follows the **leftmost-on-top** convention. Mismatched notation can lose up to **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety marker $X_0$:** When converting empty-stack ŌåÆ final-state, many students skip the $X_0$ marker. Without it, the construction cannot distinguish "stack of $M_1$ is empty" from "stack top is $Z_0$" ŌĆö this is a critical **3-mark** mistake.
> 3. **Missing rule (3) for all states:** The detection rule $\delta'(q, \varepsilon, X_0) = \{(q_f, \varepsilon)\}$ must be defined for **every** $q \in Q$, not just one. A common error is to add it for one state only.
> 4. **Determinism confusion:** When asked for a DPDA, students frequently add non-deterministic $\varepsilon$-moves. KTU deducts marks for failing to satisfy the determinism condition $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1$.
> 5. **Confusing $L(M)$ with $N(M)$:** In a 14-mark conversion problem, writing $L(M_2) = N(M_1)$ instead of $L(M_2) = N(M_1)$ (when proving equivalence) is a subtle but penalized error. Always state the **target** acceptance mode explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PDA Tuple:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ ŌĆö memorize the order; it appears in every definition.
- **Two Languages per Machine:** $L(M)$ (final state) and $N(M)$ (empty stack). Always clarify *which* is being asked.
- **Move Notation:** $\vdash$ for one step, $\vdash^*$ for zero-or-more steps. The start ID is always $(q_0, w, Z_0)$.
- **Stack Convention:** Leftmost symbol is on top. Push prepends, pop removes from the left.
- **$\varepsilon$-Moves:** Allow the PDA to operate on the stack without consuming input ŌĆö essential for the equivalence construction.
- **Equivalence Theorem:** A language has a final-state-accepting PDA **iff** it has an empty-stack-accepting PDA. The proof uses a fresh marker $X_0$ as a "tripwire" to detect when the original machine's stack is empty.
- **Conversion Recipes:**
  - *Empty-Stack ŌåÆ Final-State:* Add entry state, add final state, add $X_0$ marker, simulate original, jump to final when $X_0$ is on top.
  - *Final-State ŌåÆ Empty-Stack:* Add entry state, add empty-stack state $q_e$, simulate original, on entering $F$ transition to $q_e$ which empties stack.
- **DPDA Caveat:** Deterministic PDAs are **strictly less powerful** than non-deterministic PDAs ŌĆö they cannot accept all CFLs (e.g., even-length palindromes).
- **Trace Discipline:** Always show the ID for **every** step of input consumption. KTU examiners award partial marks for correct intermediate IDs even if the final answer is missed.
- **Distinguishing Property:** A language is context-free **iff** it is accepted by some PDA (in either acceptance mode). This is the bridge between CFLs and PDAs ŌĆö the second fundamental result of this module.

# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) ŌĆö Refresher

A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a finite automaton augmented with an auxiliary **stack** (an unbounded, LIFO ŌĆö Last-In First-Out ŌĆö memory). The stack provides the PDA with the ability to recognize **context-free languages (CFLs)**, which regular languages and DFAs/NFAs cannot fully capture.

Mathematically, a PDA is a **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

Where each component has a precise role (we will expand this in SECTION 2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆö Module 3):**
> PDA is the machine counterpart of **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)**. The transition from CFG to PDA (and vice versa) is a central KTU learning outcome. This module specifically focuses on the **deterministic vs non-deterministic** versions of PDA.

---

## 1.2 Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (DPDA):**
> A PDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ is **deterministic** if for every configuration reachable from the start configuration, **at most one** transition is possible. Formally, for every $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, and $X \in \Gamma$:
> - $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1$ **(single-symbol input case)**, AND
> - If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \neq \emptyset$, then for all $a \in \Sigma$, $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ **(no $\varepsilon$-move and input-move conflict)**.

A DPDA **accepts a language by final state** (not by empty stack ŌĆö see SECTION 2 for why).

### ­¤¬£ Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The One-Way Escalator ­¤¬£

Imagine a tourist in a metro station with an **escalator (the stack)** that only moves **one direction (up = push)** and a **single turnstile gate (the input tape)**. At every step, the tourist has **exactly one choice**: either step on the escalator with a particular ticket, or step off. There is **no fork in the road**. The escalator can only be used in a strictly determined way. This rigidity is the **determinism** of the DPDA.

---

## 1.3 Non-Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NPDA):**
> An NPDA relaxes the determinism constraint. For some states and stack symbols, **multiple** transitions may be available for the same input symbol, AND $\varepsilon$-transitions (moves without consuming input) are freely allowed. Formally, a PDA that does **not** satisfy the DPDA constraints is an NPDA.

NPDA is the **canonical acceptor for all CFLs** ŌĆö every CFG can be converted to an equivalent NPDA, but **not every CFL can be recognized by a DPDA**.

### ­¤¬£ Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Maze with Multiple Forks ­¤¬£

Now imagine the same tourist station, but the tourist is given a **map of an entire metro network** with branching tunnels. At every junction, the tourist may choose between 2 or 3 paths ŌĆö some requiring a "token" from the pocket, some not. The tourist's overall success is judged by whether **at least one path** leads to the destination. This is **non-determinism** ŌĆö exploring multiple computational branches in parallel.

---

## 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations

| Aspect | DPDA | NPDA |
|---|---|---|
| Power | Recognizes a proper subset of CFLs | Recognizes **all** CFLs |
| Acceptance mode | **By final state** only | Both final-state and empty-stack are equivalent |
| $\varepsilon$-moves | Restricted (no conflict with input moves) | Fully allowed |
| Real-world modeling | Parsers of programming languages | Theoretical CFLs (palindromes, $a^nb^nc^n$, etc.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Takeaway:** The class of languages recognized by DPDAs is called **DCFL (Deterministic Context-Free Languages)**. The strict containment is:
> **Regular $\subset$ DCFL $\subset$ CFL**
> This hierarchy is a **favorite 2-mark question** in KTU boards.

# ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Component Breakdown of a PDA (Recap Before DPDA/NPDA)

The 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$:

| Symbol | Name | Role | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of states | Control unit of the machine | Finite set |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | Symbols read from the tape | Finite set |
| $\Gamma$ | Stack alphabet | Symbols that can be pushed/popped on the stack | Finite set |
| $\delta$ | Transition function | Defines how the machine behaves | Function |
| $q_0$ | Start state | The state in which computation begins | $q_0 \in Q$ |
| $Z_0$ | Initial stack symbol | Bottom-of-stack marker present at start | $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ |
| $F$ | Set of final/accepting states | If reached, the input is accepted | $F \subseteq Q$ |

The transition function $\delta$ has the general form:

$$\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$$

In words: Given a state, an input symbol (or $\varepsilon$), and the current top-of-stack symbol, the machine can move to a new state and replace the stack top with **a string** of stack symbols (possibly empty, meaning a pop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the codomain uses $\mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}$ (finite power set). This is the **source of non-determinism** ŌĆö multiple possible outcomes for a single configuration.

---

## 2.2 Determinism vs Non-Determinism ŌĆö The Decisive Constraints

### 2.2.1 DPDA Constraints (Restating Formally)

A PDA $M$ is **deterministic** if for all $q \in Q$, $X \in \Gamma$:

1. **Single-output constraint:** $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert + \vert\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)\vert \leq 1$ for every $a \in \Sigma$.
2. **No-clash constraint:** If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is defined, then $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ for every $a \in \Sigma$.

**Interpretation:** At any moment, the DPDA has **at most one legal move**, eliminating all branching.

### 2.2.2 NPDA Relaxation

An NPDA simply **drops these two constraints**. The transition function may return a set with **multiple elements**, and $\varepsilon$-moves may coexist with symbol-moves for the same $(q, X)$ pair.

---

## 2.3 Acceptance Criteria ŌĆö A Critical Distinction

For DPDAs and NPDAs, the **way** a string is accepted matters.

### 2.3.1 Acceptance by Final State

$$L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash_{M}^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), \ q \in F, \ \gamma \in \Gamma^{*}\}$$

The string is accepted if, after reading the entire input, the machine is in a final state (the stack contents are **irrelevant**).

### 2.3.2 Acceptance by Empty Stack

$$N(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash_{M}^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$$

The string is accepted if, after reading the entire input, the **stack is completely empty** (the state is irrelevant).

### 2.3.3 Equivalence and Why DPDA Differs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For NPDA:** Acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack define the **same class of languages** (CFLs). One can be converted to the other.
>
> **For DPDA:** Acceptance by final state defines **DCFL**, but acceptance by empty stack defines a **strictly smaller** class. Therefore, a DPDA's language is always defined by **final state acceptance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry?** In a DPDA, the empty-stack condition can lead to *unwanted* acceptance. For example, the language $\emptyset$ (empty language) cannot be accepted by a DPDA with empty-stack acceptance because any DPDA will always empty its stack on some input ŌĆö but a DPDA with final-state acceptance trivially accepts $\emptyset$.

---

## 2.4 The CFL Recognition Hierarchy

The central result of this module is the **strict containment** between language classes:

$$\text{Regular Languages} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$$

| Property | Regular | DCFL | CFL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recognized by | DFA / NFA | DPDA | NPDA (or CFG) |
| Closed under complement | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No |
| Closed under intersection | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| Closed under union | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Ambiguous grammar? | N/A | Has unambiguous CFG | May be inherently ambiguous |
| Example language | $(a+b)^{*}$ | $L = \{a^nb^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $\{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The language $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ (even-length palindromes) is the classic witness** that DCFL is a proper subset of CFL. It is a CFL but **not** a DCFL ŌĆö it cannot be accepted by any DPDA because the machine would need to "guess" where the middle of the palindrome lies.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PDA formal definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | 7 components must be listed verbatim |
| 2 | Transition function signature | $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to \mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$ | Distinguishes PDA from FA |
| 3 | DPDA constraint 1 | $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1 \ \forall q, a, X$ | Determinism on input moves |
| 4 | DPDA constraint 2 | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ | No $\varepsilon$-symbol conflict |
| 5 | Final-state acceptance | $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), q \in F\}$ | Used for both DPDA and NPDA |
| 6 | Empty-stack acceptance | $N(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$ | Only used for NPDA (DCFL defined via final state) |
| 7 | Language hierarchy | $\text{Reg} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | Core theorem of the module |
| 8 | CFG $\leftrightarrow$ NPDA equivalence | Every CFL = $L(M)$ for some NPDA | Construction is examinable |
| 9 | Example DCFL | $L = \{a^nb^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | Most common KTU example |
| 10 | Example non-DCFL CFL | $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$ | Witness for DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL |
| 11 | DPDA complement closure | $\overline{\text{DCFL}} = \text{DCFL}$ | Key contrast with CFL |
| 12 | CFL complement closure | $\overline{\text{CFL}} \neq \text{CFL}$ in general | Key contrast with DCFL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Insight:** The 7-tuple definition, the DPDA constraints, and the strict containment chain $\text{Reg} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ together account for **70% of marks** in this module.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | **DPDA** models **deterministic parsers** (LL(1), LR(0), SLR, LALR, CLR) used in tools like YACC/Bison. |
| **XML/JSON Parsing** | **DPDA** is used to validate well-formed structured documents. |
| **Programming Language Syntax** | Most programming language grammars are designed to be **DCFLs** so they can be parsed deterministically. |
| **Theoretical Computer Science** | **NPDA** proves existence of CFLs and supports the **Chomsky Hierarchy**. |
| **Model Checking** | Pushdown systems model recursive programs and **call stacks**. |

# ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ is NOT a DCFL

This is the most-tested KTU argument in this module. We will derive it from the **pumping-lemma-like reasoning** using the intrinsic need for "guessing the middle."

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume, for contradiction**, that $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ is a DCFL. Then there exists a DPDA $M$ that accepts $L$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the role of the stack.** Since the input has the form $ww^R$, the DPDA must push each symbol of the first half $w$ onto the stack so that, when reading $w^R$, it can pop and match.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize the guessing problem.** Consider the input:
$$a^n b a^{2n} b a^n \quad (\text{where } w = a^nb, w^R = b a^{2n} b a^n \text{ has middle ambiguity})$$

A simpler witness: take the input $a^{n} b a^{n}$. The DPDA must:
- Push $a$ for the first $n$ symbols.
- Recognize the middle marker (the central $b$).
- Pop and match for the last $a^n$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö The DPDA's stack is deterministic but bounded in "view depth."** While reading the $w$ portion, the DPDA has a **fixed state** and the **stack depth = position in $w$**. When it sees the first symbol of $w^R$, it must **commit** to popping. However, in $L = \{ww^R\}$, the input $ab$ and $aa$ followed by $ba$ have structures that are indistinguishable to a DPDA reading the prefix.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Construct the adversarial input.** Pick $w = a^nb$ so the full palindrome is $a^n b b a^n$. A DPDA reading $a^nb$ pushes $a$s and then sees $b$ ŌĆö but it cannot know whether the next $b$ is the **start of $w^R$** or a continuation of $w$ (since $w$ may itself begin with $b$).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Contradiction.** A deterministic machine cannot "look ahead" past the input pointer. For some $n$ large enough, the configurations on two distinct valid prefixes (e.g., $a^nb$ vs $a^nb$ from different $w$'s) collide, making the next required move ambiguous. This violates determinism.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Conclusion.** $\therefore$ $L = \{ww^R\}$ is a CFL (recognizable by an NPDA that "guesses" the middle) but **not** a DCFL. Hence, $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ is **strict**.

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, DPDA is strictly weaker than NPDA in language-recognition power.}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing DPDA and NPDA Transition Functions

Below is the analytical matrix that distinguishes the two models across every input configuration.

| Configuration $(q, a, X)$ | DPDA behavior | NPDA behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ is a regular input symbol, no $\varepsilon$-move exists | Exactly **one** move: $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, \alpha)\}$ | **Zero or more** moves allowed |
| Both $\delta(q, a, X)$ and $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ could be defined | **Impossible** ŌĆö violates DPDA constraint 2 | Both can be defined freely |
| $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ | Halts (rejects by default) | May still proceed via $\varepsilon$-moves |
| $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ | At most one, and **only** if no $a$-move exists for the same $(q, X)$ | Can be multiple, can coexist with $a$-moves |
| Stack operation on transition | Replaces top $X$ with $\alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$ | Same syntax, but result set may have $> 1$ element |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Constructing a DPDA for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

This is a **standard 7-to-14-mark KTU question**. Let us derive the DPDA step by step.

**Goal:** Accept exactly those strings consisting of $n$ $a$'s followed by $n$ $b$'s, where $n \geq 1$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify states needed:**
- $q_0$: initial state, reads $a$'s.
- $q_1$: state when reading $b$'s.
- $q_2$: accepting state.
- $q_{\text{reject}}$: dead state (optional).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify stack symbols:** $\Gamma = \{Z_0, A\}$ where $Z_0$ is the bottom marker and $A$ tracks an $a$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the transition function $\delta$:**

For each $a$ read in $q_0$, push an $A$:

$$\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\}$$
$$\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Transition from reading $a$'s to reading $b$'s:** When the first $b$ appears, move to $q_1$ without changing the stack:

$$\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Pop one $A$ for each subsequent $b$:**

$$\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö When the stack is reduced to $Z_0$ and the input is exhausted, accept:**

$$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Full formal DPDA definition:**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \\
\Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\
\Gamma &= \{Z_0, A\} \\
F &= \{q_2\} \\
Z_0 &\text{ is the start stack symbol.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify on sample inputs:**

| Input | Trace | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $ab$ | $(q_0, ab, Z_0) \vdash (q_0, b, AZ_0) \vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ | Ō£ģ Accepted |
| $aabb$ | $(q_0, aabb, Z_0) \vdash (q_0, abb, AZ_0) \vdash (q_0, bb, AAZ_0) \vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0) \vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ | Ō£ģ Accepted |
| $aab$ | $(q_0, aab, Z_0) \vdash (q_0, ab, AZ_0) \vdash (q_0, b, AAZ_0) \vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, AZ_0)$ | ŌØī Halted with non-empty stack, not in $F$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Initial config has no $\varepsilon$-moves from $q_0$ on $Z_0$ | ŌØī Rejected |

**Step 9 ŌĆö Determinism check:**

For every $(q, X)$ pair, we verify:
- $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\}$ ŌĆö one move.
- $\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\}$ ŌĆö one move.
- $\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ ŌĆö one move.
- $\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ ŌĆö one move.
- $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ ŌĆö one move.

**No conflict between $\varepsilon$ and $a$-moves exists for any $(q, X)$ pair.** Therefore, $M$ is indeed a DPDA. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Constructing an NPDA for $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$

This language **cannot** be done with a DPDA. Let us build the NPDA.

**Step 1 ŌĆö States needed:** $q_0$ (push phase), $q_1$ (guess middle), $q_2$ (pop/match phase), $q_f$ (accept).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Stack symbols:** $\Gamma = \{Z_0, a, b\}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Push phase (in $q_0$):** Push the current input symbol onto the stack:

$$\delta(q_0, a, X) = \{(q_0, aX)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma$$
$$\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_0, bX)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Non-deterministic guess of the middle (in $q_0$, via $\varepsilon$-move):** The NPDA guesses that the current position is the middle, transitioning to $q_1$:

$$\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, X) = \{(q_1, X)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Pop/match phase (in $q_1$):** Match the current input with the stack top:

$$\delta(q_1, a, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$
$$\delta(q_1, b, b) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final accept (in $q_1$, via $\varepsilon$-move):** When input is empty and only $Z_0$ remains, accept:

$$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_f, Z_0)\}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Identify non-determinism:** In state $q_0$ with input symbol $X$ on stack top, the NPDA has **two possible moves**:
- The input-consuming move (continuing to push).
- The $\varepsilon$-move (guessing this is the middle).

This is the **only** way to recognize $ww^R$ ŌĆö the machine must **guess** where the middle lies. A DPDA cannot do this.

$$\boxed{\text{An NPDA recognizes } ww^R, \text{ but no DPDA can.}}$$

# ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Pushdown Automaton

Below is a high-level Mermaid block diagram showing the structural components of a PDA ŌĆö applicable to both DPDA and NPDA. The differences lie in the **transition controller logic** (highlighted in 4.2).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputTape["Input Tape"]
        T1["a1"]
        T2["a2"]
        T3["a3"]
        Tdots["..."]
        Tn["an"]
    end

    inputTape --> readHead

    subgraph fsm["Finite Control Unit"]
        readHead["Read Head"]
        stateReg["Current State q in Q"]
        transCtrl["Transition Controller delta"]
    end

    subgraph stackMem["Stack Memory LIFO"]
        stTop["Top of Stack X"]
        stBelow["Symbol Below"]
        stDeeper["..."]
        stBottom["Z0 Bottom Marker"]
    end

    stateReg --> transCtrl
    readHead --> transCtrl
    stTop --> transCtrl

    transCtrl -- "Push alpha" --> stTop
    transCtrl -- "Pop X" --> stBelow
    transCtrl -- "Update state" --> stateReg
    transCtrl -- "Advance / Epsilon" --> readHead

    transCtrl --> decisionOut{"Accept?"}
    decisionOut -- "q in F and input done" --> acceptState["ACCEPTED"]
    decisionOut -- "halted or no move" --> rejectState["REJECTED"]

    style fsm fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style stackMem fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style acceptState fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style rejectState fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **Transition Controller $\delta$** is the brain. In a **DPDA**, this block chooses **at most one** outgoing arrow at any time. In an **NPDA**, multiple outgoing arrows may be active, and the machine conceptually explores them in parallel.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree: DPDA vs NPDA Computational Paths

This Mermaid diagram illustrates how the two machines handle branching differently. Consider a configuration where two transitions are possible.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startCfg["Configuration: q, a, X"]

    startCfg --> checkDet{"Is delta deterministic?"}

    checkDet -- "YES: at most one transition exists" --> dpdaPath["DPDA Path"]
    checkDet -- "NO: multiple transitions possible" --> npdaPath["NPDA Path"]

    dpdaPath --> dpdaUnique["Single next configuration: q prime, alpha"]
    dpdaUnique --> dpdaContinue["Continue deterministically until halt"]
    dpdaContinue --> dpdaResult{"Accept by final state?"}
    dpdaResult -- "Yes" --> dpdaAccept["ACCEPTED"]
    dpdaResult -- "No" --> dpdaReject["REJECTED"]

    npdaPath --> npdaBranch1["Branch 1: q1, alpha1"]
    npdaPath --> npdaBranch2["Branch 2: q2, alpha2"]
    npdaPath --> npdaBranch3["Branch k: qk, alphak"]

    npdaBranch1 --> npdaCheck1{"All input read?"}
    npdaBranch2 --> npdaCheck2{"All input read?"}
    npdaBranch3 --> npdaCheckK{"All input read?"}

    npdaCheck1 -- "Yes, q1 in F" --> npdaAcceptAny["ACCEPTED if ANY branch accepts"]
    npdaCheck1 -- "No" --> npdaCont1["Continue exploring Branch 1"]
    npdaCont1 --> npdaDead1["Dead end: branch dies"]

    npdaCheck2 -- "Yes, q2 in F" --> npdaAcceptAny
    npdaCheck2 -- "No" --> npdaCont2["Continue exploring Branch 2"]
    npdaCont2 --> npdaDead2["Dead end: branch dies"]

    npdaCheckK -- "Yes, qk in F" --> npdaAcceptAny
    npdaCheckK -- "No" --> npdaContK["Continue exploring Branch k"]
    npdaContK --> npdaDeadK["Dead end: branch dies"]

    npdaAcceptAny --> npdaFinal["ACCEPTED"]
    npdaDead1 --> npdaFinalCheck{"Any branch alive?"}
    npdaDead2 --> npdaFinalCheck
    npdaDeadK --> npdaFinalCheck
    npdaFinalCheck -- "No" --> npdaFinalReject["REJECTED"]

    style dpdaPath fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style npdaPath fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style npdaAcceptAny fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style dpdaAccept fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style dpdaReject fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#cc0000
    style npdaFinalReject fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.3 State Transition Flow: A Worked DPDA Trace for $a^2b^2$

This diagram visualizes the step-by-step trace of the DPDA from SECTION 3.3 on input $aabb$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0

    q0: q0 - Reading a's
    q1: q1 - Reading b's
    q2: q2 - ACCEPT

    q0 --> q0 : read a, push A
    q0 --> q1 : read b, pop A
    q1 --> q1 : read b, pop A
    q1 --> q2 : epsilon move on Z0
    q2 --> [*] : input accepted

    note right of q0
        Stack grows: Z0 then AZ0 then AAZ0
    end note

    note right of q1
        Stack shrinks: AAZ0 then AZ0 then Z0
    end note
```
