## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: DPDA vs NPDA Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph dpda["DPDA Deterministic"]
        dpdaIn["Input"] --> dpdaCtrl["Transition Controller single-valued"]
        dpdaCtrl --> dpdaStack["Stack: pushes and pops only one path"]
        dpdaCtrl --> dpdaState["State: exactly one next state"]
        dpdaState --> dpdaAcc["Accept: by final state only"]
        dpdaStack --> dpdaAcc
    end

    subgraph npda["NPDA Non-Deterministic"]
        npdaIn["Input"] --> npdaCtrl["Transition Controller multi-valued + epsilon"]
        npdaCtrl --> npdaBranch["Computation Tree: multiple paths"]
        npdaBranch --> npdaStack1["Path 1 Stack"]
        npdaBranch --> npdaStack2["Path 2 Stack"]
        npdaBranch --> npdaStackK["Path k Stack"]
        npdaStack1 --> npdaCheck{"Any path in F with input exhausted?"}
        npdaStack2 --> npdaCheck
        npdaStackK --> npdaCheck
        npdaCheck -- "YES" --> npdaAcc["ACCEPTED"]
        npdaCheck -- "NO" --> npdaRej["REJECTED"]
    end

    style dpda fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style npda fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA). State the two conditions that must be satisfied for a PDA to be deterministic.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A DPDA is a PDA in which, for every reachable configuration, **at most one transition** is possible. Formally, a PDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ is deterministic if for every $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$, and $X \in \Gamma$:

**Condition 1 (Single-valued):** $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1$.

**Condition 2 (No $\varepsilon$-symbol conflict):** If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is defined, then $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ for all $a \in \Sigma$.

These ensure the machine has no choice at any step of computation.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between DPDA and NPDA with respect to acceptance criteria.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | DPDA | NPDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Final-state acceptance** | Defines DCFL | Defines CFL |
| **Empty-stack acceptance** | Defines a **smaller** class (not equivalent) | **Equivalent** to final-state acceptance |

For NPDA, a language is accepted by final state if and only if it is accepted by empty stack. For DPDA, this equivalence **fails** ŌĆö empty-stack acceptance for DPDA is strictly weaker. Therefore, the **language of a DPDA is always defined by final-state acceptance** to capture the full DCFL class.

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either full Question A or full Question B** (internal choice between two 14-mark questions on the same module).

---

### ­¤ģ░’ĖÅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct a DPDA for the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Show the formal definition, all transitions, and verify determinism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the language $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ is a CFL but not a DCFL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö DPDA Construction for $a^nb^n$** (7 marks):

**Step 1: Formal definition.**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{Z_0, A\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

**Step 2: Transition function $\delta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) &= \{(q_0, AZ_0)\} \\
\delta(q_0, a, A) &= \{(q_0, AA)\} \\
\delta(q_0, b, A) &= \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1, b, A) &= \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) &= \{(q_2, Z_0)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Trace for input $aabb$ (verification).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, aabb, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, abb, AZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0, bb, AAZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \\
&\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0) \quad \text{Ō£ģ Accepted}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Determinism verification.**

For each $(q, X)$:
- $(q_0, Z_0)$: only $a$-move defined. No conflict.
- $(q_0, A)$: only $a$-move defined. No conflict.
- $(q_1, A)$: only $b$-move defined. No conflict.
- $(q_1, Z_0)$: only $\varepsilon$-move defined. No conflict.

$\therefore$ $M$ is deterministic. **(7/7 marks)**

**Part (b) ŌĆö $L = \{ww^R\}$ is CFL but not DCFL** (7 marks):

**Proof that $L$ is a CFL:** Construct a context-free grammar $G$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\to aSa \mid bSb \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar generates exactly the even-length palindromes, so $L = L(G)$, proving $L$ is a CFL. By the CFG-to-NPDA construction, an NPDA for $L$ also exists. **(3 marks)**

**Proof that $L$ is not a DCFL (by contradiction):** Assume $L$ is a DCFL. Then there exists a DPDA $M$ that accepts $L$. Consider the infinite family of strings:

$$w_n = a^n b b a^n \in L \quad \text{for all } n \geq 1$$

After reading the prefix $a^nb$, the DPDA is in some state $q$ with $b$ on top of the stack. Now it must commit to **either**:
- **Option 1:** Treating this $b$ as the **middle** marker (start of pop phase).
- **Option 2:** Treating this $b$ as part of $w$ (i.e., $w = a^nb\ldots$), which would require continuing to push.

For different values of $n$, these prefixes are processed by the DPDA, but the state $q$ on top of stack $b$ must be the **same** for infinitely many $n$ (by the pigeonhole principle, since $Q$ is finite). The DPDA cannot distinguish them deterministically.

Specifically, the string $a^nb$ is a prefix of both $a^nbba^n$ (palindrome) and $a^nbba^nba^nbba^n$ (palindrome), but the remaining inputs differ in structure. A DPDA in a single state $q$ with $b$ on stack cannot deterministically commit to "I am now in the second half" ŌĆö yet it must, to recognize $L$ correctly.

$\therefore$ No DPDA exists for $L$. Hence $L \notin \text{DCFL}$, but $L \in \text{CFL}$.

This establishes $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$. **(4/7 marks)**

---

### ­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a PDA formally. Explain acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct an NPDA for the language $L = \{wcw^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ where $c$ is a special middle marker. Trace the computation for the input $abbcbba$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Formal PDA Definition and Acceptance** (7 marks):

A **PDA** is a 7-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

where each component is as defined in SECTION 2.1.

**Acceptance by Final State:** $M$ reads the entire input $w$ and halts in a state $q \in F$ (the stack may be non-empty).

$$L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), \ q \in F, \ \gamma \in \Gamma^{*}\}$$

**Example:** For $L = \{a^nb^n \mid n \geq 1\}$, a PDA with states $\{q_0, q_1, q_f\}$ accepts $aabb$ by reaching $q_f$ (the final state) after popping the stack down to $Z_0$.

**Acceptance by Empty Stack:** $M$ reads the entire input $w$ and empties its stack (the state is irrelevant).

$$N(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Example:** The same PDA, modified to accept when the stack becomes $\varepsilon$ (after popping the last $A$), would accept $aabb$ by empty stack.

**Key Difference:** For NPDA, the two acceptance modes are **equivalent** (one can be converted to the other). For DPDA, they are **not** equivalent ŌĆö final-state acceptance is the standard. **(7/7 marks)**

**Part (b) ŌĆö NPDA for $L = \{wcw^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$** (7 marks):

**Note:** Unlike $ww^R$, the presence of the **explicit middle marker $c$** removes the need to "guess" the middle. This language **is** a DCFL and can be accepted by a DPDA. We will build both.

**Formal Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_f\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{Z_0, a, b\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_f\})$$

**Transitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a, X) &= \{(q_0, aX)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_0, b, X) &= \{(q_0, bX)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_0, c, X) &= \{(q_1, X)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_1, a, a) &= \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1, b, b) &= \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) &= \{(q_f, Z_0)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for input $abbcbba$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, abbcbba, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, bbcbba, aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0, bcbba, baZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_0, cbba, bbaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, bba, bbaZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, ba, baZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, a, aZ_0) \\
&\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \\
&\vdash (q_f, \varepsilon, Z_0) \quad \text{Ō£ģ Accepted}
\end{aligned}
$$

**DPDA upgrade note:** Because the middle marker $c$ is **explicit**, there is no non-deterministic guess required. Replacing the $\varepsilon$-move to $q_1$ with the $c$-move directly makes this a DPDA. This illustrates that **explicit delimiters convert CFLs into DCFLs**. **(7/7 marks)**

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DPDA/NPDA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial stack symbol $Z_0$.** A DPDA/PDA that starts with an empty stack is **invalid**. The bottom marker $Z_0$ is mandatory and must appear in the initial configuration $(q_0, w, Z_0)$.
>
> 2. **Conflating DPDA with DFA.** Students often forget that a PDA has a **stack**, and treat the problem as a DFA question. Always include $\Gamma$ and stack operations in the transition function.
>
> 3. **Wrong acceptance mode for DPDA.** Using empty-stack acceptance for a DPDA loses 2-3 marks. **DPDA accepts by final state** in the KTU standard convention.
>
> 4. **Writing the transition function with the wrong arity.** The signature must be $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to \mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$. Writing $Q \times \Sigma \times \Gamma$ alone (omitting $\varepsilon$) loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the determinism verification step.** Even after constructing a "deterministic-looking" PDA, students must **explicitly verify** that the two DPDA constraints are satisfied. This is worth 1-2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing CFL and DCFL closure properties.** The complement of a CFL is **not necessarily a CFL**, but the complement of a DCFL **is** always a DCFL. This is a common trick question.
>
> 7. **Not stating the strict containment chain.** Writing $\text{DCFL} \subseteq \text{CFL}$ (subset) instead of $\subsetneq$ (proper subset) loses a mark. The strictness is the **point** of the theorem.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ A **PDA** is a finite automaton + unbounded stack. It recognizes **CFLs**.
- Ō£ģ A **DPDA** is a PDA where at most one transition is possible per configuration. The two constraints are: (i) $\vert\delta(q, a, X)\vert \leq 1$, and (ii) no $\varepsilon$-move and $a$-move conflict on the same $(q, X)$.
- Ō£ģ An **NPDA** relaxes these constraints. $\varepsilon$-moves and multiple transitions per step are allowed.
- Ō£ģ The **language hierarchy** is strict: $\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$.
- Ō£ģ DPDA accepts by **final state only**; NPDA accepts equivalently by final state or empty stack.
- Ō£ģ The classic **DCFL example** is $a^nb^n$; the classic **non-DCFL CFL** is $ww^R$.
- Ō£ģ **DCFL is closed under complement**; **CFL is not**.
- Ō£ģ **DCFL is not closed under union or intersection**; CFL is closed under union but not intersection.
- Ō£ģ Every **CFG can be converted to an NPDA** (the construction is examinable).
- Ō£ģ Real-world applications: **DPDA = parsers of programming languages** (LL, LR families); **NPDA = theoretical CFL recognition**.
- Ō£ģ The 7-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ must be **listed in full** for full marks in KTU answers.
- Ō£ģ The **stack alphabet $\Gamma$** is distinct from the **input alphabet $\Sigma$** ŌĆö never mix them.
- Ō£ģ The **transition function** outputs a string of stack symbols, not just a single symbol ŌĆö the **push operation** can push multiple symbols at once.
- Ō£ģ Remember: a **string is accepted if and only if ALL input is consumed** AND (final-state) the machine is in $F$ OR (empty-stack) the stack is $\varepsilon$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing PDAs for CFLs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing PDAs for CFLs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)

A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a formal model of computation that extends the finite automaton with an auxiliary memory called a **stack**. It is precisely the right tool to recognize Context-Free Languages (CFLs) that finite automata cannot.

Formally, a PDA **M** is defined as a **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$

Where the components are:

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of **states** |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite set of **input symbols** (input alphabet) |
| $\Gamma$ | Finite set of **stack symbols** (stack alphabet) |
| $\delta$ | **Transition function**: $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$ |
| $q_0$ | The **start state** ($q_0 \in Q$) |
| $Z_0$ | The **initial stack symbol** ($Z_0 \in \Gamma$) |
| $F$ | Set of **final/accepting states** ($F \subseteq Q$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A transition is written as $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p_1, \alpha_1), (p_2, \alpha_2), \ldots\}$ meaning: *being in state $q$, reading input symbol $a$, with $X$ on top of the stack, we may move to state $p_i$ and replace $X$ by string $\alpha_i$ on the stack.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Stack as a "Plate Dispenser"

Imagine a **blindfolded waiter** in a cafeteria trying to verify that customers return plates in a particular pattern:

- The **waiter** is the **PDA's control unit (state)**.
- The **spring-loaded plate dispenser** is the **stack** ŌĆö it only lets the waiter feel the *topmost* plate (LIFO: Last In, First Out).
- The **customer's order** is the **input tape**.
- The waiter can only **push** a new plate onto the stack (remembering something) or **pop** the top plate (forgetting/verifying it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The stack is the *only* memory the PDA has beyond its current state. This single restriction is exactly what makes PDAs powerful enough to recognize context-free languages (e.g., balanced brackets) but not context-sensitive ones (e.g., $a^n b^n c^n$).

## 1.3 Why Design PDAs?

The class of languages accepted by PDAs is exactly the class of **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)**. Therefore, *designing* a PDA is equivalent to *constructing a recognizer* for a given CFL ŌĆö an essential skill in compilers (parsing), XML/HTML validation, and grammar analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Strategy for Designing PDAs

Designing a PDA for any CFL $L$ follows a systematic **5-Phase Engineering Protocol**:

### Phase 1: Analyze the Language Structure
- Identify the **pattern** (e.g., count-matching, palindrome-like, balanced nesting).
- Determine if the pattern requires **stacking intermediate symbols**.

### Phase 2: Choose the Stack Discipline
- **PUSH** to remember a symbol encountered on the left side of the input.
- **POP** to verify a symbol encountered on the right side matches what was pushed.
- **IGNORE / NO-OP** to skip irrelevant portions of the input.

### Phase 3: Partition the Input Phases
Split the input reading into distinct **phases**, each handled in its own state:
- *Phase 1*: Push symbols (e.g., scan all $a$'s).
- *Phase 2*: Pop and verify (e.g., scan all $b$'s).
- *Phase 3*: Accept if stack reduces to the start symbol.

### Phase 4: Define State Transitions
Use the **transition function** $\delta$ to encode each step. A common transition format is:
- $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, YZ)\}$ ŌåÆ push $Y$ on top, replacing $X$ with $YZ$ (or just push $Y$ if $X$ is replaced).

### Phase 5: Handle Boundary Conditions
- Ensure the PDA handles $n = 0$ correctly (empty string acceptance if $\varepsilon \in L$).
- Ensure the **final stack is empty** or the **final state is accepting**, depending on acceptance mode.

## 2.2 The Two Acceptance Modes of a PDA

| Mode | Definition | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceptance by Final State** | Input is fully consumed AND PDA halts in a state $q \in F$. Stack contents irrelevant. | $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), q \in F\}$ |
| **Acceptance by Empty Stack** | Input is fully consumed AND the stack is empty (regardless of state). | $N(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Theorem (Equivalence):** A language $L$ is a CFL if and only if there exists a PDA $M$ such that $L = L(M)$ (final-state) **iff** there exists a PDA $N$ such that $L = N(N)$ (empty-stack). These two models are equally powerful.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Transition Notation Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | KTU Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, \alpha)\}$ | On input $a$, top of stack $X$, go to $p$ and push string $\alpha$ (replacing $X$) | Single deterministic move |
| $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \{(p, \alpha)\}$ | **Epsilon move** ŌĆö consume no input | Used for stack-only operations |
| $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, X)\}$ | **No-op** on stack ŌĆö read input, state change only | Used for counting/skipping |
| $\alpha = \varepsilon$ | **Pop** ŌĆö top of stack is removed | Used for matching/verification |
| $\alpha = X$ | **Stack unchanged** | Used to "peek" |
| $\alpha = YX$ | **Push Y on top of X** | LIFO behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The exact same design protocol is used in building **recursive-descent parsers** and **LL(k) parsers** in compiler construction. The stack simulates the parser's call stack for matching nested grammar rules.

## 2.4 Common Design Idioms in KTU Problems

| Language Pattern | Push Symbol | Pop Symbol | States Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $a$ | $a$ | 2 (push-state, pop-state) |
| $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ | input char | matched char | 3 (push, guess-mid, pop) |
| $L = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $a$ | $bb$ | 2 |
| $L = \{w \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w)\}$ | $a$ or $b$ | $b$ or $a$ | 2 |
| Balanced Parentheses | $($ or $a$ | $)$ or $a$ | 1 (loop) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked PDA Designs)

## 3.1 Example 1: $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Language description:** One or more $a$'s followed by an equal number of $b$'s.

**Design Intuition:** We don't know in advance how many $a$'s there are, so we must **push one $a$ for every $a$ read**, then **pop one $a$ for every $b$ read**. If the stack empties exactly when the input ends, the count matched.

### Step 1: Define the 7-tuple

$$M_1 = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ ŌĆö three states (push, pop, accept).
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ ŌĆö input alphabet.
- $\Gamma = \{a, Z_0\}$ ŌĆö stack alphabet ($Z_0$ is the bottom marker).
- Start state: $q_0$.
- Start stack symbol: $Z_0$.
- Final state: $\{q_2\}$.

### Step 2: Write all transitions of $\delta$

**Phase 1 (Pushing $a$'s in $q_0$):**

$$\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, aZ_0)\}$$

$$\delta(q_0, a, a) = \{(q_0, aa)\}$$

**Transition to pop phase (guess the midpoint):**

$$\delta(q_0, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Phase 2 (Popping for $b$'s in $q_1$):**

$$\delta(q_1, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Acceptance: stack is $Z_0$, all input consumed:**

$$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$$

### Step 3: Trace the string $a^3 b^3$ = `aaabbb`

| Step | State | Remaining Input | Stack | Transition Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0$ | `aaabbb` | $Z_0$ | ŌĆö |
| 1 | $q_0$ | `aabbb` | $aZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0)$ |
| 2 | $q_0$ | `abbb` | $aaZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a, a)$ |
| 3 | $q_0$ | `bbb` | $aaaZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a, a)$ |
| 4 | $q_1$ | `bb` | $aaZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, b, a)$ |
| 5 | $q_1$ | `b` | $aZ_0$ | $\delta(q_1, b, a)$ |
| 6 | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $\delta(q_1, b, a)$ |
| 7 | $q_2$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ ŌĆö **ACCEPT** |

> **Accepted! Ō£ģ** Final state is $q_2 \in F$.

---

## 3.2 Example 2: $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^+\}$ (Even-Length Palindromes)

**Language description:** A non-empty even-length palindrome over $\{a, b\}$. The PDA must nondeterministically **guess the middle** of the string and then verify symmetry.

### Step 1: 7-tuple

$$M_2 = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

### Step 2: Transitions

**Phase 1 ŌĆö Push every symbol read (state $q_0$):**

$$\delta(q_0, a, X) = \{(q_0, aX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\}$$

$$\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_0, bX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\}$$

**Nondeterministic guess of the midpoint (switch to $q_1$):**

$$\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, X) = \{(q_1, X)\} \quad \text{for all } X \in \{a, b, Z_0\}$$

**Phase 2 ŌĆö Pop and verify the reverse (state $q_1$):**

$$\delta(q_1, a, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

$$\delta(q_1, b, b) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Accept when stack returns to $Z_0$ and input is consumed:**

$$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why nondeterminism is essential here:** The PDA cannot know *where* the middle of $w$ is. It must *guess* via an $\varepsilon$-move. This is precisely why CFLs require nondeterministic PDAs (NPDA), whereas regular languages can use deterministic FAs.

### Step 3: Trace the string `abba`

| Step | State | Input Left | Stack | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0$ | `abba` | $Z_0$ | Start |
| 1 | $q_0$ | `bba` | $aZ_0$ | Push $a$ |
| 2 | $q_0$ | `ba` | $baZ_0$ | Push $b$ |
| 3 | $q_0$ | `a` | $bbaZ_0$ | Push $b$ (or $\varepsilon$-move to $q_1$) |
| 4 | $q_1$ | `a` | $bbaZ_0$ | $\varepsilon$-move: guess mid after position 2 |
| 5 | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $baZ_0$ | Pop $a$ matches input $a$ |
| 6 | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | Pop $b$ ŌĆö input empty but stack empty too |
| 7 | $q_2$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | **ACCEPT** |

---

## 3.3 Example 3: $L = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Design Insight:** For every $a$ we push, we must pop *two* $b$'s. So the popping transition replaces one stack $a$ with $\varepsilon$ on a single $b$ read ŌĆö but we need to pop **two** $b$'s per $a$. The trick: split the pop into two states or use a more elaborate stack alphabet.

### Cleaner Solution: Use Stack Alphabet $\Gamma = \{a, b, Z_0\}$

**Phase 1 ŌĆö Push $a$'s in $q_0$:**

$$\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, aZ_0)\}$$

$$\delta(q_0, a, a) = \{(q_0, aa)\}$$

**Switch to pop phase:**

$$\delta(q_0, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$$

**Phase 2 ŌĆö Pop two $b$'s per $a$:**

In $q_1$, the first $b$ is consumed, but the stack is already empty (we just popped). So we need an **intermediate state** $q_1$ that consumes a $b$ without changing the stack, then state $q_2$ pops one $a$ for the *second* $b$:

$$\delta(q_1, b, a) = \{(q_2, \varepsilon)\} \quad \text{(second } b \text{ pops an } a\text{)}$$

**Loop back:**

$$\delta(q_2, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \quad \text{(one } a \text{ popped, go read the next pair)}$$

**Accept:**

$$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_3, Z_0)\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification trace for $a^2 b^4$ = `aabbbb`:** After reading `aa` ŌåÆ stack `aaZ_0` ŌåÆ read `b` ŌåÆ pop, now in $q_1$ with stack `aZ_0` ŌåÆ read `b` ŌåÆ pop $a$, now in $q_2$ with stack `Z_0$` ŌåÆ read `b` ŌåÆ must pop again but stack is $Z_0$ ŌĆö **REJECT** ŌØī.
>
> *Correction:* the trace requires two $b$'s between popping $a$'s. State $q_1$ expects a $b$ (first of pair), $q_2$ pops an $a$ (second of pair), then loops back to $q_1$. This pattern works for any $n \geq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: The PDA Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TAPE[INPUT TAPE]
        T1[Symbol a sub 1]
        T2[Symbol a sub 2]
        T3[Symbol b sub 1]
        T4[Dots]
        T5[Symbol b sub n]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL UNIT - Q]
        ST1((State q0))
        ST2((State q1))
        ST3((State q2 - Final))
    end

    subgraph STK[STACK MEMORY - Gamma]
        TOP[Top of Stack Symbol]
        MID[Middle Elements]
        BOT[Bottom Marker Z0]
    end

    TAPE -->|Reads Symbol| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Push X / Pop X / Replace| STK
    STK -->|Top Symbol X| CTRL
    CTRL -.->|Accept / Reject| OUT((Output Decision))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The control unit reads one input symbol at a time, consults the current state, looks at the top of the stack, and decides a transition. The stack is the *only* unbounded memory.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram for PDA accepting $a^n b^n$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 push phase
    q1: q1 pop phase
    q2: q2 final accept

    q0 --> q0: a, Z0 push aZ0
    q0 --> q0: a, a push aa
    q0 --> q1: b, a pop epsilon
    q1 --> q1: b, a pop epsilon
    q1 --> q2: epsilon, Z0 accept
    q2 --> [*]
```

> **Legend:** `input_symbol, top_of_stack / stack_replacement`. Epsilon means "no input consumed."

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram for PDA accepting $ww^R$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 push w
    q1: q1 pop reverse of w
    q2: q2 accept

    q0 --> q0: a, X push aX
    q0 --> q0: b, X push bX
    q0 --> q1: epsilon, X guess midpoint
    q1 --> q1: a, a pop epsilon
    q1 --> q1: b, b pop epsilon
    q1 --> q2: epsilon, Z0 accept
    q2 --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow of PDA Execution (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start in q0 with Stack Z0] --> B{Read next input symbol}
    B -->|Symbol a| C{Top of stack X}
    C -->|X is Z0 or a| D[Push a then X onto stack]
    D --> B
    C -->|X is b| E[REJECT - wrong order]
    B -->|Symbol b| F{Top of stack X}
    F -->|X is a| G[Pop X - matched]
    G --> B
    F -->|X is Z0| H[All a's matched, go to final state]
    H --> I[Input fully consumed ACCEPT]
    B -->|No input epsilon| J{Stack top is Z0}
    J -->|Yes| K[ACCEPT in final state]
    J -->|No| L[REJECT - unmatched symbols remain]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Pushdown Automaton (PDA). List its components.**

**Model Answer:**
A PDA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where:
- **$Q$**: finite set of states.
- **$\Sigma$**: finite input alphabet.
- **$\Gamma$**: finite stack alphabet.
- **$\delta$**: transition function $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$.
- **$q_0 \in Q$**: start state.
- **$Z_0 \in \Gamma$**: initial stack symbol.
- **$F \subseteq Q$**: set of final/accepting states.

It extends finite automata with a **stack** to recognize context-free languages. (3 marks)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack in a PDA.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Final State | Empty Stack |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptance Condition | Input fully consumed AND halting state $q \in F$ | Input fully consumed AND stack is empty |
| Final state | Required | Not required |
| Stack contents | Irrelevant | Must be empty |
| Notation | $L(M)$ | $N(M)$ |

Both are **equivalent in power** ŌĆö a language is a CFL iff accepted by either mode. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always include $Z_0$ transitions.** Many students forget to handle the case when the stack only has $Z_0$ (the bottom marker) ŌĆö leading to crashes on the last symbol.
> - **Stack notation $\alpha$ replaces $X$, not adds above it.** Forgetting this swaps push/pop behavior.
> - **Nondeterminism is mandatory** for languages with ambiguous midpoints (palindromes). Use $\varepsilon$-transitions for the "guess."
> - **Show all transitions of $\delta$.** Partial definitions lose 4ŌĆō5 marks in 14-mark questions.
> - **Trace the design** on at least one example string. This is often the *only* way to earn full marks for correctness.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Design a PDA to accept the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ by empty stack. Show the transition function and trace the string `aaabbbccc`.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Construct the PDA

This language is *not* context-free and cannot be accepted by a standard PDA! The correct KTU-standard answer recognizes this limitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is a **classic context-sensitive language** and **CANNOT be accepted by any PDA**. A well-written answer that explains *why* (counting one variable requires comparing two ŌĆö stack can only track one matched pair) earns **full 7 marks**.

**Model Answer for Part (a):**
A PDA has a single stack, so it can only match **one pair** of symbols (e.g., $a^n b^n$). It cannot simultaneously match both $a^n$ with $b^n$ AND $b^n$ with $c^n$. By the **pumping lemma for CFLs**, $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is not context-free. Hence, **no PDA exists** for this language.

(7 marks awarded for correct identification + justification)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Closest Achievable Variant

Instead, design a PDA for $L' = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$:

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

| Transition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, aZ_0)\}$ | Push $a$ on first $a$ |
| $\delta(q_0, a, a) = \{(q_0, aa)\}$ | Push $a$ for subsequent $a$'s |
| $\delta(q_0, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | First $b$ of pair: pop $a$, move to $q_1$ |
| $\delta(q_1, b, a) = \{(q_2, \varepsilon)\}$ | Second $b$: pop another $a$, move to $q_2$ |
| $\delta(q_2, b, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | Next pair: loop |
| $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_3, Z_0)\}$ | Accept on final state |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Design a PDA to accept the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w)\}$ by final state. Show the transition diagram and trace the string `aabb`.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: PDA Construction

The PDA must push on reading one symbol and pop on reading the other, tracking the **balance** of $a$'s vs $b$'s.

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_1\})$$

| Transition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, aZ_0)\}$ | Push $a$ on empty |
| $\delta(q_0, b, Z_0) = \{(q_0, bZ_0)\}$ | Push $b$ on empty |
| $\delta(q_0, a, a) = \{(q_0, aa)\}$ | Push $a$ on $a$ |
| $\delta(q_0, a, b) = \{(q_0, \varepsilon)\}$ | $a$ matches $b$: pop $b$ |
| $\delta(q_0, b, b) = \{(q_0, bb)\}$ | Push $b$ on $b$ |
| $\delta(q_0, b, a) = \{(q_0, \varepsilon)\}$ | $b$ matches $a$: pop $a$ |
| $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_1, Z_0)\}$ | Accept when balanced |

(7 marks)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Trace of `aabb`

| Step | State | Input | Stack | Transition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0$ | `aabb` | $Z_0$ | ŌĆö |
| 1 | $q_0$ | `abb` | $aZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0)$ |
| 2 | $q_0$ | `bb` | $aaZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a, a)$ |
| 3 | $q_0$ | `b` | $aZ_0$ | $\delta(q_0, b, a)$ |
| 4 | $q_0$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $\delta(q_0, b, a)$ |
| 5 | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ ŌĆö **ACCEPT** Ō£ģ |

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö Designing PDAs for CFLs**

- **PDA = FA + Stack.** This single addition makes it recognize exactly the **CFLs**.
- **The 7-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ must be written explicitly in 14-mark answers.
- **$\delta$ notation:** $\delta(q, a, X) = (p, \alpha)$ means *replace top $X$ with $\alpha$*. To **push $Y$**: use $\alpha = YX$. To **pop**: use $\alpha = \varepsilon$. To **no-op**: use $\alpha = X$.
- **Two acceptance modes** (final state, empty stack) are **equivalent in power**.
- **Nondeterminism is essential** for palindromes and similar languages ŌĆö use $\varepsilon$-transitions for the "guess the midpoint" step.
- **Push-Pop Strategy:** Push on the *first* symbol type, pop on the *second* (e.g., push $a$ for $a^n b^n$).
- **Boundary condition:** Always handle the case when only $Z_0$ remains on the stack ŌĆö this is when final-state acceptance occurs.
- **Languages PDAs CANNOT accept:** $a^n b^n c^n$, $a^{2^n}$ (requires counting in two dimensions or exponentially). Use the **pumping lemma for CFLs** to prove non-CFL-ness.
- **Common exam languages to practice:** $a^n b^n$, $a^n b^{2n}$, $a^{2n} b^n$, $ww^R$, balanced parentheses, $n_a(w) = n_b(w)$, $\{w \mid w \text{ starts and ends with the same symbol}\}$.
- **Trace at least one string** in every PDA design answer ŌĆö without a trace, the KTU examiner cannot award full marks for correctness.
- **One stack = one matched pair** of symbols. This is the fundamental limitation of PDAs and the reason for the existence of **Linear Bounded Automata** (LBA) for context-sensitive languages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of NPDAs and CFGs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of NPDAs and CFGs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Context-Free Grammar (CFG)

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is formally defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

where:
- $V$ is a finite set of **variables** (non-terminals)
- $T$ is a finite set of **terminals**, with $V \cap T = \emptyset$
- $P$ is a finite set of **productions** of the form $A \to \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$
- $S \in V$ is the designated **start symbol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Context-free grammars generate exactly the class of languages recognized by **pushdown automata (PDA)**. This is the **Chomsky Hierarchy** correspondence: Regular ŌŖé Context-Free ŌŖé Context-Sensitive ŌŖé Recursively Enumerable.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Non-Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA)

A **Non-Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA)** is formally defined as a 7-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F)$$

where:
- $Q$ is a finite set of **states**
- $\Sigma$ is a finite **input alphabet**
- $\Gamma$ is a finite **stack alphabet**
- $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^{*}}$ is the **transition function**
- $q_{0} \in Q$ is the **start state**
- $Z_{0} \in \Gamma$ is the **initial stack symbol**
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **final states**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Modes:** An NPDA can accept a string either by (a) **empty stack** ŌĆö $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*}(q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$, or (b) **final state** ŌĆö $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*}(q, \varepsilon, \gamma), q \in F\}$. These two modes are **equivalent in power for non-deterministic PDAs**.

### 1.3 The Equivalence Theorem (Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Theorem (KTU 2024 ŌĆö High Priority):**
> A language $L$ is **context-free** if and only if there exists an NPDA $M$ such that $L = L(M)$.

This theorem establishes that the class of languages generated by CFGs is **identical** to the class of languages accepted by NPDAs. The proof requires two constructive directions:

$$\underbrace{\text{CFG} \Rightarrow \text{NPDA}}_{\text{Constructive Simulation}} \quad \text{and} \quad \underbrace{\text{NPDA} \Rightarrow \text{CFG}}_{\text{Grammar Extraction}}$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The Recipe Ōåö The Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine a **recipe book** (the CFG) and a **single-armed robot chef** (the NPDA) in a kitchen.
> - The **recipe** lists ingredients (terminals) and cooking steps (variables/productions) ŌĆö it is a *declarative* description: "what can be made."
> - The **robot** has a tray (the stack) and follows each step by either picking an ingredient off the counter (reading input) or replacing a tray ingredient with a sub-recipe (applying a production) ŌĆö it is an *operational* description: "how to make it."
> The theorem says: **every recipe can be turned into a robot, and every robot's behavior can be captured as a recipe.** They are two languages describing the same cuisine.

### 1.5 Why This Equivalence Matters

| Aspect | CFG (Generative) | NPDA (Recognizer) |
|---|---|---|
| Viewpoint | Language **generation** | Language **recognition** |
| Primary Use | Compiler design (parse trees) | Membership testing |
| Strength | Hierarchical structure | Computational model |

The equivalence bridges the **declarative** (what is the language?) and **operational** (does a string belong?) views of computation, forming the foundation of **syntax analysis** in modern compilers (e.g., YACC, ANTLR, LLVM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Direction 1: CFG ŌåÆ NPDA (Simulation of a Grammar)

**Big Idea:** Build a single-state NPDA that mimics leftmost derivations of $G$ on the stack.

**Construction Logic (Step-by-Step Intuition):**

1. **Single state** $q$ is sufficient ŌĆö non-determinism on the stack suffices to simulate multiple derivation paths.
2. **Initial configuration:** The NPDA starts with the start symbol $S$ on the stack (no input consumed yet).
3. **For each variable $A$ on top of the stack:** Non-deterministically choose a production $A \to \alpha$ and *replace* $A$ by $\alpha$ on the stack (this is an $\varepsilon$-move ŌĆö no input consumed).
4. **For each terminal $a$ on top of the stack:** Match it against the current input symbol. If they agree, *pop* the terminal and advance the input.
5. **Acceptance:** The string is accepted when both the input and the stack are empty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Deterministic" is essential:** A CFG may have multiple productions for the same variable. The NPDA must *guess* the correct production at each step. A deterministic PDA cannot simulate all CFGs (e.g., the language $\{ww^{R} \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$ is CFG but not recognized by any DPDA).

### 2.2 Direction 2: NPDA ŌåÆ CFG (Extracting a Grammar from a Machine)

**Big Idea:** Reverse-engineer a grammar whose derivations mimic the NPDA's stack operations. The trick is to encode "trips" between NPDA states as grammar variables.

**Key Construction Element ŌĆö The Composite Variable $[qXp]$:**

$$[qXp] \; \Longrightarrow \; \text{"starting in state } q \text{ with } X \text{ on top of stack, the NPDA can read some input string and end in state } p \text{ with the stack reduced to just below } X\text{"}$$

**Intuition:** The "below" portion of the stack is untouched ŌĆö we are tracking only what happens *above* the symbol $X$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Construction Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CFG tuple | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V$ = variables, $T$ = terminals, $P$ = productions, $S$ = start |
| 2 | NPDA tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F)$ | 7-tuple, non-deterministic $\delta$ |
| 3 | CFG ŌåÆ NPDA (state) | One state $\{q\}$ only | $\vert Q \vert = 1$ |
| 4 | CFG ŌåÆ NPDA (var. rule) | For each $A \to X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k}$: $\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) \ni (q, X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k})$ | Stack expansion |
| 5 | CFG ŌåÆ NPDA (term. rule) | For each $a \in T$: $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Input matching |
| 6 | CFG ŌåÆ NPDA (start) | $S$ is the initial stack symbol | No $\varepsilon$-move for start |
| 7 | NPDA ŌåÆ CFG (variables) | $V' = \{[qXp] \mid q, p \in Q, X \in \Gamma\}$ | Composite variables |
| 8 | NPDA ŌåÆ CFG (start) | $S' = [q_{0}Z_{0}q_{m}]$ for any $q_{m} \in Q$ | Pick any accepting target state |
| 9 | NPDA ŌåÆ CFG (move rule) | If $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, Y_{1}\ldots Y_{k})$, then $[qXp_{k}] \to a\,[rY_{1}p_{1}][p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}]\cdots[p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]$ | For all $p_{1},\ldots,p_{k} \in Q$ |
| 10 | NPDA ŌåÆ CFG (pop rule) | If $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, \varepsilon)$, then $[qXp] \to a$ for all $p \in Q$ | Stack pops $X$ |
| 11 | Equivalence | $L \text{ is CFG} \iff L = L(M) \text{ for some NPDA } M$ | Bi-directional theorem |
| 12 | Acceptance | Empty-stack: $L_{\varepsilon}(M) = L(M) = L_{F}(M)$ for NPDAs | Both modes equivalent |

### 2.4 Why This Equivalence Has Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Construction:** YACC/Bison takes a CFG (in BNF form) and synthesizes an NPDA-based parser (LALR). The CFGŌåÆNPDA conversion is the theoretical backbone.
- **XML/JSON Parsing:** Tree-structured data formats are described by CFGs and validated by pushdown-based parsers.
- **Program Verification:** Model checkers encode CFG reachability via PDA emptiness testing.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Phrase-structure grammars (CFGs) are simulated by stack-based recognizers for syntactic analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 THEOREM 1: Every CFG Has an Equivalent NPDA (CFG ŌåÆ NPDA)

#### 3.1.1 Formal Statement

$$\forall \text{ CFG } G = (V, T, P, S), \;\; \exists \text{ NPDA } M \text{ such that } L(G) = L(M)$$

#### 3.1.2 Construction

Given $G = (V, T, P, S)$, construct:

$$M = (\{q\},\; T,\; V \cup T,\; \delta,\; q,\; S,\; \emptyset)$$

where $\delta$ is defined by two rules:

**Rule 1 ŌĆö Variable Expansion (uses $\varepsilon$-move):**

$$\text{For every production } A \to X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k} \in P \text{ (where each } X_{i} \in V \cup T\text{):}$$

$$\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) \;\ni\; (q, X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k})$$

**Rule 2 ŌĆö Terminal Matching (uses input symbol):**

$$\text{For every terminal } a \in T:$$

$$\delta(q, a, a) \;=\; \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$$

#### 3.1.3 Proof of Correctness: $L(G) = L(M)$

We prove that for any string $w \in T^{*}$:

$$S \overset{*}{\underset{G}{\Rightarrow}} w \quad \iff \quad (q, w, S) \overset{*}{\underset{M}{\vdash}} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$$

**Part (ŌćÆ): If $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w$ in $G$, then $M$ accepts $w$.**

We use **induction on the number of steps** in the leftmost derivation.

- **Base case (1 step):** $S \Rightarrow w$ where $w$ is a single terminal. Then $S \to w \in P$, so by Rule 1, $\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) \ni (q, w)$. Then for each terminal in $w$, Rule 2 pops it. Hence $(q, w, S) \vdash (q, w, w) \vdash^{*}(q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$. Ō£ō

- **Inductive step:** Suppose $S \overset{n+1}{\Rightarrow} w$. The first step replaces $S$ by some $\alpha = X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k}$ where $S \to \alpha \in P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\overset{1}{\Rightarrow} X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k} \\
  &\overset{n}{\Rightarrow} w \quad \text{(by induction hypothesis, broken into } k \text{ sub-derivations)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $w = w_{1}w_{2}\ldots w_{k}$ where $X_{i} \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_{i}$. By Rule 1:

$$
(q, w, S) \;\vdash\; (q, w, X_{1}X_{2}\ldots X_{k})
$$

By the induction hypothesis applied to each sub-derivation:

$$
(q, w_{i}w_{i+1}\ldots w_{k},\; X_{i}X_{i+1}\ldots X_{k}) \;\vdash^{*}\; (q, w_{i+1}\ldots w_{k},\; X_{i+1}\ldots X_{k})
$$

Concatenating all $k$ steps yields $(q, w, S) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$. Ō£ō

**Part (ŌćÉ): If $(q, w, S) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ in $M$, then $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w$ in $G$.**

We track the correspondence between stack contents and sentential forms. At any instant, the stack holds some string $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, and the unread input is some string $x \in T^{*}$. We claim:

$$\text{If } (q, w, S) \vdash^{*} (q, x, \gamma), \text{ then } S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w' \gamma \text{ where } w' \text{ is the part of } w \text{ already read.}$$

- The invariant holds initially with $\gamma = S$ and $w' = \varepsilon$.
- Rule 1 corresponds exactly to a derivation step $A \to \alpha$ where $A$ is the stack-top variable.
- Rule 2 corresponds to matching a terminal (a "leaf" in the parse tree).
- When the stack is empty, $\gamma = \varepsilon$, and $w' = w$, giving $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w$. Ō£ō

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The single-state NPDA is essentially *running the leftmost derivation on its stack*, using the stack as a derivation history.

#### 3.1.4 Worked Example

**Given CFG:** $G$ with productions:
- $S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$

This generates $\{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$.

**Constructed NPDA:** $M = (\{q\}, \{a, b\}, \{S, a, b\}, \delta, q, S, \emptyset)$ with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) &= \{(q, aSb),\; (q, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q, a, a) &= \{(q, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q, b, b) &= \{(q, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace of $w = aabb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q,\; aabb,\; S) &\vdash (q,\; aabb,\; aSb) & & \text{[Rule 1: } S \to aSb] \\
                &\vdash (q,\; abb,\; Sb)  & & \text{[Rule 2: pop } a] \\
                &\vdash (q,\; abb,\; ab) & & \text{[Rule 1: } S \to \varepsilon \text{ (wait, stack shows } S \text{ was replaced by } \varepsilon \text{, but then } b \text{ remains)]} \\
                &\vdash (q,\; bb,\; b)   & & \text{[Rule 2: pop } b] \\
                &\vdash (q,\; b,\; b)   & & \text{[already processed]} \\
                &\vdash (q,\; \varepsilon,\; \varepsilon) & & \text{[Rule 2: pop } b]
\end{aligned}
$$

The string is **accepted by empty stack**. Ō£ō

---

### 3.2 THEOREM 2: Every NPDA Has an Equivalent CFG (NPDA ŌåÆ CFG)

#### 3.2.1 Formal Statement

$$\forall \text{ NPDA } M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F), \;\; \exists \text{ CFG } G \text{ such that } L(M) = L(G)$$

(We assume acceptance by **empty stack** for simplicity; the final-state form is converted to empty-stack form first.)

#### 3.2.2 Construction

Define the CFG $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:

- **Variables:** $V = \{[qXp] \mid q, p \in Q,\; X \in \Gamma\}$
- **Start symbol:** $S = [q_{0}Z_{0}q_{m}]$ for some chosen $q_{m} \in Q$ (or universally quantified; see below)
- **Productions $R$:** Defined by two rules over the transitions of $M$:

**Rule A ŌĆö Stack-Symbol Replacement (general move):**

If $\delta(q, a, X)$ contains $(r, Y_{1}Y_{2}\ldots Y_{k})$ with $k \geq 1$ (i.e., $X$ is replaced by $k$ symbols), then for **every** sequence of states $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{k} \in Q$:

$$[qXp_{k}] \;\to\; a\,[rY_{1}p_{1}]\,[p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}]\cdots[p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]$$

**Rule B ŌĆö Stack-Symbol Pop (terminal move):**

If $\delta(q, a, X)$ contains $(r, \varepsilon)$ (i.e., $X$ is popped), then for **every** $p \in Q$:

$$[qXp] \;\to\; a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case $a = \varepsilon$:** If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \ni (r, Y_{1}\ldots Y_{k})$, the rules above apply with $a = \varepsilon$, giving $[qXp_{k}] \to [rY_{1}p_{1}][p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}]\cdots[p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]$.

**Start Symbol Refinement (Garey & Johnson form):** A cleaner choice is:

$$S \;\to\; [q_{0}Z_{0}q] \quad \text{for every } q \in Q$$

This guarantees $L(G) = L_{\varepsilon}(M)$ for *any* accepting target state $q$.

#### 3.2.3 Proof of Correctness: $L_{\varepsilon}(M) = L(G)$

We prove the central inductive claim:

> **Claim:** For any $q, p \in Q$, $X \in \Gamma$, and $w \in \Sigma^{*}$:
> $$[qXp] \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w \quad \iff \quad (q, w, X) \vdash_{M}^{*} (p, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$$

**Part (ŌćÆ): Induction on the number of derivation steps.**

- **Base case (1 step):** $[qXp] \Rightarrow a$ comes from Rule B, meaning $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, \varepsilon)$ for some $r$, and $a = w$. So $(q, a, X) \vdash (r, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) = (p, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ with $r = p$. Ō£ō

- **Inductive step:** $[qXp_{k}] \Rightarrow a\,[rY_{1}p_{1}]\cdots[p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]$ comes from Rule A. By induction, each $[p_{i-1}Y_{i}p_{i}] \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_{i}$ where $w = a\,w_{1}w_{2}\ldots w_{k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q,\; a\,w_{1}\ldots w_{k},\; X) &\vdash (r,\; w_{1}\ldots w_{k},\; Y_{1}Y_{2}\ldots Y_{k}) & & \text{[Rule A transition]} \\
                                 &\vdash^{*} (p_{1},\; w_{2}\ldots w_{k},\; Y_{2}\ldots Y_{k}) & & \text{[IH on } [rY_{1}p_{1}]] \\
                                 &\vdash^{*} (p_{2},\; w_{3}\ldots w_{k},\; Y_{3}\ldots Y_{k}) & & \text{[IH on } [p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}]] \\
                                 &\;\;\vdots & & \\
                                 &\vdash^{*} (p_{k},\; \varepsilon,\; \varepsilon) & & \text{[IH on } [p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]]
\end{aligned}
$$

With $p_{k} = p$, we get the desired $(q, w, X) \vdash^{*} (p, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$. Ō£ō

**Part (ŌćÉ): Induction on the number of PDA moves.**

- **Base case (1 move):** $(q, a, X) \vdash (r, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ implies $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, \varepsilon)$, so Rule B gives $[qXp] \to a$ (taking $p = r$). Ō£ō

- **Inductive step:** $(q, w, X) \vdash (r, x, Y_{1}\ldots Y_{k}) \vdash^{*} (p, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ with $w = ax$ and $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, Y_{1}\ldots Y_{k})$. The remaining computation must pop the $k$ symbols one by one, passing through some intermediate states $p_{1}, \ldots, p_{k-1}, p_{k} = p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r,\; x,\; Y_{1}Y_{2}\ldots Y_{k}) &\vdash^{*} (p_{1},\; x_{2}\ldots x_{k},\; Y_{2}\ldots Y_{k}) \\
                                   &\vdash^{*} (p_{2},\; x_{3}\ldots x_{k},\; Y_{3}\ldots Y_{k}) \\
                                   &\;\;\vdots \\
                                   &\vdash^{*} (p_{k},\; \varepsilon,\; \varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $x = x_{1}x_{2}\ldots x_{k}$. By the IH on each sub-computation, $[rY_{1}p_{1}] \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} x_{1}$, $[p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}] \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} x_{2}$, etc. Rule A gives:

$$[qXp] \;\to\; a\,[rY_{1}p_{1}][p_{1}Y_{2}p_{2}]\cdots[p_{k-1}Y_{k}p_{k}]$$

Combining, $[qXp] \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} a\,x_{1}x_{2}\ldots x_{k} = w$. Ō£ō

#### 3.2.4 Worked Example

**Given NPDA:** $M$ that accepts $\{wcw^{R} \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$. Transitions:
- $\delta(q_{0}, a, Z_{0}) = \{(q_{0}, aZ_{0})\}$ ŌĆö push $a$
- $\delta(q_{0}, b, Z_{0}) = \{(q_{0}, bZ_{0})\}$ ŌĆö push $b$
- $\delta(q_{0}, a, a) = \{(q_{0}, aa)\}$ ŌĆö push $a$ on $a$
- $\delta(q_{0}, b, b) = \{(q_{0}, bb)\}$ ŌĆö push $b$ on $b$
- $\delta(q_{0}, c, a) = \{(q_{1}, a)\}$ ŌĆö switch state, peek at $a$
- $\delta(q_{0}, c, b) = \{(q_{1}, b)\}$ ŌĆö switch state, peek at $b$
- $\delta(q_{1}, a, a) = \{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)\}$ ŌĆö pop $a$ matching $a$
- $\delta(q_{1}, b, b) = \{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)\}$ ŌĆö pop $b$ matching $b$

**Constructed CFG $G$:** Variables include $[q_{0}a q_{0}]$, $[q_{0}b q_{0}]$, $[q_{0}Z_{0}q_{1}]$, etc. For instance, from $\delta(q_{0}, a, Z_{0}) \ni (q_{0}, aZ_{0})$ (a 2-symbol replacement) with $k=2$:

For all $p_{1}, p_{2} \in \{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$:
$$[q_{0}Z_{0}p_{2}] \;\to\; a\,[q_{0}a p_{1}]\,[p_{1}Z_{0}p_{2}]$$

And from $\delta(q_{1}, a, a) \ni (q_{1}, \varepsilon)$ (pop rule, $k=0$):
$$[q_{1}a p] \;\to\; a \quad \text{for all } p \in \{q_{0}, q_{1}\}$$

This grammar, though verbose, generates exactly $L(M) = \{wcw^{R}\}$. Ō£ō

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of the Two Constructions

| Property | CFG ŌåÆ NPDA | NPDA ŌåÆ CFG |
|---|---|---|
| Number of NPDA states | $\vert Q \vert = 1$ | Preserved (in variables) |
| Size of resulting system | $\vert V \vert + \vert T \vert$ stack symbols | $\vert Q \vert^{2} \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert$ variables |
| Productions in resulting CFG | N/A (transitions only) | $O(\vert Q \vert^{k+1} \cdot \vert \delta \vert)$ where $k$ = max stack growth |
| Determinism | N/A (NPDA is non-det) | Original non-determinism encoded in branching productions |
| Acceptance mode targeted | Empty stack | Empty stack (convert final-state first) |
| Construction invertibility | One-to-one grammar production | Many-to-one (different states encode same behavior) |

    Expand -->|"Replace A by alpha\non stack top"| Push1["Stack: alpha gamma\nInput: x"]
    Match -->|"Pop a from stack\nif input = a"| Pop1["Stack: gamma\nInput: x minus a"]

    Decision2 -->|"Variable"| Expand
    Decision2 -->|"Terminal"| Match

```

    PopRule --> G["CFG G\nVariables = bracket-q-X-p\nStart = bracket-q0-Z0-qm"]
    PushRule --> G
    G --> H["L G = L empty-stack M\n(Two-way equivalence established)"]
```
