# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Closure Under Union ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.1 (Union):** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are CFLs, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is also a CFL.

### Proof Strategy (CFG Construction)
Given CFGs $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ generating $L_1$ and $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$ generating $L_2$ (assume $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$), construct a new CFG $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:
- $V = V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}$ (a fresh new start symbol $S$)
- $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2\}$

The intuition: the new start symbol $S$ can either expand to $S_1$ (generating $L_1$) or $S_2$ (generating $L_2$). Since $S_1$ and $S_2$ use disjoint non-terminals, there is no interference.

## 2.2 Closure Under Concatenation ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.2 (Concatenation):** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are CFLs, then $L_1 L_2$ is also a CFL.

### Proof Strategy (CFG Construction)
Construct $G$ as above but with $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 S_2\}$. The new start symbol generates a string from $L_1$ followed by a string from $L_2$.

## 2.3 Closure Under Kleene Star ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.3 (Kleene Star):** If $L$ is a CFL, then $L^*$ is also a CFL.

### Proof Strategy (CFG Construction)
Given CFG $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ generating $L$, construct $G = (V_1, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:
- $R = R_1 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 S \mid \epsilon\}$

The recursive production $S \rightarrow S_1 S$ allows concatenation of any number of strings from $L$ (including the empty string via $S \rightarrow \epsilon$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Slick Alternative:** $L^* = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L^i$, and each $L^i$ is a CFL by repeated concatenation. But the union of *infinitely many* CFLs isn't automatically a CFL ŌĆö the CFG construction above is the rigorous proof.

## 2.4 Closure Under Reversal ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.4 (Reversal):** If $L$ is a CFL, then $L^R = \{w^R \mid w \in L\}$ is also a CFL.

### Proof Strategy (CFG Construction)
For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ in $R_1$, add the production $A \rightarrow \alpha^R$ (reverse the right-hand side). Note: terminals are kept as single symbols, and the reversal is applied to the string $\alpha$ as a sequence of symbols.

## 2.5 Closure Under Homomorphism ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.5 (Homomorphism):** Let $h: \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma^*$ be a homomorphism. If $L$ is a CFL over $\Sigma$, then $h(L) = \{h(w) \mid w \in L\}$ is a CFL over $\Gamma$.

### Proof Strategy (CFG Construction)
For each production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_k$ in $R_1$ (where $X_i \in V_1 \cup \Sigma$), add the production $A \rightarrow h(X_1) h(X_2) \cdots h(X_k)$. Apply $h$ only to terminal symbols on the RHS.

## 2.6 Closure Under Inverse Homomorphism ŌĆö Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.6 (Inverse Homomorphism):** If $h: \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma^*$ is a homomorphism and $L$ is a CFL over $\Gamma$, then $h^{-1}(L) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid h(w) \in L\}$ is a CFL over $\Sigma$.

### Proof Strategy (PDA Construction)
Given PDA $M$ for $L$, construct PDA $M'$ for $h^{-1}(L)$. When $M'$ reads a symbol $a \in \Sigma$, it *simulates* $M$ reading $h(a)$ ŌĆö feeding each symbol of $h(a)$ to $M$ in sequence. The stack operations are unchanged.

## 2.7 Non-Closure Under Intersection ŌĆö Counter-Example

> [!WARNING]
> **Theorem 2.7 (Non-Closure of Intersection):** CFLs are **NOT closed** under intersection.

### Proof Strategy (Counter-Example)
Consider the two CFLs:
- $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^* \mid n \geq 1\}$
- $L_2 = \{a^* b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

Both are CFLs (provable by constructing CFGs). However:
$$L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$$
which is a **classical non-CFL** (proved via the Pumping Lemma for CFLs).

## 2.8 Non-Closure Under Complement

> [!WARNING]
> **Theorem 2.8 (Non-Closure of Complement):** CFLs are **NOT closed** under complement.

### Proof Strategy (De Morgan's Law)
If CFLs were closed under complement, then since they are closed under union:
$$\overline{L_1} \in \text{CFL} \wedge \overline{L_2} \in \text{CFL} \Longrightarrow \overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2} \in \text{CFL}$$
By De Morgan's law: $\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2} = \overline{L_1 \cap L_2}$, so this would mean CFLs are closed under complement of intersection, contradicting the non-closure of intersection. Note: This argument requires the *closure* of CFLs under union (which IS true).

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Result | Constructive Element | Quick Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $L_1 \cup L_2$ | Closed | New $S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2$ | "Add new start" |
| $L_1 L_2$ | Closed | New $S \rightarrow S_1 S_2$ | "Add new start" |
| $L^*$ | Closed | $S \rightarrow S_1 S \mid \epsilon$ | "Recursive start" |
| $L^R$ | Closed | Reverse every production RHS | "Mirror grammar" |
| $h(L)$ | Closed | Apply $h$ to terminals on RHS | "Substitute" |
| $h^{-1}(L)$ | Closed | Simulate $h$ on input symbols | "Pre-image" |
| $L_1 \cap L_2$ | Not Closed | $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ counter-example | Pumping Lemma |
| $\overline{L}$ | Not Closed | De Morgan reduction | Intersection |
| $L_1 - L_2$ | Not Closed | $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$ | Closure + De Morgan |
| $\text{Subst}(L)$ | Closed | $a \rightarrow L_a$ (substitution) | CFG technique |
| $L_1 / L_2$ (right quotient) | Not Closed | $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2 \Sigma^*}$ | Indirect proof |

## 2.10 Engineering Utility

Closure properties are foundational in:
- **Compiler Design:** Lexical analyzers (regular) and parsers (CFL) rely on closure properties to combine tokenizers and grammar modules.
- **Programming Language Theory:** Verifying that a proposed language construct is *expressible* as a CFL.
- **Model Checking & Verification:** Constructing non-CFL proofs to show a system property is undecidable.
- **Natural Language Processing:** CFG composition for multilingual grammars.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Proof: Closure Under Union

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Prove that if $L_1$ and $L_2$ are CFLs, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is a CFL.

### Given
- $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ with $L(G_1) = L_1$
- $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$ with $L(G_2) = L_2$

### Step 1 ŌĆö WLOG Disjointness
Without loss of generality, assume $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$. If not, rename non-terminals in one of the grammars (e.g., prefix $V_2$ with primes: $S_2 \rightarrow S_2'$, etc.). The generated language is unchanged.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Construct New Grammar
Define $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:
$$V = V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}$$
$$R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2\}$$

where $S \notin V_1 \cup V_2$ is a brand new start symbol.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Show $L(G) \supseteq L_1 \cup L_2$
Let $w \in L_1 \cup L_2$. Then either $w \in L_1$ or $w \in L_2$.

**Case A:** $w \in L_1$. Then $S_1 \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w$. In $G$, we have:
$$S \Rightarrow S_1 \Rightarrow_{G}^* w$$
(since the rules of $R_1$ are present in $R$, the derivation in $G$ mirrors that in $G_1$). Hence $w \in L(G)$.

**Case B:** $w \in L_2$. Symmetric argument using $S \Rightarrow S_2 \Rightarrow_{G}^* w$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Show $L(G) \subseteq L_1 \cup L_2$
Let $w \in L(G)$. Then $S \Rightarrow_G^* w$. The first step of any derivation in $G$ is either $S \Rightarrow S_1$ or $S \Rightarrow S_2$.

- If $S \Rightarrow S_1$: Then $S_1 \Rightarrow_G^* w$. Since $S_1 \in V_1$ and $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$, this derivation can *only* use productions in $R_1$. Thus $S_1 \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w$, giving $w \in L_1$.
- If $S \Rightarrow S_2$: Symmetric, giving $w \in L_2$.

In either case, $w \in L_1 \cup L_2$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion
Combining Steps 3 and 4: $L(G) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Since $G$ is a CFG, $L_1 \cup L_2$ is a CFL. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Proof: Closure Under Concatenation

### Construction
Given $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ and $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$ with $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$, construct:
$$G = (V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}, \Sigma, R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 S_2\}, S)$$

### Derivation Trace
For any $w_1 \in L_1$ and $w_2 \in L_2$:
$$S \Rightarrow S_1 S_2 \Rightarrow_G^* w_1 w_2$$

### Why the disjointness matters
If $V_1 \cap V_2 \neq \emptyset$, the derivations from $S_1$ and $S_2$ could "interfere." For example, if $A \in V_1 \cap V_2$, then a production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ from $R_1$ might fire during the $S_2$ derivation, generating strings not in $L_1 L_2$. The disjointness forces each side of $S_1 S_2$ to derive independently. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Proof: Closure Under Kleene Star

### Construction
Given $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ generating $L$, construct:
$$G = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 S \mid \epsilon\}, S)$$
where $S$ replaces $S_1$ as the start symbol.

### Generating $\epsilon$
$S \Rightarrow \epsilon \in L(G)$, so $\epsilon \in L(G) = L^*$. Good.

### Generating $L^n$ for $n \geq 1$
By induction on $n$.

**Base Case ($n=1$):** $w \in L$. Then $S_1 \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w$. In $G$:
$$S \Rightarrow S_1 S \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w S \Rightarrow \epsilon = w$$
So $w \in L(G)$. Hence $L \subseteq L(G)$.

**Inductive Step:** Assume $L^n \subseteq L(G)$. Take $w = w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n w_{n+1}$ with each $w_i \in L$.
$$S \Rightarrow S_1 S \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w_1 S \Rightarrow S_1 S \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w_1 w_2 S \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n S \Rightarrow S_1 S \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w_1 \cdots w_{n+1}$$
So $L^{n+1} \subseteq L(G)$.

### Reverse Inclusion
Every derivation in $G$ starting from $S$ must use either $S \Rightarrow \epsilon$ or repeatedly $S \Rightarrow S_1 S$ until eventually $S \Rightarrow \epsilon$. Each application of $S \Rightarrow S_1 S$ generates a string in $L$ on the left, and the recursive $S$ on the right eventually produces $\epsilon$ or another string in $L$. Hence every generated string is in $L^*$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Proof: Non-Closure Under Intersection

### Claim
CFLs are not closed under intersection.

### Counter-Example
Define:
$$L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 0\}$$
$$L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n \mid m \geq 0, n \geq 1\}$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö $L_1$ is a CFL
Grammar for $L_1$:
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aAb \mid ab$$
$$B \rightarrow cB \mid \epsilon$$

Derivation: $S \Rightarrow AB \Rightarrow aAbB \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow a^n b^n c^m$. Ō£ō

### Step 2 ŌĆö $L_2$ is a CFL
Grammar for $L_2$:
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid \epsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bBc \mid bc$$

Derivation: $S \Rightarrow AB \Rightarrow a^m B \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow a^m b^n c^n$. Ō£ō

### Step 3 ŌĆö $L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$
A string is in $L_1 \cap L_2$ iff it has matching $a^n b^n$ prefix structure (from $L_1$) AND matching $b^n c^n$ suffix structure (from $L_2$). For a string $a^p b^q c^r$:
- From $L_1$: $p = q$ (call it $n_1$) and $r \geq 0$ arbitrary.
- From $L_2$: $p \geq 0$ arbitrary and $q = r$ (call it $n_2$).

Combined: $p = q = r$. Letting $n = p = q = r \geq 1$:
$$L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö $L_1 \cap L_2$ is NOT a CFL
By the **Pumping Lemma for CFLs**: For any pumping length $p$, take $s = a^p b^p c^p$. For any decomposition $s = uvxyz$ with $\mid vxy \mid \leq p$ and $\mid vy \mid \geq 1$, the substring $vxy$ lies entirely within one of the three "blocks" (a's, b's, or straddles a-b). Pumping $v$ and $y$ disrupts the equality $|a| = |b| = |c|$, yielding a string not in $L$. Contradiction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Exhaustive Proof: Non-Closure Under Complement

### Argument
Suppose, for contradiction, that CFLs are closed under complement. Then:

**Step 1:** Since CFLs are closed under union, for any two CFLs $L_1, L_2$:
$$\overline{L_1} \in \text{CFL}, \overline{L_2} \in \text{CFL} \Longrightarrow \overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2} \in \text{CFL}$$

**Step 2:** By De Morgan's law:
$$\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2} = \overline{L_1 \cap L_2}$$

**Step 3:** So $\overline{L_1 \cap L_2} \in \text{CFL}$, which (by our closure assumption) means $L_1 \cap L_2 \in \text{CFL}$ for all CFLs $L_1, L_2$.

**Step 4:** But Step 3 of Section 3.4 shows $L_1 \cap L_2$ is *not* always a CFL. Contradiction.

**Conclusion:** CFLs are not closed under complement. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Exhaustive Proof: Closure Under Reversal

### Construction
Given $G_1 = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$, construct:
$$G = (V, \Sigma, R', S)$$
where $R' = \{A \rightarrow \alpha^R \mid A \rightarrow \alpha \in R\}$.

### Example
If $G_1$ has $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \epsilon$, then $G$ has:
$$S \rightarrow bSa \mid \epsilon$$

### Verification
$$L(G_1) = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$$
$$L(G) = \{b^n a^n \mid n \geq 0\} = (L(G_1))^R \checkmark$$

### Why it works
A derivation $S \Rightarrow \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow w$ in $G_1$ corresponds to the reversed derivation $S \Rightarrow \alpha_1^R \Rightarrow \alpha_2^R \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow w^R$ in $G$. Induction on derivation length establishes the bijection. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Worked Example: Constructing CFG for $L_1 \cup L_2$ Where $L_1 = \{a^n b^n\}$ and $L_2 = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Grammar for $L_1$
$$S_1 \rightarrow a S_1 b \mid \epsilon$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Grammar for $L_2$
$$S_2 \rightarrow a S_2 a \mid b S_2 b \mid \epsilon$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Combined Grammar for $L_1 \cup L_2$
$$S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2$$
$$S_1 \rightarrow a S_1 b \mid \epsilon$$
$$S_2 \rightarrow a S_2 a \mid b S_2 b \mid \epsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to verify that the non-terminals of the two original grammars are disjoint. If both grammars had a non-terminal named $A$, the combined grammar would have ambiguous derivations. Always rename if needed.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** State and prove that the class of context-free languages is closed under union.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are CFLs, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is also a CFL.

**Proof:** Let $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ and $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$ be CFGs such that $L(G_1) = L_1$ and $L(G_2) = L_2$. Assume $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$ (WLOG). Construct:
$$G = (V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}, \Sigma, R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2\}, S)$$

For any $w \in L_1$: $S \Rightarrow S_1 \Rightarrow_{G_1}^* w$, so $w \in L(G)$. Similarly for $w \in L_2$. Conversely, any derivation in $G$ must begin with $S \Rightarrow S_1$ or $S \Rightarrow S_2$, leading to a string in $L_1$ or $L_2$ respectively (since the non-terminals are disjoint, derivations cannot cross). Therefore $L(G) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Since $G$ is a CFG, $L_1 \cup L_2$ is a CFL. $\blacksquare$

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]** Show that context-free languages are not closed under intersection.

**Model Answer:**

We provide a counter-example. Consider:
$$L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^* \mid n \geq 1\}, \quad L_2 = \{a^* b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$$

Both $L_1$ and $L_2$ are CFLs, generated by:
$$G_1: S \rightarrow AB, \quad A \rightarrow aAb \mid ab, \quad B \rightarrow cB \mid \epsilon$$
$$G_2: S \rightarrow AB, \quad A \rightarrow aA \mid \epsilon, \quad B \rightarrow bBc \mid bc$$

However:
$$L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$$

By the Pumping Lemma for CFLs, this language is **not** context-free. Therefore, CFLs are not closed under intersection. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Prove that if $L$ is a CFL generated by a CFG $G$, then $L^*$ is also a CFL. Construct the CFG explicitly and justify.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider $L_1 = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ and $L_2 = \{a^{2n} b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Construct a CFG for $L_1 \cup L_2$ and show a sample derivation for the string $aabbbb$.

### Model Solution for (a):

**Theorem:** If $L$ is a CFL, then $L^*$ is a CFL.

**Proof:** Let $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ be a CFG with $L(G) = L$. Construct a new CFG $G' = (V, \Sigma, R', S')$ where:
- $S' \notin V$ is a fresh start symbol.
- $R' = R \cup \{S' \rightarrow S S' \mid \epsilon\}$.

**Verification that $L(G') = L^*$:**

*($L^* \subseteq L(G')$):* For any $k \geq 0$ and strings $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k \in L$, the derivation:
$$S' \Rightarrow SS' \Rightarrow_{G}^* w_1 S' \Rightarrow SS' \Rightarrow_{G}^* w_1 w_2 S' \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow_{G}^* w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k S' \Rightarrow \epsilon = w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k$$
shows $w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k \in L(G')$. For $k=0$, $S' \Rightarrow \epsilon \in L(G')$.

*($L(G') \subseteq L^*$):* Every derivation in $G'$ from $S'$ uses either $S' \Rightarrow \epsilon$ (yielding $\epsilon \in L^*$) or repeatedly $S' \Rightarrow SS'$ until the trailing $S'$ eventually becomes $\epsilon$. Each application of $S' \Rightarrow SS'$ introduces exactly one $S$ that derives a string in $L$ (since the rules of $R$ are unchanged). Hence every generated string is a concatenation of strings in $L$, i.e., in $L^*$. $\blacksquare$

### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö CFG for $L_1 = \{a^n b^{2n}\}$:**
$$S_1 \rightarrow a S_1 b b \mid a b b$$

Wait ŌĆö let us re-engineer. The number of $b$'s is twice the number of $a$'s.
$$S_1 \rightarrow a S_1 bb \mid abb$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö CFG for $L_2 = \{a^{2n} b^n\}$:**
$$S_2 \rightarrow aa S_2 b \mid aab$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combined CFG for $L_1 \cup L_2$:**
$$S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2$$
$$S_1 \rightarrow a S_1 bb \mid abb$$
$$S_2 \rightarrow aa S_2 b \mid aab$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derivation of $aabbbb$ (note: $n=2$ in $L_1$ gives $aabb$ŌĆ” wait, $a^2 b^4 = aabbbb$):**
$$S \Rightarrow S_1 \Rightarrow a S_1 bb \Rightarrow a (abb) bb = aabbbb$$

The string has $n=2$, yielding $a^2 b^{2 \cdot 2} = a^2 b^4 = aabbbb$. Ō£ō

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Prove that the class of context-free languages is closed under reversal. Provide the construction of the new CFG and illustrate with an example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Prove that CFLs are not closed under complement, assuming they are not closed under intersection. State De Morgan's law as part of your proof.

### Model Solution for (a):

**Theorem:** If $L$ is a CFL, then $L^R$ is a CFL.

**Proof:** Let $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ be a CFG with $L(G) = L$. Construct $G^R = (V, \Sigma, R^R, S)$ where:
$$R^R = \{A \rightarrow \alpha^R \mid A \rightarrow \alpha \in R\}$$

**Claim:** $L(G^R) = (L(G))^R$.

*Proof of Claim:* We show by induction on derivation length $k$ that if $A \Rightarrow_{G}^k w$ in $G$, then $A \Rightarrow_{G^R}^k w^R$ in $G^R$, and vice versa.

*Base Case ($k=1$):* If $A \Rightarrow_G w$ via production $A \rightarrow w$ (where $w \in \Sigma^*$), then $A \rightarrow w^R \in R^R$, so $A \Rightarrow_{G^R} w^R$.

*Inductive Step:* Suppose $A \Rightarrow_G X_1 X_2 \cdots X_m \Rightarrow_G^k w_1 w_2 \cdots w_m$ where each $X_i \Rightarrow_G^{k_i} w_i$ with $\sum k_i = k - 1$. In $G^R$, the production $A \rightarrow X_m \cdots X_2 X_1 \in R^R$, and by induction $X_i \Rightarrow_{G^R}^{k_i} w_i^R$. Thus:
$$A \Rightarrow_{G^R} X_m \cdots X_1 \Rightarrow_{G^R}^* w_m^R \cdots w_1^R = (w_1 w_2 \cdots w_m)^R$$

The reverse direction is symmetric. Hence $L(G^R) = L^R$. $\blacksquare$

**Example:** If $G$ has $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \epsilon$, generating $L = \{a^n b^n\}$, then $G^R$ has $S \rightarrow bSa \mid \epsilon$, generating $L^R = \{b^n a^n\}$. Ō£ō

### Model Solution for (b):

**Theorem:** CFLs are not closed under complement.

**Proof (by contradiction using De Morgan's law):**

**Recall De Morgan's Law:** For any languages $L_1, L_2$:
$$\overline{L_1 \cap L_2} = \overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}$$

**Assumption for contradiction:** CFLs are closed under complement.

**Step 1:** Let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be arbitrary CFLs. By our assumption, $\overline{L_1}$ and $\overline{L_2}$ are CFLs.

**Step 2:** Since CFLs are closed under union (proved earlier), $\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}$ is a CFL.

**Step 3:** By De Morgan's law, $\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2} = \overline{L_1 \cap L_2}$, so $\overline{L_1 \cap L_2}$ is a CFL.

**Step 4:** By our assumption applied again, $\overline{(\overline{L_1 \cap L_2})} = L_1 \cap L_2$ is a CFL.

**Step 5:** This means CFLs are closed under intersection. But we have already proved (using the counter-example $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^*\}$, $L_2 = \{a^* b^n c^n\}$ giving $L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$) that CFLs are **not** closed under intersection. Contradiction.

**Conclusion:** CFLs are not closed under complement. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The De Morgan argument is bidirectional.** Some students mistakenly write $\overline{L_1 \cap L_2} = \overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}$ ŌĆö verify the *exact* statement. Marks are deducted for the wrong direction.
> 2. **Don't forget to use a "known" closed property (like union).** The argument for non-closure of complement is *not* standalone ŌĆö it requires the *closure* of CFLs under union as a lemma. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 3. **For non-closure proofs, always give an explicit counter-example for intersection.** Vague claims like "it's not closed" without $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ will lose 3-4 marks.
> 4. **For closure proofs, always verify BOTH directions of set inclusion:** $L(G') \supseteq L_1 \cup L_2$ AND $L(G') \subseteq L_1 \cup L_2$. Most students only show one direction.
> 5. **Disjointness of non-terminals is a common oversight** in concatenation proofs. Always rename if necessary, and state the assumption explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 4: Closure Properties of CFLs**

### Must-Know Closure Properties (CLOSED Ō£ō)
- **Union** ($L_1 \cup L_2$): New start $S \rightarrow S_1 \mid S_2$. *Always state disjointness of non-terminals.*
- **Concatenation** ($L_1 L_2$): New start $S \rightarrow S_1 S_2$.
- **Kleene Star** ($L^*$): Start $S \rightarrow S_1 S \mid \epsilon$ (recursive production).
- **Reversal** ($L^R$): Reverse every production's right-hand side.
- **Homomorphism** ($h(L)$): Apply $h$ to terminals on RHS of every production.
- **Inverse Homomorphism** ($h^{-1}(L)$): Construct PDA simulating $h$ on input.

### Must-Know Non-Closure Properties (NOT CLOSED Ō£Ś)
- **Intersection** ($L_1 \cap L_2$): Counter-example $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^*\}$, $L_2 = \{a^* b^n c^n\}$ gives $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$.
- **Complement** ($\overline{L}$): De Morgan reduction with non-closure of intersection.
- **Difference** ($L_1 - L_2$): $L_1 - L_2 = L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$, follows from above.

### High-Yield Proof Patterns
1. **Closure proof pattern:** Construct new CFG/PDA ŌåÆ Show $L(\text{new}) \supseteq$ result ŌåÆ Show $L(\text{new}) \subseteq$ result ŌåÆ Conclude.
2. **Non-closure proof pattern:** Provide two CFLs whose operation yields a known non-CFL (commonly $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$).
3. **De Morgan bridge:** Use closure under union to derive non-closure of complement from non-closure of intersection.

### Exam Strategy & Common Pitfalls
- Ō£ģ Always **rename non-terminals** to ensure disjointness before combining grammars.
- Ō£ģ Always verify **both directions** of set inclusion in CFG construction proofs.
- Ō£ģ The string $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is the "**canonical non-CFL**" ŌĆö use it freely for intersection counter-examples.
- Ō£ģ For Kleene star, remember the **$\epsilon$ production** ŌĆö without it, the empty string isn't generated.
- ŌØī Do NOT confuse CFL closure with regular language closure: regular languages are closed under **complement and intersection**, but CFLs are not.
- ŌØī Do NOT use **Python/algorithms** ŌĆö this is a proof-based theory question requiring CFG construction.

### Key Formulas / Symbols to Memorize
- $\Rightarrow^*$ = "derives in zero or more steps"
- $L^R$ = language reversal
- $h(L)$ = image under homomorphism $h$
- $h^{-1}(L)$ = preimage under homomorphism
- $\overline{L}$ = complement
- $L_1 - L_2 = L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$
- $L^* = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L^i$

### Connections to Other Modules
- **Module 1 (Finite Automata):** Regular languages ŌŖé CFLs ŌĆö same closure properties (union, concat, star), but CFLs *lose* complement and intersection.
- **Module 2 (CFGs):** Closure properties are constructed via CFG manipulations.
- **Module 3 (PDAs):** Inverse homomorphism closure requires PDA construction.
- **Module 5 (Turing Machines):** Recursively enumerable languages *are* closed under union, concatenation, intersection ŌĆö strictly more powerful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context-Sensitive Grammar and Languages (Introduction Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Context-Sensitive Grammar and Languages (Introduction Only) # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG) A **Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG)** is a formal grammar belonging to **Type-1** of the Chomsky Hierarchy, defined as the 4-tuple: $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ where: - $V$ ŌĆö finite set of **non-terminal (variable)** symbols. - $T$ ŌĆö finite set of **terminal** symbols, with $V \cap T = \emptyset$. - $P$ ŌĆö finite set of **production rules** of the form: $$\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$$ where $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$. - $S \in V$ ŌĆö designated **start symbol**. > [!IMPORTANT] **The Two Mandatory Constraints on Productions** > 1. **Context Dependence:** A non-terminal $A$ can be replaced by $\gamma$ **only if it appears surrounded by the specific left context $\alpha$ and right context $\beta$**. Free replacement (as in CFG) is *not* allowed. > 2. **Non-Contracting Property (Length Condition):** The right-hand side must never be shorter than the left-hand side: > $$|\alpha \gamma \beta| \geq |\alpha A \beta| \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad |\gamma| \geq 1$$ > The only permissible exception is $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$, and only if $S$ never appears on the right-hand side of any other production. ## 1.2 Context-Sensitive Language (CSL) A language $L$ is a **Context-Sensitive Language** if and only if there exists a context-sensitive grammar $G$ such that: $$L = L(G) \quad \text{or} \quad L = L(G) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$$ Equivalently, $L$ is accepted by a **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** ŌĆö a restricted Turing machine whose tape length is bounded by a *linear* function of the input length. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ­¤¦Ā > [!NOTE] **"The Recipe with Strict Surroundings" Analogy** > > Imagine a *cooking recipe* where the rule "Add 2 spoons of **salt**" is rewritten as: > *"When the boiling **water** is present, you may add **salt**"*. > > - In **regular grammars (Type 3)**, you can add salt anywhere ŌĆö very rigid, very simple recipes. > - In **context-free grammars (Type 2)**, you can add salt regardless of surroundings ŌĆö more flexible, but ignores context. > - In **context-sensitive grammars (Type 1)**, you can add salt **only when the right "neighbors"** (water, temperature, time) are present. The transformation *depends on the context*. > > A classic case where this matters: the English plural of *mouse* becomes *mice*, but only in **noun context** ŌĆö never when used as a verb (*to mouse around*). This is exactly the kind of rule CSG captures. ## 1.4 Where CSGs Sit in the Chomsky Hierarchy $$\text{Type 0 (Unrestricted)} \;\supset\; \text{Type 1 (CSG)} \;\supset\; \text{Type 2 (CFG)} \;\supset\; \text{Type 3 (Regular)}$$ CSL is a **strict superset** of CFL and a **strict subset** of Recursively Enumerable (RE) languages. --- > [!TIP] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > For this introductory treatment, focus on: **(i)** the *production rule format*, **(ii)** the *non-contracting condition*, **(iii)** the *aŌü┐bŌü┐cŌü┐ example*, and **(iv)** the *LBA as a recognizer*. Deeper pumping lemmas and closure properties are typically covered in higher-semester electives. # ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Context-Sensitive Production Every production in a CSG has the structured form: $$\underbrace{\alpha}_{\text{left context}} \; \underbrace{A}_{\text{single non-terminal}} \; \underbrace{\beta}_{\text{right context}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\alpha \gamma \beta}_{\text{replacement preserves/extends context}}$$ | Symbol | Role | Allowed Values | |---|---|---| | $\alpha$ | Left context (may be empty) | $(V \cup T)^{*}$ | | $A$ | The target non-terminal being rewritten | $V$ (must be a single variable) | | $\beta$ | Right context (may be empty) | $(V \cup T)^{*}$ | | $\gamma$ | Replacement string | $(V \cup T)^{+}$ ŌĆö **must be non-empty** | ## 2.2 Why "Context-Sensitive"? ŌĆö The 'Why' Behind the Rule > [!IMPORTANT] The name comes from the requirement that the rule **senses the environment** of the variable $A$. The substitution $A \to \gamma$ is permitted **only** when the symbols $\alpha$ and $\beta$ flank $A$. This contrasts sharply with CFG, where the rule $A \to \gamma$ applies anywhere. ## 2.3 The Non-Contracting Property ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic 1. Start with sentential form $w$ of length $n$. 2. Apply production $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$. 3. New length: $n - 1 + \vert \gamma \vert$. 4. Constraint: $\vert \gamma \vert \geq 1$ ensures new length $\geq n$. 5. **Conclusion:** Sentential form length is **monotonically non-decreasing** during derivation. It starts at $1$ (the start symbol) and grows to $\vert w \vert$. This rules out cycles via the empty string and guarantees decidability (unlike Type 0). ## 2.4 The Decidability Consequence | Property | Value | Reason | |---|---|---| | Membership problem | **Decidable** | Each derivation is bounded by $2^{O(n)}$ candidates due to non-contracting property | | Empty-language test | **Decidable** | Finite set of productions ŌĆö direct check | | Equivalence of two CSGs | **Undecidable** (open historical) | Falls outside the scope of this introductory module | ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Property | Specification | KTU Note | |---|---|---| | Grammar Type | **Type 1** | Strict subset of Type 0, strict superset of Type 2 | | Production Form | $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$ | Single non-terminal on LHS | | Length Condition | $\vert \alpha \gamma \beta \vert \geq \vert \alpha A \beta \vert$ | "Non-contracting" | | Replacement String | $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ | $\gamma$ cannot be empty (except $S \to \varepsilon$) | | Recognizer | **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** | Tape bounded by $c \cdot \vert x \vert$ | | Closure under Union | **Yes** | Constructive proof via disjoint non-terminals | | Closure under Concatenation | **Yes** | Add new start $S \to S_1 S_2$ | | Closure under Kleene Star | **Yes** | Add $S \to S_1 S \mid \varepsilon$ | | Closure under Complement | **Yes** | (Not proven at introductory level) | | Closure under Intersection | **Yes** | (Not proven at introductory level) | | Pumping Lemma | Exists but complex | Out of scope for this introduction | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Programming Language Semantics:** Type systems that depend on surrounding context (e.g., scope rules in ML/Haskell type checkers) are *technically* context-sensitive. - **Compiler Front-Ends:** Some macro expansions in C/C++ depend on surrounding preprocessor directives. - **Natural Language Processing:** Agreement in gender/number across phrases (e.g., "the dog barks" vs. "the dogs bark") is a context-sensitive feature. - **Bioinformatics:** DNA/RNA secondary-structure prediction uses rules where the *folding* of one segment depends on its neighbor. # ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö The Canonical CSG for $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ > [!NOTE] The language $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n}\}$ is the **textbook example** of a language that is **NOT context-free** (proved via the CFG pumping lemma) but **IS context-sensitive**. A CFG cannot generate it because a CFG cannot enforce the **third equality** ($a$'s $= b$'s $= c$'s) simultaneously. ### 3.1.1 The Grammar Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where: $$V = \{S, B, C\}, \quad T = \{a, b, c\}, \quad S = S$$ Productions $P$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{(1)} \quad & S \rightarrow aSBC \quad \mid \quad aBC \\ \text{(2)} \quad & CB \rightarrow BC \\ \text{(3)} \quad & aB \rightarrow ab \\ \text{(4)} \quad & bB \rightarrow bb \\ \text{(5)} \quad & bC \rightarrow bc \\ \text{(6)} \quad & cC \rightarrow cc \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Why each production obeys the context-sensitive rule:** > - $S \to aSBC$: LHS length $= 1$, RHS length $= 4$. Ō£ō > - $CB \to BC$: Permutation of adjacent symbols ŌĆö non-contracting. Ō£ō > - $aB \to ab$: Length preserved ($2 = 2$). Ō£ō ŌĆö and note that $B$ is replaced **only when it follows $a$** (left context = $a$). > - $bB \to bb$, $bC \to bc$, $cC \to cc$: All length-preserving. Ō£ō ### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Derivation for $n = 2$ (target string: $aabbcc$) We will derive $aabbcc$ from $S$ in a fully explicit, step-by-step trace. | Step | Sentential Form | Rule Applied | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | $S$ | ŌĆö | Start symbol. | | 1 | $aSBC$ | $S \to aSBC$ | Expand $S$ once to get one $a$ and the $BC$ shell. | | 2 | $aaBCBC$ | $S \to aBC$ | Expand inner $S$ to terminate with $aBC$. | | 3 | $a a B B C C$ | $CB \to BC$ | Reorder the central pair $CB$ to $BC$ (context: $B$ on left, $C$ on right). | | 4 | $a \, a \, b \, B \, C \, C$ | $aB \to ab$ | **First $a$ meets first $B$** (left context $= a$) ŌåÆ becomes $ab$. | | 5 | $a \, a \, b \, b \, C \, C$ | $bB \to bb$ | The newly-formed $b$ now sits as left context for the remaining $B$ ŌåÆ becomes $bb$. | | 6 | $a \, a \, b \, b \, c \, C$ | $bC \to bc$ | The second $b$ acts as left context for $C$ ŌåÆ becomes $bc$. | | 7 | $a \, a \, b \, b \, c \, c$ | $cC \to cc$ | The newly-formed $c$ now acts as left context for the trailing $C$ ŌåÆ becomes $cc$. | Final string: $aabbcc$ Ō£ō (matches $a^{2} b^{2} c^{2}$) ### 3.1.3 Length-Monitor Table | Step | String Length | Action Type | |---|---|---| | 0 | $1$ | Start | | 1 | $4$ | Expansion | | 2 | $6$ | Expansion | | 3 | $6$ | **Permutation (length-preserving)** | | 4 | $6$ | **Length-preserving rewrite** | | 5 | $6$ | **Length-preserving rewrite** | | 6 | $6$ | **Length-preserving rewrite** | | 7 | $6$ | **Length-preserving rewrite** | > [!TIP] **Length remains non-decreasing** at every step. This is the **non-contracting property** in action ŌĆö visible in the trace. ### 3.1.4 General Derivation Sketch for arbitrary $n$ $$\begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aSBC \quad (\text{repeat } n-1 \text{ times}) \\ &\Rightarrow a^{n-1}(aBC)(BC)^{n-1} \\ &\Rightarrow a^{n-1} a B C (BC)^{n-1} \\ &\Rightarrow a^{n} B^{n} C^{n} \quad \text{(via } CB \to BC \text{ repeated } \binom{n}{2} \text{ times)} \\ &\Rightarrow a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} \quad \text{(via length-preserving left-context rewrites)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} d^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ A **stronger** language that requires even more bookkeeping. Productions: $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aSBCD \mid aBCD \\ DB &\rightarrow BD \\ DC &\rightarrow CD \\ CB &\rightarrow BC \\ aB &\rightarrow ab \\ bB &\rightarrow bb \\ bC &\rightarrow bc \\ cC &\rightarrow cc \\ cD &\rightarrow cd \\ dD &\rightarrow dd \end{aligned}$$ The intuition: $CB \to BC$, $DB \to BD$, $DC \to CD$ allow the $B, C, D$ symbols to *bubble* past one another, while the left-context rules perform the *final terminalization*. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Grammars Side by Side | Feature | Type 3 (Regular) | Type 2 (CFG) | **Type 1 (CSG)** | Type 0 (Unrestricted) | |---|---|---|---|---| | LHS Form | $A \to aB \mid a$ | $A \to \gamma$ | $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$ | $\alpha \to \beta$ (any) | | Length Condition | $\vert RHS \vert \leq 2$ | $\vert RHS \vert \geq 1$ | $\vert RHS \vert \geq \vert LHS \vert$ | None | | Recognizer | DFA / NFA | PDA | **LBA** | Turing Machine | | Membership | $O(n)$ | $O(n^3)$ (CYK) | **Decidable (exponential)** | Undecidable | | $a^{n} b^{n}$ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | | $a^{n} b^{n} c^{n}$ | ŌØī | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | | $a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} d^{n}$ | ŌØī | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | | Closure under Complement | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | ## 3.4 Mini-Derivation: $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{+}\}$ (Copy Language) The "copy language" $L = \{ww : w \in \{a,b\}^{+}\}$ is context-sensitive but not context-free. A sample CSG: $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aSA \mid bSB \mid T \\ TA &\rightarrow aT \quad \mid \quad ATa \mid \ldots \\ TB &\rightarrow bT \quad \mid \quad BTb \mid \ldots \\ AT &\rightarrow Ta \quad \mid \quad aT \mid \ldots \\ BT &\rightarrow Tb \quad \mid \quad bT \mid \ldots \\ T &\rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \text{(only after marking done)} \end{aligned}$$ (Intuition: $T$ walks through the first half, "recording" each symbol in a buffer that then writes the second half in reverse, then unrolls.) # ­¤ōĢ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chomsky Hierarchy ŌĆö Where CSG Sits ```mermaid graph TD classDef type0 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff classDef type1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff classDef type2 fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000000 classDef type3 fill:#c62828,stroke:#c62828,color:#ffffff classDef autom fill:#eceff1,stroke:#455a64,color:#000000 L0["L0 - Recursively Enumerable
Unrestricted Grammar"]:::type0 L1["L1 - Context-Sensitive
Linear Bounded Automaton"]:::type1 L2["L2 - Context-Free
Pushdown Automaton"]:::type2 L3["L3 - Regular
Finite Automaton DFA / NFA"]:::type3 A0["Turing Machine"]:::autom A1["Linear Bounded Automaton"]:::autom A2["Pushdown Automaton"]:::autom A3["DFA or NFA"]:::autom L0 --- L1 L1 --- L2 L2 --- L3 L0 -. Recognized by .-> A0 L1 -. Recognized by .-> A1 L2 -. Recognized by .-> A2 L3 -. Recognized by .-> A3 ``` ## 4.2 Derivation Flow for $a^{2} b^{2} c^{2}$ ```mermaid graph LR classDef state fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1 classDef finalState fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 classDef rule fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c S0["S"]:::state S1["aSBC"]:::state S2["aaBCBC"]:::state S3["aaBBCC"]:::state S4["aabBCC"]:::state S5["aabbCC"]:::state S6["aabbcC"]:::state S7["aabbcc"]:::finalState R1["S to aSBC"]:::rule R2["S to aBC"]:::rule R3["CB to BC"]:::rule R4["aB to ab"]:::rule R5["bB to bb"]:::rule R6["bC to bc"]:::rule R7["cC to cc"]:::rule S0 -- R1 --> S1 S1 -- R2 --> S2 S2 -- R3 --> S3 S3 -- R4 --> S4 S4 -- R5 --> S5 S5 -- R6 --> S6 S6 -- R7 --> S7 ``` ## 4.3 Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) ŌĆö Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR classDef block fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c classDef tape fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#e65100 classDef io fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#00695c,color:#004d40 Input["Input String x
on Tape"]:::io Tape["Tape of Length
c times x"]:::tape LBACore["LBA Controller
Read Write Move"]:::block LeftBound["Left End Marker"]:::tape RightBound["Right End Marker"]:::tape State["Finite
Control Unit"]:::block Accept["ACCEPT if
Reaches qf"]:::io Reject["REJECT if
Halts Non-Accept"]:::io Input --> Tape Tape --> LBACore LeftBound --- Tape RightBound --- Tape LBACore --> State State --> Accept State --> Reject ``` > [!NOTE] **Reading the LBA diagram:** The tape is *not* infinite like a standard Turing machine. It is *bounded* by two end markers whose separation is a **linear** function $c \cdot \vert x \vert$ of the input length. This boundedness is precisely what makes the membership problem for CSL **decidable**. ## 4.4 Production-Rule Anatomy Visual ```mermaid graph LR classDef ctx fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#01579b classDef nt fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c classDef rep fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 Alpha["alpha - Left Context
0 or more symbols"]:::ctx A["A - Single
Non Terminal"]:::nt Beta["beta - Right Context
0 or more symbols"]:::ctx Arrow["becomes"]:::ctx NewAlpha["alpha - Preserved"]:::ctx Gamma["gamma - Replacement
1 or more symbols"]:::rep NewBeta["beta - Preserved"]:::ctx Alpha --> A --> Beta A --> Arrow Arrow --> NewAlpha NewAlpha --> Gamma Gamma --> NewBeta ``` # ­¤ōō SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** ### Q1. Define Context-Sensitive Grammar. State the form of its production rules. [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** A **Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG)** is a formal grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ in which every production in $P$ has the form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$, where $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$. The key constraint is the **non-contracting property**: the length of the right-hand side is at least the length of the left-hand side. The non-terminal $A$ can be replaced by $\gamma$ only when flanked by the specific context $\alpha$ on the left and $\beta$ on the right. ### Q2. What is a Linear Bounded Automaton? Why is it associated with context-sensitive languages? [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** A **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** is a restricted Turing machine whose tape is bounded by two end markers, with the total tape length proportional to the input length $n$ (i.e., tape length $\leq c \cdot n$ for some constant $c$). It is associated with context-sensitive languages because **LBA accepts exactly the class of context-sensitive languages**, just as DFAs accept regular languages and PDAs accept CFLs. The bounded tape ensures the membership problem remains decidable for CSLs. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** ### ŌØ¢ Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A (a)** Define Context-Sensitive Grammar. Explain the **non-contracting property** with an example. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** A **Context-Sensitive Grammar** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is a Type-1 grammar in the Chomsky Hierarchy whose production rules are of the form $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$, with $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$. **Non-Contracting Property:** For every production $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$: $$|\alpha \gamma \beta| \geq |\alpha A \beta| \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad |\gamma| \geq |A| = 1$$ This means the length of the sentential form **never decreases** during derivation. **Example to illustrate:** In the production $aB \to ab$, the length is preserved ($2 = 2$). The non-terminal $B$ becomes terminal $b$ **only when it is preceded by $a$** ŌĆö demonstrating the context-sensitivity. If $B$ were preceded by some other symbol, the rule would **not** apply, even though $B$ is still in the string. **Consequence:** Since the start symbol has length 1, and lengths only grow, derivations of strings of length $n$ are bounded in search space ŌĆö making the membership problem **decidable**. --- **Q.A (b)** Construct a CSG for $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ and show the derivation for $n = 3$. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Grammar:** $$G = (\{S, B, C\}, \{a, b, c\}, P, S)$$ Productions: $$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad & S \to aSBC \mid aBC \\ (2) \quad & CB \to BC \\ (3) \quad & aB \to ab \\ (4) \quad & bB \to bb \\ (5) \quad & bC \to bc \\ (6) \quad & cC \to cc \end{aligned}$$ **Derivation for $n = 3$ (target: $aaabbbccc$):** $$\begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aSBC \quad \text{(rule 1)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaSBCBC \quad \text{(rule 1)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaaBCBCBC \quad \text{(rule 1)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaaBBCCBC \quad \text{(rule 2: } CB \to BC) \\ &\Rightarrow aaaBBCBCC \quad \text{(rule 2)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaaBBBCCC \quad \text{(rule 2)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaabBBCCC \quad \text{(rule 3: } aB \to ab) \\ &\Rightarrow aaabbBCCC \quad \text{(rule 4: } bB \to bb) \\ &\Rightarrow aaabbbCCC \quad \text{(rule 4)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaabbbcCC \quad \text{(rule 5: } bC \to bc) \\ &\Rightarrow aaabbbccC \quad \text{(rule 6: } cC \to cc) \\ &\Rightarrow aaabbbccc \quad \text{(rule 6)} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ --- ### ŌØ¢ Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B (a)** Compare **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** and **Context-Sensitive Grammars (CSG)** with suitable examples. Show that $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is **not** context-free. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | CFG (Type 2) | CSG (Type 1) | |---|---|---| | Production Form | $A \to \gamma$ | $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$ | | Context Required | No (free replacement) | Yes (must have $\alpha, \beta$) | | Length Condition | $|\gamma| \geq 1$ (no upper bound) | $|\alpha \gamma \beta| \geq |\alpha A \beta|$ (non-contracting) | | Recognizer | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) | Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) | | Power | Cannot generate $a^n b^n c^n$ | Can generate $a^n b^n c^n$ | | Pumping Lemma | Exists (simple) | Exists (more complex) | | Closure under Complement | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | Decidable Membership | $O(n^3)$ (CYK) | Decidable (exponential) | **Showing $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is not CFG:** By the **CFG Pumping Lemma**: if $L$ were context-free, there would exist a pumping length $p$ such that every string $s \in L$ with $|s| \geq p$ can be written as $s = uvxyz$ with: $$|vxy| \leq p, \quad |vy| \geq 1, \quad uv^{i}xy^{i}z \in L \;\forall i \geq 0$$ Choose $s = a^{p} b^{p} c^{p}$. By the pigeonhole principle, $vxy$ can span at most two of the three blocks (say, all of $vxy$ lies in the $a$ŌĆō$b$ region or $b$ŌĆō$c$ region, or fully within one block). - **Case 1:** $vy$ contains only $a$'s. Pumping $v, y$ yields $a^{p + k} b^{p} c^{p}$ for $k \geq 1$, which is **not in $L$** (counts unequal). ŌØī - **Case 2:** $vy$ spans $a$ and $b$. Then pumping increases both $a$ and $b$ counts, but the $c$ count stays at $p$ ŌĆö not in $L$. ŌØī - **Case 3:** Similar argument for the $b$ŌĆō$c$ boundary. ŌØī In all cases, pumping produces a string **not in $L$**, contradicting the lemma. Therefore $L$ is **not context-free**. $\blacksquare$ --- **Q.B (b)** Construct a CSG for $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{+}\}$, the "copy language." Briefly explain how the grammar works. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Intuition:** A CSG can use a non-terminal $T$ that *migrates* through the first half of the string while leaving markers. After the first half, the markers unroll the second copy. The grammar must track the **order** of symbols in the first half to write the second half in matching order. **Grammar Sketch:** $$\begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aAS \mid bBS \mid T \\ AT &\rightarrow Ta \\ BT &\rightarrow Tb \\ aA &\rightarrow Aa \\ bA &\rightarrow Ab \\ aB &\rightarrow Ba \\ bB &\rightarrow Bb \\ T &\rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \text{(only after the string is fully marked)} \end{aligned}$$ **How it works:** 1. **$S \to aAS \mid bBS \mid T$**: Recursively build the first half. Every time a terminal $a$ or $b$ is read, push a marker $A$ or $B$ to remember the symbol. 2. **$T$**: The "separator" non-terminal marks the end of the first half. 3. **$AT \to Ta$** and **$BT \to Tb$**: $T$ walks *backward* through the markers. As $T$ passes an $A$ (or $B$), it converts it to the corresponding terminal $a$ (or $b$) on the *right* side of $T$. This unrolls the second half in **reverse-then-correct** order. 4. **$aA \to Aa$**, etc.: These allow the $A/B$ markers to "bubble" past the first-half terminals so the markers group together. 5. **$T \to \varepsilon$**: Once $T$ has moved past all markers, the second half is fully written, and $T$ disappears. The result is a string of the form $w \cdot w$, satisfying $L$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Where students typically lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the context $\alpha, \beta$:** Writing $B \to b$ instead of $aB \to ab$ loses full credit ŌĆö the *context-sensitive* aspect is the whole point. > 2. **Violating non-contracting property:** Using a production like $aB \to a$ (contracting) instantly disqualifies the grammar from being a CSG. > 3. **Confusing CFG and CSG:** Remember, in CFG, $A \to aSb$ is valid (just one non-terminal on LHS). In CSG, the left context $\alpha$ and right context $\beta$ are **mandatory** structure. > 4. **Skipping the length-monotonicity argument:** Examiners love asking "why is the CSL membership problem decidable?" ŌĆö always mention the **non-contracting property** as the reason. > 5. **Incorrect ordering in $a^n b^n c^n$ derivation:** The $CB \to BC$ swaps must happen *before* the $aB \to ab$ rules. Wrong order leads to non-termination. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** > > - Ō£ģ CSG = **Type 1** grammar in the Chomsky Hierarchy. > - Ō£ģ Production form: $\alpha A \beta \to \alpha \gamma \beta$, with $|\gamma| \geq 1$. > - Ō£ģ The single non-terminal $A$ is rewritten **only** in the presence of the specific context $\alpha\beta$. > - Ō£ģ **Non-contracting property** is the defining feature ŌĆö RHS length $\geq$ LHS length. > - Ō£ģ Recognized by a **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** ŌĆö tape bounded by $c \cdot n$. > - Ō£ģ $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is **context-sensitive but not context-free** ŌĆö classic CSG example. > - Ō£ģ Derivation strategy: first expand (using $S \to aSBC$), then **bubble** ($CB \to BC$), then **terminalize** ($aB \to ab$, $bB \to bb$, $bC \to bc$, $cC \to cc$). > - Ō£ģ Membership problem for CSL is **decidable** (exponential time) ŌĆö unlike Type 0. > - Ō£ģ CSL is **closed** under union, concatenation, and Kleene star. > - Ō£ģ Applications: natural language agreement, macro expansion, type checking, bioinformatics. > - Ō£ģ The **Pumping Lemma for CSL exists** but is more complex than the CFG version (not required at intro level). > - Ō£ģ Three classic "beyond-CFG" examples: $a^n b^n c^n$, $w w$, and $a^{n!} \mid n \geq 1$. > - Ō£ģ LBA tape markers act as *physical* walls ŌĆö they cannot be crossed, enforcing the linear bound. > - Ō£ģ Be prepared to **construct** a CSG for a given language in the exam ŌĆö practice $a^n b^n c^n$ and $a^n b^n c^n d^n$ thoroughly.

topics.title: Turing Machines (TMs) as Language Acceptors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turing Machines (TMs) as Language Acceptors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 ŌĆö TURING MACHINES (TMs) AS LANGUAGE ACCEPTORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PECIT413 ŌĆö Module 4):** *Turing Machines (TMs) ŌĆö TM as language acceptors, transition function, instantaneous descriptions, language accepted by a TM, design of TMs for sample languages.*

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple mathematical model of computation defined as:

$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)$$

where each component is precisely defined below:

- **$Q$** ŌĆö A finite, non-empty set of **states** the machine can occupy.
- **$\Sigma$** ŌĆö A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the alphabet of the language to be recognised). It is strictly required that $B \notin \Sigma$ and $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$.
- **$\Gamma$** ŌĆö A finite set of **tape symbols** (the *tape alphabet*). It always contains the blank symbol $B$ and satisfies $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$.
- $\delta$ ŌĆö The **transition function** (or *next-move function*), formally a partial mapping:
$$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \longrightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$$

  Given the current state and the symbol scanned, $\delta$ specifies the next state, the symbol to overwrite, and the direction in which the readŌĆōwrite head moves (**L**eft or **R**ight).
- $q_0 \in Q$ ŌĆö The designated **start state** of the machine.
- $B$ ŌĆö The **blank symbol**, a distinguished tape symbol that initially fills every cell not containing the input word.
- $F \subseteq Q$ ŌĆö The set of **final (accepting) states**. The machine accepts an input precisely when it enters a state in $F$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU Board Definition):**
> A string $w \in \Sigma^{\star}$ is **accepted** by a TM $M$ if the computation, beginning in the *initial configuration* $q_0\,w$, eventually reaches a configuration in which the state is a member of $F$. Formally,
> $$w \in L(M) \iff q_0 w \ \vdash^{\star}\ \alpha\,q_f\,\beta \quad \text{for some } q_f \in F,\ \alpha,\beta \in \Gamma^{\star}.$$

### 1.2 Intuition ŌĆö A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "The Clerk with an Infinite Roll of Paper":**
> Imagine a very patient **clerk** seated at a desk with an **infinitely long scroll of paper** (the *tape*) divided into identical cells. The clerk can:
> 1. **Read** the single letter currently visible in one cell.
> 2. **Erase** that letter and **Write** a new one in its place.
> 3. **Move** the scroll one cell to the **Left** or **Right**.
> 4. **Change mood** ŌĆö i.e., move to a different "mental state" that determines what to do next.
>
> The scroll is initially blank except for the customer's input word written at the far left. The clerk follows a **rigid rule-book** (the transition function $\delta$): "If I am in *mood X* and I see *letter Y*, then write *Z*, shift *Left/Right*, and switch to *mood XŌĆ▓*." If the clerk ever enters a special *"Approved"* mood (a final state), the word is **accepted**; otherwise, the clerk may either **reject** or **loop forever**.

This analogy captures the three powers that distinguish a TM from finite automata and pushdown automata:
- **Infinite, random-access memory** (the unbounded tape).
- **Read/Write capability** on the memory.
- **Two-way head movement**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fire Fact:** A TM is the most powerful *acceptor* in the Chomsky hierarchy ŌĆö it recognises exactly the **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** languages. Languages it *decides* (always halts) are the **Recursive** languages.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Languages Recognised by TMs

| Class | TM Behaviour | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| **Recursive (Decidable)** | TM **always halts** on every input ŌĆö accepts strings in $L$, rejects strings not in $L$ | Decidable language |
| **Recursively Enumerable (Semi-decidable)** | TM halts and accepts strings in $L$; may **loop forever** on strings not in $L$ | Acceptable / Recognisable language |
| **Non-RE** | No TM exists that accepts it | Non-recognisable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Module 4 of PECIT413, our focus is on **constructing TMs that act as language acceptors** ŌĆö i.e., the machine enters a final state on strings in the language.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics of a TM

A TM processes input by repeatedly applying its transition function. The key concepts every KTU answer must demonstrate are listed below.

### 2.1.1 Configuration (Instantaneous Description, ID)

An **Instantaneous Description** captures *the entire state* of the TM at a single instant: the tape contents, the head position, and the current state. It is written as:

$$\alpha\,q\,\beta$$

where:
- $q \in Q$ is the current state,
- $\alpha \in \Gamma^{\star}$ is the tape string **to the left** of the head (including the symbol under the head as the last character of $\alpha$ ŌĆö but in KTU notation, the convention is $\alpha$ ends at the cell just before the head, and $\beta$ starts at the cell under the head).
- $\beta \in \Gamma^{\star}$ is the tape string from the head position onwards (the symbol under the head is the first character of $\beta$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Notation:** The initial ID for input $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ is $q_0\,w$ (head scanning $a_1$, all other cells blank).

### 2.1.2 The Move Relation (ŌŖó)

A single computation step is denoted by the symbol $\vdash$ (read: *"yields in one move"*). Suppose at some step the machine is in configuration $X a q Y$ (head scanning $a$, where $X,Y \in \Gamma^{\star}$), and the transition function dictates:

$$\delta(q, a) = (q',\, b,\, R)$$

Then the next configuration is:

$$X\, b\, q'\, Y$$

i.e., the symbol $a$ is overwritten with $b$, the head moves one cell Right, and the state changes to $q'$. The reflexiveŌĆōtransitive closure $\vdash^{\star}$ denotes zero or more moves.

### 2.1.3 Acceptance vs Halting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance by Final State (Standard in KTU 2024):** A TM $M$ accepts $w$ if there exists a sequence of moves leading to a configuration whose **state component is in $F$**.
> **Halting:** A TM halts when $\delta$ is undefined for the current (state, tape-symbol) pair.
> The two are *not* equivalent: a TM may halt in a non-final state (**rejection**), or it may enter an infinite loop without ever halting (**non-acceptance by looping**).

### 2.1.4 Recursive vs Recursively Enumerable ŌĆö A Worked Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> Every *recursive* language is recursively enumerable, but not conversely. The language
> $$L = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that halts on } w \}$$
> is the famous **Halting Problem**, which is RE but **not recursive** ŌĆö a guaranteed KTU-style viva question.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master the items in this table verbatim ŌĆö they are the syntax that examiners expect.**

| # | Notation / Concept | Formal Statement | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TM definition | $M=(Q,\Sigma,\Gamma,\delta,q_0,B,F)$ | Start any design answer with this 7-tuple. |
| 2 | Transition function | $\delta(q,X) = (p,Y,D),\ q,p\in Q,\ X,Y\in\Gamma,\ D\in\{L,R\}$ | Drawing state-transition tables. |
| 3 | Initial ID | $q_0\,w$ | Standard starting point for traces. |
| 4 | One-move step | $\alpha\,q\,X\beta \vdash \alpha\,Y\,p\,\beta$ if $\delta(q,X)=(p,Y,R)$ | Building ID chains. |
| 5 | One-move step (Left) | $\alpha\,Y\,q\,X\beta \vdash \alpha\,p\,Y\,X\beta$ if $\delta(q,X)=(p,Y,L)$ | Head moves left. |
| 6 | Acceptance | $L(M)=\{w\in\Sigma^{\star}\mid q_0 w \vdash^{\star}\alpha q_f\beta,\ q_f\in F\}$ | Defining the language. |
| 7 | Recursive | TM **halts** on every input, with accept/reject | Decidability proofs. |
| 8 | Recursively Enumerable | TM halts on all $w\in L(M)$, may loop on $w\notin L(M)$ | Acceptance proofs. |
| 9 | Tracking symbol | $X$ marks visited $a$'s, $Y$ marks visited $b$'s | Common in $a^n b^n$ designs. |
| 10 | Boundary symbol | $B$ (blank) used to detect tape end | Locating right end of input. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing $\delta(q,X) = (p,Y,D)$ where $D$ is left unspecified. Always write $D\in\{L,R\}$ **explicitly** ŌĆö partial marks are awarded for completeness of the 5-tuple output of $\delta$.

---

## 2.3 Why TMs are Needed ŌĆö Power Beyond CFLs

Finite Automata recognise **regular** languages. Pushdown Automata recognise **context-free** languages. But languages such as:

$$L_1 = \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1 \}, \quad L_2 = \{ ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{\star} \}, \quad L_3 = \{ a^{n^2} \mid n \geq 1 \}$$

are *not* context-free. None of the earlier formalisms (FA, PDA, CFG) can accept them. A TM, with its **unbounded tape**, can.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** TMs formalise the limits of *what a computer program can, in principle, compute*. Every modern programming language that can read/write unbounded memory is, in essence, a TM (modulo finite-memory limits). The TM gives us the yardstick for the famous **P vs NP vs Decidable** hierarchy that underpins theoretical computer science, compiler design, and cryptographic security proofs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-4 KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark TM question typically asks: *"Design a TM to accept the language $L = \{\ldots\}$. Show the transition function/diagram and trace the computation for a sample string."* The worked derivation below follows that exact pattern.

## 3.1 Worked Design ŌĆö TM for $L = \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1 \}$

This is the canonical non-context-free language. KTU examiners love it because it exercises every TM-design technique.

### 3.1.1 Informal Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Strategy (Intuition before Formals):**
> 1. **Mark** the leftmost $a$ as $X$ (a tracking symbol).
> 2. **Scan** right past all $a$'s, then past all $b$'s, looking for the **first** $c$. Mark it as $Z$.
> 3. **Reverse-scan** left, past all $b$'s, to the first unmarked $a$. Mark it as $X$.
> 4. **Reverse-scan** right, past all $b$'s, to the first unmarked $c$. Mark it as $Z$.
> 5. Repeat steps 3ŌĆō4 until **all** $a$'s are marked.
> 6. If, at the end, no unmarked $a$ or $b$ or $c$ remain, **accept**.

### 3.1.2 Formal TM Definition

We now convert the algorithm into the 7-tuple.

$$
M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)
$$

- $Q = \{ q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_f \}$ ŌĆö 7 states.
- $\Sigma = \{ a, b, c \}$.
- $\Gamma = \{ a, b, c, X, Y, Z, B \}$ ŌĆö input symbols + tracking symbols ($X,Y,Z$) + blank.
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state).
- $B$ ŌĆö blank.
- $F = \{ q_f \}$ ŌĆö single final/accepting state.

The transition function $\delta$ is given by the table below. Each row is of the form $\delta(\text{state},\ \text{symbol}) = (\text{next-state},\ \text{write-symbol},\ \text{direction})$.

> [!TIP]
> **Notation Convention:** $\mid$ separates columns. To keep the markdown table parser happy, we use a hyphen "-" rather than the pipe inside the rule.

| Current State | Scan Symbol | Next State | Write | Move | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $X$ | $R$ | Mark first $a$, move right. |
| $q_1$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $a$ | $R$ | Skip remaining $a$'s. |
| $q_1$ | $Y$ | $q_1$ | $Y$ | $R$ | Skip already-matched $b$'s. |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $Y$ | $R$ | Mark first $b$, move right. |
| $q_2$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $b$ | $R$ | Skip remaining $b$'s. |
| $q_2$ | $Z$ | $q_2$ | $Z$ | $R$ | Skip already-matched $c$'s. |
| $q_2$ | $c$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ | Mark first $c$, reverse direction. |
| $q_3$ | $Z$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ | Skip marked $c$'s while reversing. |
| $q_3$ | $b$ | $q_3$ | $b$ | $L$ | Skip $b$'s while reversing. |
| $q_3$ | $Y$ | $q_3$ | $Y$ | $L$ | Skip marked $b$'s. |
| $q_3$ | $X$ | $q_3$ | $X$ | $L$ | Skip marked $a$'s. |
| $q_3$ | $a$ | $q_4$ | $X$ | $R$ | Found next $a$; mark and go right. |
| $q_4$ | $X$ | $q_4$ | $X$ | $R$ | Skip marked $a$'s. |
| $q_4$ | $a$ | $q_4$ | $a$ | $R$ | (Defensive) skip extras if any. |
| $q_4$ | $Y$ | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $R$ | Skip marked $b$'s. |
| $q_4$ | $b$ | $q_4$ | $b$ | $R$ | Skip $b$'s. |
| $q_4$ | $c$ | $q_4$ | $c$ | $R$ | Defensive: pass through $c$. |
| $q_4$ | $Z$ | $q_4$ | $Z$ | $R$ | Skip marked $c$'s. |
| $q_4$ | $B$ | $q_5$ | $B$ | $L$ | Reached blank ŌĆö only $Z$'s after. |
| $q_5$ | $Z$ | $q_5$ | $Z$ | $L$ | Move left through $Z$'s. |
| $q_5$ | $X$ | $q_5$ | $X$ | $L$ | Pass over marked $a$'s. |
| $q_5$ | $Y$ | $q_5$ | $Y$ | $L$ | Pass over marked $b$'s. |
| $q_5$ | $B$ | $q_f$ | $B$ | $R$ | Returned to left end ŌĆö **accept**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The transition from $q_5$ on $B$ is the *only* way to enter $q_f$. Forgetting the $B$-symbol check before $q_f$ is the single most common cause of "machine accepts the wrong strings" errors.

### 3.1.3 Exhaustive Computation Trace for $w = a^3 b^3 c^3 = \texttt{aaabbbccc}$

We now walk through the machine step by step, using IDs. The notation $X\alpha\,q\,Y\beta$ means the tape to the left of the head is $X\alpha$, the state is $q$, and $Y\beta$ is to the right (with $Y$ under the head).

$$
q_0\, \texttt{aaabbbccc}
\vdash X\, q_1\, \texttt{aabbbccc}
$$

Explanation: Reading $a$ in state $q_0$, rule $\delta(q_0,a) = (q_1,X,R)$ applies ŌĆö write $X$, go to $q_1$, move right.

$$
X\, q_1\, \texttt{aabbbccc}
\vdash X a\, q_1\, \texttt{abbbccc}
\vdash X aa\, q_1\, \texttt{bbbccc}
$$

Explanation: In $q_1$, reading $a$ keeps the machine in $q_1$, writing $a$, moving right.

$$
X aa\, q_1\, \texttt{bbbccc}
\vdash X a a b\, q_2\, \texttt{bbccc}
$$

Explanation: Reading $b$ in $q_1$ triggers $\delta(q_1,b) = (q_2,Y,R)$ ŌĆö write $Y$, go to $q_2$.

$$
X aab\, q_2\, \texttt{bbccc}
\vdash X aab b\, q_2\, \texttt{bccc}
\vdash X aabb b\, q_2\, \texttt{ccc}
\vdash X aabbb\, q_2\, \texttt{ccc}
$$

Explanation: In $q_2$, reading $b$ writes $b$ and stays in $q_2$, moving right.

$$
X aabbb\, q_2\, \texttt{ccc}
\vdash X aabb b Y\, q_3\, \texttt{cc}
$$

Explanation: First $c$ is encountered ŌĆö $\delta(q_2,c)=(q_3,Z,L)$ ŌĆö write $Z$, go to $q_3$, move **left**.

$$
X aabb b Y\, q_3\, \texttt{cc}
\vdash X aabb\, Y\, q_3\, \texttt{c}
\vdash X aab b\, Y\, q_3\, \texttt{c}
\vdash X aab\, b Y\, q_3\, \texttt{c}
$$

Explanation: In $q_3$, symbols $b$ and $Y$ are passed over while moving left.

$$
X aab\, b Y\, q_3\, \texttt{c}
\vdash X aa\, b b Y\, q_3\, B \quad \text{(symbol under head is blank after }c\text{)}
\vdash X a\, a\, b b Y\, q_3\, B B
\vdash X\, a\, b b Y\, q_3\, B B B
\vdash B\, X\, q_3\, a\, b b Y\, B B B
$$

Explanation: Continue left through $Y$ and $X$ until the blank $B$ is reached (or, more accurately, the leftmost marked $a$).

> [!NOTE]
> The exact left boundary handling can be presented in many equivalent ways; the key requirement is that the head returns to the *next unmarked* $a$. KTU examiners accept any correct equivalent trace.

Continuing the matching round:

$$
B X\, q_3\, a\, b b Y B B B
\vdash B X X\, q_4\, b b Y B B B
$$

Explanation: $\delta(q_3,a) = (q_4,X,R)$ ŌĆö mark the second $a$.

$$
B X X\, q_4\, b b Y B B B
\vdash \ldots
$$

The machine now moves right past the $b$'s and $Y$'s, finds the next $c$, marks it $Z$, and reverses again. This **matching round** is repeated $n$ times. After $n = 3$ rounds, the tape reads $B X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B B B \ldots$ and the head reaches the blank while in state $q_4$:

$$
\ldots \vdash B X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z\, q_4\, B
\vdash B X Y Z X Y Z X Y\, Z\, q_5\, B B
$$

Explanation: $\delta(q_4,B) = (q_5,B,L)$ ŌĆö reached blank, enter $q_5$, move left.

$$
B X Y Z X Y Z X Y\, Z\, q_5\, B B
\vdash B X Y Z X Y Z X Y\, q_5\, Z B
\vdash \ldots \vdash B\, q_5\, X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B
\vdash q_5\, B\, X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B
\vdash B\, q_f\, X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B
$$

Explanation: $\delta(q_5,B) = (q_f,B,R)$ ŌĆö return to left end and **accept**.

$$
q_0\, \texttt{aaabbbccc} \ \vdash^{\star}\ B\, q_f\, X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance proof:** The computation terminates in a configuration whose state component is $q_f \in F$. Hence $\texttt{aaabbbccc} \in L(M)$. This is the *exact* KTU-trace format the examiner expects.

### 3.1.4 Rejection Trace ŌĆö $w = a^2 b^2 c^3$ (one extra $c$)

The same machine, when given $\texttt{aabbbccc}$ ŌĆö an *invalid* string ŌĆö will fail to find a matching $a$ during the second reverse-scan. The head will encounter a blank in state $q_3$ without finding another $a$, and since $\delta(q_3,B)$ is **undefined**, the machine **halts in a non-final state** ŌĆö the string is correctly **rejected**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Vignette ŌĆö Why undefined $\delta$ is the correct rejection mechanism:** In acceptance-by-final-state, the *only* way to accept is to enter $F$. Halting in a non-$F$ state is rejection. Looping forever is *not* rejection in the strict sense ŌĆö for recursive languages, the TM must halt on every input.

---

## 3.2 Compact Trace ŌĆö TM for $L = \{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1 \}$

| Step | ID | Rule Used |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0\, \texttt{aab}$ | Initial. |
| 1 | $X\, q_1\, \texttt{ab}$ | $\delta(q_0,a)=(q_1,X,R)$ |
| 2 | $X a\, q_1\, \texttt{b}$ | $\delta(q_1,a)=(q_1,a,R)$ |
| 3 | $X a Y\, q_2\, B$ | $\delta(q_1,b)=(q_2,Y,L)$ |
| 4 | $X\, q_3\, a Y B$ | $\delta(q_2,a)=(q_3,a,L)$ |
| 5 | $q_3\, X a Y B$ | $\delta(q_3,X)=(q_3,X,L)$ ŌĆö continue to blank |
| 6 | $q_3\, B X a Y B$ | head at leftmost blank |
| 7 | $q_0\, X a Y B$ | $\delta(q_3,B)=(q_0,B,R)$ ŌĆö restart |
| 8 | $X\, q_1\, a Y B$ | scan next $a$ |
| 9 | $X X\, q_4\, Y B$ | $\delta(q_1,a)=(q_4,X,R)$ then $\delta(q_4,Y)=(q_4,Y,R)$ |
| 10 | $X X Y\, q_5\, B$ | $\delta(q_4,B)=(q_5,B,L)$ ŌĆö reached blank end |
| 11 | $X X\, q_5\, Y B$ | Move left. |
| 12 | $X\, q_5\, X Y B$ | Continue left. |
| 13 | $q_5\, B X X Y B$ | Head at leftmost blank. |
| 14 | $q_f\, B X X Y B$ | $\delta(q_5,B)=(q_f,B,R)$ ŌĆö **accept**. |

> [!NOTE]
> This 14-state-style design is a **standard** KTU exemplar. The "X"ŌĆō"Y" tracking pattern (mark $a$, mark $b$, reverse) is reusable for any $a^n b^n$-style language.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Choosing a TM Strategy

| Strategy | Best Suited For | Key Idea | Marking/Tracking Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Match-and-Reverse** | $a^n b^n$, $a^n b^n c^n$ | Mark one pair per round, reverse head | $X, Y, Z$ |
| **Two-Pass Compare** | $ww$, palindromes | Mark first char, scan to find match, then symmetrically compare second half | $X$ for the "first half" marker |
| **Count-and-Verify** | $a^{n^2}$, $a^{2^n}$ | Mark $a$'s in groups of 1, 2, 4, 8, ŌĆ” | Multiple tally counters $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots$ |
| **Simulation** | $\langle M, w \rangle$ problems | Run another TM step-by-step | A "scratch tape" segment |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State-Transition Diagram ŌĆö TM for $L = \{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1 \}$

> [!NOTE]
> Each transition arrow is labelled with the convention **symbol-read / symbol-written, head-direction**. A self-loop is rendered as a node with a self-arrow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS([Start q0])
    nodeQ1[q1: scan a's then mark b]
    nodeQ2[q2: scan b's then reverse to leftmost a]
    nodeQ3[q3: skip left past X and Y, find next a]
    nodeQ4[q4: scan right to end, mark last a]
    nodeQ5[q5: check all marked, return to left end]
    nodeQf([Accept qf])

    nodeS -- "a, X, R" --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- "a, a, R" --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- "Y, Y, R" --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- "b, Y, L" --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- "Y, Y, L" --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- "a, a, L" --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- "X, X, L" --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- "B, B, R" --> nodeS
    nodeQ1 -- "B, B, L" --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -- "X, X, R" --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -- "Y, Y, R" --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -- "B, B, L" --> nodeQ5
    nodeQ5 -- "X, X, L" --> nodeQ5
    nodeQ5 -- "Y, Y, L" --> nodeQ5
    nodeQ5 -- "B, B, R" --> nodeQf
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each edge is annotated as `input / output, head-direction`. For example, the edge from `q1` to itself labelled `a, a, R` means: "On reading $a$, write $a$, move Right, and stay in $q_1$." This format is identical to the table you would draw in the exam.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Turing Machine

The diagram below shows how the components of a TM interact. The **finite control** issues commands to the **read/write head**, which mutates the **tape**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    finCtrl["Finite Control\n(Q, delta)"]
    head["Read/Write Head"]
    tapeL["Tape - Left Segment"]
    tapeC["Tape - Cell Under Head"]
    tapeR["Tape - Right Segment"]

    finCtrl -- "current state q, symbol X" --> head
    head -- "writes symbol Y" --> tapeC
    head -- "moves L or R" --> tapeL
    head -- "moves L or R" --> tapeR
    head -- "feeds symbol back" --> finCtrl
    tapeC -- "reads X" --> head
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"What happens if $\delta$ is undefined for a particular (state, symbol) pair?"*
> **Answer:** The machine **halts immediately** in its current configuration. If that state is in $F$, the input is **accepted**; otherwise, it is **rejected**. If the machine is *required* to decide a language, this is the only valid halting mode (no infinite loops).

---

## 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram ŌĆö TM for $a^n b^n c^n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["State q0: Mark first a as X"]
    step2["State q1: Skip a's, find first b"]
    step3["State q2: Mark first b as Y, skip b's, find first c"]
    step4["State q3: Mark first c as Z, reverse-scan left"]
    step5["State q4: Skip marked cells, find next unmarked a"]
    step6["Decision: another a present?"]
    step7["Yes - go to State q3 repeat match"]
    step8["No - transition to q5"]
    step9["State q5: Verify only X, Y, Z and B remain"]
    step10["Final State qf: ACCEPT"]
    step11["Reject path: undefined delta on B in q3"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6
    step6 -- "Yes" --> step7 --> step4
    step6 -- "No" --> step8 --> step9 --> step10
    step5 -. "no unmarked a" .-> step11
```
