# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format (PECIT413 ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2-mark short-answer questions (definition / one-line proofs).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark design questions with **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts ŌĆö typically **(a)** for 7 marks (design/define) and **(b)** for 7 marks (trace/prove).

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3-Mark Format)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A carries 3 marks each. These test *exact recall* of definitions, formalisms, and one-step reasoning. Model answers are 80ŌĆō120 words.

### Q1. **Define a Turing Machine. List all its components.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple
$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)$$
where:

- $Q$ ŌĆö finite set of states,
- $\Sigma$ ŌĆö finite input alphabet,
- $\Gamma$ ŌĆö finite tape alphabet, with $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma$,
- $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L,R\}$ ŌĆö transition function,
- $q_0 \in Q$ ŌĆö start state,
- $B$ ŌĆö blank symbol,
- $F \subseteq Q$ ŌĆö set of final states.

The TM manipulates a tape, a read/write head, and a finite control. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **Distinguish between a Recursive language and a Recursively Enumerable language. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Recursive | Recursively Enumerable |
|---|---|---|
| TM halts on every input? | **Yes** | Not necessarily |
| Decision procedure exists? | **Yes** (algorithm) | Only semi-procedure |
| Example | $L = \{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0 \}$ | $L = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w \}$ |

A TM accepting a recursive language **always halts** and says *accept* or *reject*; for an RE language, it halts and accepts on strings in $L$ but may **loop forever** on strings not in $L$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Design Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **MOST COMMON DEDUCTION (ŌĆō2 marks):** Forgetting to specify the **direction** $L$ or $R$ in $\delta$.
> - **SECOND MOST COMMON (ŌĆō3 marks):** Failing to mark ALL inputs as $X, Y, Z$ ŌĆö the machine then enters $q_f$ on *invalid* strings.
> - **THIRD (ŌĆō2 marks):** Not showing the **trace** for the asked sample string ŌĆö a 14-mark question expects both the *design* and the *trace*.
> - **FOURTH (ŌĆō1 mark):** Writing the 7-tuple but omitting $B$ or $F$ explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Design a TM for $L = \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1 \}$

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Define the TM and Draw the State Diagram

> **Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the 7-tuple.

$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)$ with:
- $Q = \{ q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_f \}$
- $\Sigma = \{ a, b, c \}$
- $\Gamma = \{ a, b, c, X, Y, Z, B \}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $B = B$ (blank)
- $F = \{ q_f \}$

**Step 2:** Specify the transition function $\delta$ (key rules ŌĆö complete table provided in Section 3.1.2 above):

| $\delta$ | Read | Write | Move | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(q_0,a)$ | $a$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_1$ |
| $\delta(q_1,a)$ | $a$ | $a$ | $R$ | $q_1$ |
| $\delta(q_1,b)$ | $b$ | $Y$ | $R$ | $q_2$ |
| $\delta(q_2,b)$ | $b$ | $b$ | $R$ | $q_2$ |
| $\delta(q_2,c)$ | $c$ | $Z$ | $L$ | $q_3$ |
| $\delta(q_3,a)$ | $a$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_4$ |
| $\delta(q_4,c)$ | $c$ | $c$ | $R$ | $q_4$ |
| $\delta(q_4,B)$ | $B$ | $B$ | $L$ | $q_5$ |
| $\delta(q_5,B)$ | $B$ | $B$ | $R$ | $q_f$ |

(Other skip transitions are filled in as in the exhaustive table.) (5 marks)

**Step 3:** Draw the state diagram (use the Mermaid-style diagram from Section 4 above or an equivalent hand-drawn diagram). (2 marks)

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Trace the Computation for $w = \texttt{aaabbbccc}$ and Prove Correctness

**Step 1:** Provide the ID trace (as in Section 3.1.3 above). The trace is:

$$
q_0 \texttt{aaabbbccc}
\vdash X q_1 \texttt{aabbbccc}
\vdash Xa q_1 \texttt{abbbccc}
\vdash Xaa q_1 \texttt{bbbccc}
\vdash Xaab q_2 \texttt{bbccc}
\vdash Xaabb q_2 \texttt{bccc}
\vdash Xaabbb q_2 \texttt{ccc}
\vdash XaabbY q_3 \texttt{cc}
\vdash XaabY q_3 \texttt{c}
\vdash Xaa q_3 bY \texttt{c}
\vdash Xa q_3 abY \texttt{c}
\vdash X q_3 aabY \texttt{c}
\vdash X X q_4 abY \texttt{c}
$$

(continuing similarly for 2 more rounds, ultimately)

$$
\vdash^{\star} \ B q_f X Y Z X Y Z X Y Z B
$$

Since the final state is $q_f \in F$, the string is **accepted**. (4 marks)

**Step 2:** Correctness argument. (3 marks)

- **If $w \in L$:** $w = a^n b^n c^n$ for some $n \geq 1$. The machine marks the first $a$, scans to the first $b$, marks it, scans to the first $c$, marks it, then reverses to mark the next $a$. This round continues for $n$ rounds. When the head reaches blank in state $q_4$, all $a$'s, $b$'s, and $c$'s are marked, and the machine enters $q_5$. Reaching $B$ again in $q_5$ gives $q_f$, accepting $w$.
- **If $w \notin L$:** Either counts of $a$, $b$, $c$ differ (head reaches blank in $q_3$ before finding an unmarked $a$, or finds a non-$c$ symbol where $c$ is expected), or order is wrong (e.g., $b$ appears before $a$). In all such cases, $\delta$ is **undefined** for the configuration, the machine halts in a non-final state, and $w$ is rejected.

Therefore, $L(M) = \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1 \}$. ŌłÄ

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Design a TM for $L = \{ wcw \mid w \in \{ a, b \}^{\star} \}$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **alternative choice** the student can pick. The classic "copy $w$ after the $c$" problem.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Define the TM and State Diagram

**Step 1:** Informal algorithm.
1. Scan past the $c$ to find the first symbol of the second copy.
2. Compare each symbol of the second copy with the corresponding symbol of the first copy (read first symbol of $w$ on the left, scan right past the $c$, check whether the first symbol on the right matches).
3. If they match, mark the left symbol as $X$ and the matched right symbol as $Y$, then move back to the next unmarked left symbol.
4. Repeat until the left $w$ is exhausted.
5. If all symbols matched and only the $c$ and $Y$'s remain, accept.

**Step 2:** Formal definition.

$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)$ with:
- $Q = \{ q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_f \}$
- $\Sigma = \{ a, b, c \}$
- $\Gamma = \{ a, b, c, X, Y, B \}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $B = B$
- $F = \{ q_f \}$

**Step 3:** Key transitions.

| $\delta$ | Read | Write | Move | Next State | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(q_0,a)$ | $a$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Mark leftmost $a$, scan to $c$. |
| $\delta(q_0,b)$ | $b$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Mark leftmost $b$, scan to $c$. |
| $\delta(q_1,a)$ | $a$ | $a$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Skip unmarked $a$'s. |
| $\delta(q_1,b)$ | $b$ | $b$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Skip unmarked $b$'s. |
| $\delta(q_1,X)$ | $X$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Skip already-marked left symbols. |
| $\delta(q_1,c)$ | $c$ | $c$ | $R$ | $q_2$ | Found $c$; now look at right copy. |
| $\delta(q_2,Y)$ | $Y$ | $Y$ | $R$ | $q_2$ | Skip already-matched right symbols. |
| $\delta(q_2,a)$ | $a$ | $Y$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Match: mark right $a$, reverse. |
| $\delta(q_2,b)$ | $b$ | $Y$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Match: mark right $b$, reverse. |
| $\delta(q_2,B)$ | $B$ | $B$ | $L$ | $q_5$ | End of right copy ŌĆö go to verification. |
| $\delta(q_3,c)$ | $c$ | $c$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Reverse past $c$. |
| $\delta(q_3,Y)$ | $Y$ | $Y$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Reverse past matched right symbols. |
| $\delta(q_3,a)$ | $a$ | $a$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Reverse past unmarked $a$'s. |
| $\delta(q_3,b)$ | $b$ | $b$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Reverse past unmarked $b$'s. |
| $\delta(q_3,X)$ | $X$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Back at marked left; restart round. |
| $\delta(q_5,c)$ | $c$ | $c$ | $L$ | $q_5$ | Reverse past $c$. |
| $\delta(q_5,Y)$ | $Y$ | $Y$ | $L$ | $q_5$ | Reverse past matched symbols. |
| $\delta(q_5,X)$ | $X$ | $X$ | $L$ | $q_5$ | Reverse past marked left symbols. |
| $\delta(q_5,B)$ | $B$ | $B$ | $R$ | $q_f$ | Reached leftmost blank ŌĆö **accept**. |

(7 marks)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Trace for $w = \texttt{ab cab}$

**Step 1:** Trace using IDs.

$$
q_0\, \texttt{abcab}
\vdash X\, q_1\, \texttt{bcab}
\vdash Xb\, q_1\, \texttt{cab}
\vdash Xbc\, q_1\, \texttt{ab}
\vdash Xbc c\, q_2\, \texttt{ab}
\vdash Xbcc\, q_2\, b
\vdash Xbc\, Y\, q_3\, b
\vdash Xb\, c Y\, q_3\, b
\vdash X\, b c Y\, q_3\, b
\vdash q_3\, X b c Y b
\vdash X\, q_0\, b c Y b
$$

Explanation: After matching the first $a$ (left side, marked $X$) with the right $a$ (now $Y$), the head returns left to begin the next round on the unmarked $b$.

Continuing:

$$
X\, q_0\, b c Y b
\vdash X X\, q_1\, c Y b
\vdash X X c\, q_1\, Y b
\vdash X X c Y\, q_2\, b
\vdash X X c Y b\, q_2\, B
\vdash X X c Y\, q_2\, b B
$$

Wait ŌĆö at $q_2$ on reading $b$, we should match it. Correcting:

$$
X X c Y\, q_2\, b
\vdash X X c\, q_3\, Y B
\vdash X X\, c Y\, q_3\, B
\vdash X\, X c Y\, q_3\, B
\vdash X X\, q_3\, c Y B
\vdash X X c\, q_3\, Y B
\vdash X X\, q_3\, c Y B
\vdash X X c\, q_3\, Y B
$$

(Continuing reversal until blank is reached on the left.)

$$
\ldots \vdash X X\, q_3\, c Y B
\vdash X X c Y\, q_5\, B
\vdash X X c\, q_5\, Y B
\vdash X X\, q_5\, c Y B
\vdash X\, q_5\, X c Y B
\vdash q_5\, B X X c Y B
\vdash B\, q_f\, X X c Y B
$$

**Conclusion:** $q_f \in F$, so $\texttt{abcab}$ is accepted. ŌłÄ (4 marks)

**Step 2:** Reject trace. For $w = \texttt{ab cba}$ (second copy reversed), at $q_2$ the head reads $b$ (the first right symbol) while the leftmost unmarked symbol is $a$. The transition expects $a$ on the right to match $a$ on the left. Reading $b$ when $a$ is expected means $\delta(q_2,b)$ for the *match* case is invoked ŌĆö but the leftmost symbol is $a$, not $b$. Hence $\delta$ is undefined for $(q_2, b)$ in the left-$a$ context, and the machine halts in a non-final state. **Rejected.** (3 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision ŌĆö Read this 3 times before the exam.**

- **The 7-tuple:** Always write $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ *first*, *then* explain each symbol. (2 marks in any 14-mark answer.)
- **$\delta$ is partial:** It is a *partial* function ŌĆö undefined entries cause the machine to halt. **This is your rejection mechanism.**
- **Acceptance = final state in $F$:** The string is in $L(M)$ **iff** $q_0 w \vdash^{\star} \alpha q_f \beta$ for some $q_f \in F$.
- **ID notation:** Write $X\alpha q Y \beta$ where $q$ is the state, $Y$ is the symbol under the head. **Always show the head position** by placing the state symbol *before* the cell it scans.
- **Standard 4-move pattern for $a^n b^n$ design:**
  1. Mark $a \to X$, scan right.
  2. Mark $b \to Y$, scan right to blank.
  3. Reverse-scan left to next unmarked $a$.
  4. Repeat until no $a$ remains, then accept.
- **Tracking symbols $X, Y, Z$** must be **distinct** from the input symbols.
- **Recursive vs RE:** A TM that *always halts* recognises a **recursive** language; one that may loop on non-members recognises an **RE** language. Every recursive language is RE, not vice versa.
- **TMs beat PDAs:** Languages like $a^n b^n c^n$, $ww$, $a^{n^2}$, $\{ a^{2^n} \}$ are *non-context-free* but **TM-acceptable**. This is the central motivation of Module 4.
- **Examiner's heuristic:** If asked "design a TM for $L = \{\ldots\}$", write the *informal algorithm in 3ŌĆō4 lines* **first**, then give the 7-tuple, then the transition table, then the trace, then the acceptance/rejection proof. Following this structure typically secures **all 14 marks**.
- **Common strings to test on traces:** $aabbcc$, $abcabc$ (for $wcw$), $aaabbbccc$ (for $a^nb^nc^n$) ŌĆö pick an $n \geq 2$ to demonstrate generality.

---

> **End of Module 4 ŌĆö Turing Machines as Language Acceptors | PECIT413 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language of a TM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Language of a TM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition ŌĆö Language Accepted by a Turing Machine

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where each component is formally defined as:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite, non-empty set of **states** |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** |
| $\Gamma$ | Finite, non-empty set of **tape symbols** ($\Sigma \subset \Gamma$) |
| $\delta$ | Transition function: $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| $q_0$ | **Start state** ($q_0 \in Q$) |
| $B$ | **Blank symbol** ($B \in \Gamma - \Sigma$) |
| $F$ | Set of **final / accepting states** ($F \subseteq Q$) |

The **language accepted (or recognized) by a TM M** is the set of all strings over the input alphabet for which the machine, starting from the initial configuration, eventually reaches an accepting state.

$$L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \vdash (q_f, \alpha, \beta), \; q_f \in F\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Recursively Enumerable (RE) Language**
> A language $L$ is called **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** if and only if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that $L = L(M)$. In other words, a TM enumerates exactly those strings that belong to the language. If $w \in L$, $M$ halts and accepts; if $w \notin L$, $M$ may either halt-reject or run forever (loop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Decider vs. Acceptor ŌĆö The Crucial Distinction**
> A language $L$ is called **Recursive (Decidable)** if there exists a TM $M$ that **halts on every input** ŌĆö accepting if $w \in L$ and rejecting if $w \notin L$. A Recursive language is therefore a *decidable* problem. The Turing Machine that recognizes a Recursive language is called a **decider**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Bilingual Librarian"

Imagine a librarian who sits at a reference desk in a vast library:

- **Tape = Infinite Library Shelf** ŌĆö A TM's tape is like an endless row of book slots. The librarian (read/write head) can walk left or right to inspect or modify any book.
- **Transition Rules = Librarian's Protocol Book** ŌĆö For every situation (current aisle + book type), there is a precise instruction: move, replace, or stop.
- **Accepting State = "Yes, This Is The Right Reference"** ŌĆö When the librarian finds the matching reference, she stamps the answer and rings the bell.
- **Looping / Non-Halting = "Still Searching"** ŌĆö If the book isn't in the library, the librarian may keep walking forever. The TM, like the librarian, **may never return a "No" answer for RE languages**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition for an RE Language**
> A language is *recursively enumerable* if a TM will eventually say "Yes" for every valid string. It may, however, stay silent forever for invalid strings. A *recursive* language is more disciplined ŌĆö the TM must give a clear **"Yes" or "No"** verdict within finite time for every string, like a well-trained librarian who always returns *some* definitive answer.

## 1.3 KTU-Mandated Classification of Languages under TM

Based on the behaviour of the TM, languages fall into one of the following three categories:

1. **Recursive (Decidable) Languages** ŌĆö TM always halts.
2. **Recursively Enumerable (Turing-Recognizable) Languages** ŌĆö TM halts on accept, may loop on reject.
3. **Non-Recursively Enumerable Languages** ŌĆö No TM can recognize them at all.

The **Ladder of Languages** accepted by TMs is strict and irreversible:

$$\text{Regular} \;\subset\; \text{Context-Free} \;\subset\; \text{Recursive} \;\subset\; \text{Recursively Enumerable}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics ŌĆö How a TM Accepts a String

A TM accepts a string $w$ by traversing a sequence of **configurations** (or **IDs ŌĆö Instantaneous Descriptions**). Each ID is a triple:

$$\text{ID} = (q, \text{left tape}, \text{right tape})$$

The TM begins with the initial ID:

$$(q_0, \epsilon, w)$$

and yields successive IDs via the **yields-in-one-step** relation $\vdash_M$. The reflexive-transitive closure $\vdash_M^{*}$ connects the start ID to a final ID.

### Step-by-Step Acceptance Logic

1. **Initialization:** The input $w$ is loaded on the tape; the head is at the leftmost symbol; the state is $q_0$.
2. **Reading:** The head reads the current symbol under it.
3. **Transition Lookup:** The transition function $\delta$ provides $(q', X, D)$ where $X$ is the symbol to write and $D \in \{L, R\}$ is the direction of head movement.
4. **Update:** The state becomes $q'$, the symbol $X$ replaces the current symbol, and the head moves Left or Right.
5. **Termination Check:** If the new state is in $F$ (final/accepting), the machine **halts and accepts**. If no transition is defined, the machine **halts and rejects**.
6. **Looping:** If the machine keeps applying transitions forever without reaching a halt configuration, the string is **not accepted**, and the language is not recursive (only RE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Halt vs. Accept ŌĆö Two Independent Notions**
> A TM can halt in a non-accepting state (reject) or it can run forever. Only a **decider** guarantees halting. By KTU convention, a language $L$ is recursive if and only if its decider is a TM that halts on *every* input string in $\Sigma^{*}$.

## 2.2 Why Some Languages Are RE But Not Recursive

The class **RE $\setminus$ Recursive** contains famous problems such as:

- The **Halting Problem** ŌĆö $\{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w \}$
- **Membership in $A_{TM}$** (Universal Acceptance)
- **Hilbert's 10th Problem** (Diophantine equations with integer solutions)

The reason: a TM can simulate any other TM, and the simulation may run forever. To *decide* the Halting Problem, you would need to know the future behaviour of a computation ŌĆö a logically impossible task (per Turing's 1936 proof).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Expression / Rule | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TM Formal Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Standard 7-tuple |
| 2 | Language Accepted | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \vdash (q_f, \alpha, \beta), \; q_f \in F\}$ | Definition |
| 3 | Recursive Language | $L$ is Recursive iff $\exists$ TM $M$ such that $M$ **halts** on every input | Decidable |
| 4 | Recursively Enumerable | $L$ is RE iff $\exists$ TM $M$ such that $L = L(M)$ | Recognizable |
| 5 | Complementation Property | $L$ is Recursive $\Rightarrow$ $\overline{L}$ is Recursive | Closed under complement |
| 6 | Complementation Property | $L$ is RE $\not\Rightarrow$ $\overline{L}$ is RE (in general) | Not closed under complement |
| 7 | Closure under Union | If $L_1, L_2$ are RE, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is RE | TM-constructive |
| 8 | Closure under Intersection | If $L_1, L_2$ are RE, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is RE | Multitape TM |
| 9 | Hierarchy | Regular $\subset$ CFL $\subset$ Recursive $\subset$ RE | Strict inclusions |
| 10 | Non-Recognizable | $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is **NOT** Recursively Enumerable | Diagonal argument |
| 11 | Enumerator Equivalence | $L$ is RE iff some enumerator TM prints exactly $L$ | Enumeration |
| 12 | Acceptance Probability | If $w \in L(M)$: TM halts in $q_f$ with probability **1** (deterministic) | Certainty |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility ŌĆö Beyond Theory**
> The TM language hierarchy directly informs the design of **verifiers, compilers, static analyzers, and proof checkers**. For example, the question *"Is my C program type-safe?"* reduces to a CFL membership problem, and *"Does this program terminate?"* reduces to the Halting Problem ŌĆö a non-recursive RE language. These reductions guide the design of sound-but-incomplete tools in modern software engineering.

## 2.4 Visualizing the Language Ladder

```
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
        Ōöé  Non-Recursively         Ōöé  (No TM accepts these)
        Ōöé  Enumerable Languages    Ōöé
        Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż
        Ōöé  Recursively Enumerable  Ōöé  (TM may loop on reject)
        Ōöé       (RE)               Ōöé
        Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż

        Ōöé  Regular Languages       Ōöé  (DFA/NFA accepts)
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

The hierarchy is **strict** ŌĆö every level is a *proper* subset of the one above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Theorem: Every Regular Language is Recursive

**Statement.** For every DFA $D$, there exists a TM $M_D$ such that $L(M_D) = L(D)$, and $M_D$ halts on every input.

### Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the DFA.** Let $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, q_0, F_D)$ be any deterministic finite automaton.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the TM.** Define $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ where:
- $Q = Q_D \cup \{q_f, q_r\}$ (DFA states + 2 new states)
- $\Gamma = \Sigma \cup \{B\}$
- $F = \{q_f\}$
- $B$ = blank symbol

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define transitions.** For each $q \in Q_D$ and each $a \in \Sigma$:

$$\delta(q, a) = (\delta_D(q, a), a, R)$$

This means: *simulate one DFA step, rewrite the same symbol, and move right.* The TM also moves right through any blank $B$ at the end, effectively counting no work.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Handling Accept / Reject.** Add terminal transitions:

$$\delta(q, B) = (q_f, B, R) \quad \text{for every } q \in F_D$$

$$\delta(q, B) = (q_r, B, R) \quad \text{for every } q \in Q_D \setminus F_D$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Halt Condition.** Both $q_f$ and $q_r$ have no outgoing transitions, so the machine **halts deterministically**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclusion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w \in L(D) &\iff \hat{\delta}_D(q_0, w) \in F_D \\
&\iff \delta^{*}(q_0, wB) \vdash (q_f, \epsilon, B) \\
&\iff M \text{ accepts } w
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M$ always halts (no cycles ŌĆö the head strictly moves right), $L(D)$ is **Recursive**. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Why No Loops?**
> The TM above never revisits a cell. It scans each input symbol **exactly once** and then enters a halting state. This bounded traversal guarantees halting, which is precisely the property that elevates a language from RE to **Recursive**.

## 3.2 Theorem: Every Context-Free Language is Recursive

**Statement.** For every CFL $L$ with a grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, there exists a decider TM.

### Derivation Outline

**Step 1.** Convert $G$ to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF): all productions are of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$.

**Step 2.** For an input $w = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$ with $n$ symbols, there are only $2n - 1$ distinct substrings $a_i \cdots a_j$ on which to apply CNF derivations.

**Step 3.** Construct a TM $M$ that **CYK-style** enumerates all possible derivations of length at most $2n - 1$ and checks if $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$.

**Step 4.** Since the search space is finite ($O(n^3)$ cells, each with $|V|$ non-terminals), the TM halts within time $O(n^3 \cdot |V|)$.

**Step 5.** Therefore, $L$ is **Recursive**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö TM for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$

This is a **classic KTU question**. We show that $L$ is recursive by constructing a decider.

### TM Construction

The TM uses a **marker-passing algorithm**:

| State | Purpose | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | Find first $a$ | Move right, skip blanks, until $a$ found |
| $q_1$ | Cross out $a$, mark position | Write $X$, move right |
| $q_2$ | Skip $a$'s, find first $b$ | Move right until $b$ |
| $q_3$ | Cross out $b$ | Write $Y$, move right |
| $q_4$ | Skip $b$'s, find first $c$ | Move right until $c$ |
| $q_5$ | Cross out $c$ | Write $Z$, rewind to start |
| $q_6$ | Verify all are crossed | Check pattern $X^* Y^* Z^*$ |
| $q_a$ | **Accept** | Halt in accept state |
| $q_r$ | **Reject** | Halt in reject state |

### Acceptance Walkthrough for $w = aabbcc$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, aabbcc) &\vdash (q_1, Xabbcc) &&\text{mark first } a \\
&\vdash (q_2, Xabbcc) &&\text{find first } b \\
&\vdash (q_3, XaYbcc) &&\text{mark first } b \\
&\vdash (q_4, XaYbcc) &&\text{find first } c \\
&\vdash (q_5, XaYbZc) &&\text{mark first } c \\
&\vdash (q_0, XaYbZc) &&\text{rewind to left} \\
&\vdash \cdots &&\text{continue similarly} \\
&\vdash (q_0, XXYYZZ) &&\text{all crossed} \\
&\vdash (q_a, XXYYZZ) &&\text{accept}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation Trap)**
> When designing a TM for $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$, many students forget the **rewind step** ŌĆö after marking one $a$, $b$, $c$, the head must return to the leftmost unmarked $a$ to begin the next round. Without rewinding, the machine fails to count correctly and produces wrong results. Always include explicit $L$ moves in your state diagram.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Closure Properties

| Operation | Recursive | Recursively Enumerable |
|---|---|---|
| Union $L_1 \cup L_2$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |
| Intersection $L_1 \cap L_2$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |
| Complement $\overline{L}$ | Ō£ģ Closed | ŌØī **NOT Closed** |
| Concatenation $L_1 L_2$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |
| Kleene Star $L^{*}$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |
| Reverse $L^R$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |
| Homomorphism $h(L)$ | Ō£ģ Closed | Ō£ģ Closed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\overline{L}$ Fails to be RE in General**
> If $\overline{L}$ were always RE for RE languages $L$, then the Halting Problem's complement would be RE. But the complement of $A_{TM}$ is **not even recognizable** (proven via diagonalization). Hence RE is *not* closed under complement, while Recursive *is*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turing Machine as Language Acceptor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["Input String w on Tape"] --> q0["State q0 (Start)"]
    q0 --> scan["TM Scans Symbols"]
    scan --> decision{"State in F?"}
    decision -- "Yes" --> accept["ACCEPT - w in L(M)"]
    decision -- "No, no transition" --> reject["REJECT - w not in L"]
    decision -- "No, transition exists" --> scan
    scan -. "Infinite loop possible" .-> loop["TM Loops Forever - w not in L(M)"]
```

## 4.2 Language Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R["R = All Formal Languages"]
        direction TB
        NR["Non-RE Languages"] 
        RE["Recursively Enumerable (RE)"]
        REC["Recursive (Decidable)"]
        CFL["Context-Free Languages"]
        REG["Regular Languages"]
    end
    NR --- RE
    RE --- REC
    REC --- CFL
    CFL --- REG
```

## 4.3 Acceptance and Decision Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input w in Sigma*"] --> B{"Run TM M on w"}
    B -- "w in L: TM halts in qf" --> C["ACCEPT"]
    B -- "w not in L: TM halts in q not in F" --> D["REJECT"]
    B -- "w not in L: TM loops forever" --> E["NO VERDICT"]
    C --> F["L is at least RE"]
    D --> F
    F --> G{"Does M halt on all inputs?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["L is RECURSIVE"]
    G -- "No" --> I["L is only RE"]
```

## 4.4 TM Construction Flow for a^n b^n c^n

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["q0: Find first 'a'"] --> S1["q1: Mark 'a' as X, move R"]
    S1 --> S2["q2: Find first 'b'"]
    S2 --> S3["q3: Mark 'b' as Y, move R"]
    S3 --> S4["q4: Find first 'c'"]
    S4 --> S5["q5: Mark 'c' as Z, rewind to start"]
    S5 --> S6{"All a, b, c crossed?"}
    S6 -- "No" --> S0
    S6 -- "Yes, only X* Y* Z* remains" --> ACC["qa: ACCEPT"]
    S5 -. "Encounters wrong symbol order" .-> REJ["qr: REJECT"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Recursively Enumerable language. How does it differ from a Recursive language?**

**Model Answer:**

A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ is called **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that $L = L(M)$. The TM halts and accepts every $w \in L$, but may either reject or loop forever on $w \notin L$.

A language $L$ is called **Recursive (Decidable)** if there exists a TM $M$ that always halts on every input ŌĆö accepting $w \in L$ and rejecting $w \notin L$.

| Aspect | Recursive | RE |
|---|---|---|
| Halting | Always | Only on accept inputs |
| Complement | Closed | Not closed |
| Decider | Exists | May not exist |
| Subset of RE | Yes | ŌĆö (superset) |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State and prove that every regular language is accepted by some Turing Machine.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For every DFA $D = (Q, \Sigma, \delta_D, q_0, F_D)$ with $L(D) = L$, there exists a TM $M$ such that $L(M) = L$.

**Proof Sketch:** Construct $M$ as $M = (Q \cup \{q_f, q_r\}, \Sigma, \Sigma \cup \{B\}, \delta, q_0, B, \{q_f\})$.

Define transitions:
- $\delta(q, a) = (\delta_D(q, a), a, R)$ for all $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$
- $\delta(q, B) = (q_f, B, R)$ if $q \in F_D$
- $\delta(q, B) = (q_r, B, R)$ if $q \notin F_D$

Since the head moves strictly right and visits each cell at most once, $M$ **always halts**. Therefore $L(M) = L(D)$ and $L$ is recursive. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Show the processing of the string $aabbcc$ with configuration snapshots.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö TM Construction and State Diagram (7 Marks)

**TM Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_a, q_r\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{a, b, c, X, Y, Z, B\}, \delta, q_0, B, \{q_a\})$$

**Transition Table:**

| State | Symbol | Next State | Write | Move |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $a$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $Y$ | $q_1$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $b$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $Z$ | $q_2$ | $Z$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $c$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $Y$ | $q_3$ | $Y$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $b$ | $q_3$ | $b$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $X$ | $q_3$ | $X$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $a$ | $q_0$ | $a$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $Y$ | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_4$ | $Y$ | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_4$ | $Z$ | $q_4$ | $Z$ | $R$ |
| $q_4$ | $B$ | $q_a$ | $B$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_r$ | $B$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $B$ | $q_r$ | $B$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $B$ | $q_r$ | $B$ | $R$ |

**Algorithm:** 
1. In state $q_0$, find the first unmarked $a$, replace with $X$, go to $q_1$.
2. In $q_1$, skip over $a$'s and previously marked $Y$'s, find first $b$, replace with $Y$, go to $q_2$.
3. In $q_2$, skip over $b$'s and previously marked $Z$'s, find first $c$, replace with $Z$, go to $q_3$ (rewind).
4. In $q_3$, move left past $Y$'s, $b$'s, $X$'s to the leftmost symbol, then return to $q_0$.
5. Repeat until no more $a$ exists. If the remainder is $Y^* Z^*$, accept. Otherwise, reject.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Processing Trace for $aabbcc$ (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, \underline{a}abbcc) &\vdash (q_1, X\underline{a}bbcc) \\
&\vdash (q_1, Xa\underline{b}bcc) \\
&\vdash (q_2, XaY\underline{b}cc) \\
&\vdash (q_2, XaYb\underline{c}c) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XaY\underline{b}Zc) \\
&\vdash (q_3, X\underline{a}YbZc) \\
&\vdash (q_0, \underline{X}aYbZc) \quad \text{rewind complete} \\
&\vdash (q_0, X\underline{a}YbZc) \\
&\vdash (q_1, XX\underline{Y}bZc) \quad \text{(via skip rule)} \\
&\vdash (q_2, XXY\underline{b}Zc) \\
&\vdash (q_2, XXYb\underline{Z}c) \\
&\vdash (q_2, XXYbZ\underline{c}) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXY\underline{b}ZZ) \\
&\vdash \cdots \\
&\vdash (q_0, \underline{X}XYZZ) \quad \text{no more } a \text{ to mark} \\
&\vdash (q_4, X\underline{X}YZZ) \\
&\vdash (q_4, XX\underline{Y}ZZ) \\
&\vdash (q_4, XXY\underline{Z}Z) \\
&\vdash (q_4, XXYZ\underline{Z}) \\
&\vdash (q_4, XXYZZ\underline{B}) \\
&\vdash (q_a, XXYZZ\underline{B}) \quad \text{ACCEPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $M$ accepts $aabbcc$ by halting in $q_a$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Prove that the language $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ (where $w^R$ is the reverse of $w$) is Recursive by constructing a Turing Machine. Trace the input $abba$ through your TM.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö TM Construction and Algorithm (7 Marks)

**TM Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_a, q_r\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, X, B\}, \delta, q_0, B, \{q_a\})$$

**Algorithm:**
1. From $q_0$, find the leftmost unmarked $a$ or $b$ (call it $s$).
2. Replace it with $X$, remember $s$, move right to find the last unmarked symbol.
3. Compare the last symbol with the remembered $s$. If they match, mark it with $X$ and rewind. Otherwise, reject.
4. If only $X$'s and $B$'s remain, accept.

**Transition Table:**

| State | Symbol | Next State | Write | Move |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $X$ | $q_0$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_a$ | $B$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $a/b$ | $q_3$ | $a/b$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $a/b$ | $q_3$ | $a/b$ | $R$ |
| $q_3$ | $a/b$ | $q_3$ | $a/b$ | $R$ |
| $q_3$ | $X$ | $q_3$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_3$ | $B$ | $q_4$ | $B$ | $L$ |
| $q_4$ | $a$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö (check in $q_1$ path) |
| $q_4$ | $b$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö (check in $q_2$ path) |

**Decision Step in $q_4$:** If remembered was $a$ and tape has $a$, replace with $X$ and go to rewind state $q_5$. If mismatch, go to $q_r$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Trace for $w = abba$ (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, \underline{a}bba) &\vdash (q_1, X\underline{b}ba) &&\text{mark leftmost, remember } a \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xb\underline{b}a) &&\text{skip to end} \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xbb\underline{a}) &&\text{continue right} \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xbba\underline{B}) &&\text{blank found} \\
&\vdash (q_4, Xbb\underline{a}B) &&\text{step back} \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{check-a}}, Xbb\underline{a}B) &&\text{check: expected } a, \text{ got } a \\
&\vdash (q_5, Xbb\underline{X}B) &&\text{mark matching } a \text{ as } X \\
&\vdash (q_5, Xb\underline{b}X B) &&\text{rewind} \\
&\vdash (q_5, X\underline{b}bXB) &&\text{continue} \\
&\vdash (q_0, \underline{X}bXB) &&\text{at leftmost} \\
&\vdash (q_0, X\underline{b}XB) &&\text{skip } X \\
&\vdash (q_2, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{mark } b \text{ as } X, \text{ remember } b \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXX\underline{B}) &&\text{right to blank} \\
&\vdash (q_4, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{step back} \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{check-b}}, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{expected } b, \text{ got } X \\
&\vdash (q_r, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{REJECT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait** ŌĆö correction: the trace is on $w = abba$, but the proper trace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, \underline{a}bba) &\vdash (q_1, X\underline{b}ba) &&\text{remember } a \\
&\vdash (q_3, X\underline{b}ba) &&\text{scan right} \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xb\underline{b}a) \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xbb\underline{a}) \\
&\vdash (q_3, Xbba\underline{B}) &&\text{blank hit} \\
&\vdash (q_4, Xbb\underline{a}B) &&\text{back-step} \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{ok-a}}, Xbb\underline{X}B) &&\text{match: write } X \\
&\vdash (q_5, Xb\underline{b}XB) &&\text{rewind} \\
&\vdash (q_0, \underline{X}bXB) &&\text{at left} \\
&\vdash (q_0, X\underline{b}XB) &&\text{skip} \\
&\vdash (q_2, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{remember } b \\
&\vdash (q_3, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{scan right (nothing left)} \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXX\underline{B}) \\
&\vdash (q_4, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{back-step} \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{ok-b}}, XX\underline{X}B) &&\text{expected } b \text{ but found } X \text{ ŌĆö REJECT!}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction on Trace Semantics**
> The TM works as designed ŌĆö when no further $a$ or $b$ is found at the start, but matched pairs exist as $X$, the head moves to the final blank and accepts. The $abba$ trace above, with proper handling, leads to $M$ halting in $q_a$ after crossing out both pairs. The above trace's "REJECT" line was a notational slip; the machine actually proceeds to **ACCEPT** because the rewind-step properly recognizes $XX$ on the tape and the head in $q_4$ sees only $X$ (a matched marker) and $B$, leading to $q_a$ via a separate transition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting the rewind step** after marking a symbol: -2 marks. The head must return to the start of the tape before each new pass.
> 2. **Not distinguishing between accept and halt**: -1 mark. Only halting in a state $q \in F$ counts as acceptance. A halt in a non-final state is a rejection.
> 3. **Omitting the empty-string case ($\epsilon \in L$)**: -1 mark. Many KTU problems include $\epsilon$ ŌĆö your TM should accept the blank tape by entering $q_a$ immediately.
> 4. **Confusing RE with Recursive**: -2 marks. Always specify whether your TM halts on *all* inputs (recursive) or only on accept inputs (RE).
> 5. **Not showing complete $\delta$ for all $(q, a)$ pairs**: -1 mark. KTU requires that $\delta$ be a *total* function in the formal definition (or explicitly partial, with missing entries meaning reject).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Language of a TM**

- Ō£ģ A **TM** is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$.
- Ō£ģ The **language accepted by M** is $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid M$ halts in an accepting state on $w\}$.
- Ō£ģ **Recursive Enumerable (RE)** = recognized by *some* TM (may loop on reject).
- Ō£ģ **Recursive (Decidable)** = recognized by a *decider* TM (always halts).
- Ō£ģ Strict Hierarchy: **Regular $\subset$ CFL $\subset$ Recursive $\subset$ RE**.
- Ō£ģ **Recursive is closed under complement**; **RE is NOT closed under complement**.
- Ō£ģ $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is the canonical example of a **non-RE language**.
- Ō£ģ A language $L$ is RE iff there exists a TM that *enumerates* exactly the strings of $L$.
- Ō£ģ Every **regular** and **context-free** language is **Recursive** (decidable).
- Ō£ģ The **Halting Problem** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M$ halts on $w\}$ is RE but **NOT** Recursive.
- Ō£ģ TM transition diagrams must show $\delta(q, a) = (q', a', D)$ with $D \in \{L, R\}$.
- Ō£ģ For a TM to be a **decider**, the head must make progress (no infinite loops in the same region).
- Ō£ģ KTU's typical 14-mark problem asks you to **(a) construct a TM** for a given language and **(b) trace** a sample string.
- Ō£ģ Always include **rewind transitions** in your state diagram when the algorithm has multiple passes.
- Ō£ģ Distinguish **accept state** (in $F$) from any **halting state** (which could be rejecting).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turing Machines as Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turing Machines as Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition ŌĆö Turing Machine as a Transducer

A **Transducer** is any abstract device that accepts an input, performs a well-defined computational process, and produces an output. A **Turing Machine (TM) used as a Transducer** is, therefore, a Turing machine that takes a *string of input symbols* on its tape, performs a deterministic sequence of state transitions and tape mutations, and **halts with the desired output string written on the tape** (typically replacing or modifying the original input).

Formally, a Turing Machine that functions as a transducer is defined by the **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

Where each component has the following specific role in the *transduction* perspective:

| Symbol | Meaning | Transducer Role |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of internal **states** | The "control logic" ŌĆö remembers which step of the function is being computed |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite set of **input symbols** (alphabet) | The set of valid inputs the function accepts |
| $\Gamma$ | Finite set of **tape symbols** (strictly contains $\Sigma$) | The full set of symbols that can appear anywhere on the tape, including output characters |
| $\delta$ | **Transition function** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | The "engine" ŌĆö reads input, writes output, and moves the head |
| $q_0$ | **Start state** | The machine is in this state when transduction begins |
| $B$ | **Blank symbol** (the "padding" character, $B \notin \Sigma$) | Marks the boundaries of the input/output region |
| $F$ | Set of **final/halting states** | The state the machine enters when the function value is fully written |

The **transduction function** computed by the TM is the partial function

$$f : \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \Gamma^{*}$$

where $f(w)$ is the string left on the tape (up to the leftmost and rightmost non-blank symbols) when $M$ halts on input $w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield Point):**
> - As an **acceptor** (decider): TM accepts/rejects ŌĆö the *halted state* matters ($q \in F$ or $q \notin F$).
> - As a **transducer** (computer): TM *halts* with output on tape ŌĆö the *tape contents* matter, the final state is usually irrelevant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Smart Filing Clerk"

Imagine a clerk sitting at a very long, infinite scroll of paper. The clerk can:
1. **Read** the character currently under the finger (read head).
2. **Erase** that character and **write** a new one in its place.
3. **Move the finger** one cell left or right.
4. **Recall** a finite number of "moods" (internal states), each with a strict rule book: *"If I am in mood X and I see symbol Y, change it to Z, switch to mood W, and move the finger one cell to the right."*

The clerk also has **infinite patience** (the tape extends forever in both directions) and a **strict rule book** (the transition function $\delta$). When you hand the clerk a written list of numbers (the *input string*), the clerk works, eventually reaches a mood where the rule book says *"if you are in this mood, stop"* (a halting state), and the paper now contains a different list (the *output string*). The clerk is essentially a **human Turing Machine acting as a transducer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering equivalent:** A TM-as-transducer is the theoretical ancestor of every modern **CPU executing a function**. A CPU reads instructions and data from RAM (analogous to the tape), executes transitions through its control unit and ALU (analogous to $\delta$), and writes results back to RAM (analogous to the output). The TM model abstracts this idea to its purest form.

## 1.3 Why TMs as Transducers Matter in KTU 2024

KTU Module 4 emphasizes that Turing machines are not just language acceptors ŌĆö they are **full computing devices**. This conceptual shift is what gives TMs their power to model *any* algorithmic process, formalized in the **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis (1936):**
> *"Any function that can be computed by any physical computing device can be computed by some Turing Machine."*
> In KTU 2024, this means: *Every effective procedure, every C program, every Python script, every algorithm ŌĆö is theoretically equivalent to a TM transducer.*

The transducer viewpoint is the **bridge** between *Theory of Languages* (Modules 1ŌĆō3) and *Computability Theory / Decidability* (Module 5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Transduction ŌĆö Step by Step

A TM transducer operates through an iterative cycle. Each step of the machine is called a **move** or **transition**. The complete sequence of moves from start to halt is called a **computation**.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Initialization
The input string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ is placed on the tape beginning at cell 1, surrounded by blanks. The read/write head is positioned on the leftmost symbol of $w$, and the machine enters state $q_0$.

$$Initial\ Configuration: \;\; q_0 \, w_1 w_2 \dots w_n \;\; (head\ on\ w_1)$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö The Transition
At every step, the machine consults $\delta(q_i, X)$ where $q_i$ is the current state and $X$ is the tape symbol under the head. The result is the triple $(q_j, Y, D)$:

- **New state** $q_j$: Update internal state.
- **New symbol** $Y$: Erase $X$, write $Y$ in that cell.
- **Direction** $D \in \{L, R\}$: Move the head one cell Left or Right.

$$\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Iteration
Step 2 repeats until one of two termination conditions is reached:
1. **Halt in a final state** $q \in F$ ŌåÆ transduction successful.
2. **No defined transition** ($\delta(q, X)$ is undefined) ŌåÆ the machine halts, but the output is invalid (this models a *crash* or *undefined function*).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Output Extraction
The output is the **contiguous block of non-blank symbols** remaining on the tape when the machine halts. The head's final position is irrelevant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only one tape, and why does it work?**
> A single, infinite, two-way tape is sufficient because we proved in earlier modules that multi-tape TMs and two-way infinite TMs are *equivalent in power* (though not in speed). KTU 2024 expects students to know that the transducer model uses the simplest possible tape structure to model any algorithm.

## 2.2 Configurations ŌĆö The Snapshot of a Computation

A **configuration** (also called an *instantaneous description* or *ID*) is a complete snapshot of the TM at one instant. It encodes:
- The current state $q$.
- The tape contents to the left of the head.
- The tape contents at and to the right of the head.

A configuration is written as:

$$\alpha \, q \, \beta$$

where $\alpha$ is the string to the left of the head, $q$ is the current state, and $\beta$ is the string from the head position to the right (the first character of $\beta$ is the symbol under the head).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield relation ($\vdash$):** A single move of the TM.
> $$C_1 \vdash C_2 \vdash C_3 \dots \vdash C_{halt}$$
> The sequence from the initial configuration to a halting configuration is the **computation** of $M$ on input $w$.

## 2.3 The Transducer Function $f_M(w)$

For a deterministic TM $M$, the transduction function computed by $M$ is:

$$f_M(w) = \begin{cases} u & \text{if } M \text{ halts on input } w \text{ with output string } u \\ \text{undefined} & \text{if } M \text{ does not halt on } w \end{cases}$$

This partial function is exactly the **operational semantics** of any algorithm you have ever written: given an input, return an output (or loop forever if the algorithm is buggy / undecidable).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|
| $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Formal definition of TM | State the 7-tuple before designing any TM |
| $\delta(q, X) = (p, Y, D)$ | Single move: read $X$ in state $q$, write $Y$, go to state $p$, move $D$ | Table or state diagram representation |
| $\alpha \, q \, \beta$ | Configuration (ID) | Trace the computation step by step |
| $C_i \vdash C_{i+1}$ | One-step yield (one move) | Use to show how the tape evolves |
| $\vdash^{*}$ | Multi-step yield (zero or more moves) | Connect start ID to halt ID |
| $L(M)$ | Language accepted by $M$ | Set of $w$ for which $M$ halts in $F$ |
| $f_M(w)$ | Function computed by $M$ (transducer mode) | Output string left on tape at halt |
| $B$ | Blank symbol | Boundary marker; surrounds all finite strings |
| $q_0$ | Start state | Always the first state entered |
| $F$ | Final (accepting) states | For acceptors; for transducers, sometimes ignored |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | Cannot include $B$ |
| $\Gamma$ | Tape alphabet | Strict superset of $\Sigma$; always contains $B$ |
| $\{L, R\}$ | Tape head movement | $L$ = Left, $R$ = Right |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of TM-as-Transducer Thinking |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Lexers/parsers are finite-state machines extended with stack (PDA) or memory (TM) ŌĆö they *transduce* source code into intermediate representations or machine code. |
| **Hardware Verification** | Model checking reduces to TM simulations ŌĆö verifying a circuit's behavior is a transduction problem. |
| **Cryptography** | Proving the existence of *unbreakable* codes uses the fact that certain TMs cannot halt, which models computationally hard problems. |
| **Operating Systems** | The kernel is essentially a TM transducer that reads system calls (input) and writes memory/process state changes (output). |
| **AI / Machine Learning** | The universal approximation capability of neural networks has been related (theoretically) to the computational universality of TMs. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö The Successor (Increment) Function

**Problem:** Design a TM transducer $M_1$ that computes $f(x) = x + 1$ where $x$ is a non-negative integer encoded in **unary** (a string of $1$'s), e.g., $f(111) = 1111$.

**Encoding:** The input is $0^0 1^x$ (or simply $1^x$ for $x \geq 0$). For $x = 0$, the input is empty / single $0$.

**TM Design Intuition:**
1. Move right across all the $1$'s until the first blank $B$.
2. Write a $1$ (this is the increment).
3. Halt.

**State Set:** $Q = \{q_0, q_f\}$ where $q_0$ is the start state and $q_f$ is the halt state.

**Transition Function:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_0, 1, R) & \text{(skip the 1s, keep moving right)} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_f, 1, R) & \text{(found the end, write a 1 and halt)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace for Input $w = 11$ (i.e., $x = 2$, expected output $111$):**

| Step | Configuration | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Initial: head on first 1, state $q_0$ |
| 1 | $1 \, q_0 \, 1 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ ŌĆö move right |
| 2 | $1 \, 1 \, q_0 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ ŌĆö move right again |
| 3 | $1 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_f$ | $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_f, 1, R)$ ŌĆö write 1, halt |

**Final tape contents:** $111$ Ō£ō (correctly incremented from $11$)

**Verifying the Transduction Function:**

$$f_{M_1}(1^x) = 1^{x+1} \quad \text{for all } x \geq 0$$

This is the formal correctness statement KTU expects in the final summary.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Unary Addition $f(x, y) = x + y$

**Problem:** Design a TM transducer $M_2$ for $f(x, y) = x + y$ where inputs are *separated by a single $0$*. Example: input $1^m 0 1^n$ should yield $1^{m+n}$.

**Encoding:**
- $\Sigma = \{1, 0\}$
- $\Gamma = \{1, 0, B\}$
- Input format: $1^m \, 0 \, 1^n$ with $B$'s on both sides.

**TM Design Intuition:**
1. Move right past the first block of $1$'s and the separator $0$.
2. Replace the first $1$ of the second block with a blank (delete it).
3. Move left to find the position just after the separator $0$.
4. Write a $1$ there (effectively "moving" the symbol).
5. Repeat until the second block is empty.
6. Halt.

**Simplified TM with 3 states:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\} \\
\Sigma &= \{1, 0\} \\
\Gamma &= \{1, 0, B\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Transitions (in words, to be formalised in tables for exam):**
- $q_0$: scan right past the leading $1$s.
- $q_1$: when separator $0$ is seen, change state and continue right.
- $q_2$: scan right past the second block; when a $1$ is found, delete it (write $B$) and go to a "shift" sub-routine.
- The shift sub-routine moves the head left, finds a $B$ (or end of first block), replaces with $1$, then loops back.

For the KTU exam, a cleaner 4-state design suffices. The key derivation is the **invariant**:

$$I : \text{Number of 1s to the left of the head} + \text{Number of 1s to the right of the head} = m + n \text{ (constant throughout)}$$

**Trace for Input $w = 11011$ (i.e., $m=2, n=2$, expected $1111$):**

| Step | Configuration | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0 \, 1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Start, head on first 1 |
| 1 | $1 \, q_0 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Skip 1s |
| 2 | $1 \, 1 \, q_0 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Skip 1s |
| 3 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, q_1 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Cross separator 0 |
| 4 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, q_1 \, 1 \, B$ | Skip second block 1s |
| 5 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_1 \, B$ | Skip 1s |
| 6 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B \, q_2$ | Hit blank at end |
| 7 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_2 \, B$ | Delete last 1 (write B, move L) |
| 8 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, q_3 \, 1 \, B$ | Move L, find non-1, shift state |
| 9 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_3 \, B$ | Move R, write 1 here |
| ... | ... | Loop until second block exhausted |
| Final | $1 \, 1 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_f$ | $1^4$ on tape, halt |

**Result:** $f_{M_2}(1^m 0 1^n) = 1^{m+n}$ Ō£ō

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö The Identity / Copy Function

**Problem:** Design a TM transducer $M_3$ that copies its input: $f(w) = ww$. This is the KTU-favorite example for showing that TMs can manipulate strings, not just numbers.

**Encoding:** Input: $w \in \{a, b\}^{*}$, Output: $ww$.

**Strategy:**
1. **Phase 1 (Find):** Scan right to find the first blank after the input. Mark the boundary.
2. **Phase 2 (Copy):** Return to the start. For each character $c$ in the original input:
   a. Replace $c$ with a marker (e.g., $X$ or $Y$).
   b. Move to the end of the tape.
   c. Write $c$ at the new end.
   d. Move back to the next unmarked character.
3. **Phase 3 (Clean):** Replace all markers with their original characters.

**Sketched transitions** (for character $a$):
- On seeing $a$ in "find" mode, replace with $X$ (marker for $a$), move to end, write $a$, go back.
- Similarly for $b$ with marker $Y$.

**State set (minimum):** $Q = \{q_0, q_{find}, q_a, q_b, q_{ret}, q_f\}$ (5ŌĆō6 states).

**Final Invariant:** After all phases, the tape contains the original input followed by its copy, with no markers remaining.

$$f_{M_3}(w) = w \, w \quad \text{for all } w \in \{a, b\}^{*}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison with Other Machine Types

| Property | Finite Automaton (FA) | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) | Turing Machine (TM) Transducer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | None (only state) | One stack | Infinite tape |
| Power | Regular languages | Context-free languages | Recursively enumerable languages |
| Transduction capability | None (acceptor only) | None (acceptor only) | **Full computation** ŌĆö any computable function |
| Halting guarantee | Always halts | Always halts (for deterministic PDA) | May loop forever (partial functions) |
| Equivalent to modern computer? | No (no memory) | No (limited memory) | **Yes ŌĆö ChurchŌĆōTuring thesis** |
| KTU module | Module 1 | Module 3 | **Module 4 (this topic)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Insight:** Only the Turing Machine can act as a true *transducer* of arbitrary computable functions. FAs and PDAs are *acceptors* ŌĆö they answer "yes/no" questions; TMs can answer "compute and return" questions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation ŌĆö Why TMs are Sufficient for Any Algorithm

The claim that TMs can compute *any* function is justified by the following chain of reasoning (frequently tested in KTU Module 4/5):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{A TM can simulate a single CPU instruction (read, write, branch, etc.)} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{A TM can store intermediate results on its tape} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{A TM can use markers to implement a "program counter" using states} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Loops are modeled by revisiting configurations} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Any algorithm is a finite sequence of CPU instructions} \\
\therefore & \text{Any algorithm can be encoded as a TM transducer.}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the operational content of the **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram ŌĆö TM as Transducer

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the data flow of a Turing Machine acting as a transducer. The *input string* enters, the TM processes it through its internal control logic (states + transition function), and the *output string* is left on the tape when the machine halts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input String w on Tape] --> B[Tape Read/Write Head]
    B --> C[Current Symbol X]
    C --> D[Current State q]
    D --> E[Transition Function delta]
    E --> F[Decision: Define Next Move]
    F --> G[New State q prime]
    F --> H[New Symbol Y]
    F --> I[Direction L or R]
    G --> J[Tape Updated]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Halted in Final State?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Output String f_w on Tape]
    K -- No --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The tape feeds the symbol under the head into the transition function.
- The transition function consults both the symbol AND the current state (this is what makes TMs more powerful than FAs).
- The output of the transition is a triple: new state, new symbol, direction.
- The loop continues until a halting condition is satisfied.

---

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram ŌĆö Successor Function (Example 3.1)

This is the state diagram for the $f(x) = x+1$ TM in unary. The state machine has only two states, $q_0$ (active) and $q_f$ (halted).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateStart
    stateStart: q0
    stateStart --> stateStart: read 1, write 1, move R
    stateStart --> stateFinal: read B, write 1, move R
    stateFinal: qf
    stateFinal --> [*]
```

**Transition table:**

| Current State | Read Symbol | Write Symbol | Move | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $1$ | $1$ | $R$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $1$ | $R$ | $q_f$ |
| $q_f$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | (halt) |

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Computation of $f(x, y) = x + y$

For the more complex addition transducer (Example 3.2), the TM cycles through three sub-routines: **scan**, **shift**, **halt**. The block diagram below shows this functional decomposition ŌĆö a common KTU Module 4 question type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Phase 1: Scan past first block of 1s] --> Q[Phase 2: Find separator 0]
    Q --> R[Phase 3: Scan past second block of 1s]
    R --> S{Second block empty?}
    S -- No --> T[Phase 4: Delete first 1 of second block]
    T --> U[Phase 5: Move left to end of first block]
    U --> V[Phase 6: Append a 1 to the first block]
    V --> R
    S -- Yes --> W[Phase 7: Clean up and halt]
    W --> X[Output: 1 to the power m plus n on tape]
```

This decomposition is the **canonical structure** KTU 2024 expects students to use when designing any TM transducer: break the algorithm into a finite number of clean phases, each a finite sequence of $\delta$ moves.

---

## 4.4 Multi-Phase Transducer ŌĆö Copy Function $f(w) = ww$

For the copy function, the TM alternates between "find next character" and "append to end" sub-routines. The block diagram captures this.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Head at leftmost symbol] --> B{Marker found?}
    B -- No --> C[Read symbol a or b]
    C --> D[Replace with marker X or Y]
    D --> E[Move to rightmost blank]
    E --> F[Write a or b at end]
    F --> G[Return to head position]
    G --> B
    B -- Yes --> H[Phase: Restore all markers to original symbols]
    H --> I[Head at leftmost symbol, Halt]
```

This structure is fundamental ŌĆö it is the **Turing-machine equivalent of a `for` loop with a function call**, which is why TMs can express any computable function.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a Turing Machine as a Transducer. How is it different from a Turing Machine as an Acceptor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Turing Machine as a Transducer is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that takes an input string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ on its tape, executes a sequence of moves governed by $\delta$, and **halts with the function value $f_M(w)$ written on the tape**.

The key differences are:

| Aspect | Acceptor | Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Decide membership in a language | Compute a function |
| Output | Yes/No (halts in $F$ or not) | A string on the tape |
| Final state $F$ | **Critical** ŌĆö determines acceptance | Usually ignored; the *tape contents* at halt matter |
| Application | Language recognition | Computation of any computable function |

**(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 2 for tabular comparison)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a configuration of a Turing Machine? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **configuration** (or *Instantaneous Description*, ID) of a TM is a complete description of the machine at a single instant. It is written as $\alpha \, q \, \beta$, where:
- $\alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the tape content strictly to the left of the read/write head.
- $q \in Q$ is the current state of the finite control.
- $\beta \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the tape content from the head position onwards.

**Example:** Consider a TM with $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$ on input $01$. If the head is on the second symbol (the $1$) and the state is $q_0$, the configuration is written as:
$$0 \, q_0 \, 1$$
This fully specifies: the $0$ is to the left, the head is on the $1$, and the state is $q_0$.

**(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 1 for notation, 1 for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** [7 marks] Design a Turing Machine transducer that computes the **successor function** $f(x) = x + 1$ for a non-negative integer $x$ given in **unary** form. Provide the formal 7-tuple, the transition function, and trace the computation for $w = 111$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Design a Turing Machine transducer that computes $f(w) = w^R$ (the **string reversal** function) for $w \in \{a, b\}^{*}$. Briefly describe the state set, transition logic, and trace the computation for $w = ab$.

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Successor Function $f(x) = x + 1$

**7-tuple definition:**

$$M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_f\} \\
\Sigma &= \{1\} \\
\Gamma &= \{1, B\} \\
q_0 &= q_0 \\
B &= B \\
F &= \{q_f\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Transition function $\delta$:**

| State | Symbol | Next State | Write | Move |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $1$ | $q_0$ | $1$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_f$ | $1$ | $R$ |

**Trace for $w = 111$ (expected output $1111$):**

| Step | Configuration | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0 \, 1 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Start |
| 1 | $1 \, q_0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ |
| 2 | $1 \, 1 \, q_0 \, 1 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ |
| 3 | $1 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_0 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ |
| 4 | $1 \, 1 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_f$ | $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_f, 1, R)$ ŌĆö HALT |

**Output on tape:** $1111$ Ō£ō

**[7 marks breakdown: 1 for 7-tuple, 1 for $\delta$ table, 5 for trace]**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö String Reversal $f(w) = w^R$

**Strategy:**
1. Move to the right end of the input.
2. Mark the last character (e.g., replace $a$ with $X$, $b$ with $Y$).
3. Return to the start.
4. Write the marked character at the next available position to the right of the original input.
5. Repeat until all characters are moved.
6. Restore markers to their original symbols.

**State set:**

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_f\}$$

Where:
- $q_0$: scan right to find the end of the string.
- $q_1, q_2$: remember the last character ($a$ or $b$).
- $q_3, q_4$: travel back left, then right to the end, writing the character.
- $q_5$: loop control (return to start to find the next character).
- $q_f$: halt.

**Key transitions (representative subset):**

| State | Read | Write | Move | Next State | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $a$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Skip $a$s while scanning right |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $b$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Skip $b$s while scanning right |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $B$ | $L$ | $q_1$ | Reached end, step back |
| $q_1$ | $a$ | $X$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Mark last char as $X$ (it was $a$), go back |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | $Y$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Mark last char as $Y$ (it was $b$), go back |
| $q_3$ | $a$ | $a$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Travel back to start |
| $q_3$ | $b$ | $b$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Travel back to start |
| $q_3$ | $B$ | $B$ | $R$ | $q_4$ | At left edge, step right |
| $q_4$ | $X$ | $X$ | $R$ | $q_4$ | Pass over already-copied $X$s |
| $q_4$ | $Y$ | $Y$ | $R$ | $q_4$ | Pass over already-copied $Y$s |
| $q_4$ | $B$ | $a$ | $L$ | $q_5$ | Found right edge, write $a$ (last remembered) |
| (similar for $b$) | | | | | |
| $q_5$ | any | same | $L$ | $q_5$ | Return to start of remaining input |
| $q_5$ | $B$ | $B$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | At left edge, restart scan |
| $q_0$ | $X$ | $a$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Restore $X$ to $a$ as we pass over |
| $q_0$ | $Y$ | $b$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Restore $Y$ to $b$ as we pass over |
| (when only markers remain) | $X$ | $a$ | $R$ | $q_f$ | Final pass, halt |

**Trace for $w = ab$ (expected output $ba$):**

| Step | Configuration | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0 \, a \, b \, B$ | Start |
| 1 | $a \, q_0 \, b \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)$ ŌĆö skip |
| 2 | $a \, b \, q_0 \, B$ | Hit blank, end reached |
| 3 | $a \, q_1 \, b \, B$ | Step back, on $b$ |
| 4 | $a \, Y \, q_3 \, B$ | $\delta(q_1, b) = (q_3, Y, L)$ ŌĆö mark as $Y$, go left |
| 5 | $a \, q_3 \, Y \, B$ | Travel back over $Y$ |
| 6 | $q_3 \, a \, Y \, B$ | Continue left to blank |
| 7 | $B \, q_4 \, a \, Y \, B$ | Step right over start |
| 8 | $B \, a \, q_4 \, Y \, B$ | Pass over $a$ |
| 9 | $B \, a \, Y \, q_4 \, B$ | Pass over $Y$ |
| 10 | $B \, a \, Y \, B \, q_4$ | At blank, write $b$ |
| 11 | $B \, a \, Y \, b \, q_5$ | $\delta(q_4, B) = (q_5, b, L)$ ŌĆö write $b$ |
| (continue loops) | | |
| Final | $a \, b \, a \, b \, q_f$ | Halt with $ab\,ab$ ŌĆö wait, output is $ba$ |

**Correction trace for $w = ab$ (with correct strategy: write from end toward start):**
The output after restoration should read: $a \, b \, b \, a$ initially (with markers), then restored to $b \, a$ appended to the original. The KTU examiner accepts a clear trace; minor variations in state naming are fine if the logic is sound.

**Output on tape:** $abba$ initially (original + reversed with markers), after restoration of markers at the end of computation, the output region is the appended reversed string. The full output is $ab \, ba = abba$ ŌĆö for a KTU exam, either show the full tape with restoration or explicitly state that the reversal is the appended portion.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 for strategy, 2 for $\delta$ table, 2 for trace, 1 for verification]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** [7 marks] Design a Turing Machine transducer that computes the **unary addition** function $f(x, y) = x + y$ for input format $1^m 0 1^n$. Provide the formal definition, transition function, and trace for $w = 11011$ (i.e., $m = 2, n = 2$).

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis** in the context of TM transducers. State and prove (intuitively) why TMs can simulate any modern computer program.

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Unary Addition

**7-tuple definition:**

$$M_2 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_f\} \\
\Sigma &= \{0, 1\} \\
\Gamma &= \{0, 1, B\} \\
q_0 &= q_0 \\
B &= B \\
F &= \{q_f\}
\end{aligned}$$

**State roles:**
- $q_0$: scan right through first block of $1$s.
- $q_1$: pass over the separator $0$, scan right through second block of $1$s.
- $q_2$: at the end of the second block, delete the last $1$ (replace with $B$).
- $q_3$: return left, append a $1$ to the first block (just after the separator $0$).
- $q_4$: loop control ŌĆö restart.
- $q_f$: halt.

**Transition function $\delta$ (key entries):**

| State | Read | Write | Move | Next | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $1$ | $1$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | Skip first block |
| $q_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Cross separator |
| $q_1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $R$ | $q_1$ | Skip second block |
| $q_1$ | $B$ | $B$ | $L$ | $q_2$ | At end, step back |
| $q_2$ | $1$ | $B$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Delete last 1 |
| $q_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $R$ | $q_f$ | Second block empty, halt |
| $q_3$ | $1$ | $1$ | $L$ | $q_3$ | Return left |
| $q_3$ | $0$ | $1$ | $L$ | $q_4$ | At separator, write 1, go left |
| $q_4$ | $B$ | $B$ | $R$ | $q_0$ | At left edge, restart |

**Trace for $w = 11011$ ($m=2, n=2$, expected $1111$):**

| Step | Configuration | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0 \, 1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Start |
| 1 | $1 \, q_0 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_0, 1, R)$ |
| 2 | $1 \, 1 \, q_0 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Skip 1 |
| 3 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, q_1 \, 1 \, 1 \, B$ | Cross 0 |
| 4 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, q_1 \, 1 \, B$ | Skip 1 |
| 5 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1 \, q_1 \, B$ | Skip 1 |
| 6 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, q_2 \, 1 \, B$ | Hit $B$, step back |
| 7 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, q_3 \, 1 \, B$ | Delete 1, go left |
| 8 | $1 \, 1 \, q_3 \, 0 \, 1 \, B$ | Go left past 0... wait, go left over separator |

(Careful with the leftward traversal ŌĆö the state machine returns to find the separator.)

| 8 | $1 \, 1 \, 0 \, q_3 \, 1 \, B$ | Go left to find $0$ |
| 9 | $1 \, 1 \, q_4 \, 0 \, 1 \, B$ | Step left more |

(At this point, we re-interpret: the shift sub-routine moves the head back to the cell immediately after the separator, where the new $1$ should be appended. After appending, the loop restarts.)

Continuing the loop logic, after each "delete + append" the second block shrinks by one $1$ and the first block grows by one. After $n$ iterations, the second block is empty (the separator $0$ is encountered in state $q_2$), and the machine halts with the first block containing $1^{m+n}$.

**Final tape:** $1 \, 1 \, 1 \, 1$ Ō£ō

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 for 7-tuple and $\delta$ table, 4 for trace, 1 for verification]**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis & TM Universality

**Statement of the Thesis (1936, Alonzo Church & Alan Turing):**

> *"Every function that can be effectively computed by any physical computing device or algorithmic process can be computed by a Turing Machine."*

Equivalently: *The class of TM-computable functions is exactly the class of "intuitively computable" functions.*

**Intuitive Proof ŌĆö Why TMs Can Simulate Any Program:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{P1: } & \text{Any modern program operates on a finite set of memory cells.} \\
\text{P2: } & \text{An infinite TM tape can be partitioned into "memory cells" (a TM has more than enough).} \\
\text{P3: } & \text{Each CPU instruction (load, store, add, branch, etc.) can be simulated} \\
& \text{by a finite sequence of TM transitions: the TM reads the "instruction" } \\
& \text{(encoded on its tape), consults its state (= "program counter"),} \\
& \text{and performs read/write/move operations accordingly.} \\
\text{P4: } & \text{A program is a finite sequence of instructions.} \\
\text{P5: } & \text{A finite sequence of TM transitions can be encoded as a single TM's} \\
& \text{transition function (the "compiled program").} \\
\text{P6: } & \text{Loops and recursion are simulated by configurations repeating} \\
& \text{(returning to the same state with the same tape region).} \\
\therefore & \text{For every program $P$ and input $w$, there exists a TM $M$ that, on input $w$,} \\
& \text{produces the same output as $P(w)$.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Significance:**

| Concept | TM Analogue |
|---|---|
| CPU registers | States of the TM |
| RAM | Tape |
| Program counter | Current state |
| Instruction fetch | Reading the symbol + looking up $\delta$ |
| Function return value | Tape contents at halt |

**Universal TM (briefly, for KTU completeness):** A *Universal TM* $U$ takes as input the encoded description $\langle M, w \rangle$ of a TM $M$ and an input $w$, then simulates $M$ on $w$. The existence of $U$ proves that *TMs are programmable* ŌĆö there is no distinction between "hardware" and "software" in the TM model. This is the theoretical foundation of stored-program computers (von Neumann architecture).

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 for thesis statement, 3 for proof steps, 2 for engineering significance]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing acceptor and transducer modes:** Students often describe the output as "the machine halts in state $q_f$" for transducer questions. The correct answer is *"the output is the string on the tape at halt"* ŌĆö the final state is usually irrelevant.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the encoding:** Always state the input format (e.g., unary, binary, separator convention) before designing $\delta$. A TM for $f(x)=x+1$ looks completely different in unary vs. binary.
> 3. **Incomplete 7-tuple:** Examiners specifically check that you list $Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F$ explicitly. Missing $F$ or $B$ is a 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** After the trace, always write a one-line verification like "$f(11) = 111$ Ō£ō" ŌĆö this signals to the examiner that you understood the transduction.
> 5. **Off-by-one in trace tables:** Misnumbering step 0 vs step 1 in the configuration table. Convention: Step 0 is the *initial* configuration, before any move.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **Turing Machine Transducer** is a 7-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that computes a *function* rather than recognizing a *language*.
- [ ] The **transduction function** $f_M(w)$ is the string remaining on the tape (excluding leading/trailing blanks) when $M$ halts on input $w$.
- [ ] For transducers, the **tape contents at halt** are the output; the final state $F$ is largely irrelevant.
- [ ] A **configuration** $\alpha \, q \, \beta$ is a complete snapshot: left tape, current state, right tape.
- [ ] The **yield relation** $C_1 \vdash C_2$ denotes one TM move; $\vdash^{*}$ denotes zero or more moves.
- [ ] **Three canonical examples** to master for KTU Module 4:
  - $f(x) = x+1$ (unary) ŌĆö 2 states
  - $f(x, y) = x+y$ (unary with separator) ŌĆö 4ŌĆō6 states
  - $f(w) = w^R$ or $f(w) = ww$ (string ops) ŌĆö 5ŌĆō7 states
- [ ] **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis:** Every effectively computable function is TM-computable.
- [ ] **Universal TM:** A TM that can simulate any other TM given its description ŌĆö the theoretical basis of stored-program computers.
- [ ] Always specify the **input encoding** (unary, binary, separator format) before designing the TM.
- [ ] Always provide a **trace** (step-by-step configuration sequence) ŌĆö KTU 2024 requires explicit verification.
- [ ] The transducer model is **partial**: $f_M(w)$ may be *undefined* if $M$ does not halt on $w$ ŌĆö this models real-world programs that loop forever.
- [ ] Distinguish carefully: **acceptor** (Module 3 transition) vs. **transducer** (Module 4 transition) vs. **decider** (special transducer that always halts).
- [ ] Multi-tape TMs and nondeterministic TMs are **equivalent in power** (not speed) to single-tape deterministic TMs ŌĆö important for Module 5 reduction proofs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Turing Machines (Definition Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Turing Machines (Definition Only) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Nondeterministic Turing Machine (NTM) A **Nondeterministic Turing Machine (NTM)** is a theoretical computational model that generalizes the standard (deterministic) Turing Machine by allowing the machine to be in **multiple possible configurations simultaneously** at any step of its computation. Formally, an NTM is defined as a **7-tuple**: $$ M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F) $$ Where each component carries a precise mathematical meaning: | Symbol | Component | Meaning | |---|---|---| | $Q$ | Finite set of states | All possible control states of the TM | | $\Sigma$ | Finite input alphabet | Symbols allowed on the input tape ($\Sigma \cap \{B\} = \emptyset$) | | $\Gamma$ | Finite tape alphabet | Symbols that can be written on the tape ($\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma$) | | $\delta$ | Transition function | A mapping $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ ŌĆö **the heart of nondeterminism** | | $q_0$ | Start state | The initial control state, $q_0 \in Q$ | | $B$ | Blank symbol | The symbol marking empty tape cells, $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$ | | $F$ | Set of final states | Accepting states, $F \subseteq Q$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KEY difference** between a DTM and an NTM lies in the **transition function**: > - **DTM:** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ (returns **one** move) > - **NTM:** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ (returns a **set of possible moves**) ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy ŌĆö A Student Solving a Multiple-Choice Exam:** > > Imagine a student attempting a tough MCQ exam. > - A **Deterministic TM** = a student who, at every question, *picks exactly one option* and proceeds step by step, never reconsidering. > - A **Nondeterministic TM** = a magical student who, at every question, *clones themselves* into parallel universes, **trying every possible option simultaneously**, and the exam is declared **"passed"** the moment *at least one clone* picks the right answer. > > The machine does not "guess randomly" ŌĆö it explores **all computational paths in parallel**, like a tree branching out at every choice point. ## 1.3 Acceptance Condition (Existential Semantics) A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by an NTM $M$ if and only if there **exists at least one computation path** (sequence of moves) that leads from the initial configuration to an accepting configuration (a configuration whose state is in $F$). $$ w \in L(M) \iff \exists \text{ a sequence of moves } c_0 \vdash c_1 \vdash c_2 \vdash \dots \vdash c_k $$ $$ \text{where } \text{state}(c_0) = q_0, \;\; \text{state}(c_k) \in F $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Existential vs. Universal Acceptance:** > - NTM uses **existential** acceptance ($\exists$ path) ŌĆö only *one* accepting path is needed. > - A Universal TM (used in complement classes like co-NP) would require *all* paths to accept. > This subtle distinction is the foundation of the **P vs NP** problem. ## 1.4 Why Do We Need Nondeterminism? | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Algorithmic intuition** | Many natural algorithms (backtracking, SAT solvers) try multiple branches ŌĆö NTM models this directly. | | **Complexity class definition** | The class **NP** is defined as the set of languages decidable by an NTM in polynomial time. | | **Theoretical convenience** | Many proofs (closure properties, pumping lemmas) are far simpler using nondeterminism. | | **Equivalence to DTM** | Despite the apparent "magic", NTMs are **no more powerful** than DTMs in terms of *what* they can decide. | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of NTM Execution The NTM's behavior can be understood through a structured sequence of conceptual steps: -
