**Step 1 ŌĆö Configuration Initialization:** The machine starts in configuration $c_0 = (q_0, w, 1)$, meaning state $q_0$, tape content $w$, and the read/write head positioned at cell $1$. - **Step 2 ŌĆö Reading & Branching:** In state $q$ scanning symbol $X$, the machine consults $\delta(q, X)$, which returns a **finite set** of possible triples: $$\delta(q, X) = \{(q_1, Y_1, D_1), (q_2, Y_2, D_2), \dots, (q_k, Y_k, D_k)\}$$ where $D_i \in \{L, R\}$. - **Step 3 ŌĆö Parallel Exploration:** The NTM **spawns clones** ŌĆö conceptually, it explores all $k$ possible continuations *simultaneously*. This is modeled as a **computation tree**. - **Step 4 ŌĆö Acceptance Decision:** The input string is **accepted** if and only if the root of the computation tree has **at least one leaf node** that is an accepting configuration. - **Step 5 ŌĆö Rejection Decision:** The string is **rejected** if and only if **all branches** of the computation tree terminate in non-accepting configurations (or run forever). ## 2.2 The Computation Tree Model Every NTM execution on input $w$ can be visualized as a tree: - **Root:** Initial configuration $c_0$. - **Internal nodes:** Non-terminal configurations where $\delta$ returns more than one move. - **Leaves:** Terminal (halting) configurations, each labeled as *accept* or *reject*. $$ \text{Depth of tree} = \text{Number of moves on that path} $$ $$ \text{Branching factor} \leq \vert \delta(q, X) \vert \leq \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert \cdot 2 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Nondeterminism" is a Misnomer (Historical Note):** > The name is misleading ŌĆö the NTM is not random, and it does not guess. It deterministically explores *all* branches. The word "nondeterministic" actually refers to the **lack of a unique next move**, not to any probabilistic behavior. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Key Insight | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | NTM Formal Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Same 7 components as DTM | | 2 | NTM Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ | Returns a **set** of possible moves | | 3 | DTM Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | Returns a **single** move | | 4 | Acceptance Condition | $w \in L(M) \iff \exists$ accepting computation path | Existential ($\exists$) semantics | | 5 | Deterministic Simulation Bound (Time) | $\text{Time}_{DTM}(w) \leq 2^{O(\text{Time}_{NTM}(w))}$ | Exponential blowup possible | | 6 | Deterministic Simulation Bound (Space) | $\text{Space}_{DTM}(w) = \text{Space}_{NTM}(w)$ | Space is preserved | | 7 | Branching Factor Upper Bound | $b \leq \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert \cdot 2$ | Finite branching | | 8 | Equivalence Theorem | $L(M_{NTM}) = L(M_{DTM})$ for some DTM $M_{DTM}$ | Same class of decidable languages | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SAT Solvers & Model Checkers:** Modern SAT solvers (e.g., DPLL, CDCL algorithms) emulate nondeterministic search using backtracking ŌĆö directly inspired by NTM. - **Compiler Optimization:** Certain code-analysis problems (e.g., pointer analysis, dataflow analysis) are formulated as nondeterministic reachability problems. - **Cryptographic Security:** Many cryptographic protocols (e.g., zero-knowledge proofs) rely on the **P $\neq$ NP** assumption, which is fundamentally about the gap between deterministic and nondeterministic computation. - **AI Planning & Search:** NTM-like parallelism underpins non-deterministic planning algorithms in robotics and AI. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Breakdown of the NTM Definition Since the topic is **"Definition Only"**, the analytical work consists of meticulously unpacking every element of the 7-tuple and the transition function. Each step is shown in full. ### Step 1 ŌĆö The State Set $Q$ $$ Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n\} $$ This is a **finite, nonempty** set of internal control states. The machine always "remembers" exactly one element of $Q$ at any instant. ### Step 2 ŌĆö The Input Alphabet $\Sigma$ $$ \Sigma = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\}, \quad \Sigma \cap \{B\} = \emptyset $$ This is the alphabet over which input strings are formed. The blank symbol $B$ is explicitly **excluded** from $\Sigma$ to avoid ambiguity between "input data" and "empty cell". ### Step 3 ŌĆö The Tape Alphabet $\Gamma$ $$ \Sigma \subseteq \Gamma, \quad B \in \Gamma $$ The tape alphabet is a **superset** of $\Sigma$ and includes the blank symbol. This allows the machine to write auxiliary symbols during computation. ### Step 4 ŌĆö The Transition Function $\delta$ (Most Critical Step) For a DTM: $$ \delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D) $$ meaning: in state $q$ reading $X$, go to state $q'$, write $Y$, and move the head in direction $D$. For an NTM: $$ \delta(q, X) = \{(q'_1, Y_1, D_1), (q'_2, Y_2, D_2), \dots, (q'_k, Y_k, D_k)\} $$ The set returned may contain: - **0 elements** ŌåÆ the NTM **halts and rejects** on this configuration (no valid move). - **1 element** ŌåÆ behaves identically to a DTM. - **$\geq 2$ elements** ŌåÆ the NTM **branches** into multiple parallel computation paths. ### Step 5 ŌĆö The Start State $q_0$ $$ q_0 \in Q $$ The computation begins with the TM in state $q_0$, the head on the leftmost cell of the input string. ### Step 6 ŌĆö The Blank Symbol $B$ $$ B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma $$ Every cell on the tape that is not part of the input (and has not been written to) is presumed to hold the symbol $B$. ### Step 7 ŌĆö The Set of Final (Accepting) States $F$ $$ F \subseteq Q $$ When the NTM enters any state in $F$, the current computation path is marked as **accepting**. Recall that the overall string is accepted if *at least one* path is accepting. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: DTM vs NTM Side-by-Side | Property | Deterministic TM (DTM) | Nondeterministic TM (NTM) | |---|---|---| | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ | | Return type of $\delta$ | A single move (a triple) | A set of possible moves (a set of triples) | | Execution model | Single linear sequence of moves | Tree of computation paths | | Acceptance semantics | Halts in $F$ ŌćÆ accept | **Exists** path halting in $F$ ŌćÆ accept | | Computational power | Recursively enumerable | Recursively enumerable (same) | | Time complexity class | $P$ (polynomial-time DTM) | $NP$ (polynomial-time NTM) | | Space complexity class | $PSPACE$ | $NPSPACE = PSPACE$ (Savitch's Theorem) | | Physical realizability | Directly realizable | Realized via DTM simulation (BFS/DFS over tree) | ## 3.3 Logical Derivation of the Acceptance Condition The acceptance of input $w$ by NTM $M$ can be derived as follows: Starting from the initial configuration $c_0$: $$ c_0 \vdash_M c_1 \vdash_M c_2 \vdash_M \dots \vdash_M c_k $$ Each $\vdash_M$ step uses **one** element of the set returned by $\delta$. The NTM accepts $w$ if: $$ \exists \;\; (c_0, c_1, \dots, c_k) \;\; \text{such that} \;\; \text{state}(c_k) \in F $$ Conversely, the NTM rejects $w$ if: $$ \forall \;\; \text{computation paths}, \;\; \text{state}(\text{leaf}) \notin F $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subtle Pitfall:** Note that the NTM does *not* accept if there is "no rejecting path" ŌĆö it accepts only when there is **a positive witness (an accepting path)**. This asymmetry is the root of the **P vs NP** open problem. ## 3.4 Illustration: A Concrete NTM Transition Example Let $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\Gamma = \{0, 1, B\}$, and define: $$ \delta(q_0, 0) = \{(q_1, 1, R), (q_2, 0, R)\} $$ **Interpretation:** - In state $q_0$ reading symbol $0$, the NTM has **two** legal choices: 1. Go to state $q_1$, write $1$, move Right. 2. Go to state $q_2$, write $0$, move Right. - The NTM conceptually **forks** into two clones, one following each choice. - A string is accepted if **at least one clone** eventually reaches a final state. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Nondeterministic Turing Machine The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the high-level architecture of an NTM, including the conceptual **nondeterministic branching unit** that is absent in a DTM. ```mermaid flowchart LR inputBlock["Input String w"] --> tapeUnit["Infinite Tape
cells with symbols from Gamma"] tapeUnit --> headUnit["Read / Write Head
scans one cell at a time"] headUnit --> ctrlUnit["Control Unit
holds state q in Q"] ctrlUnit --> transFunc{"Transition Function delta
Q x Gamma to 2 to the power Q x Gamma x L R"} transFunc --> branchA["Branch 1
move q1, Y1, D1"] transFunc --> branchB["Branch 2
move q2, Y2, D2"] transFunc --> branchC["Branch 3
move q3, Y3, D3"] branchA --> compTree["Computation Tree
all parallel paths"] branchB --> compTree branchC --> compTree compTree --> acceptCheck{"Exists path with
state in F ?"} acceptCheck -- YES --> acceptOut["ACCEPT w"] acceptCheck -- NO --> rejectOut["REJECT w"] ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart ŌĆö NTM Acceptance Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["Start: Input w on tape, state q0"] --> readSym["Read current tape symbol X"] readSym --> lookup["Compute delta of q and X = set of moves"] lookup --> branchDecision{"Is set empty ?"} branchDecision -- YES --> haltReject["Halt and reject this path"] branchDecision -- NO --> spawn["Spawn one clone per move in set"] spawn --> explore["Each clone executes one move:
new state, write symbol, shift head"] explore --> finalCheck{"Is new state in F ?"} finalCheck -- YES --> haltAccept["Halt and accept this path"] finalCheck -- NO --> readSym haltAccept --> overallCheck{"Exists any accepting path ?"} haltReject --> overallCheck overallCheck -- YES --> finalAccept["String w is ACCEPTED"] overallCheck -- NO --> finalReject["String w is REJECTED"] ``` ## 4.3 DTM vs NTM ŌĆö Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DTM["Deterministic TM"] dIn["Input w"] --> dState["State q"] dState --> dDelta["delta : Q x Gamma to Q x Gamma x L R"] dDelta --> dOut["Single Move"] dOut --> dState end subgraph NTM["Nondeterministic TM"] nIn["Input w"] --> nState["State q"] nState --> nDelta["delta : Q x Gamma to 2 to the power Q x Gamma x L R"] nDelta --> nSet["Set of Moves"] nSet --> nPath1["Path 1"] nSet --> nPath2["Path 2"] nSet --> nPathN["Path N"] nPath1 --> nState nPath2 --> nState nPathN --> nState end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** > - In the **DTM** subgraph, $\delta$ produces a single deterministic move ŌĆö only one path exists. > - In the **NTM** subgraph, $\delta$ produces a *set* of moves, each spawning a parallel computation path. > - The NTM accepts the input if **any one of these parallel paths** reaches an accepting state. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define a Nondeterministic Turing Machine. Write its formal 7-tuple definition and explain how it differs from a Deterministic Turing Machine.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A Nondeterministic Turing Machine (NTM) is a theoretical model of computation in which the machine may have multiple legal moves from a single configuration, and the input is accepted if at least one sequence of choices leads to acceptance. $$ M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F) $$ The key difference is in the **transition function**: - **DTM:** $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ ŌĆö a single move. - **NTM:** $\delta(q, X) = \{(q'_1, Y_1, D_1), \dots, (q'_k, Y_k, D_k)\}$ ŌĆö a set of possible moves. *(1 mark ŌĆö 7-tuple definition, 1 mark ŌĆö DTM transition, 1 mark ŌĆö NTM transition with difference.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary]** **State and explain the acceptance condition of a Nondeterministic Turing Machine.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A string $w$ is accepted by an NTM $M$ if and only if there **exists** at least one computation path (sequence of moves) starting from the initial configuration and ending in a configuration whose state belongs to $F$. $$ w \in L(M) \iff \exists \;\; \text{computation path leading to a state in } F $$ In contrast, a string is **rejected** only if **all** possible computation paths fail to reach a final state. This is called **existential acceptance** semantics. *(1 mark ŌĆö statement, 1 mark ŌĆö formal condition, 1 mark ŌĆö rejection condition.)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. ### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define a Nondeterministic Turing Machine formally as a 7-tuple. Explain each component in detail with suitable examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Deterministic and Nondeterministic Turing Machines with respect to their transition functions, acceptance conditions, and computational power. #### Model Solution **(a) Formal Definition with Component Explanation [7 Marks]:** A Nondeterministic Turing Machine is defined as: $$ M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F) $$ **Component-by-component explanation:** 1. **$Q$ ŌĆö Finite set of states:** $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n\}$. The control unit is always in exactly one of these states. 2. **$\Sigma$ ŌĆö Input alphabet:** A finite set of symbols that can appear in the input string. The blank symbol $B$ is **not** in $\Sigma$. 3. **$\Gamma$ ŌĆö Tape alphabet:** $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma$. The tape cells can hold any symbol from $\Gamma$ during computation. 4. **$\delta$ ŌĆö Transition function:** The most distinctive component: $$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$$ For example, $\delta(q_1, 0) = \{(q_2, 1, R), (q_3, 0, L)\}$ means that in state $q_1$ reading $0$, the NTM can either go to $q_2$ writing $1$ and moving right, **or** go to $q_3$ writing $0$ and moving left. 5. **$q_0$ ŌĆö Start state:** The initial state in which computation begins. 6. **$B$ ŌĆö Blank symbol:** Marks all unused tape cells. 7. **$F$ ŌĆö Set of final/accepting states:** $F \subseteq Q$. Reaching any state in $F$ makes the current computation path accepting. *(Each component explained: 1 mark ├Ś 7 components = 7 marks, with examples integrated.)* --- **(b) Comparison of DTM and NTM [7 Marks]:** | Aspect | DTM | NTM | |---|---|---| | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ | | Return value | A single move | A set of possible moves | | Execution model | Single linear path | Computation tree (multiple paths) | | Acceptance | Halts in $F$ ŌćÆ accept | $\exists$ path halting in $F$ ŌćÆ accept | | Rejection | Halts in non-$F$ (or no move) ŌćÆ reject | $\forall$ paths fail to reach $F$ ŌćÆ reject | | Computational power | Recursively enumerable | Recursively enumerable (same) | | Time complexity | Polynomial-time DTM = $P$ | Polynomial-time NTM = $NP$ | | Space complexity | $PSPACE$ | $NPSPACE = PSPACE$ (Savitch) | **Key Insight:** Both DTM and NTM recognize **exactly the same class of languages** ŌĆö the recursively enumerable languages. The difference lies in **efficiency**, not **capability**. This equivalence is the content of the **ChurchŌĆōTuring Thesis**. *(3 marks for transition function comparison, 2 marks for acceptance condition, 2 marks for computational power equivalence.)* --- ### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Nondeterministic Turing Machine and describe its computation tree model. **(b) [7 Marks]** A NTM has $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$, $\Gamma = \{0, 1, B\}$, and the following transitions: $\delta(q_0, 0) = \{(q_1, 1, R), (q_2, 0, R)\}$, $\delta(q_1, 1) = \{(q_2, 0, L)\}$, $\delta(q_2, B) = \{(q_2, B, R)\}$. With $F = \{q_2\}$, trace the NTM's behavior on input $w = 01$ and determine whether it accepts. #### Model Solution **(a) Architecture and Computation Tree [7 Marks]:** **Architecture of NTM:** The NTM consists of: - **Input tape** containing the input string $w$ followed by blanks. - **Read/Write head** that scans one cell at a time. - **Finite control unit** holding the current state. - **Nondeterministic branching unit** that, on a single configuration, may invoke multiple successor configurations. **Computation Tree Model:** Every NTM execution on input $w$ generates a **computation tree**: - **Root:** Initial configuration $c_0 = (q_0, w, 1)$. - **Branches:** Each element in the set returned by $\delta$ creates a child node. - **Leaves:** Halting configurations, marked as either accepting (state in $F$) or rejecting. The depth of the tree equals the number of moves on a path; the branching factor is bounded by $|Q| \cdot |\Gamma| \cdot 2$. **Acceptance:** $w \in L(M)$ if and only if the tree has at least one leaf labeled as accepting. *(3 marks ŌĆö architecture, 2 marks ŌĆö tree structure, 2 marks ŌĆö acceptance/rejection semantics.)* --- **(b) Trace of NTM on $w = 01$ [7 Marks]:** **Initial Configuration:** $$ c_0 = (q_0, \; 01, \; 1) \quad \text{(state } q_0, \text{ tape } 01, \text{ head at position 1)} $$ **Step 1:** In state $q_0$ reading symbol $0$: $$ \delta(q_0, 0) = \{(q_1, 1, R), \;(q_2, 0, R)\} $$ The NTM branches into **two parallel paths**: - **Path A:** New configuration: $(q_1, \; 11, \; 2)$ ŌĆö state $q_1$, wrote $1$, head at position 2. - **Path B:** New configuration: $(q_2, \; 01, \; 2)$ ŌĆö state $q_2$, wrote $0$, head at position 2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Path A:** In state $q_1$ reading symbol $1$ (at position 2): $$ \delta(q_1, 1) = \{(q_2, 0, L)\} $$ New configuration: $(q_2, \; 10, \; 1)$ ŌĆö state $q_2$, wrote $0$, head at position 1. **Step 2 ŌĆö Path B:** In state $q_2$ reading symbol $1$ (at position 2): $$ \delta(q_2, 1) = \emptyset \quad \text{(no transition defined ŌĆö path halts and rejects)} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Path A continued:** In state $q_2$ reading symbol $1$ (at position 1): $$ \delta(q_2, 1) = \emptyset \quad \text{(no transition defined ŌĆö path halts and rejects)} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Path A continued (alternative):** In state $q_2$ reading symbol $B$ (after moving): $$ \delta(q_2, B) = \{(q_2, B, R)\} $$ This produces an infinite loop ŌĆö Path A never halts in a final state on this branch. **Conclusion:** - **Path A:** No halting accepting configuration reached (either rejects or loops). - **Path B:** No transition defined ŌåÆ halts and rejects. Since **no computation path** leads to a configuration with state in $F = \{q_2\}$ (recall: simply being in $q_2$ is not enough ŌĆö the path must *halt* in an accepting state, or we accept upon entering $F$ in the standard definition), $$ w = 01 \notin L(M) \quad \text{(String REJECTED)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the set-return nature of $\delta$** ŌĆö students often write the NTM transition as if it were a DTM, losing 2-3 marks. > 2. **Confusing acceptance semantics** ŌĆö saying "NTM accepts if all paths accept" instead of "at least one path accepts" ŌĆö loses 2 marks. > 3. **Drawing the computation tree incorrectly** ŌĆö branches should correspond to elements of $\delta$, not to separate inputs. > 4. **Missing the existence of equivalence** ŌĆö failing to state that NTM and DTM recognize the same class of languages costs 1-2 marks. > 5. **In trace problems, ignoring undefined transitions** ŌĆö students often "make up" transitions not given in $\delta$, which the examiner marks as wrong. *(2 marks ŌĆö initial configuration, 2 marks ŌĆö branching, 2 marks ŌĆö tracing both paths, 1 mark ŌĆö final decision.)* --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for NTM (Definition):** - Ō£ģ **NTM** is a 7-tuple: $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$. - Ō£ģ The **transition function** is the only structural difference from DTM: - DTM: $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ - NTM: $\delta(q, X) = \{(q'_1, Y_1, D_1), \dots, (q'_k, Y_k, D_k)\}$ - Ō£ģ NTM models **parallel computation** via a **computation tree**, not probabilistic guessing. - Ō£ģ **Acceptance is existential** ŌĆö there must exist *at least one* accepting path. - Ō£ģ **Rejection is universal** ŌĆö *all* paths must fail to accept. - Ō£ģ NTM and DTM have **equal computational power** (both recognize recursively enumerable languages). - Ō£ģ They differ in **efficiency**: NTM can be exponentially faster (DTM simulation of NTM may take $2^{O(n)}$ time). - Ō£ģ The class **NP** is defined using polynomial-time NTMs. - Ō£ģ **Savitch's Theorem:** $NPSPACE = PSPACE$ ŌĆö space is preserved between NTM and DTM. - Ō£ģ The branching factor is **bounded**: $\leq |Q| \cdot |\Gamma| \cdot 2$. - Ō£ģ NTM transitions can return an **empty set** $\emptyset$, in which case the path **halts and rejects**. - Ō£ģ The "blank symbol" $B$ is in $\Gamma$ but **never** in $\Sigma$. - Ō£ģ NTM is a **theoretical model** ŌĆö no physical computer is truly nondeterministic; we *simulate* NTM on a DTM using BFS/DFS over the computation tree.

topics.title: Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition: Recursive Languages (Decidable Languages) A language **L** is called **Recursive** (also called a **Decidable** language) if there exists a **Turing Machine (TM)** that: - **Halts** on every input string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$. - **Accepts** the string if $w \in L$. - **Rejects** the string if $w \notin L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > A language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a decider Turing machine $M$ such that $L = L(M)$, where $M$ halts on **every** input $w \in \Sigma^{*}$. A recursive language is also called a **decidable** language because membership in it can always be determined in finite time. ### 1.2 Formal Definition: Recursively Enumerable Languages A language **L** is called **Recursively Enumerable (r.e.)** (also called **Turing-Recognizable** or **Semi-Decidable**) if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that: - $M$ **accepts** every string $w \in L$ (by entering an accepting state $q_{accept}$). - For strings $w \notin L$, $M$ either **rejects** explicitly or **loops forever**. > [!NOTE] > Every **recursive language is recursively enumerable**, but the converse is **NOT** always true. The class of r.e. languages is strictly larger than the class of recursive languages (in the limit). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **The Recursive Language ŌĆö The Strict Judge:** Imagine a court judge who **always** delivers a verdict. If the accused is guilty, the judge declares "GUILTY"; if innocent, declares "NOT GUILTY". The judge **never** makes the court wait indefinitely. This is a **recursive** language ŌĆö the TM is a strict judge who never loops. **The Recursively Enumerable Language ŌĆö The Biased Judge:** Imagine a judge who **cheerfully confirms** every guilty person ("GUILTY!"), but when faced with an innocent person, the judge simply **does nothing** ŌĆö sits there thinking forever, never saying "NOT GUILTY". You can never be sure whether the person is innocent or whether the judge is still "thinking". This is an **r.e.** language. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Analogy (Compilers):** Consider a compiler checking if a C program has syntax errors. A **recursive** check is one that always returns either `PASS` or `FAIL`. An **r.e.** check is one that returns `PASS` for valid programs but may "hang" (infinite loop) on a buggy program because the bug causes an endless parser loop. ### 1.4 Notation & Standard Sets Used - $\mathcal{R}$ = the class of all **Recursive** languages. - $\mathcal{RE}$ = the class of all **Recursively Enumerable** languages. - $\Sigma^{*}$ = the set of all finite strings over alphabet $\Sigma$. - $L(M)$ = the language recognized/accepted by Turing machine $M$. - $A_{TM}$ = $\{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$. ### 1.5 Key Distinction: Recognizer vs. Decider | Property | Recursive (Decider) | Recursively Enumerable (Recognizer) | |----------|---------------------|--------------------------------------| | Halts on every input? | **Yes** (always halts) | **Not necessarily** (may loop) | | Verdict for $w \in L$ | Accepts | Accepts | | Verdict for $w \notin L$ | Rejects | Rejects or Loops | | Complement is also in class? | **Yes** (closed under complement) | **Not necessarily** | | Equivalent to | Decidable | Semi-decidable / Turing-recognizable | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Language Classes The relationship between language classes is a cornerstone of Module 4: $$\mathcal{R} \subsetneq \mathcal{RE} \subsetneq \mathcal{P}(\Sigma^{*})$$ Where $\mathcal{P}(\Sigma^{*})$ denotes the power set of $\Sigma^{*}$ ŌĆö i.e., the set of **all** languages over $\Sigma$. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why $\mathcal{R} \subseteq \mathcal{RE}$ 1. Let $L$ be any recursive language. 2. By definition, there exists a decider TM $M$ for $L$ that always halts. 3. The same TM $M$ trivially satisfies the definition of a recognizer (it halts, accepts, or rejects). 4. Therefore $L$ is also recursively enumerable. 5. Hence $\mathcal{R} \subseteq \mathcal{RE}$. > [!NOTE] > The strictness $\mathcal{R} \subsetneq \mathcal{RE}$ is proven by exhibiting languages like $A_{TM}$ which are r.e. but **not** recursive (via the Halting Problem reduction). ### 2.3 Closure Properties (High-Yield for KTU Board) | Operation | Recursive (R) | Recursively Enumerable (RE) | |-----------|:---:|:---:| | Union ($\cup$) | **Closed** | **Closed** | | Intersection ($\cap$) | **Closed** | **Closed** | | Complement ($\overline{L}$) | **Closed** | **NOT closed** | | Kleene Star ($L^{*}$) | **Closed** | **Closed** | | Concatenation ($L_1 \cdot L_2$) | **Closed** | **Closed** | | Reversal ($L^{R}$) | **Closed** | **Closed** | | Homomorphism | **Closed** | **Closed** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Trap:** A very common mistake is to claim r.e. languages are closed under complement. They are **NOT**. The language $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is famously **not** recursively enumerable, although $A_{TM}$ itself is. ### 2.4 Co-Recursively Enumerable Languages A language $L$ is **co-recursively enumerable** (co-r.e.) if its complement $\overline{L}$ is recursively enumerable. **Theorem (Kleene's Theorem on Complements):** $$L \in \mathcal{R} \iff L \in \mathcal{RE} \cap \text{co-}\mathcal{RE}$$ In words: a language is **decidable** if and only if it is **both r.e. and co-r.e.** ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Meaning | KTU Board Significance | |------------------|---------|------------------------| | $A_{TM}$ | $\{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ | r.e. but **NOT** recursive | | $A_{REX}$ | $\{R \mid R \text{ is a regex generating } \Sigma^{*}\}$ | Recursive (decidable) | | $E_{TM}$ | $\{\langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset\}$ | co-r.e. (not r.e.) | | $EQ_{TM}$ | $\{\langle M_1, M_2 \rangle \mid L(M_1) = L(M_2)\}$ | NOT r.e. | | $HALT_{TM}$ | $\{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ | r.e. but NOT recursive | | $\overline{A_{TM}}$ | Complement of $A_{TM}$ | NOT r.e. (co-r.e.) | | $L_{d}$ (Diagonal) | $\{ \langle M \rangle \mid \langle M \rangle \notin L(M) \}$ | NOT r.e. | | $\overline{L_{d}}$ | $\{ \langle M \rangle \mid \langle M \rangle \in L(M) \}$ | r.e. but NOT recursive | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Design:** Type checking in strongly-typed languages is **decidable** (recursive). Whole-program optimization questions are often **undecidable** (r.e. but not recursive). - **Software Verification:** Model checking finite-state systems is decidable; model checking pushdown/recursive systems is undecidable. - **Network Security:** Determining whether a given firewall ruleset blocks all attacks is **r.e.** but not **recursive** ŌĆö there is no algorithm that always terminates. - **Database Theory:** Query containment in relational algebra is decidable; in first-order logic with arithmetic it's r.e. but not recursive. ### 2.7 Decidability Quick-Reference Table | Problem | Recursive? | Recursively Enumerable? | |---------|:---:|:---:| | $A_{DFA}$ (DFA acceptance) | **Yes** | Yes | | $A_{NFA}$ | **Yes** | Yes | | $A_{REX}$ (regex generates string) | **Yes** | Yes | | $E_{DFA}$ (DFA accepts nothing) | **Yes** | Yes | | $EQ_{DFA}$ (DFA equivalence) | **Yes** | Yes | | $A_{CFG}$ (CFG generates string) | **Yes** | Yes | | $E_{CFG}$ (CFG generates nothing) | **Yes** | Yes | | $EQ_{CFG}$ (CFG equivalence) | **No** | co-r.e. (not r.e.) | | $A_{TM}$ (TM acceptance) | **No** | Yes | | $HALT_{TM}$ | **No** | Yes | | $E_{TM}$ (TM accepts nothing) | **No** | No (co-r.e.) | | $EQ_{TM}$ (TM equivalence) | **No** | No | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Proof That $A_{TM}$ is Recursively Enumerable **Statement:** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is recursively enumerable. **Construction of TM Recognizer $U$ ("Universal TM"):** On input $\langle M, w \rangle$: 1. Simulate $M$ on input $w$ for **one step**. 2. If $M$ enters the accept state $q_{accept}$, **accept** and halt. 3. If $M$ enters the reject state $q_{reject}$, **reject** and halt. 4. Otherwise, repeat step 1 (simulate another step of $M$ on $w$). **Correctness Analysis:** - If $\langle M, w \rangle \in A_{TM}$: TM $M$ will eventually reach $q_{accept}$, so $U$ accepts in finite time. Ō£ō - If $\langle M, w \rangle \notin A_{TM}$: TM $M$ never accepts. $U$ simulates forever ŌĆö this is allowed for an r.e. recognizer. Ō£ō Hence $U$ recognizes $A_{TM}$, and $A_{TM} \in \mathcal{RE}$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Proof That $A_{TM}$ is NOT Recursive (via Diagonalization) **Theorem:** $A_{TM}$ is undecidable. **Proof by Contradiction:** Assume, for contradiction, that $A_{TM}$ is decidable. Then there exists a decider TM $H$ such that: $$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$ **Step 1:** Construct a new TM $D$ that uses $H$ as a subroutine. On input $\langle M \rangle$ (encoding of any TM): 1. Run $H$ on input $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$. 2. If $H$ accepts, then **reject**. 3. If $H$ rejects, then **accept**. Formally: $$D(\langle M \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } \langle M \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } \langle M \rangle \end{cases}$$ **Step 2:** Run $D$ on its own description $\langle D \rangle$. $$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } D \text{ does not accept } \langle D \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } D \text{ accepts } \langle D \rangle \end{cases}$$ **Step 3:** This is a **contradiction**: - If $D$ accepts $\langle D \rangle$, then by construction it must reject. - If $D$ rejects $\langle D \rangle$, then by construction it must accept. **Conclusion:** Our assumption that $H$ exists is false. Therefore, $A_{TM}$ is **not recursive**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Proof That Recursive Languages Are Closed Under Complement **Theorem:** If $L$ is recursive, then $\overline{L}$ is recursive. **Proof:** 1. Since $L$ is recursive, there exists a decider TM $M$ that always halts. 2. Construct TM $M'$ that swaps the accept and reject states of $M$: $$M'(\langle w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ rejects } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \end{cases}$$ 3. Since $M$ always halts, $M'$ also always halts. 4. $M'$ decides $\overline{L}$. Hence $\overline{L} \in \mathcal{R}$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.4 Proof That R.E. Languages Are NOT Closed Under Complement **Theorem:** $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not recursively enumerable. **Proof by Contradiction:** 1. Suppose $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is r.e. 2. We know $A_{TM}$ is r.e. (Section 3.1). 3. By assumption, $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is r.e. 4. Since $A_{TM} \in \mathcal{RE} \cap \text{co-}\mathcal{RE}$, by **Kleene's Theorem**, $A_{TM}$ would be **recursive**. 5. But Section 3.2 proved $A_{TM}$ is **not** recursive ŌĆö contradiction! Therefore $\overline{A_{TM}} \notin \mathcal{RE}$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.5 The Diagonalization Language $L_d$ (Detailed Construction) **Definition:** Enumerate all TM encodings as $M_1, M_2, M_3, \ldots$ The diagonalization language is: $$L_d = \{ \langle M_i \rangle \mid \langle M_i \rangle \notin L(M_i) \}$$ **The Membership Table (Conceptual):** $$ \begin{array}{c|ccccc} & \langle M_1 \rangle & \langle M_2 \rangle & \langle M_3 \rangle & \langle M_4 \rangle & \cdots \\ \hline M_1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & \cdots \\ M_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots \\ M_3 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & \cdots \\ M_4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \\ \end{array} $$ $L_d$ corresponds to the **complement of the diagonal** of this infinite table. Suppose $L_d = L(M_k)$ for some TM $M_k$. Then: - $M_k$ accepts $\langle M_k \rangle \iff \langle M_k \rangle \notin L(M_k)$ ŌĆö **contradiction**. Therefore $L_d$ is **not** r.e. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Decision Procedure for Proving Decidability | To prove $L$ is **Recursive**, show: | To prove $L$ is **R.E. but not Recursive**, show: | To prove $L$ is **Not R.E.**, show: | |--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------| | Build a TM decider $M$ for $L$ | Build a TM recognizer for $L$ | Reduce $\overline{A_{TM}}$ or $L_d$ to $L$ | | $M$ must halt on **all** inputs | Show $L \in \mathcal{RE}$ (easy) | Show $L$ is not r.e. by reduction | | | Show $L \notin \mathcal{R}$ (reduce $A_{TM}$ to $L$) | | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Most 14-mark Module 4 questions ask you to prove a language is **r.e. but not recursive**. Always perform two sub-proofs: (a) r.e. by constructing a recognizer, (b) not recursive by reducing $A_{TM}$ to it. ### 3.7 Worked Example: Proving $HALT_{TM}$ is r.e. but not recursive **Part (a) ŌĆö $HALT_{TM}$ is r.e.:** Construct TM $T$ that on input $\langle M, w \rangle$ simulates $M$ on $w$. If $M$ halts (either accepts or rejects), $T$ accepts. If $M$ loops, $T$ loops. **Part (b) ŌĆö $HALT_{TM}$ is not recursive:** Suppose decider $H$ exists. To decide $A_{TM}$, build $S$ that on input $\langle M, w \rangle$ runs $H(\langle M, w \rangle)$. If $H$ accepts (i.e., $M$ halts), then simulate $M$ on $w$ ŌĆö accept if $M$ accepts, reject otherwise. This makes $S$ a decider for $A_{TM}$, contradicting Section 3.2. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Language Classes (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef power fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef re fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef r fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef core fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef co fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff POWER["Power Set P Sigma Star
All Languages over Sigma"]:::power RE["Recursively Enumerable RE
Turing Recognizable"]:::re CORE["Intersection of RE and co-RE
Decidable Region"]:::core R["Recursive Decidable
Always Halts"]:::r CO["co-Recursively Enumerable
Complement is RE"]:::co POWER -->|contains| RE RE -->|strict superset| CORE CORE -->|equals| R CO -.->|complement of| RE CO -->|intersects| CORE CORE --> R ``` ### 4.2 TM Recognizer vs. Decider ŌĆö Operational Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef input fill:#0277bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef accept fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef reject fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef loop fill:#f57c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef proc fill:#4527a0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff IN["Input w in Sigma Star"]:::input SIM["Simulate Turing Machine M on w"]:::proc ACC["ACCEPT
Halt in q_accept"]:::accept REJ["REJECT
Halt in q_reject"]:::reject LP["LOOP FOREVER
No Verdict"]:::loop IN --> SIM SIM -->|w in L| ACC SIM -->|w not in L| REJ SIM -.->|Recognizer may choose| LP note1["Decider: Always reaches ACC or REJ
Recognizer: May take LP path"]:::proc note1 -.- SIM ``` ### 4.3 Reduction Flowchart: Proving $A_{TM} \leq L \Rightarrow L$ is not recursive ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef start fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef step fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef out fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff S["Assume for contradiction: L is recursive
So there exists decider D for L"]:::start A1["Step 1: Construct reduction TM R
R takes input M comma w"]:::step A2["Step 2: R transforms M comma w into
some string x where x is in L
iff M accepts w"]:::step A3["Step 3: Run D on x
D decides x in L or not"]:::step A4["Step 4: This gives a decider for A_TM"]:::step A5["Step 5: But A_TM is undecidable
CONTRADICTION"]:::out A6["Conclusion: L is not recursive"]:::out S --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 ``` ### 4.4 Computation History Tree (Used in $A_{TM}$ Undecidability Proofs) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef cfg fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef accept fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef reject fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff C0["Config 0: q0 w
Initial Configuration"]:::cfg C1["Config 1
After Step 1 of M on w"]:::cfg C2["Config 2
After Step 2 of M on w"]:::cfg CN["Config N
Final Configuration"]:::cfg CA["q_accept
M accepts w"]:::accept CR["q_reject
M rejects w"]:::reject CL["Infinite Loop
M does not halt"]:::cfg C0 --> C1 --> C2 --> CN CN --> CA CN --> CR CN -.-> CL ``` ### 4.5 Closure Property Summary (Visual) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef op fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef yes fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef no fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff Q1{"Operation"}:::op A1["Recursive: YES
R.E.: YES"]:::yes Q2{"Operation"}:::op A2["Recursive: YES
R.E.: NO"]:::no Q3{"Operation"}:::op A3["Recursive: YES
R.E.: YES"]:::yes Q1 -->|Union| A1 Q2 -->|Complement| A2 Q3 -->|Intersection| A3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define a recursive language. Is the language $A_{DFA}$ recursive? Justify.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A language $L$ is **recursive** (or **decidable**) if there exists a Turing machine $M$ such that $M$ halts on every input $w \in \Sigma^{*}$, accepting if $w \in L$ and rejecting if $w \notin L$. Yes, $A_{DFA}$ is recursive. The decider simulates the DFA on the input string and accepts if it ends in a final state, rejects otherwise ŌĆö this simulation always halts in $O(n)$ time where $n$ is the string length. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *State two differences between recursive and recursively enumerable languages.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. A **recursive** language has a TM decider that always halts, while a **recursively enumerable** language only has a recognizer that may loop on inputs not in $L$. (1.5 marks) 2. Recursive languages are **closed under complement**, but recursively enumerable languages are **NOT closed under complement**. For example, $A_{TM}$ is r.e. but $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not r.e. (1.5 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Define the language $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that accepts } w\}$. Prove that $A_{TM}$ is recursively enumerable. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Prove that $A_{TM}$ is not recursive using diagonalization. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) ŌĆö $A_{TM}$ is r.e. (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Construct a Universal Turing Machine $U$ that recognizes $A_{TM}$. On input $\langle M, w \rangle$: 1. Check that $\langle M, w \rangle$ is a valid encoding of a TM $M$ and string $w$. If not, **reject**. 2. Simulate $M$ on input $w$ for **one step**. 3. If $M$ enters $q_{accept}$, then $U$ **accepts** and halts. 4. If $M$ enters $q_{reject}$, then $U$ **rejects** and halts. 5. Otherwise, go back to step 2 and continue simulation. **Step 2 ŌĆö Correctness:** - If $w \in L(M)$, then $M$ eventually reaches $q_{accept}$ in some finite number of steps, say $k$ steps. $U$ simulates $M$ for $k$ steps and then accepts. So $U$ halts and accepts. **(2 marks)** - If $w \notin L(M)$, then $M$ never reaches $q_{accept}$. $U$ keeps simulating forever without halting. This is permitted for an r.e. recognizer. **(2 marks)** - Hence $U$ recognizes $A_{TM}$, and $A_{TM} \in \mathcal{RE}$. **(1 mark)** **Part (b) ŌĆö $A_{TM}$ is not recursive (7 marks):** **Proof by contradiction using diagonalization:** **Step 1:** Assume, for contradiction, that $A_{TM}$ is recursive. Then there exists a decider TM $H$ that halts on every input. **(1 mark)** **Step 2:** Construct a new TM $D$ that uses $H$ as a subroutine. $D$ takes as input $\langle M \rangle$ (an encoding of a TM) and: 1. Runs $H$ on $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$. 2. If $H$ accepts, then $D$ **rejects**. 3. If $H$ rejects, then $D$ **accepts**. Formally: $$D(\langle M \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } \langle M \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } \langle M \rangle \end{cases}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3:** Now run $D$ on its own encoding $\langle D \rangle$: $$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } D \text{ does not accept } \langle D \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } D \text{ accepts } \langle D \rangle \end{cases}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 4:** This is a **logical contradiction** ŌĆö $D$ cannot simultaneously accept and reject its own encoding. Therefore our initial assumption must be false. **(1.5 marks)** **Conclusion:** $A_{TM}$ is **not recursive**. **(0.5 marks)** --- **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Define recursive and recursively enumerable languages. State and prove the closure property of recursive languages under complement. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Prove that $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not recursively enumerable. Hence state the relationship between r.e. and co-r.e. languages. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Definitions and Complement Closure (7 marks):** **Definitions (2 marks):** - A language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a TM $M$ that always halts and $L(M) = L$. - A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable (r.e.)** if there exists a TM $M$ that accepts all strings in $L$ and either rejects or loops on strings not in $L$. **Theorem:** If $L$ is recursive, then $\overline{L}$ is recursive. **(0.5 marks)** **Proof:** Let $M$ be a decider TM for $L$. Construct $M'$ that is identical to $M$ except that the accept state $q_{accept}$ and reject state $q_{reject}$ are **swapped**. **(2 marks)** $$M'(w) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M(w) = \text{reject} \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M(w) = \text{accept} \end{cases}$$ **Correctness Analysis:** **(2 marks)** - Since $M$ is a decider, it always halts on every input $w$. - Therefore $M'$ also always halts on every input $w$ (same computation steps, just swapped final state). - $M'$ accepts $w$ iff $w \notin L$ iff $w \in \overline{L}$. - $M'$ rejects $w$ iff $w \in L$ iff $w \notin \overline{L}$. Hence $M'$ is a decider for $\overline{L}$, so $\overline{L}$ is recursive. **(0.5 marks)** **Part (b) ŌĆö $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not r.e. (7 marks):** **Proof by contradiction:** **Step 1:** Assume, for contradiction, that $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is recursively enumerable. **(1 mark)** **Step 2:** We have the following facts: - $A_{TM}$ is r.e. (proven in Question A(a)). **(1 mark)** - $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is r.e. (by our assumption). **(1 mark)** **Step 3:** Therefore $A_{TM} \in \mathcal{RE} \cap \text{co-}\mathcal{RE}$. **(1 mark)** **Step 4:** Apply **Kleene's Theorem**: If a language is in both $\mathcal{RE}$ and co-$\mathcal{RE}$, then it is **recursive**. **(1 mark)** **Step 5:** This would imply $A_{TM}$ is recursive, which contradicts the result of Question A(b) that $A_{TM}$ is undecidable. **(1.5 marks)** **Conclusion:** Our assumption is false. $\overline{A_{TM}} \notin \mathcal{RE}$. **(0.5 marks)** **Relationship between RE and co-RE:** A language is recursive if and only if it belongs to **both** $\mathcal{RE}$ and co-$\mathcal{RE}$. The classes $\mathcal{RE}$ and co-$\mathcal{RE}$ are not complements of each other ŌĆö there exist languages outside their union. **(1 mark)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify "halts on every input"** in the definition of recursive language. This single phrase carries 1 mark. Always write: "TM that halts on **every** input $w \in \Sigma^*$". > 2. **Confusing recognizer and decider constructions.** A recognizer may loop; a decider must not. Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if your constructed TM is not shown to halt on all inputs when proving decidability. > 3. **In diagonalization proofs, students often forget the self-reference step.** Always end with "$D$ on input $\langle D \rangle$ leads to contradiction" ŌĆö this is the crux worth 2 marks. > 4. **Not closing the proof.** Always end with "Hence $A_{TM}$ is not recursive" or "Contradiction. $\blacksquare$". Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for incomplete closure. > 5. **Confusing "closed under complement" with "complement is decidable".** If $L$ is recursive, $\overline{L}$ is also recursive. If $L$ is r.e., $\overline{L}$ may NOT be r.e. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Recursive = Decidable**: TM halts on **every** input, accepts/rejects definitively. - [x] **Recursively Enumerable = Turing-Recognizable**: TM accepts strings in $L$; may loop on strings not in $L$. - [x] **Strict Hierarchy**: $\mathcal{R} \subsetneq \mathcal{RE} \subsetneq \mathcal{P}(\Sigma^{*})$. - [x] **$A_{TM}$** is the canonical r.e.-but-not-recursive language ŌĆö appears in nearly every KTU paper. - [x] **Diagonalization** is the proof technique for showing languages are not r.e. or not recursive. - [x] **Closure under complement** holds for $\mathcal{R}$ but **NOT** for $\mathcal{RE}$. - [x] **Kleene's Theorem**: $L \in \mathcal{R} \iff L \in \mathcal{RE} \cap \text{co-}\mathcal{RE}$. - [x] **Standard r.e. languages**: $A_{TM}$, $HALT_{TM}$, $A_{CFG}$. - [x] **Standard non-r.e. languages**: $\overline{A_{TM}}$, $E_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $L_d$. - [x] **Self-reference trick** is central to all undecidability proofs. - [x] **Universal TM $U$** exists and recognizes $A_{TM}$ ŌĆö first proved by Turing (1936). - [x] **Co-r.e. but not r.e.**: $E_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $\overline{A_{TM}}$. - [x] **Decidable problems on DFA/NFA/Regex/CFG** are safe bets; **TM problems** are almost always undecidable. - [x] **Reduction strategy** (not diagonalization) is the primary technique for showing $L$ is not recursive given $A_{TM}$ is not recursive.

topics.title: Unrestricted Grammar (Definition Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unrestricted Grammar (Definition Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Unrestricted Grammar** (also called a **Phrase-Structure Grammar** or **Type-0 Grammar** in the Chomsky Hierarchy) is formally defined as a **4-tuple**:

$$
G = (V, T, P, S)
$$

where each component has a precise mathematical meaning:

- **$V$** ŌĆö A finite, non-empty set of **variables** (also called **non-terminals** or **syntactic categories**). These are placeholders that get replaced during derivation.
- **$T$** ŌĆö A finite, non-empty set of **terminals** (the actual alphabet/symbols of the language being generated). $V \cap T = \emptyset$.
- **$P$** ŌĆö A finite set of **production rules** of the general form:
$$
\alpha \rightarrow \beta
$$
where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
- **$S \in V$** ŌĆö The **start symbol** (also called the *sentence symbol*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition:** The production rules of an Unrestricted Grammar are called "unrestricted" because the left-hand side $\alpha$ is **NOT** restricted to being a single non-terminal ŌĆö it can be any non-empty string of variables and terminals, in any order. This single relaxation makes it the most powerful (and most general) grammar in the Chomsky hierarchy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **free-form sentence builder**:
- In English class, you are taught that a sentence must start with a **Subject**, then a **Verb**, then an **Object** (a strict rule: $S \rightarrow NP\,VP$).
- Now imagine that rule book is **completely thrown away**, and you are allowed to rewrite **any chunk of symbols** into **any other chunk of symbols** ŌĆö as long as the chunk you are replacing contains **at least one symbol**.
- That is exactly what an **Unrestricted Grammar** does. It is the "wild west" of grammars ŌĆö no structural rules, no constraints on the length of replacement, no requirement that the left side be a single variable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** If Context-Free Grammars are like "well-disciplined sentence templates," Unrestricted Grammars are like "a free notebook where any scribble can be transformed into any other scribble, provided something is on the page."

## 1.3 The Critical Constraint (The Only Rule)

The **single rule** that an Unrestricted Grammar must obey is:

$$
\boxed{\alpha \neq \varepsilon}
$$

That is, the left-hand side of any production must contain **at least one symbol** (it cannot be the empty string). Everything else is fair game.

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is assuming that $\beta$ (the right-hand side) can be empty. Yes, it can! $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ allows $\beta = \varepsilon$. Only $\alpha$ is forbidden from being empty. This is the precise reason why Unrestricted Grammars can generate the empty string, but their productions are still considered "non-contracting" on the LHS.

## 1.4 Position in the Chomsky Hierarchy

Unrestricted Grammars sit at the **top** (most powerful) tier of the Chomsky Hierarchy:

| Grammar Type | Production Form | Power |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Type 0** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$ | **Most powerful** |
| Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) | $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ | More restricted |
| Type 2 (Context-Free) | $A \rightarrow \beta$, $A \in V$ | More restricted |
| Type 3 (Regular) | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Least powerful |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Type-0 grammars generate exactly the class of **Recursively Enumerable (RE) Languages**. The languages generated by Unrestricted Grammars are precisely those recognized by a **Turing Machine**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Production Rule ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

Let us dissect a sample Unrestricted Grammar production step by step to cement the understanding:

**Example Production:** $aBbc \rightarrow aAbBc$

- **Left-hand side (LHS) $\alpha$:** $aBbc$ ŌĆö this is a **mixed string** containing both terminals ($a, c$) and variables ($B$). It is **non-empty**, so the rule is valid.
- **Right-hand side (RHS) $\beta$:** $aAbBc$ ŌĆö also a mixed string, possibly even **longer** than the LHS.
- **Application:** Anywhere in a sentential form that the exact substring $aBbc$ appears, it can be replaced wholesale by $aAbBc$.

### Why Each Element Matters
- The fact that $\alpha$ contains **terminals** (the $a, b, c$) is what makes the rule *context-sensitive in spirit* ŌĆö you are not just rewriting a variable, you are rewriting symbols in a specific "context" (i.e., the surrounding terminals act as context).
- The fact that $\beta$ is allowed to be **shorter** than $\alpha$ (i.e., $\vert\beta\vert < \vert\alpha\vert$) is what distinguishes Unrestricted from Context-Sensitive grammars. In CSG, $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ is mandatory.

## 2.2 The Three Relaxations vs. Context-Free Grammars

An Unrestricted Grammar relaxes **three structural rules** that govern Context-Free Grammars:

1. **LHS is not a single variable** ŌĆö it can be any string $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$.
2. **RHS can be empty** ŌĆö $\beta$ may equal $\varepsilon$, allowing symbols to be deleted.
3. **No length constraint** ŌĆö the LHS and RHS can be of any relative length (RHS can be longer, shorter, or equal).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table ŌĆö Memorize for KTU Exams**

| Symbol | Meaning | Constraint / Allowed Values |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $G$ | The grammar | Tuple $(V, T, P, S)$ |
| $V$ | Variables (non-terminals) | Finite, non-empty set |
| $T$ | Terminals (alphabet) | Finite, non-empty set, $V \cap T = \emptyset$ |
| $P$ | Set of production rules | Finite set |
| $\alpha$ | Left-hand side of production | $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$, i.e., $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$ |
| $\beta$ | Right-hand side of production | $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, i.e., $\beta$ may equal $\varepsilon$ |
| $S$ | Start symbol | $S \in V$ |
| Language generated | $L(G)$ | Recursively Enumerable (RE) |
| Recognized by | Computational model | **Turing Machine** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Unrestricted Grammars are not merely theoretical curiosities ŌĆö they have direct engineering relevance:

- **Compiler Theory Foundations:** Although no practical compiler uses a pure Type-0 grammar, the *concept* of unrestricted rewriting underpins advanced code-transformation tools and term-rewriting systems used in formal verification.
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Early computational linguistics models used phrase-structure (unrestricted) grammars before statistical and neural methods took over.
- **Theorem Proving & Proof Assistants:** Systems like Coq and Isabelle internally use unrestricted rewrite rules to manipulate logical expressions.
- **DNA Computing & Molecular Computation:** Biological string-rewriting systems (e.g., splicing systems) are modeled using Type-0 grammar analogs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Takeaway:** Anything in computer science that involves **arbitrary symbol rewriting** ŌĆö from proof tactics to biological computation ŌĆö is, at its mathematical core, an Unrestricted Grammar.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation Logic & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Since the topic is "Definition Only," no formal proofs or numerical derivations are required. However, we provide the **complete logical derivation of the language class** and a **step-by-step verification** of the definition's constraints on a sample grammar, to fully satisfy the KTU board's standard of explanation.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation ŌĆö Why Type-0 Grammars Generate RE Languages

The connection between Unrestricted Grammars and Recursively Enumerable Languages is established through the following logical chain. Each step is fully explained.

**Step 1: Define the language generated by $G$.**

The language $L(G)$ is the set of all terminal strings derivable from the start symbol $S$ in a finite number of steps:

$$
L(G) = \{ w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}
$$

where $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes the reflexive-transitive closure of the single-step derivation relation $\Rightarrow$.

**Step 2: Define the single-step derivation relation.**

A single derivation step replaces a substring matching some $\alpha$ with the corresponding $\beta$:

$$
\text{If } u\alpha v \text{ is a sentential form and } (\alpha \rightarrow \beta) \in P, \text{ then } u\alpha v \Rightarrow u\beta v
$$

The symbols $u$ and $v$ are arbitrary strings over $(V \cup T)^{*}$ ŌĆö they serve as the "context" within which the rewrite happens.

**Step 3: Construct a Turing Machine simulator.**

Given a Type-0 grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, we can construct a **non-deterministic Turing Machine (NTM)** $M$ that recognizes $L(G)$ as follows:

$M$ operates in two phases:

- **Phase 1 (Generate):** Non-deterministically apply productions of $G$ in reverse to the input string $w$, attempting to reduce $w$ to $S$.
- **Phase 2 (Verify):** If the reduction succeeds, $M$ accepts $w$.

**Step 4: Show $M$ accepts exactly $L(G)$.**

- *If $w \in L(G)$:* There exists a derivation $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$. Reversing this derivation gives a sequence of reverse-rewrites that $M$ can follow, so $M$ accepts.
- *If $w \notin L(G)$:* No reverse-derivation from $w$ to $S$ exists, so every non-deterministic branch of $M$ either halts without accepting or loops forever. Since $M$ is non-deterministic and acceptance requires *some* branch to succeed, $M$ rejects.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

$$
L(G) = L(M) \in RE
$$

where $RE$ denotes the class of Recursively Enumerable Languages. This establishes the deep equivalence:

$$
\boxed{\text{Type-0 Grammars} \iff \text{Turing Machines} \iff \text{Recursively Enumerable Languages}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Verifying the Definition on a Sample

Let us verify the definition against a sample grammar.

**Sample Grammar:** $G = (\{S, A, B\}, \{a, b, c\}, P, S)$

where $P$ contains the following productions:

1. $S \rightarrow aAbc$
2. $Ab \rightarrow bA$
3. $bA \rightarrow Ba$
4. $Bc \rightarrow Bbc$
5. $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$

**Verification Step-by-Step:**

| Step | Production Applied | Sentential Form | Validity Check |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | (start) | $S$ | Initial |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow aAbc$ | $aAbc$ | LHS = $S \in V$ Ō£ō |
| 2 | $Ab \rightarrow bA$ | $abAc$ | LHS = $Ab \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ Ō£ō |
| 3 | $bA \rightarrow Ba$ | $aBac$ | LHS = $bA \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ Ō£ō |
| 4 | $Bc \rightarrow Bbc$ | $aBbac$ | LHS = $Bc \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ Ō£ō |
| 5 | $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $a bac$ | RHS = $\varepsilon$ allowed Ō£ō |
| Final | ŌĆö | $abac$ | $abac \in T^{*}$ Ō£ō |

**All productions satisfy the constraint $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$**, so $G$ is a valid Unrestricted Grammar, and $abac \in L(G)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Notice that production #4 ($Bc \rightarrow Bbc$) has a RHS **longer** than its LHS. This is permitted in an Unrestricted Grammar but would be a *lengthening* operation. Productions #2, #3, #5 have RHS shorter than or equal to their LHS. This mix of lengthening and shortening is a hallmark of Type-0 grammars.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Anatomy of an Unrestricted Grammar

The following diagram visually maps the formal 4-tuple structure and the production rule anatomy, which is the focus of the "Definition Only" KTU syllabus topic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["UNRESTRICTED GRAMMAR G"]:::main
    A --> B["V : Finite set of VARIABLES"]:::set
    A --> C["T : Finite set of TERMINALS"]:::set
    A --> D["P : Finite set of PRODUCTIONS"]:::set
    A --> E["S : START SYMBOL in V"]:::set

    D --> D1["Production Rule"]:::prod
    D1 --> D2["alpha : LHS string"]:::lhs
    D1 --> D3["beta : RHS string"]:::rhs
    D2 --> D2a["alpha is in V+T+"]:::constraint
    D2 --> D2b["alpha is NOT empty"]:::constraint
    D3 --> D3a["beta is in V+T*"]:::constraint
    D3 --> D3b["beta MAY be empty"]:::constraint

    E --> F["Generated Language L G"]:::lang
    F --> G["Recursively Enumerable"]:::langClass
    F --> H["Recognized by Turing Machine"]:::langClass

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef set fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#1f4e79
    classDef prod fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#7f6000
    classDef lhs fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#660000
    classDef rhs fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#274e13
    classDef constraint fill:#e7e6e6,stroke:#666666,color:#333333
    classDef lang fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
    classDef langClass fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#1c3d0f
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Derivation Flow of an Unrestricted Grammar

This second diagram illustrates the **derivation flow** ŌĆö how sentential forms evolve by application of unrestricted production rules.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0["Sentential Form 0 S"]:::start
    S1["Sentential Form 1 aAbc"]:::step
    S2["Sentential Form 2 abAc"]:::step
    S3["Sentential Form 3 aBac"]:::step
    S4["Sentential Form 4 aBbac"]:::step
    S5["Final String abac"]:::final

    S0 -->|"apply S to aAbc"| S1
    S1 -->|"apply Ab to bA"| S2
    S2 -->|"apply bA to Ba"| S3
    S3 -->|"apply Bc to Bbc"| S4
    S4 -->|"apply B to epsilon"| S5

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#7f6000
    classDef final fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#1c3d0f,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Chomsky Hierarchy Position

This block situates Unrestricted (Type-0) Grammar at the apex of the Chomsky Hierarchy, showing its power relative to other grammar types.

```mermaid
graph TD
    T0["TYPE 0 Unrestricted Grammar alpha to beta"]:::type0
    T1["TYPE 1 Context-Sensitive Grammar alpha to beta, abs alpha less equal abs beta"]:::type1
    T2["TYPE 2 Context-Free Grammar A to beta"]:::type2
    T3["TYPE 3 Regular Grammar A to aB or A to a"]:::type3

    T0 -->|"restriction on LHS"| T1
    T1 -->|"LHS becomes single variable"| T2
    T2 -->|"RHS restricted forms"| T3

    R0["RE Languages"]:::langT0
    R1["Context-Sensitive Languages"]:::langT1
    R2["Context-Free Languages"]:::langT2
    R3["Regular Languages"]:::langT3

    T0 -.generates.-> R0
    T1 -.generates.-> R1
    T2 -.generates.-> R2
    T3 -.generates.-> R3

    classDef type0 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef type1 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#1f4e79
    classDef type2 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#1f4e79
    classDef type3 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#1f4e79
    classDef langT0 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#1c3d0f
    classDef langT1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
    classDef langT2 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
    classDef langT3 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The blue solid arrows show the *restrictions* applied as we descend the hierarchy. The dotted arrows show the *language class* generated by each grammar type. Type-0 is the **most general** (fewest restrictions) and generates the **largest** class of languages.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### Question 1: Define Unrestricted Grammar. State the general form of its production rules. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

An Unrestricted Grammar is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where $V$ is a finite set of variables, $T$ is a finite set of terminals with $V \cap T = \emptyset$, $P$ is a finite set of production rules, and $S \in V$ is the start symbol.

The general form of the production rules is:

$$
\alpha \rightarrow \beta
$$

where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ (i.e., the LHS is a non-empty string of variables and terminals) and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ (i.e., the RHS may be empty). The grammar is called "unrestricted" because the LHS is not restricted to being a single non-terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Full 3 marks require: (1) the 4-tuple definition (1 mark), (2) the production form (1 mark), (3) the constraint $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$ explicitly stated (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: What class of languages is generated by Unrestricted Grammars? Name the corresponding computational model. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Unrestricted Grammars generate the class of **Recursively Enumerable (RE) Languages**. The corresponding computational model that recognizes exactly this class of languages is the **Turing Machine**.

The relationship can be summarized as:

$$
\boxed{L(G) \text{ is RE} \iff L(G) \text{ is recognized by some Turing Machine}}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Define Unrestricted Grammar formally. Explain each component of the 4-tuple with an example. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

An Unrestricted Grammar $G$ is formally defined as the ordered 4-tuple:

$$
G = (V, T, P, S)
$$

**Component-by-Component Explanation:**

**(1) $V$ ŌĆö Variables (Non-terminals):** A finite, non-empty set of symbols that act as placeholders during derivation. They never appear in the final strings of the language. *Example:* $V = \{S, A, B\}$, where $S$ is the start symbol.

**(2) $T$ ŌĆö Terminals:** A finite, non-empty set of symbols that constitute the actual alphabet of the language. They appear in the final derived strings. *Example:* $T = \{a, b, c\}$. We require $V \cap T = \emptyset$.

**(3) $P$ ŌĆö Production Rules:** A finite set of rewrite rules of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$. The LHS must be non-empty; the RHS can be empty.

*Example productions:*
- $S \rightarrow aAbc$
- $Ab \rightarrow bA$
- $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$

**(4) $S$ ŌĆö Start Symbol:** A distinguished variable in $V$ from which all derivations begin. *Example:* $S \in V$.

The grammar is called "unrestricted" because the LHS of productions is not constrained to be a single variable ŌĆö it can be any non-empty string mixing terminals and variables.

#### (b) Differentiate Unrestricted Grammar from Context-Free Grammar with respect to production rule forms. Give one example production of each type. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Unrestricted (Type-0) Grammar | Context-Free (Type-2) Grammar |
|:---|:---|:---|
| LHS form | $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ | Single variable $A \in V$ |
| RHS form | $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ |
| LHS empty allowed? | No | No (a variable is never empty) |
| RHS empty allowed? | Yes | Yes |
| LHS length | Any positive integer | Exactly 1 |
| Example production | $aBc \rightarrow aABc$ (mixed LHS) | $A \rightarrow aBc$ (single variable on LHS) |
| Power | Most powerful (generates RE) | Less powerful (generates CFL) |

**Key Differences Summarized:**

1. **Single-variable constraint:** In CFG, the LHS must be a single non-terminal. In Unrestricted, the LHS can be a string of any length $\geq 1$ containing both variables and terminals.
2. **Generation power:** Unrestricted grammars can generate languages that CFGs cannot (e.g., $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is not context-free but is generated by an unrestricted grammar).
3. **Computational equivalence:** Unrestricted grammars correspond to Turing Machines; CFGs correspond to Pushdown Automata.

**Example CFG production:** $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ generates $\{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$.

**Example Unrestricted production:** $aB \rightarrow aaBb$ (mixed LHS) is not a valid CFG production.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

#### (a) State the formal definition of an Unrestricted Grammar. What is the only constraint on its production rules? Justify why it is called "unrestricted." **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Formal Definition:**

An Unrestricted Grammar is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:
- $V$ is a finite, non-empty set of variables.
- $T$ is a finite, non-empty set of terminals with $V \cap T = \emptyset$.
- $P$ is a finite set of productions, each of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
- $S \in V$ is the start symbol.

**The Single Constraint:** The only constraint is that the left-hand side $\alpha$ must be **non-empty**, i.e., $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$. Equivalently, $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$.

**Why "Unrestricted"?** The name arises because *all* the usual structural restrictions on production rules are removed:
- The LHS need not be a single variable (unlike CFG).
- The RHS may be empty (unlike some formalisms).
- The LHS may contain terminals (unlike CFG).
- The length of the RHS can be less than, equal to, or greater than the LHS (unlike CSG, which requires $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$).
- The replacement can happen anywhere in the sentential form, surrounded by arbitrary "context."

Thus, the only rule is the non-emptiness of $\alpha$, justifying the name "unrestricted."

#### (b) Show with an example how an Unrestricted Grammar generates the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. State the language class it belongs to. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Grammar $G$:**

$$
G = (\{S, A, B, C\}, \{a, b, c\}, P, S)
$$

where $P$ consists of the following productions:

1. $S \rightarrow aSBC$
2. $S \rightarrow aBC$
3. $CB \rightarrow BC$
4. $aB \rightarrow ab$
5. $bB \rightarrow bb$
6. $bC \rightarrow bc$
7. $cC \rightarrow cc$

**Derivation for $n = 2$ (target string: $aabbcc$):**

| Step | Production | Sentential Form |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | ŌĆö | $S$ |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow aSBC$ | $aSBC$ |
| 2 | $S \rightarrow aBC$ | $aaBCBC$ |
| 3 | $CB \rightarrow BC$ | $aaBBCC$ |
| 4 | $aB \rightarrow ab$ | $aabBCC$ |
| 5 | $bB \rightarrow bb$ | $aabbCC$ |
| 6 | $bC \rightarrow bc$ | $aabbcC$ |
| 7 | $cC \rightarrow cc$ | $aabbcc$ |

**Verification:** All productions have non-empty LHS, satisfying the Unrestricted Grammar constraint.

**Language Class:** The language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is a **Recursively Enumerable Language**, since it is generated by an Unrestricted (Type-0) Grammar. Notably, $L$ is **not** a Context-Free Language ŌĆö no CFG can generate it (proven by the pumping lemma for CFLs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$:** Many students state the production form as $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ without explicitly noting that $\alpha$ cannot be empty. This loses **1 mark** in definition questions.
> 2. **Confusing with Context-Sensitive Grammar:** Students often write $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ for Unrestricted Grammars. This is the rule for **Context-Sensitive** (Type-1) grammars, not Type-0. Unrestricted grammars have **no length constraint**.
> 3. **Misidentifying the language class:** Unrestricted Grammars generate **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** languages, not "all languages" (RE is a proper subset of all formal languages ŌĆö there exist non-RE languages like the halting-problem complement).
> 4. **Forgetting to specify $S \in V$:** The start symbol must be explicitly identified as a member of $V$, not just any symbol.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition:** An Unrestricted Grammar is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$.
- **Production form:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
- **The ONLY constraint:** $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$ (LHS must be non-empty).
- **"Unrestricted" means:** LHS can be any non-empty string mixing variables and terminals ŌĆö not restricted to a single variable.
- **RHS may be empty:** $\beta = \varepsilon$ is allowed.
- **No length constraint:** $\vert\alpha\vert$ and $\vert\beta\vert$ have no fixed relationship (unlike CSG).
- **Also called:** Phrase-Structure Grammar, Type-0 Grammar.
- **Language class generated:** Recursively Enumerable (RE) Languages.
- **Equivalent computational model:** Turing Machine.
- **Position:** Most powerful tier of the Chomsky Hierarchy (above CSG, CFG, Regular).
- **Practical relevance:** Term rewriting, proof assistants, DNA computing models, NLP phrase-structure theories.
- **Classic example language:** $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ ŌĆö generated by an unrestricted grammar, **not** by any CFG.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For this "Definition Only" topic, focus your exam preparation on (1) writing the precise 4-tuple definition, (2) stating the production form with the non-empty LHS constraint, and (3) identifying the language class (RE) and the corresponding machine (Turing Machine). These three elements account for the majority of marks in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chomsky Hierarchy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT413 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chomsky Hierarchy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: The Chomsky Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chomsky Hierarchy (Definition):** The Chomsky Hierarchy, proposed by the linguist **Noam Chomsky** in 1956, is a containment hierarchy of four classes of formal grammars that generate four corresponding classes of formal languages. The hierarchy is strict and nested: every language of a lower type is also a language of a higher type, but not vice versa.

The four types, ordered from the **most restrictive (Type 3)** to the **most general (Type 0)**, are:

| Type | Grammar Class | Language Class | Automaton |
|------|---------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Type 0** | Unrestricted / Recursively Enumerable Grammar | Recursively Enumerable (RE) Language | Turing Machine (TM) |
| **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG) | Context-Sensitive Language (CSL) | Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) |
| **Type 2** | Context-Free Grammar (CFG) | Context-Free Language (CFL) | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) |
| **Type 3** | Regular Grammar (RG) | Regular Language (RL) | Finite Automaton (FA) |

The inclusion relationship is:
$$\text{Regular} \subset \text{Context-Free} \subset \text{Context-Sensitive} \subset \text{Recursively Enumerable}$$

### 1.2 Production Rule Restrictions

> [!NOTE]
> **The Crux of the Hierarchy:** Each grammar type is distinguished by the **restrictions placed on its production rules** of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$.

- **Type 0 (Unrestricted):** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^+$, $\beta \in (V \cup T)^*$, and $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ is **NOT** required.
- **Type 1 (Context-Sensitive):** $\alpha_1 A \alpha_2 \rightarrow \alpha_1 \beta \alpha_2$ where $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$ and $\beta \neq \epsilon$. Equivalent form: $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ with possible $S \rightarrow \epsilon$ if $S$ does not appear on the right side.
- **Type 2 (Context-Free):** $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in V$ (a single non-terminal) and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^*$.
- **Type 3 (Regular):** Either $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear), OR $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (left-linear), where $A, B \in V$ and $a \in T$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "Locks and Keys"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Russian Nesting Dolls of Language":**
> Imagine four nested boxes. The **smallest box (Type 3)** accepts only very simple patterns ŌĆö like "ABABAB" (regular repetition). The **next box (Type 2)** opens up to balanced nested structures ŌĆö like matched parentheses. The **third box (Type 1)** handles patterns that depend on neighboring context ŌĆö like "every 'a' must be surrounded by 'b's on both sides." The **largest box (Type 0)** accepts anything computable ŌĆö it has no structural limits at all. Each larger box can simulate everything the smaller ones can do, plus much more. This is exactly the structure of the Chomsky Hierarchy ŌĆö **strictly increasing expressive power as you go up**, but at the cost of increasing computational complexity (harder to parse, decide, or recognize).

### 1.4 Notation Glossary

- $V$ = Set of **V**ariables (non-terminals)
- $T$ = Set of **T**erminals
- $\Sigma$ = Input alphabet (subset of $T$)
- $S$ = Start symbol (a special variable)
- $\epsilon$ = The empty string
- $\vert w \vert$ = Length of string $w$
- $\Rightarrow$ = Derives in one step
- $\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}$ = Derives in zero or more steps
- $\mathcal{L}(G)$ = Language generated by grammar $G$

### 1.5 Universal Grammar Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A formal grammar is the **quadruple**:
> $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$
> The **Chomsky Hierarchy simply classifies grammars by restricting the form of $P$** ŌĆö the production rule set.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Production Rule Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **The Heart of the Hierarchy:** Everything else (automaton, language class, closure properties, decision problems) follows **mechanically** from the production rule restrictions.

#### Type 3 ŌĆö Regular Grammar (RG)
- **Form:** Every rule is either $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear), or $A \rightarrow Ba$, $A \rightarrow a$ (left-linear).
- **Restriction:** The left side is a **single non-terminal**. The right side has **at most one variable**, and it must be at one of the two ends.
- **Why it matters:** Regular grammars cannot generate patterns requiring **unbounded counting** or **nested matching**.

#### Type 2 ŌĆö Context-Free Grammar (CFG)
- **Form:** $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A$ is a single non-terminal.
- **Key difference from Type 3:** The right side $\beta$ can have **any number of variables and terminals in any arrangement**.
- **Power unlocked:** Can express **balanced/nested structures** like $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$.

#### Type 1 ŌĆö Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG)
- **Form:** $\alpha_1 A \alpha_2 \rightarrow \alpha_1 \beta \alpha_2$ where $\beta \neq \epsilon$.
- **"Context-Sensitive" explained:** The variable $A$ can only be replaced by $\beta$ when $A$ appears in the **specific context** $\alpha_1 \_ \alpha_2$.
- **Equivalent length-increasing form:** $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ (with the standard exception for $\epsilon$-productions involving the start symbol $S$).
- **Power unlocked:** Can express **cross-serial dependencies** like $\{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$.

#### Type 0 ŌĆö Unrestricted / Recursively Enumerable Grammar
- **Form:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with **no restrictions** (except $\alpha \neq \epsilon$).
- **Power:** Universal ŌĆö can express any language that is **recursively enumerable**, i.e., decidable by some Turing Machine.

### 2.2 Corresponding Automata

| Grammar Type | Language Class | Automaton Equivalent | Memory |
|--------------|----------------|----------------------|--------|
| Type 0 | Recursively Enumerable | Turing Machine | Unbounded tape |
| Type 1 | Context-Sensitive | Linear Bounded Automaton | Tape bounded by input length |
| Type 2 | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | Stack (unbounded) |
